members? They came
> > into being to restrict entry into their specialty, e.g.,
silversmith, by
> > requiring a lengthy apprenticeship with a master, finding a
master as a
> > sponsor, etc. It was a way to create a monopoly at the labor
end of the
> > economy, which enabled the guild, of course, to control the
price paid
> > to their members.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jay

    Sounds like we have a union man!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hl2fts41umdc8a3mcsfp7ue2s3au63574a@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <Kd9B5.1468$QF1.222186@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39D2B62D.8F2515E@kg.hsanet.net> <39D57BE6.26AF23A9@optonline.net> <39D630C1.7675F362@jay.jay.org> <8r6imc$4qf$2@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
>> The programming field is a shithole filled with incompetents.
>> The software produced is mostly sewage. A trivial level of
>> qualifications for being a software engineer is necessary.

i'll agree that the software is mostly sewage. the last few upgrades
to most operating systems have been to improve the systems performance
(defragmenters and 32 bits of disk access and caches) not to add
features (like the browser). also, it'd be nice if a certain OS came
with a programming tool to fill in the gaps that the user wants.

i think also, that a trivial level of qualifications should apply, but
i don't think unions have any clue what this trivial level is. if it
consists of a test, there will be cheating on the test. if it consists
of a project, people will complain unfair to this or that group. the
one thing the market holds over the programmers is they can be fired,
so if the product doesn't work, and the owner decides not to maintain
it...

well, you get the picture. but it's a double edged sword, like
everything else is. the more the owner gets productivity, the more the
owner can use that extended productivity to make mistakes.

if nothing else, a union could enforce a certain ratio of how many
apprentices there are to a master. today's class size of 30 or more
(100 or more for universities) is a waste of labor. this would slow
the growth rate of the jobs, and ensure the software is valuable.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<GY4C5.2741$2Q4.15756@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl> <39D2B90D.1926787D@kg.hsanet.net>`

```
"DPH" <kgclg15@kg.hsanet.net> wrote:
> So you picked one of the most violent, corrupt unions around for
> your example.  Why not choose instead another well-know union, the
> Screen Actors Guild.  Have you seen Arnold or Sly shoot at the CEO
> of Paramount or MGM?  Do the old guys with seniority make the most
> money in show buisness without regard to anything but their longevity?

Look, what I disagree with is harrassment of the company and other
employees by striking unions members.  I have no problem with people
forming a labor union, and if they are unhappy enough with their job
situation, all walk off the job.  But then stay the heck away from
the company, and any other employee who want to work.  *That* would
freedom.  But picketing, and forcing people to join unions, and
harrassing people crossing picket lines is extortion, period.  We
put people in prision for extortion, unless it is a labor union.

> Even skilled workers need unions to keep the big business hacks
> (PHBs?) in check.  Without Unions, factory workers would still be
> earning pennies per hour.

Only if they *agreed* to do so!  Duh!

> They don't like it, they can quit and go to another factory that
> pays 2 cents more per hour?  Big whoop.

Don't you know that the great majority of people in this country
work for companies with less than 20 employees?  Last figures I
saw it was 77%, over twice as many as work at larger companies.
Looking at Big Business (which is where unions live) as the prime
issue is like the tail wagging the dog.

> There wouldn't be a middle class without unions - that's the way
> it was heading until unionization hit the country.

Duh!  There wouldn't have been a large middle class without mass
production.  Unions had nothing to do with it.  When productivity
is low, there *cannot* be a large middle class, because there simply
aren't enough goods to supply a large middle class.  When productivity
is high, there *must* be a middle class, or there is no one to design,
build and run the many machines that do the production.  Without the
middle class there would be no one to buy all the goods.  It was
people like Henry Ford (jackass that he was) that did more than any
other factor to create a large middle class in America.  Other factors
that are necessary is an educated population and a free market.  Labor
unions *reduce* productivity, and actually work against the forces
that eventually build a large, prosperous middle class.

> Virtual slave labor, child labor (because the familes had to
> contribute the kids to the workforce just to eat), and so forth.

Perhaps you haven't heard, but there are child labor laws.  Which
do you think management fears most, a strike or being thrown in
prison?  A strike, or OSHA shutting down their plant?  A strike
or the EPA shutting down their plant?  If all the work that has
been put into organizing labor unions had been put into seeking
releif through political means, the situation could have been
corrected sooner, with little or no bloodshed.  And millions of
poor, ignorant working people wouldn't be burdened by corrupt
unions which, in many cases, could care less about the members.

What is the difference between greed in the boardroom and greed on
the picket lines?  Greed is greed, period.  I would rather see
greed than violence against the innocent.
```

- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl> <39D2B90D.1926787D@kg.hsanet.net> <GY4C5.2741$2Q4.15756@news1.atl> <8rdm63$ims$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Interesting point, philots, but we're in the computer software game 
here.

On Tue, 3 Oct 2000 22:22:02, philotsopher <philotsopher@my-deja.com> 
wrote:

  
> Oh phooey. Unions have to get tough because companies will try every
> dirty trick to destroy the union. What you advocate is taking all the
> real power unions have and giving it back to management.

Generally us programmer types aren't in unions.  The reason, we provide 
good value for the buck.
  
> Duh to you!!! Companies will just wait until the people are starving
> because hungry people can't choose to work for higher paying companies.

Again, as programmers and assorted cornputer cornsultants our problem 
isn't that we'll starve.  It's that some of us are Unca Toms.  These 
Unca Toms think that it's unseemly to earn a living wage because, by 
gum, if they earned $10,000/year in 1970 on their first job that a 
cornsultant is being uppity by presenting an invoice for that amount for
ten day's work.

It's not the company, if you think the CEO cares how much you bill, 
you're mistaken.  The CEO cares about share prices, perception 
engineering, and his personal bottom line to include his options.

The problem is the Unca Toms, maybe they once knew this stuff, maybe 
they never did. 
  
> Unions were the reason for the middle class. If it was up to business,
> 99.9% of the U.S. population would be paupers. Companies will never
> share the wealth unless forced to do so.

So, start your own company.  Go pure indie.  Your pals know what you can
do.  If you're any good, are serious about cranking code, they'll hand 
you work and won't care that you bill $60/hour, $80/hour, $100/hour or 
whatever your market value is.  Become what you fear.

Here's the choice.  They can pay you the company's money or they can 
stay at the office all night instead of cuddling with their trophy wife,
a 32 year old tanned, cut, poofed, super-vixen, semi-pro, beach 
vollyball player.  (This description fits one of my pals, I'm not 
saying who.)

In fact, any company will reward a mid-manager who "effectively manages 
a multi-million dollar IT project assuring successful completion." 
Well, it ain't a multi-million dollar project unless a bunch of 
cornsultants get paid their fair share of the bucks, -nudge-nudge-, you 
get the picture?
  
Fact is, most IT projects fail unless staffed by talent with the right 
stuff. 

> They fear strikes the most. Most of these corporate types have rich
> lawyers and never worry about going to jail. The also know that courts
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  

rich lawyers, strike fear?  Do a google search for "Wolff" and "Byrd" 
for some really scary lawyer stories, soon to be a major moovee from 
Universal.  

> --
> philotsopher
```

"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> hehe. i often wonder who threw the first punch between unions and
> companies.

That is easy to answer.  Greedy company owners tried to squeeze the
workers as much as possible, instead of permitting them to benefit
reasonably from the profits of their labors.  Their greed bread the
desire and motivation for labor unions, and now they're stuck with
the monsters.  Unfortunately, so are the poor workers, and the rest
of society.  The workers (though I sympathize with their situation)
created a monster to fight a monster, and now we all have to live
with both.  The problem was never capitalism, it was greed.  Someone
forgot that Union bosses are just as subject to greed as company
owners (or Socialist dictators).

Just look at what happened when Henry Ford raised the daily wages in
his plants to an unprecedented $5 a day.  His profits soared.  They
seem to forget that lesson, too.  Sigh.
```

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>

- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D995D2.CA5E3EF6@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl> <39D2B90D.1926787D@kg.hsanet.net> <GY4C5.2741$2Q4.15756@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> > Virtual slave labor, child labor (because the familes had to
> > contribute the kids to the workforce just to eat), and so forth.
> 
> Perhaps you haven't heard, but there are child labor laws.

Judson, time for a chill pill, okay? It took the US a long time &
several trips through the Supreme Court before we had child labor laws.
Prior to that the 9 wise men struck them down & affirmed the child's
*right* to work 12 hours/day, 7 days/week. Unregulated capitalism (free
enterprise, if you will) is pretty brutal. Without the law or a union on
their side, the workers have no leverage at all and are powerless. They
have to work 7 days/week, because the boss posts a sign saying "If you
don't come in on Sunday, don't bother to come in on Monday". Also, it
wasn't just the workers who died in the Triangle Shirtwaist Co. fire in
NYC earlier this century, many of their children did, too. The women had
no one to look after them, so they brought the kids to work with them.
They were ALL locked in, of course, when the fire broke out. I'm sure
you've also heard of "No Irish need apply".

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<l%lC5.4545$2Q4.29973@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl> <39D2B90D.1926787D@kg.hsanet.net> <GY4C5.2741$2Q4.15756@news1.atl> <39D995D2.CA5E3EF6@optonline.net>`

```
"Liam Devlin" <liamddd@optonline.net> wrote:
>
> Judson, time for a chill pill, okay? It took the US a long time &
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> you've also heard of "No Irish need apply".

Believe me, I despise the abuses of management as much as anyone,
and I understand the difference in power between a large company and
one individual.  I do not believe in a total laisser-faire, where
businesses can wield their power unrestricted.  But remember, it is
a very different thing to do as you wish with what you own, and
forcing someone else to do what you want with what they own!  Let me
explain the essence of what I see the problem with unions, as they
are today, to be.

First, as free citizens we should have the right to join any union
available (or *not* join any union), or work to form one with other
employees.  Reality is that many workers today are required to join
the union to get a job, and where workers technically have a choice,
they are frequently harassed and threatened into joining the union.
Because the union mentality is based on forcing others to their will,
it comes naturally to those who think along those lines, and the
unions actively encourage such thinking.  Coercive extortion is
their stock and trade.

When a person takes a job with a company, it is like other forms
of exchange, such as buying and selling.  The deal is made when it
is of sufficient benefit to both parties.  If one party is unhappy
with the exchange, they are free not to make it.  Consider this
example.  If a large group of people wanted to buy some product at
a better price, they could form a cooperative, approach the dealer
with the offer "We will buy your product at such-and-such price, or
we will all buy elsewhere."  If the group is large enough, it can
put considerable pressure on the dealer.  And this fair, because
there is no coercive threat, certainly no threat of force.  Since
everyone is free to deal with whomever they like, and the dealer is
free to seek other customers, both parties are respecting the
rights and property of the other.  Now consider a similar situation
with unions.  If the unions simply said to management "If you do
not comply with our requests (*not* demands), we will not work for
your company."  If the company complied, fine, if they did not,
the union workers would quit.  This would be comparable to the
fair marketing exchange above, and I have no objection to such a
situation.  But in actuality, when the unions strike, they make
enormous efforts to harass and interfere with the company's
operations, harass any workers crossing picket lines, etc.  Many
people have been seriously wounded, killed and/or their property
damaged, for crossing picket lines.  Now we are way, way out of
bounds of any definition of 'fair exchange'.  Methods such as
these would be correctly labeled "extortion", under any other
circumstances.  Any free citizen or group of citizens should have
the right to deal with or work for anyone who is willing to deal
with or hire them, or refuse to do so, for any reason they like.
But no citizen or group of citizens should have any 'right' to
forcefully extort others to comply to their will.  Unions, as they
are set up today, are tools of extortion, pure and simple.  Their
whole psychology is ordered that way.  And sadly, there have been
laws passed to support them in activities that would otherwise be
illegal.  Collective Bargaining would be fine, but Collective
Extortion is what they practice, and that should be a crime, as it
is anywhere else.

As far as "No Irish need apply", and other forms of discrimination,
let me first say that, though I was born and raised in Alabama, I
am neither bigoted or prejudiced.  I have three daughters, the two
oldest are married, the youngest is 13.  Though I am white, if my
daughter were to fall in love with a man of another race, my only
concern as far as race goes, is that such marriages are subject to
great social pressures from the outside, and children of mixed
marriages can be shunned by some members of both races.  My daughter
would tell you I have expressed the same thing to her.  I want her
to marry a good man who will love her, be faithful and good to her.
They will have a much easier time of it if he's white, but I have
no moral or ethical objections if he's not.

That said, I believe that if someone owns a personal business, they
should be able to hire whomever they like.  If they are partial to
left handed, red headed Irishmen, then fine, let them hire all they
can find.  People should be able to have a club of all white, all
black, all Hottentots, or all anything else, if they want.  People
should be able to associate with, or not associate with, whomever
they want.  People should be able to sell their home or other
property to anyone they want, or refuse to sell it to whomever they
want, for whatever reason they like.  This people, is called freedom.
I might despise someone who is a bigot, but that doesn't mean their
freedom to be one should be abridged by law!  Laws are about keeping
people from doing damage to someone else, not about being a jerk!
The government, or any publicly held company, should be completely
unbiased in hiring or otherwise doing business.  But if a person
owns their own business, it should be their right to run it as they
see fit, as long as they don't damage other people.  Refusing to do
business with someone is *not* "damaging" them.  When we start
restricting people's freedoms because we find them unpleasant or
unsavory, that is a very dangerous form of tyranny!  The new Hate
Crime laws that are currently being enacted are bad, bad, bad!  If
someone ties a man to a truck and drags him to death, what does it
matter if it was because the man was of a different race, or because
it was over a woman?  If a man is tied to a fence, tortured and left
to starve, what does it matter if it was because he was a homosexual
or because he was ugly?  Execute the murderers because they murdered
other human beings!  I'll be happy to flip the switch or pull the
trigger on such a person; I'll even pay for the bullet or the
electricity.  But when you start making what someone *thinks* or
*believes* a crime, you are heading for serious trouble!  Why do we
want "thought police" patrolling our country?  Are we insane?  Who
is to determine which thoughts are legal and which are not?  What
if a parent shoots a pedophile who is in the act of molesting their
small child?  Is that a hate crime if the parent happens to despise
pedophiles, but okay if the parent doesn't?  Who is to decide?  This
is a bad, bad road to start down.  The last century should have
taught us better than this, the result of such screwed up 'thinking'.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> Consider this
>example.  If a large group of people wanted to buy some product at
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>put considerable pressure on the dealer.

when was the last time you grouped together with anyone to purchase a
large number of items? this example just doesn't exist. when i need to
buy a car, do i do this? nope, and that's a big thing. how about razor
blades? nope and thats a small thing. in fact, i can't remember a time
i ever got together with other people to buy things, other than,
perhaps, a plane ticket, and that was still a singlular purchase. i
believe the only reason we pooled resources at that time was because
we needed to stay on the same plane, not because we saved money. and
since we paid a deposit, there was no, we'll take your offer or go
somewhere else, it was, the dealer gives us an offer and we pay money
down or find a different plane. and even then, i have to admit that
the air traffic system had a monopoly on the airports.

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Whooee, did you miss that one! ;-)  Probably every county in the United
>States has at least one Farmer's Cooperative store.  What do you think
>stores like Sam's Club purport to be?  Not all of them actually function
>as such, but many of them do.  What do you think group insurance policies
>are based on?  Ever heard of AARP?

the aarp doesn't buy any physical product. i don't know what a farmers
coop is, but i do know that sams club doesn't get together a bunch of
people to buy an item, rather, a bunch of people pay membership dues
and get discounts for products.

ok, my gripe with the sams club example is they don't purchase a
specific product. same with insurance. i'm looking for something along
the lines of buying a physical good that everyone gets a consensus on.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kykD5.12540$bg6.69686@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl> <39D2B90D.1926787D@kg.hsanet.net> <GY4C5.2741$2Q4.15756@news1.atl> <39D995D2.CA5E3EF6@optonline.net> <l%lC5.4545$2Q4.29973@news1.atl> <tqkkts8trkep3n4qikils331ctcklmudqi@4ax.com> <nj%C5.504$5l.3181@news1.atl> <904qts8vtcpeeimrprl8odje9ktv6m6nl1@4ax.com>`

```
"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the aarp doesn't buy any physical product.

AARP arranges group discounts for its members.

> i don't know what a farmers coop is

Obviously.  It is precisely the type of cooperative which you say
does not exist.

> but i do know that sams club doesn't get together a bunch of
> people to buy an item,

Neither do unions get together a bunch of people to accept jobs,
either. :-)  They represent a pre-existing group of people on
a whole host of job related issues.  Other cooperatives do the
same thing, on one or a number of issues of interest to the group
of people in the co-op.

> rather, a bunch of people pay membership dues and get discounts
> for products.

Unions pay membership dues to supposedly get better job conditions.
What's the difference?

> ok, my gripe with the sams club example is they don't purchase a
> specific product. same with insurance. i'm looking for something
> along the lines of buying a physical good that everyone gets a
> consensus on.

Insurance isn't a "specific product"?  Which "specific product" are
unions for?  Higher wages, shorter hours, better pension, safer
working conditions, more job stability, what?  Your seem to be
having real difficulty making a point here. :-)

Are you telling me that unions only act by consensus?  Do you really
believe all those union members really read and understand all those
contracts prior to voting?  Like sheep, they do what the union bosses
tell them to do.  They have been so brainwashed by union propaganda
(which is why they belong to a union in the first place, of course)
that many actually believe the union bosses are concerned about the
member's best interests.  It would be funny, if it wasn't so sad.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:24:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i3vutsch1q722047jf3d06sna2tn80apov@4ax.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>> i don't know what a farmers coop is

>Obviously.  It is precisely the type of cooperative which you say
>does not exist.

what i said i couldn't find an example of was a series of people
pooling their money to purchase a good. not a service in the future,
not a series of goods over time, a good today. like a consensus
purchase on a mainframe brand (from the 70s). except that was a
topdown decision also. it just doesn't exist. you cannot get together
with a bunch of people to make a group decision with money, because it
is like "herding cats".

>> rather, a bunch of people pay membership dues and get discounts
>> for products.

>Unions pay membership dues to supposedly get better job conditions.
>What's the difference?

the dif is there is no physical product.

>> ok, my gripe with the sams club example is they don't purchase a
>> specific product. same with insurance. i'm looking for something
>> along the lines of buying a physical good that everyone gets a
>> consensus on.

>Insurance isn't a "specific product"?  Which "specific product" are
>unions for?  Higher wages, shorter hours, better pension, safer
>working conditions, more job stability, what?  Your seem to be
>having real difficulty making a point here. :-)

i'm looking for a product which doesn't exist, it would seem rather
likely i can't make a point, because i have yet to find a group of
people who, by consensus, reach an agreement towards the purchase of a
*physical* good. not a service sometime down the line.

>Are you telling me that unions only act by consensus?  Do you really
>believe all those union members really read and understand all those
>contracts prior to voting?  Like sheep, they do what the union bosses

the only thing i like about the union boss is that at one time, they
did work in the fields. i'd rather have that kind of person than
someone who got an MBA and that is their qualification.

>tell them to do.  They have been so brainwashed by union propaganda
>(which is why they belong to a union in the first place, of course)
>that many actually believe the union bosses are concerned about the
>member's best interests.  It would be funny, if it wasn't so sad.

and yet, without the unions, we'd probably be back on the railroad.
which isn't a bad thing, but i'd like to think the product's cost has
something to do with the person selling it.

like a standard commission. except it isn't standard, and they know to
set the commission at a living wage through precedent.

and the free market really doesn't work, especially where research is
concerned. because thieves at the top win, and so does loyalty at the
bottom.

You mean like a group buying a home?

```
"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> >"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> pooling their money to purchase a good.

So you contend that labor unions "pool their money to purchase a good?"

> >Unions pay membership dues to supposedly get better job conditions.
> >What's the difference?
>
> the dif is there is no physical product.

Please explain the "physical product" labor unions provide? :-)

> >> ok, my gripe with the sams club example is they don't purchase a
> >> specific product. same with insurance. i'm looking for something
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> i'm looking for a product which doesn't exist,

On the contrary, it appears you are struggling unsuccessfully to
create a definition of something to satisfy your contention that
cooperatives do not exist, because you opened your mouth without
putting your brain in gear.  Of course, this is the natural state
of those who think labor unions are a Good Thing. :-)

> and the free market really doesn't work,

That's a really amazing statement!  Then how do you explain the
continued prosperity of the United States, where the free market has
more reign than in any other developed country?  Other countries
have more natural resources, and surely U.S. citizens don't corner
the world in intelligence.  But the United States is the premiere
country in the world in promotion of personal freedom and free market
enterprise for over 200 years.  Has it escaped your notice that, in
a period of the decline of labor unions, the United States has seen
it's longest period of economic growth in history?  Are you aware
that there are quite a number of recent millionaires in the U.S. who
are/were common workers who had stock options in their companies?
And you say "the free market really doesn't work."  Duh!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unionization the answer? Can IT people learn to follow politics and lobby?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00

Sjince the first U.S. Supreme Court decision regarding labor unions was
in the 1830 - 1850 time frame from what I recall from Economics 101
which I took in 1957-8, I doubt that the Communists were the first to
come up with the idea of unions or to support them.  Incidentally that
decision found them a restraint of trade and interstate commerce.  Note
that I am oversimplifying, in part because I remeber this from over 40
years ago.

Clark Morris, cfmtech@istar.ca   

Phillip Johnson wrote:
> 
> >  snip
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> see that in fact the labor union movement grew out of the socialist movement.
```

- **References:** `<39C84CD0.59970393@dio.com> <39C5ECB6.2BCDDEC9@kg.hsanet.net> <39CAFB14.E7DD4E23@optonline.net> <8qg2qd$965$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BPsz5.358$583.32719@news1.onlynews.com> <39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <39D49948.CAD12A43@brazee.net> <qB2B5.11134$bD5.69664@news3.atl> <VaeB5.1632$144.83024@news1.onlynews.com>`

```
keith smyth wrote:
> 
> Communists believe in central control.  Unions take away from central
> control. 

They both believe in central control, as does business - as long as THEY
are doing the controlling.  

Programmers who believe that they have control over their careers are
unlikely to join unions.  Programmers who believe that they have no
control over their careers are likely to support an organization which
can counter whomever DOES have control.

Craft unions gained their initial power right after the plague killed
off most workers in Europe.  The politicians still wanted to build their
monuments, but there weren't enough workers to go around.  Supply and
demand shifted power to the workers.

Labor unions gained power when there were a few large employers which
offered all of the best jobs in a community.  The workers didn't have
much choice, and thus they didn't have much power.  The unions had
enough power to make the companies negotiate with them.

The programmer of today can hawk his skills all over town, and soon, all
over the world.  The company of today can look for skilled workers all
over the world.  So what is the role of a union?  Where is the union's
power base?  What can it provide the worker that the worker doesn't
already have?
```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>The programmer of today can hawk his skills all over town, and soon, all
>over the world.  The company of today can look for skilled workers all

i have yet to find a job which doesn't require at least *at the
machine* typing. this would ensure that unions which work locally can
still function. on the other hand, i have been the target of people
who boycotted products, but because of my minority-type status, they
couldn't touch me. it's really an interesting thing, because on one
hand they want to say it's bad, but on the other, the person who needs
to use that product represents what they are trying to help. except
they didn't want to actually do any work to help anyone. they just
wanted to look like they were. ok, this was a school thing, but it's
their fault for creating a social system and then expecting us all <s>
to pull together in times of need.

i'm not sure if this applies to unions, because i have yet to see any
system whereby people doing equal jobs get equal pay in any fashion.

>over the world.  So what is the role of a union?  Where is the union's
>power base?  What can it provide the worker that the worker doesn't

> Ever hear of a company using violence to suppress union

guns into
> crowds of striking workers to break up the strikes (Ford was

> the rest, which was the whole point. I suspect management is
more
> cautious in recent times because unions have legal recognition
and

happened to

matter.

```
"Liam Devlin" <liamddd@optonline.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Odd point here, IMHO.

Well, union members have many times 'beat the crap' out of people who
didn't want to join the union.

> Ever hear of a company using violence to suppress union activity?
> Henry Ford Harry Bennett who hired goons in the 30's to fire machine
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the IWW (the "Wobblies" back in the late teens).

Yes, companies did respond to unionizers with force.  But they never
responded with force to anyone who simply did not want to work for
them, which is what I meant.  Unions do force people to join them,
and often harass people into joining when there is a choice at the
company where they work.  The point is that no one should *ever* be
forced to join a union to get a job.  This is tyranny, not freedom.

Believe me, I despise those management types who abused their workers
just as much as I despise the unions who abuse their members, and
others.  In my opinion, the Ford managers who decided not to fix
the gas tanks on the Pinto, because they thought the lawsuits would
be cheaper, should have been criminally prosecuted for manslaughter
at least, if not murder.  They *knew* people would be killed.
```

...

Thought about that for a while.  I read some early u.s. social history
once.  It seems that we always behaved that way.  Get off the boat, get
some money, spend it on the biggest flashiest trash you can.  Over
consumption appears to have always been a national character and wo the
one who tries to impede it. I recall the Whiskey rebellion, when the
government wanted to tax whiskey, all hell broke loose.  In the 1700's
and early 1800's u.s. per capita whiskey consumption was something over
five gallons a year!
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ron Todd <rltodd@ix.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers

- **References:** `<enODdtoo9NcB-pn2-IHlsjbuDKmC5@localhost> <8q5rf7$7sq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <0Awx5.794$D83.53886@nnrp3.sbc.net> <39C6A322.DFAF296@blarney.att.net> <39C75CF5.BD7D151E@bellatlantic.net> <39C76BAF.E831ECD4@brazee.net> <39C7BA1C.346875E@jay.jay.org> <39C7CBF8.48CDD91B@brazee.net> <8q9v9j$prq$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


Margaret Bartley wrote:
...

> US economy and the Mexican economy is that in the US we have
> minimum wage laws that result in working people having money
> to spend. ....

Nonsense.  The difference between the U.S. and Mexican economy is (1)
they tolerate a much higher degree of institutionalized corruption than
we do and (2)they really are socialist.

Up until the (appx) 1910 revolution Mexico was doing quite well and was
a favored place for European immigration.  After the revolution, the
movers and shakers pulled up their capital and headed for other places. 
Many have done quite well in U.S. business.

The only rational behind the minimum wage laws are political.  They kill
opportunity and force people in to sanctioned pursuits.
```

If your business is strictly intrastate, then you come under your
state's minimum wage law which can be very different than the Federal
Minimum Wage Law (and is usually lower).

their neighborhood.

"keith smyth" <k17731NO@SPAMMhome.com> wrote:

>For years the software industry has worked on reducing the skill and
>intelligence required to program computers.

>For years the software industry has worked on creating packages to replace
>custom written systems, in order to reduce the number of programmers
>necessary to provide a function, or to eliminate them entirely.

and anytime they want to do anything outside the custom system, not
only are they back to square one, but usually, it takes more effort to
connect through the interface.

at least with the un-compiled version, you can see whats happening,
under the hood. the first few times a programmer will attempt to use
dll's, or ole or linking, but after that, they throw up their hands,
and scream at the software producer.

>For years the hardware and operating systems industry has worked on making
>operating systems and hardware easy enough to install without the need for
>specialist help.

ever go to microsoft? there is a knowledge base chock full of articles
on how well their OS works. i will not even mention the pages of shit
that linux and mainframes have. i got a db2 manual because i wanted to
see how easy it'd be to understand mainframes. 11 megs.

>And for years educators have worked on making elementary computer
>programming a skill ever person graduates with.

they've been doing the same for algebra, yet how many people who
graduate can even do a simple equation?

>These groups aren't there yet ... but they aren't done yet.

>That aside, I think you would concede that one day Visual Basic will be
>obsolete, and VB programmers will face the same difficulties that COBOL
>programmers face today, even discounting age discrimination.

about a year ago, i looked in the ads for employment and they had
something called pic basic or business basic or someshit. that
software package was 16 bits and hasn't been sold in many years. do i
think vb will die? answer: the bigger the market and the longer the
maintenance, the longer it can stay around. it's the snowball effect,
where the program takes on a life of its own because it can't be
changed between the interaction.

>Some of us even imagine a world where the computers largely program
>themselves.

u r dreaming. that would be akin to saying, there is going to be a
search engine which indexes code, and when a uhman requests it, finds
the code, in the proper language, and connects it to the interface.

whats the closest we have today? a human who can look at webpages,
find source code examples, compile them, test them and verify the
answers. in each step, the human is still in the cogs of the machine.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
In article <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com>,
  "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:
> I think this program is pretty good, but I see one problem...
>
> programmers, even good ones, come along and assume, with such thorough
> comments, that the comments are correct.

Hi:

I think the same is true of paragraph-names.  Just because the
paragraph is called DETERMINE-KUMQUAT-QUOTIENT doesn't necessarily mean
that that is what it does. It might have when the program was
originally written, but, over time, it might have been changed and
the change not documented, nor paragraph re-named.

Anyway, in my minimalist fashion, I don't use meaningful para-names.
Instead, I use short names which serve merely as a reference point
within the program. If you want to know what the code in a para does,
then one must read the code.

My paragraph-names look like this:

B.
   BLAH BLAH
B10.
   BLAH BLAH
   GO TO BX.
B20.
   BLAH BLAH
BX.
   EXIT.
C.
   and so on.

Short, meaningless names are easier to use. The code is less
cluttered than with big, long, 'meaningful' paragraph names.
They take less time to type.

A comment on comments - I have code which I wrote years ago
and I hope and pray I never have to figure it out.  It is not
so much what the program does, but more as to WHY it does what
it does in the way that it does it that requires comment.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Are you familiar, Mr Dilworth, with the aphorism of 'Penny wise and pound

Bingo... Mr Dilworth, please remember that in order to be allowed to do
their jobs quite a few other programmers are expected to code in a fashion
which would allow the archetypical two-year programmer to be able to read,
understand and modify at 2:AM.

The rest of the 'Coding Universe' appears to obey different laws than the
ones which you claim apply in the confluence of circumstances which have
formed the 'Dilworth Anomaly'.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20HB5.7893$l35.159266@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

> Universe' to build, maintain and expand systems the size of which -
both

makes

Hi:

Kindly explain why the standard of coding should vary

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001001161630.04855.00000057@ng-fn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>Are you saying that you have one set of 'standards' for small
>programs and another set for large?
>
>Why should a paragraph-naming convention be dependant upon
>application size?

Um, PMFJI to such a nice exchange, but I DO think that there are different
standards for large systems of programs as opposed to small ones.

In large systems of programs, the extra time spent in observing careful
structure, verbose naming conventions, and things like paragraph numbers in
which control Levels are implicit (B1000 instead of 1000, eg), error messages
that clearly identify the area of code emitting them, and other careful
crafting, pay off in a matter of hours once you hit System Integration test.

In small systems of programs, or small single programs, this extra effort is
often 
unappreciated as it has a low immediate ROI. 

Now, in an IDEAL world, this would not be true, but when faced with real world
schedules and budgets, there are different standards applied.  I don't say I
like it, though.
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D88BC6.320D943A@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20HB5.7893$l35.159266@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Kindly explain why the standard of coding should vary
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> application size?

A large system will be maintained by multiple programmers of varying
abilities.  These programmers will NOT be intimately familiar with each
program and subroutine.  

Since maintenance is an expensive task (and downtime is even more
expensive), programs of all sizes should be designed to make maintenance
easy.  If you are the only person doing maintenance, and if you are very
familiar with your code, you may have techniques which work for you -
kind of like being able to read your own handwriting.  A project which
is too large for these assumptions will require that you consider the
other people who will be doing maintenance.
```

- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20HB5.7893$l35.159266@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D88BC6.320D943A@brazee.net>`

```

>
> A large system will be maintained by multiple programmers of varying
> abilities.  These programmers will NOT be intimately familiar with
each
> program and subroutine.

Of course they won't, and the odds are that if the system is not new
it will have been developed using different standards, styles, methods,
not to mention umpteen different software thingies,etc. and it would be
unreasonable to expect anyone to be familiar with them all as well as
the entire application. I worked on big systems at one time, too.
>
> Since maintenance is an expensive task (and downtime is even more
> expensive), programs of all sizes should be designed to make
maintenance
> easy.


Couldn't agree more.


 If you are the only person doing maintenance, and if you are very
> familiar with your code, you may have techniques which work for you -
> kind of like being able to read your own handwriting.  A project which
> is too large for these assumptions will require that you consider the
> other people who will be doing maintenance.

I don't agree that size should affect your standards.

I just don't see why everybody hates my pithy paragraph-names
so much.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D9E2CA.C520BCE0@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20HB5.7893$l35.159266@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D88BC6.320D943A@brazee.net> <8rchqm$i4n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> I just don't see why everybody hates my pithy paragraph-names
> so much.

I guess we're at heart, lazy.  We want to be able to look at a PERFORM
with a pretty good guess about what it will do.  We would rather get our
maintenance done quickly and easily and go on to other things.

We weren't born hating obfuscated code - we learned to hate it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T07:58:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DAD528.E8581991@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20HB5.7893$l35.159266@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D88BC6.320D943A@brazee.net> <8rchqm$i4n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> I just don't see why everybody hates my pithy paragraph-names
> so much.

It is probably because they have been given programs like that to work
on where the original author didn't understand either.

There is a vast difference between the writing of programs for your own
needs (where you can later utilise your memory of what it does), and
writing for others' needs.

What _you_ see as 'easy', 'clear' and 'obvious', others may see as
obscure and disjointed.  Having to read every line to  find out what the
program does is time consuming and error prone.

Of course you don't 'see' this, there has been no mechanism to feed back
to you that you are travelling the wrong way.  Yes, your style is easy
to write and saves key-strokes, you p[robably don't bother with a design
stage either being experienced enough to have several designs in your
head to call on.

Your code won't survive you though.  It will be tossed away like a cheap
thriller rather than be savioured like a novell by the next generation
(or whoever your clients may try to get to look after it).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


> Mr Dilworth, I am saying that what you do for your small set of
programs - programs which only you touch - may not be appropriate for
systems which are not subject to the 'Dilworth Anomaly'.

Hi:

I think that they are appropriate regardless of the size of the
system, therefore, all systems are subject to the 'Dilworth Anomaly'.

How do you know how small my set of programs is?

> >BTW, how do you know what I am familiar with? All you know is
> >what I currently do.
>
> Have you forgotten, Mr Dilworth?  What you're currently doing, by
your own assertion, is what you've been doing for decades.
>
> DD

That's right, it worked then and it works now. The reason I do
things the way that I do is because it is fast, inexpensive,
efficient, produces dependable and maintainable code, is highly
portable, easy-to-use, self-explanatory, never fails, etc. etc.,
so why should I change?

Can you say the same about your efforts?

BTW, why don't you tell us about your standards, methods, procedures
etc. rather than just sneering at things which you don't like? Better
yet provide us with an example of your work so that we can judge
you better and possibly learn from your example.

For what it is worth, I continue and will continue to post to
this forum because I think that newcomers should have an alternate
point-of-view. See the thread 'Only for COBOL newbies'.

I am perfectly willing to present samples of my work in the
forum, are you?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

> Okay, hold on . . .
>
> How can you claim efficiency in light of:
>
Hi:

There is efficiency of development and there is efficiency of
execution. I give little concern to efficiency of execution
because the computers are so fast.  I am, therefore, more
interested in efficiency of development. If a program may
be inefficient in execution, there are lots of ways to speed
it up.

>
> > produces dependable and maintainable code,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone *other than* the original author doing the maintaining.

I don't agree at all.  A decent programmer should be able to
see whether his code is clear and should provide appropriate
comments at the time it is written, when necessary (Yes, I know that I
said elsewhere that I have code which I would be reluctant to approach.
But not much.)

> > is highly portable, easy-to-use, self-explanatory, never fails, etc.
> > etc., so why should I change?
>
> Frankly, I'm skeptical. It isn't as if you were the first programmer
to
> give his own code and development methods a glowing review . . .

I merely state facts and offer an alternate, minimalist approach. I
wonder why you all are so hostile and sarcastic?

All I know is that I have about 600 users all over the world and
I NEVER receive calls at 3 in the morning or any other time. The
ONLY time I receive calls are when the customer has had some sort
of hardware problem usually occasioned by a power failure. I have
customers who have been using my software for more then ten years
and they have never called once in that entire time.

Again, I state facts. People accuse me of bragging and other
horrible things. All I do is state facts.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D9E07B.3050863C@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> There is efficiency of development and there is efficiency of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it up.

There are occasions where your style of programming will speed up
execution.  There aren't occasions where it will make it easier for
someone else to maintain your code.


> > With all due respect, you are in no position to judge your own code's
> > maintainability, since maintainability is based on the concept of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> But not much.)

Pretty much true - but until you have maintained other people's code,
written in various style, you really cannot compare.

I can guess that OO CoBOL can be hard to debug in an enterprise
environment - but I would lose any argument with someone who had
experience with both types of CoBOL in enterprise environments.

> I merely state facts and offer an alternate, minimalist approach. I
> wonder why you all are so hostile and sarcastic?

The hostility comes from having maintained various styles of code -
after swearing at a programmer for failing to use structured programming
styles, it is easy to resent it when someone tells the world to do more
of the same.

There is a strong correlation which we find in the code we maintain
between the style of the code, how well it works, and how easy it is to
maintain.  Sure, there is some good unstructured stuff, and certainly
some bad structured code.  But everybody with experience maintaining
other people's code with these various styles sees this obvious
difference.
```

can it be to optimize spaghetti as compared to structured code?

> > > produces dependable and maintainable code,
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> approach. But not much.)

A "decent programmer" will not write spaghetti in the first place, for a
"decent programmer" will consider structured code, based on a solid
structured design, to be more clear than spaghetti code with interpersed
comments.  It's far easier to maintain a small paragraph of structured
code with comments in the header block than to maintain a ten-page block
of spaghetti with interpersed comments.

Trust me, I've been there.

> > > is highly portable, easy-to-use, self-explanatory, never fails,

CB] the hacked kludge working before sun up.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rhrr7$tt9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rd5rn$40j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

>
> Also, again, the actual coding of a program or system represents as
> little as 5 percent of the overall life cycle of that program
orsystem,
> so why sweat over that 5% when it will have such an adverse effect on
> maintenance (which can represent as much as 75 percent of the life
> cycle)?
>

Perhaps this is the crux of the problem. If the 5% were
greater, the 75% would be less.  Think about that for more
than a millisecond or two.

> Nobody is claiming that your code doesn't *work* Mr. Dilworth. What I
> keep harping on is the issue of maintenance, and it's an issue that
you
> seem to ignore.

Sorry for ignoring it, but I don't have a problem with maintenance.
Some of the software in my POS software systems was written by
some one else. Most of the code was originally written in 1986. I
am extremely responsive to customer requests and normally incorporate
sensible requests which may mean maintaining the other person's code
written in the eighties.  So, I am maintaining them all the time and
I also enjoy tinkering with them to make improvements in appearance
or whatever. Every program was written in strict accordance with a
comprehensive set of standards all designed with ease-of-maintenance
in mind.

There is a lot more to my minimalist methodology than you are aware
of. My standards cover far more than paragraph-names and extend to
every aspect of system development even to the extent of COBOL
code generators written my me in COBOL. (I also have a screen design
program for DOS written in Pascal replaced by the excellent SP2)
To judge me for my meaningless paragraph-names alone is to
judge me in the dark - they are part of the methodology.

Perhaps, if you could see the entire picture, your opinion might be
different, you might even learn a thing or two - ha ha! If nothing
else, perhaps I am entitled to the benefit of the doubt, from a
professional point-of-view, of course.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 14)_

```

On  5-Oct-2000, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

> > Also, again, the actual coding of a program or system represents as
> > little as 5 percent of the overall life cycle of that program
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> than a millisecond or two.

Reality check here - the original comment asserted that since more time is
spent in maintaining and enhancing the program than is spent developing it,
additional effort should be spent up front to reduce the back.  My
millisecond of thought leads me to believe that you just said the same
thing.

> There is a lot more to my minimalist methodology than you are aware
> of. My standards cover far more than paragraph-names and extend to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> professional point-of-view, of course.

Personally, I'm impressed by anyone who has built a successful business. 
The fact that you have customers, keep them happy and make a living from
your own efforts scores you pretty high on my impress-o-meter.

We're all ears (well, eyes anyway).  Can you post your methodology?  As I've
said, I'm always ready to look at other's methods.  And I, for one, am here
to learn from a successful (if controversial) veteran.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:12:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rj5d5$34c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rd5rn$40j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rhrr7$tt9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8rhrr7$tt9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> than a millisecond or two.

Well, let's recall your statement to which I was responding:

TD] I am, therefore, more interested in efficiency of development.

Presumably, your objective is to "make development faster" so that you
can code up a module in, say 16 hours, whereas I, with my structured
methods, may take a whole 24-36 hours.  Is that not what you mean by
"efficiency of development?"

Now, your spaghetti-coded module only took 16 hours to develop, and mine
took perhaps twice as long, ergo, I'm "not efficient" based on this
particular criterion. However, consider that this module will be running
in production for, say ten years, and during that time, government
regulations will change, or the company will be bought out, or the
markets will change, or a dozen other things may happen that will
require changes to that module.

Six years from now, when some junior programmer is working on that code,
he will thank me for laying things out so even he, with his minimal
grasp of the business rules and shop standards, can easily grasp what
the module is doing, where it's doing it, and why. He can zero in on the
paragraph(s) that will be affected by whatever change he needs to
implement, and can easily verify that the rest of the paragraphs in that
module will not be affected.

If he has to modify your module, he will have to contend with GOTOs that
go who knows where, he will have to contend with paragraph names that
are meaningless, he will have to deal with spaghetti.  How much time
will he have to spend figuring out what your module does, where it does
it, and why?

So great, you managed to code your module in half the time mine took,
but at this point in time six years from now, who bloody well cares?
Which is more efficient to maintain?

*THAT* is what I meant when I said, "why sweat over that 5% when it will
have such an adverse effect on . . .  as much as 75 percent . . . ?"

Be honest, did you "think about that for more than a millisecond or
two?"  ;)

> > Nobody is claiming that your code doesn't *work* Mr. Dilworth. What
> > I keep harping on is the issue of maintenance, and it's an issue
> > that you seem to ignore.
>
> Sorry for ignoring it, but I don't have a problem with maintenance.

That you personally "don't have a problem" with it doesn't matter,
because *the industry as a whole* DOES have a problem with it, a major
problem.  Like I've said before, the odds of working in a "large" (i.e.,
more than one programmer) shop are much higher than working in a one-man
shop. Also, like I've said before, at some point in time, *your* code
will have to be maintained by someone other than you, or else be
scrapped.

> Some of the software in my POS software systems was written by
> some one else. Most of the code was originally written in 1986. I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> in mind.

"Ease of maintenance" by whom, exactly? By someone already familiar with
the business, its programs and systems, and who hasn't changed his
methods in decades? What about the junior programmer who doesn't know
jack about the business, and doesn't have decades of experience in
programming?

> There is a lot more to my minimalist methodology than you are aware
> of. My standards cover far more than paragraph-names and extend to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> judge me in the dark - they are part of the methodology.

I can only judge by what I perceive. As I've stated elsewhere, you admit
to writing spaghetti and using meaningless paragraph names. Also, when
deriding the EVALUATE statement, you posted a code snippet that was
***NOT*** functionly equivalent to the EVALUATE example that was posted.

Like I said, I can only judge by what I perceive.

> Perhaps, if you could see the entire picture, your opinion might be
> different, you might even learn a thing or two - ha ha!

Anything is possible, but keep the context of this discussion in mind; I
have used your methods (spaghetti) and moved on to better ones, while
you have remained with "what worked thirty years ago." Who's in the
better position to make value judgements with regard to methodologies
and techniques, the one who has used the old and the new, or the one
who has only used the old?

> If nothing else, perhaps I am entitled to the benefit of the doubt,
> from a professional point-of-view, of course.

Perhaps, but again, I an only judge by what I can perceive. Feel free to
fill in any blanks any way you see fit.


Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rd5rn$40j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rhrr7$tt9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rj5d5$34c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I read a book (I think it it was "Gates" by Steven Manes, but I'm not sure)
in which Bill Gates was quoted saying that IBM did not 'get it', as a major
result of several months of Microsoft contract work had resulted in a forty
percent reduction in the number of lines of some IBM-written code, and IBM
was trying to renegotiate the price based on the low net klocs (thousands of
lines of code) the Microsoft team had produced.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rofg90lb6@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rd5rn$40j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rhrr7$tt9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rj5d5$34c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I agree 100%.  It is much wiser to spend the time up front coding, 
documenting and testing, testing, testing thouroughly.  As I like to say,
test now, or fix later.  Once that system goes live, you won't want to have
to touch it.  The time you spend typing, punching in code, is irrelevant in
the big picture.

And what about the business; our consumers.  All they care about is it's
correct.  Correctness is the #1 goal, right?

They don't want to have programs that bring down the system.  If things are
written to Shop Standards, then the odds that a programmer can make changes
sucessfully is much higher.  Isn't this what it's all about really?  A
computer is just a machine ya know, and a programmer is kinda like an
engineer or a mechanic.

I still stand on the basis of Shop Standards as the key to maintainability.
I started this whole mess remember.  I'm not proud of it really; I didn't
think this discussion would have made it this far.  I said originally, that
Shop Standards would make such comments unnecessary.

This is like the Dilworth Anomaly, yes DD, since there are no Shop
Standards, then there doesn't need to be a structured mentality.  Imagine
Dilworth having to write code for a Big Shop, and have to go thru code
reviews and stuff.  hehe

Jeff

----------
In article <8rj5d5$34c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, cbass2112@my-deja.com wrote:


> Now, your spaghetti-coded module only took 16 hours to develop, and mine
> took perhaps twice as long, ergo, I'm "not efficient" based on this
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> it, and why?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T08:15:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DD7C19.8DACE1D3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rala6$24h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgjh$h98$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rd5rn$40j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rhrr7$tt9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> > Also, again, the actual coding of a program or system represents as
> > little as 5 percent of the overall life cycle of that program
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> than a millisecond or two.

Yet it was you, Tony, that was advocating that writing meaningful
paragraph names was a 'waste of time' and short meaningless names saved
keystrokes even if it meant that the whole program code had to be read
to work out what each paragraph did.  So you were advocating techniques
to reduce initial coding at the expense of later work on the code (even
if you failed to notice that).

Also, maintenance is _not_ about fixing up programs to make them work as
designed, it is about making them do different things because the
business model has changed or because of new requirements.  The 75% over
the life time is irreducable (in general) because programs are initially
written to specifications at that time and are not written to
unformulated and unknown future specifications. 

Yes, taking more care over writing the initial code does ease the
problems of later maintenance.  Your 'solutions' do not work in anything
other than very restricted environments such as one man bands (even if
there are two working with much the same style).


> > Nobody is claiming that your code doesn't *work* Mr. Dilworth. What I
> > keep harping on is the issue of maintenance, and it's an issue that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sorry for ignoring it, but I don't have a problem with maintenance.

No. _YOU_ don;t have a problem because you _know_ what the code does
when you look at it because you rely on your memory.  That is there is
no need for you to communicate to the future in your _code_.  Where the
problem lies is in someone new attempting to maintain your code where
your memory is not part of what they will be looking at.

> Some of the software in my POS software systems was written by
> some one else. 

Presumably under your direction and following your 'style'.

> Every program was written in strict accordance with a
> comprehensive set of standards all designed with ease-of-maintenance
> in mind.

Exactly, though from your comments I don't see 'comprehensive' as being
valid. Perhaps you mean that in 1986 you had a coder 'do it Tony's way'.
 
> There is a lot more to my minimalist methodology than you are aware
> of. My standards cover far more than paragraph-names and extend to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> judge me in the dark - they are part of the methodology.

I judge you on what you say.  Not so much for your 'minimalist
methodology' but for the rejection that you have of everyone else's
ideas.  I can recognise your position and sympathise with where you have
gone, I, too, have worked alone for many years.  It is very tempting to
take minimalist views and rely on memory.  However, I have grown out of
that and recognise to failings that the others deride you for.

 
> Perhaps, if you could see the entire picture, your opinion might be
> different, you might even learn a thing or two - ha ha! If nothing
> else, perhaps I am entitled to the benefit of the doubt, from a
> professional point-of-view, of course.

Been there, done that, grew up.
```

Hi:

Your cave comment is silly.

From what I have seen of newer methods, I do not see any
advantage to using them over the methods I use. See the
thread 'Only for COBOL newbies'.

I think OO is a good example. People go on about OO as
though it was the answer to everything. Judging from
the posts on OO, it seems that hardly anyone really gets it,
hardly anyone actually uses it, and I don't like it.

So there.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Obviously you don't.  Equally obviously, people who have maintained

methods do see the big advantage.
 
> I think OO is a good example. People go on about OO as

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rd4de$2ja$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4c1$g6l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgth$hji$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8rcgth$hji$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
> In article <8rb4c1$g6l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
>   cbass2112@my-deja.com wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your cave comment is silly.

It may be "silly" to the recipient of the implied criticism, but it is
accurate nonetheless.

> From what I have seen of newer methods, I do not see any
> advantage to using them over the methods I use.

You've *seen* the methods; have you actually *tried* them? Also, is the
fact that you "do not see any advantage" in no way due to your not
*wanting* to see any advantage?

You have posted a coding example to justify not using EVALUATE, but the
code sample is not functionally equivalent to the EVALUATE example:

http://x62.deja.com/[ST_rn=ps]/threadmsg_ct.xp?AN=670543266.1

You admit to writing spaghetti code:

http://x62.deja.com/[ST_rn=ps]/threadmsg_ct.xp?AN=670349085.1

You admit to using meaningless paragraph names:

http://x62.deja.com/[ST_rn=ps]/getdoc.xp?AN=676164548

Yet you call your approach "minimalist" which gives it far too much
credit. Your methods aren't "minimalist," they are simply archaic.  Yes,
your code works, but the point you keep not getting is that your methods
are:

a) Not maintainable, your claims to the contrary notwithstanding, and

b) Not scalable.

You keep asking,"why have different standards for different sized
projects?" but that's the wrong question; the same standard should apply
to all projects regardless of size. The problem is that your
"minimalist" methods do break down when a project size expands beyond a
one-man shop and moves into the realm of a dozen (or a hundred)
programmers producing 50,000 (or several million) lines of code spread
out over a couple of dozen (or a few hundred) source modules.

Your methods also break down when someone other than you is doing the
maintenance.

These are facts learned painfully over decades of software development
practice. These facts are why the methods you disdain were developed in
the first place; they were not created in an ivory tower vacuum.

If you wish to ignore the lessons of history because you are unwilling
to learn something new, that's fine. However, don't expect me or others
like me to remain silent when you try to foist your methods on newbies,
because they don't need to learn such outdated methods, beyond
understanding *why* they are outdated.

The odds of their working in a multi-person shop are far greater than
the odds of their working in their own one-programmer shops, and even if
they were lone programmers working in one-man shops, at some point,
their code *will* have to be maintained by someone else, or simply be
scrapped.

That holds true for your code as well, Mr. Dilworth.

> See the thread 'Only for COBOL newbies'.

I have *participated* in that thread, Mr. Dilworth:

http://x62.deja.com/[ST_rn=ps]/threadmsg_ct.xp?AN=672056914.1

http://x62.deja.com/[ST_rn=ps]/threadmsg_ct.xp?AN=671413104.1

http://x62.deja.com/[ST_rn=ps]/threadmsg_ct.xp?AN=671627295.1

The last one discusses scalability.

> I think OO is a good example. People go on about OO as
> though it was the answer to everything.

Anyone who goes on about *anything* as if though it was "the answer to
everything" should be taken with several grains of salt. However, that
should not necessarily invalidate whatever it was that they were "going
on" about.  One must learn to separate facts from hyperbole.

> Judging from the posts on OO, it seems that hardly anyone really gets
> it,

How would you be able to know? Do *you* get it?

> hardly anyone actually uses it

You're kidding, yes?

> and I don't like it.

Well, that doesn't surprise me.

Look, I understand the object paradigm as well as the structured
paradigm. I understand where each should and shouldn't be used. I do
*not* understand advocating spaghetti code, nor do I understand how
anyone could claim spaghetti code to be "maintainable" or "efficient."



Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
