- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4c1$g6l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rcgth$hji$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

>
> I think OO is a good example. People go on about OO as
> though it was the answer to everything.

It is,  this is my opinion and I am entitled to it.  I respect that your
opinions differ and request that you respect my opinions in the same way.
I regularly find that you lack this respect and therefore your opinions get
like response.


> Judging from the posts on OO,

It is hard to 'get' from a procedural frame.  It is easy to 'get' from a
non programming background because your do not have to unlearn things.
This is an interesting issue don't you think!

You have invested years learning how to do programming your way in a safe
reliable fashion (maintainability by someone else is a totally different
issue notice).  To change this takes a fair amount of personal training and
commitment.


> hardly anyone actually uses it.

In this industry there are no golden bullets!   Work is required for all
real solutions.  The  density of books on OO-cobol is increasing telling me
there isa market.  I hope that these changes start small,  I have seen to
many big bang failures (CS90 - Westpac Australia is one).

> and I don't like it.

This really is the point isn't it.  Is this really relevant, you have said
it,  you are entitled to say it again to remind us.   I would prefer it was
not so regular and in totally unrealted threads though!
```

- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Never fails, eh?  These are awfully lofty statements for anyone, in any 
profession.  These types of statements make some people immediately de-value
or dis-credit you.

Part of being a good programmer is understanding when you are wrong, and
taking advice.  And not acting like your methods are the only way to write
good code.

The question is, what impact, percentage wise, does your coding style have
on meeting the goals stated below?

What else can you attribute your amazingly unbreakable programs to?  Also, I
have no desire to see source code.  Thanks.

Jeff


----------
In article <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
wrote:


> That's right, it worked then and it works now. The reason I do
> things the way that I do is because it is fast, inexpensive,
> efficient, produces dependable and maintainable code, is highly
> portable, easy-to-use, self-explanatory, never fails, etc. etc.,
```

NOT call because my software fails.

I am sorry if this annoys you.

have
> on meeting the goals stated below?


I think the style makes it all possible.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T08:26:33+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DCF208.4C7BEA4B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4gn021ct@enews3.newsguy.com> <8rchae$huc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DAE798.F57A2871@zip.com.au> <8rhsei$uds$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> >
> > This does not mean the software does not fail, it just means they do
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> for two days either.

User's actually work around problems and not tell you,  this is a fact of
life.  Perhaps you have a memory overrun and if they reboot the program
then works.   They have now got a work around and simply do not tell you.

Perhaps they have an incredibly complex way of using your software,  they
follow procedures for years without change,  they just do not work
efficiently.   This is as much a bug as a crash.

Perhaps there is a message on a report that does not affect anything but
there is a still a problem.  For example if you have this message add $50
onto the total to balance.  These are things I have seen people actually
do for years before I sat next to them when they were doing their work.

There is no such thing as bug free!

Ken
```

Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> Never fails, eh?  These are awfully lofty statements for anyone, in any
> profession.  These types of statements make some people immediately de-value
> or dis-credit you.

When he works on these programs which never fail, he has some huge
number of bug fixes per hour.  

I have been trying to picture how someone could be working on 60 (I
don't remember his figure) bugs in the same week, much less the same
hour.  Maybe he misspells environment division and counts the 60 errors
it made as 60 bugs.

Maybe the inferior structured style I use just hides bugs so that I
don't find them and they don't effect the results.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC8DD2.8C9DA673@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4gn021ct@enews3.newsguy.com> <39D9DBBA.EEDA636B@brazee.net> <8rhsjs$uhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> 
> I am sorry, but I do not know what all this is about. What 60 bugs?
> Please explain.
> 

It might be my memory playing tricks on me.  Someone who advocates a
drop through structure was saying that he can fix a certain number of
bugs per hour.  I don't remember the number, but 60 sounds about right.  

I figure he must be a sloppy coder who gets 60 syntax messages on his
first compile and fixes them.  

But if they really are 60 bugs, my hat is off to the programmer.  To
discover them, analyze them, fix them, unit test the programs, system
test the programs, document the fix, and put them into production in one
hour is pretty good.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mb5D5.6710$Ly1.76538@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4gn021ct@enews3.newsguy.com> <39D9DBBA.EEDA636B@brazee.net> <8rhsjs$uhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC8DD2.8C9DA673@brazee.net>`

```

On  5-Oct-2000, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> > I am sorry, but I do not know what all this is about. What 60 bugs?
> > Please explain.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> bugs per hour.  I don't remember the number, but 60 sounds about right.

I think he said that he could write 70 debugged lines of COBOL per hour,
besting the old saw that a COBOL programmer averaged about 6 lines of
debugged code a day.  60's babble.

I don't think most organizations put much stock in that old stuff.  They
used to count the planning time (zero lines per day), bathroom time (zero
lines per ...), meetings, HMO presentations, vacation, ad nauseum.  How do
you quantify this crap?  If I use a copybook, do I get to count the lines? 
If not, do I get credit if I just paste the copybook stuff in my program? 
Are they hard or easy programs?

And... debugged lines?  It simply means that you've found all the problems
you're capable of finding, not all the problems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T08:24:55+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DD7E47.F5D4215@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4gn021ct@enews3.newsguy.com> <39D9DBBA.EEDA636B@brazee.net> <8rhsjs$uhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

> > I have been trying to picture how someone could be working on 60 (I
> > don't remember his figure) bugs in the same week, much less the same
> > hour.  Maybe he misspells environment division and counts the 60
> errors
> > it made as 60 bugs.

> I am sorry, but I do not know what all this is about. What 60 bugs?
> Please explain.

The number was 76.  You claimed that you fixed 76 bugs per hour.  

I suspect that you really meant that you wrote a program and debugged it
and dividing the lines by hours gave 76 debugged lines per hour, but you
didn't phrase it well.

While this number may well look good against quoted 10 or 11 lines per
hour in corporate sites, I can claim much higher rates over sjort time
scales, such as 100,000 line new system running live after 8 weeks from
go ahead (including system design and installation).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DF7858.1D072C0D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <8r7lvq$qci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <BeJB5.7916$l35.160331@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r7u85$e7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <vFQB5.7961$l35.163121@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8r9q6j$bu1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rb4gn021ct@enews3.newsguy.com> <39D9DBBA.EEDA636B@brazee.net> <8rhsjs$uhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DD7E47.F5D4215@Azonic.co.nz> <d1ruts4vm6k456dkv6123s2e0q90g87t63@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote:
> 
> well, if you knew the bugs, or if the bugs were well-defed (such as
> make this y2k compatible) 76 per hour is only about 1 per minute.

Well, if they were 76 occurrences of the same bug in one program, and
there was no analysis necessary, we could correct all occurrence in 5
minutes, compile into a test development, spend 20 minutes unit testing
all possibilities, 20 minutes in system test, and 15 minutes in
implementation - if the documentation is done simultaneously with system
testing.

But there is ALWAYS some analysis necessary, and I wouldn't call 76
occurrences of the same Y2K problem in a program 76 bugs.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<39D889E3.9554144B@brazee.net>`

Foodman wrote:
> 
> I think the same is true of paragraph-names.  Just because the
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> it does in the way that it does it that requires comment.

I can see the advantage of that.  But it fits your philosophy where you
know what you have written.  People who use structured code like being
able to glance at a paragraph and have a good idea what each performed
paragraph does.  In the middle of the night, we can pull out someone
else's code and quickly see the structure and point to relevant code.

Sure, it occasionally is a lie, but unless we're into OO, we quickly see
the lie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T08:30:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D98B33.41CD049A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D889E3.9554144B@brazee.net>`

```
> Foodman wrote:
> >
> > I think the same is true of paragraph-names.  Just because the
> > paragraph is called DETERMINE-KUMQUAT-QUOTIENT doesn't necessarily mean
> > that that is what it does. 

Is this a comment on your _own_ code ?  You should try to improve your
naming technique.

> > It might have when the program was
> > originally written, but, over time, it might have been changed and
> > the change not documented, nor paragraph re-named.

Why has the name not been changed ?  Why do you change the content of
the paragraph without renaming ?

> > Anyway, in my minimalist fashion, I don't use meaningful para-names.
> > Instead, I use short names which serve merely as a reference point
> > within the program. If you want to know what the code in a para does,
> > then one must read the code.

If you designed a new language would you use short meaningless names for
the statements and functions on the basis that 'if they want to know
what a statement does they can read the machine code' ?

> > Short, meaningless names are easier to use. The code is less
> > cluttered than with big, long, 'meaningful' paragraph names.
> > They take less time to type.

I, too, primarily work alone and have the luxury of setting my own
standards.  However, I moved on from what I did in the 60s.  I inhereted
some systems written by an associate where he had a style similar to how
you write.  He had started programming in the 50s on a LEO machine and
retired.

I can recognise both sides of your argument.  I, too, can look at my
code and know exactly what it does without needing 'meaningful'
paragraph names, I can also look at the inhereted code and see the
problems when someone writes in your style and it has to be maintained
by someone else.

Presumably when you retire the whole lot will be binned and your
customers will get other software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8raub5$a11$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D889E3.9554144B@brazee.net> <39D98B33.41CD049A@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:39D98B33.41CD049A@Azonic.co.nz...

<snip>

> However, I moved on from what I did in the 60s.  I inhereted
> some systems written by an associate where he had a style similar to how
> you write.  He had started programming in the 50s on a LEO machine and
> retired.

This brought up a memory.  The single WORST COBOL program I ever had the
misfortune to examine (some twenty years ago now) was originally written as
a cross-reference and flowcharting utility for COBOL programs.  It was
obvious from the code that the author had clearly spent most of his/her
programming career writing in some early dialect of FORTRAN (numeric labels,
most subscripts in the form "I", "J", etc., following the default integer
typing of such items in that language, and so forth).

It was also clear that this person had very recently discovered the ALTER
verb, thought to him/herself "That's really neat!", and used this
application as a good place to explore, and even exhaust, its possibilities,
even in environments in which it added no functionality.   As a result, the
entire program had as I recall been structured around unnamed GO TO's in
ALTERed paragraphs; named GO TO's to unaltered labels were few and far
between.

By the time I examined the program, only the cross-reference portion was
still functional; the flowcharting part had been disabled and in large part
(but by no means totally) excized.

Figuring out the program flow was well-nigh impossible, and all my efforts
at deleting what, based on internal evidence, was dead code involving the
now-disabled flowcharting functionality (to help clarify the program flow!)
were unsuccessful.

The utility, along with the platform on which it ran, has long since been
rendered obsolete.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

by someone with a lot of experience with S/360 Assembler. It used 8-char
names, lots of GO TO's, etc. It wasn't really a COBOL program, it was an
Assembler program written in COBOL notation. (if I remember, I'll try to
find it & post it for the world to see. It's not very big and might be
of interest to some. Might also spark some lively debate about COBOL, of
all things <g>).

LiamD

... but then you ran it throught a Restructuring Program... and now
instead of

GO TO GRLMBLZM

... it reads ...

PERFORM D9321570-GRLMBLZM-ROUTINE THRU D9321570-GRLMBLZM-ROUTINE-EXIT.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rf5ct$mnp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D889E3.9554144B@brazee.net> <39D98B33.41CD049A@Azonic.co.nz> <8rcidi$iit$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DAE934.6DE5B794@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <39DAE934.6DE5B794@zip.com.au>,
  waratah@spamcop.net wrote:
> Foodman wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

Hi Ken:

One reason I don't is for the newcomers. Obviously, if the entire
CLC consisted of dwarves, it would be pointless other than to
raise their ire.

As you well know, the world of DP, sorry, IT, is a horrible
hodge-podge of different languages, etc. As you also know,
finding people with the exact skill-set required can be
difficult.

This hodge-podge makes it costly to develop and run applications.

Judging by the code presented by students, the standards of
teaching COBOL seem to be pretty poor.

I believe that my minimalist approach is cost-effective on many
levels. Keeping things simple just has to make sense. If you
don't agree with that then I don't know what to say.

Again, I bring up the thread 'Only for COBOL newbies'. In case
you don't remember, I found it silly that new programmers
would be given an umpteen step process to accomplish elementary
funtions. My five-step alternative was derided. All I can
say is that if people choose to use umpteen-step activities when
the same can be accomplished with five, then, again,
I don't know what to say. Perhaps they should take up
time-and-motion study.

Anyway, maybe someone will benefit from my posts. It might make
life simpler if those who consistently sneer at my stuff to just
ignore it. On the other hand, they might think about it more.

So, it is an alternative point of view for the newcomers. Expect
more in the future.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth






Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

>

How about 'Gosh... maybe there *are* things unthought-of in the Dilworth
Anomaly!'?

(such as... Production Standards Reviews)

>

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>time-and-motion study.

Mr Dilworth, time-and-motion studies (pioneered, as everyone knows, by
Frederick Winslow Taylor in his 1914 classic 'The Theory of Scientific
Management') are just *fine* for situations which require a human being to
be reduced to mechanised procedures.  Programmers are sometimes called
'knowledge workers'.

>
>Anyway, maybe someone will benefit from my posts. It might make
>life simpler if those who consistently sneer at my stuff to just
>ignore it. On the other hand, they might think about it more.

Oh, I *cannot* resist... 

... and maybe they'll see just how *not* to do things, as well.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T08:46:41+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DBA541.3B648B49@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D889E3.9554144B@brazee.net> <39D98B33.41CD049A@Azonic.co.nz> <8rcidi$iit$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DAE934.6DE5B794@zip.com.au> <8rf5ct$mnp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> As you well know, the world of DP, sorry, IT, is a horrible
> hodge-podge of different languages, etc. As you also know,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> This hodge-podge makes it costly to develop and run applications.

100% agree.   This unfortunately includes a hodgepodge of writing using
the same language.

> Judging by the code presented by students, the standards of
> teaching COBOL seem to be pretty poor.

Agreed.  They should be taught to use:

- Meaningful variable and section names.
- Sub programs to break up programs
- To use 'end' constructs to make their code stronger.
- To know how to develop an application not a single two page program.

> I believe that my minimalist approach is cost-effective on many
> levels. Keeping things simple just has to make sense. If you
> don't agree with that then I don't know what to say.

I agree strongly with this however using the full production techiniques
must ingrained from day one for students,  knowing WHEN to break them is
another matter this takes experience.

I have also found when I skimp on my coding standards

a)  That one off program ends up running daily.
b)  Following good coding practice has saved me time, not following it
has cost me time.

The question is, what is minimalist, what is rapid application
development techinques that are reliable in the setting that you are
working.   Is is just possible that the techniques that are using are
working because you are a single programmer shop?

Ken
```

- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com> <8r7ab2$ifv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39D889E3.9554144B@brazee.net> <39D98B33.41CD049A@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On  2-Oct-2000, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> > > Anyway, in my minimalist fashion, I don't use meaningful para-names.
> > > Instead, I use short names which serve merely as a reference point
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> what a statement does they can read the machine code' ?

My worst assignment was working on a system written by a guy who believed
that paragraph names and variables should be named after cars.

In another case, I looked at a FORTRAN system where all the variables were
swear words.  I ran from that one, though.

There are still people who try to put this in every COBOL program:
ADD VERMOUTH TO VODKA GIVING MARTINI.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comment on comments

- **Date:** 2000-10-01T02:31:24+00:00

- **References:** `<20000930094644.23407.00000275@ng-ft1.aol.com> <x6qB5.5224$bK6.965365@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <8r61oe02rqg@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
well actually I wrote this program and the rest of the system it is part of
in college. the reason for the comments is that the instructor INSISTED on
them I actually wrote the program and came back and put the comments in I
felt at the time they were a waste of time myself and almost deleted them
after I turned in the project. I left them in in this post because I thought
that maybe the person asking for them was looking for something to help him
teach himself cobol and I thought the comments might help. but I appreciate
the compliment that it is good.

Thank you very much
Doug_xx2
```

on �Hate Incidents, 1999)

________________________

CALIFORNIA)
Visalia �������������������������������� Sept. 16, 1999
Michael McGee, 23, was sentenced to 32 months in prison for shouting
racial slurs at a black man.

Lancaster �������������������������������� May 10, 1999
Three white men, Jonathan Bexley, 18, Samson Thomas, 21, and Lloyd
Gregory, 19, were charged with a hate crime for allegedly yelling
racial slurs and threatening to kill a Latino man at a gas station.

NEW YORK)
Camillus �������������������������������� Feb. 16, 1999
Scott C. Miller, 20, pleaded guilty to second-degree
aggravated harassment as a bias crime and first-degree harassment for
yelling racially biased threats at a black woman at a mall in June

(NEW JERSEY)
Bridgewater ���������������� October 1999
A 16-year-old high school student was charged with allegedly sending
anti-Semitic and racist e-mails to more than 100 classmates.

(ILLINOIS)
Gurnee ���������������� April 13, 1999Eric D. Hanson, 24, was convicted
of assault and disorderly conduct for committing a hate crime when he
threatened a biracial couple outside a grocery store in February. In
May he was sentenced to one year in prison.

Peoria ���������������� April 25, 1999
Two white men were arrested after they allegedly yelled racial epithets
at a group of black people. Joel Banda III, 25, was charged with
disorderly conduct and hate crime and Heath Garland, 26, was charged
with obstructing police.

________________________

This is just a sampling. Each and every case listed above WAS
considered a ��Hate Crime��, and NONE involved violence or any physical
altercation whatsoever. All consisted of nothing more than name-
calling, hollow threats, or �Hate e- mail�.

Serious �Hate Crimes� (murder, aggravated assault, rape, robbery,
arson, burglary, etc.) Account for only 0.0115% of ALL serious crimes
committed in the US. (Source: FBI Uniform Crime Reports)

Do �Hate Crimes� deserve all of the attention that the media and
politicians are giving them? Do they warrant the money spent on them?

More importantly, are they worth sacrificing the First Amendment?

Waldo

Observer at Large

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net
>
> >As to the 'hate crime' situation, I can't see how 'hating' a class
can
> >elevate any of the above to the more severe crime above it (well,
> >maybe Murder could be promoted to Capital Murder).
>
> Ahhhhhh... but if intent or mental state is a necessary element of
the
> offense as you assert, and hatred is a component of one's mental
state
> then hatred is a component of a necessary element.  As to whether
*you*
> see it or not... well, there's some aspects of some laws that
confuse
> someone at just about any time.  Ever hear of the OAT Agreement (or
> Exemption or whatever it was they called it)?

I should have been clearer. Mental state is an element, but not all
mental states. Our state defines Malicious, Intentional (willful),
Reckless, and Negligent as possible mental states when categorizing a
crime. Others, such as humble, giddy, lustful, greedy, euphoric,
blank, confused, etc., are not part of the criminal justice lexicon.

I've not heard of OAT Agreement. I have heard of Oat Willy (a
character in an underground comic). Is that the same thing?
```

states supplied the lawmakers who generated the statutes!)

DD

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net>

> >>
> >> Really... then whence came hatred?  I thought it was now included
among
> >> the various mental states; has something changed?
> >
> >Not in my state (Texas). Sorry about yours.
>
> No need to apologise for one of the facts of State Law (the laws of
every
> state are not the same)... but I thought there was something to do
with
> this (hate-motives) at the Federal level, as well.  If I'm wrong, of
> course, it would be nothing new.

It wasn't an apology; it was an expression of pity.

You're correct in that the Federals have their own laws ("Ve haf our
vays"), but the number of prosecutions for federal criminal laws is a
vanishingly small percentage of all criminal prosecutions. Most people
don't realize, for example, that the FBI spends maybe 80% of it's time
on two crimes: Interstate transportation of stolen cars and stolen
Postal Money Orders.

I think we have more state courts handling criminal matters (including
Justices of the Peace) in my county (and there are 254 counties in
Texas) than there are Federal District Courts in the entire state.

live with it, work to change it or find someplace else more to your
liking; in the United States of America it is designed so *just* this
phenomenon (dissimilar laws by state) can occur.

>
>You're correct in that the Federals have their own laws ("Ve haf our
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

in Texas hatred was not considered among the various mental states
considered when a crime is assessed.

level.  Federal actions take place in Texas; the inescapable conclusion i
that the consideration of hatred as a mental state when a crime is
assessed *does* take place in Texas.  It may not be by Texas legal
personnel enforcing Texas laws, true... but it *does* happen; your
assertion has been shown to be wrong.

followed by a statement of pity, was, by your own admission, wrong.

factually-challenged.

- **Message-ID:** `<wE8D5.506$PU4.74997@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <329C5.78$0a3.82672@nnrp1.sbc.net> <0M9C5.10126$l35.172118@iad-read.news.verio.net> <_EPC5.1231$rw.66808@nnrp2.sbc.net> <g8RC5.12820$l35.189742@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net
>
> I think this bit of trivia does not alter the fact that your
assertion,
> followed by a statement of pity, was, by your own admission, wrong.
> Perhaps you might consider reserving your pity for the
> factually-challenged.

Actually I pity several groups. One group, as I mentioned, live under
a legal system corrupted by feel-gooders. That's truly a shame. But I
don't pity the factually-challenged: that can be fixed.

What does engender a sigh and a shake of the head are those who know
something that is wrong, or, in the present case, using the exception
to prove the rule.

I've done some research. As I said earlier, there are no hate crimes
in Texas (we don't recognize such a silly, silly thing). While the
federals do recognize some (politically correct) forms of hate as
being worthy of sanction, in the 14 months the law has been on the
books, I've been able to find no federal prosecutions in my state (and
damn few, if any, anywhere else).

Therefore: There are no hate crimes defined by my state's laws, and
there are no hate crimes worthy of attention by the federals in my
state.
```

- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers

- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <39D49575.C6396B12@brazee.net> <39D534B8.421F8A5@blarney.att.net> <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net> <PhkB5.7672$l35.149437@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:
> 
> Eh?  'Murder is murder'?  I'm not a lawyer so I now need to ask... aren't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> state before and after the act?

But I argue with this as well.  If someone gets angry and kills his
wife, he should not get a lesser sentence than if someone gets greedy
and kills his wife.  He is not a lesser threat to society.  He is not
less likely to do a similar crime in the future.

People learn to respond with anger at a young age, and they condition
themselves to make that response.  Just because they decide ahead of
time how to respond instead of thinking about it at the time, doesn't
make them less culpable.  And it certainly doesn't make them less
dangerous.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<95CFF8C714E301C6.A2DA4C6F0392BB5E.B82D390885BD5C3F@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <39D49575.C6396B12@brazee.net> <39D534B8.421F8A5@blarney.att.net> <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net> <PhkB5.7672$l35.149437@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39D8914A.69F7BF32@brazee.net>`

```
On Mon, 02 Oct 2000 07:44:42 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
enlightened us:

>NA wrote:
>> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not necessarily.  First degree murder is generally defined as
pre-mediated murder or murder of a police man or certain other
government officials.  It is slightly different depending on what
state (Arizona, Texas etc) you are in when the murder occurs.  The
person's mental state usually only comes into play as part of a
defense, i.e., not guilty by reason of insanity.

Generally, all other murders are second degree.  A murder that is
accidental, i.e. occurred during a fight, or is the result of some
other illegal act is defined as manslaughter.

>But I argue with this as well.  If someone gets angry and kills his
>wife, he should not get a lesser sentence than if someone gets greedy
>and kills his wife.  He is not a lesser threat to society.  He is not
>less likely to do a similar crime in the future.
>

In what you described above, both would most likely be second degree
murder unless pre-planned.  The motive for the killing would be used
by the prosecution to explain the act and would have more bearing
during the sentencing portion of a trial.  The minimum in most states
for second degree murder is 15 years to life.

>People learn to respond with anger at a young age, and they condition
>themselves to make that response.  Just because they decide ahead of
>time how to respond instead of thinking about it at the time, doesn't
>make them less culpable.  And it certainly doesn't make them less
>dangerous.

Very true.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Why does Sea World have a seafood restaurant? I'm halfway through my
fishburger and I realize, Oh my....I could be eating a slow learner.

http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Steve
```

In article <39D8914A.69F7BF32@brazee.net>,

>NA wrote:
>>

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:39D8914A.69F7BF32@brazee.net...
> NA wrote:
> >
> > Isn't the highest of these - first degree murder - based on one's
mental
> > state before and after the act?
>
> But I argue with this as well.  If someone gets angry and kills his
> wife, he should not get a lesser sentence than if someone gets
greedy
> and kills his wife.  He is not a lesser threat to society.  He is
not
> less likely to do a similar crime in the future.

But he does. Society seems to believe that homicide in the heat of
passion is less of a threat than, say, a contract killing. 'Passion'
killings are so-called 'crimes of opportunity' and, unless the same
circumstances recur, are unlikely to be repeated.

Here's something that will shock you.

Drive by a prison. See the trustys outside the prison mowing the
grass, sweeping the walks, etc.? Virtually all Outside Trustys are
murderers. Why? Because they are basically rule-followers: "Go mow the
grass, come back at 5:00" and they do. Robbers, burglars, and the
like, PLAN to break the rules (laws) in advance and have no respect
for the rules.

Hundreds of years of penology experience proves the above rule.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D7519F.38AAFD8C@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <QHdA5.6985$583.294276@news1.onlynews.com> <k7b5tssdb0rclbaqam5ran5104ngtl1adl@4ax.com> <3URA5.1964$HO3.141800@news1.onlynews.com> <39D40051.5AAF24BA@blarney.att.net> <39D49575.C6396B12@brazee.net> <39D534B8.421F8A5@blarney.att.net> <39D57EF8.4BF49809@optonline.net> <39D605C2.8EF6D37B@blarney.att.net>`

```
Phil Eamon wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >
> > Don't agree, murder is murder.

> Oh, I don't think that's true. You can shoot a guy invading your house,
> you can do the same mistakenly believing that your house it being
> invaded, or you can tie some guy to a truck and take him apart alive
> because you think that's what you do to this [insert] kind of people.
> Seems like the three murders above are different.

Those aren't all murders. I believe they are three deaths which get
categorized differently by the legal system and, if he can't get you off
completely, one's defense attorney argues loud & long for the category
with the least severe penalty. In the case of someone invading your home
while you're there, it's probably a case of justifiable homicide (the
term "murder" is not used). If you rig a booby trap to kill someone who
breaks in while you're not home, that's murder.

> > It's not worse if I'm straight & the
> > corpse was gay and not so bad if the killer is gay the the corpse was
> > straight. Same for race, same for sex.

> Again, I disagree with that. The priority being to avoid crimes, it is
> important what specifically causes people to commit crimes. Getting back
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> others. But you can strive to achieve such an acceptance, otoh.

What fredom of expression is inherent in "you can think whatever you
want". Expression suggests, to me anyway, speech, putting up posters,
and the like. 

> > What this does is create a two-tier criminal system, e.g., a felony -
> > murder, and a worse felony, hate murder.

> I'm not sure I disagree with that. I think it is in fact different if
> you shoot someone in the head because he did your woman

In my limited knowledge & even more limited experience, most western
societies give a husband a pass if he kills someone he finds in bed with
his (the first guy's) wife, but you have to do it right away for it to
be a crime of passion.

> vs you do the
> same because he was some kind of irrational hate target, like gay or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > thought/speech, etc., the logic of which escapes me.

> The logic is that hate as in "hate crimes" is irrational. Now, there's
> no law that everyone be rational, but you can't allow people run around
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> that?

IMHO your argument is irrational and I really hope my attorney didn't go
to the same law school:-) And when did crimes become "resolutions of
conflicts"? What's so rational about contract murders, or two white guys
chaining up a 3rd white guy behind their pickup and taking him out for a
scrape, etc.? 

> > We're supposed to
> > change the way we speak and behave because *somewhere*, *sometime*,
> > *someone*'s feelings MIGHT be hurt?

> You're not supposed to change your thinking. Behaving--well, that would
> have to depend on what exactly that means. If it means dragging a guy
> behind your truck because of hell knows what in your head went boom,
> then sorry, that can't be allowed.

Right on, brother. That's always been illegal. IMHO we don't need any
new laws in this area.

> > Isn't that the dept. of tough
> > noughies? This also turns our legal system around so it punishes crimes
> > before they actually occur. AFAIK, hurting someone's feelings is NOT a
> > crime, even a misdemeanor.

> Hurting someone's feeling is not a crime. What is specifically a problem
> here?

That's the goal of politically correct speech & behavior - avoiding a
situation in which someone's feelings *might* be hurt, sometime,
somewhere. I believe it's the extension of this kind of thinking which
leads to classifying certain crimes with certain attributes as "hate"
crimes. These always seem to me to be one way streets, e.g., whites
commit hate crimes against blacks or heterosexuals commit hate crimes
against homosexuals. Anyone else here remember the "wilding" incident in
NYC's Central Park in 1989? A bunch of black & Hispanic males, mid to
late teens, who were essentially good kids, i.e., they attended school,
had good homes, didn't have criminal records, etc., decided to go
"wilding" after school one day (so whatever happened was premeditated).
Wilding is essentially committing a series of felonies until the group
is tired & calls it a day. This time they went through Central Park
harrassing & assaulting various people and robbing them, but ended up
gangraping and brutally beating & leaving for dead a young white woman
(IIRC, she was 29) who was jogging. Prior to attacking this woman they
started after other women but someone called out to leave the sisters
alone and to get a white bitch. It seemed pretty obvious to me then and
still does now, 11 years later, that this was a double hate crime, both
racial and sexual, yet it wasn't prosecuted as a hate crime and NY law
provided for it. Can anyone give a rational explanation why this was not
prosecuted as a hate crime? 

LiamD
```

Bill, yes, PC and legacy are divided worlds as far as use, no argument
there.  I figured that out more than 5yrs ago when I started posting
here.

Most of my work is on mainframe.  And in the past 20yrs, I've seen
less and less use of Report Writer.  Many shops ban the use of Report
Writer.  Most COBOL programmers either don't know how to use it or
avoid using it.  It might be in the next standard, but so is the ALTER
verb.


On Thu, 28 Sep 2000 04:22:17 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>sites/programmers/applications that use RW all over the place and those that
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T08:44:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DD82C8.2A5A83E5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net> <39d27892.443921990@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D34DC2.6E6780DF@brazee.net> <39d493ba.581966863@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
> 
> I'm glad that you clarified that - for a moment, I thought that COBOL
> programmers were taking a clue from C++ programmers, and using
> pointers into memory. 

C programmers seem to have this idea that 'pointers' means 'memory
addresses'.  In fact a pointer can be anything that 'points' to data and
may contain a 'handle' or an index or any other value that is used to
reference particular data.  It just happens that the C model adopted
(for most implementations) raw memory addresses.

> Speaking of pointers, I noticed that the term
> now refers to a feature for extracting sub-strings - is their use at
> all prevalent in the real world?

The STRING and UNSTRING have always (since '74) had a WITH POINTER
variable option used to 'point' to specific place within the string. 
'Extracting sub-strings' seems to be 'reference notation'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DB9B39.D11C9E8B@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net> <39d27892.443921990@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
Robaire wrote:

> On Wed, 27 Sep 2000 11:13:05 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Good idea ...

I've got a number of unstructured programs I've been cleaning up. If you need
ideas...

Execute open/initialization code then GOTO the middle of a loop (say
MR-TEST), fall thru to the end of the loop, indicated by GOTO beginning of
loop (but don't call it a loop-call it MR-READ or something). Exit from the
middle of the loop by GOTO the paragraph following the loop, or maybe the
middle of the next loop, then fall thru, etc. Be sure to include lots of
stuff like adding zero to amounts (particularly if you use variables like
KBYTE0 instead of literals). I'm told there was a reason for this but I don't
remember what it was. (Something about aligning the data in memory, I think.)

I'm pretty mean, but I'm not sure I'm mean enough to include ALTER statements
like these programs use, but you can if you want to. While not really related
to unstructured code, there's also the technique of updating a field in a
record by redefining the record as an array of single bytes, looking up the
field in a table that contains offsets & field lengths, then replacing the
data character by character. I still haven't figured out why they did it that
way.

BTW, these are *commercial* programs purchased in the early '80s. This
probably isn't the worst that's out there, but it's the worst I've seen in 15
years of programming. (So I came to it late-I got sidetracked by military
service after my first introduction to programming in the '70s and didn't
return to it until the mid-80s.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC7F7B.C04B4009@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39D22AA1.EB543D38@brazee.net> <39d27892.443921990@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <39DB9B39.D11C9E8B@dced.state.ak.us> <8rhbea$48i$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> 
>     I can't speak for other environments, but with an earlier
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> fixed it.

I once had to change programs to move unsigned numeric variables to
signed numeric variables and back (or was it the other way around?).  We
changed from IBM Mainframe DOS to OS, and they changed their default
method of storing unsigned signs (an oxymoron!).

Wherever we had to compare data with old data they needed to match. 
What they SHOULD have done was to convert the old data instead of having
to do this kludge from then on.  I bet they probably changed a couple of
decades ago.

Adding +0 to the old data would probably have been a better fix than
what we did - then we could take our time and convert the old files. 
Whenever we were done, the programs would still have worked correctly
with an unneeded add.
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<39D88EE3.B326FEA7@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<Dc0B5.10929$bD5.68217@news3.atl> <20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com> <ndlB5.7676$l35.149839@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:
> 
> >MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OUTPUT
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OUTPUT, THE-OTHER-OUTPUT.

I sometimes code one way, sometimes the other - when it is compiled it
won't be redundant, cutting & pasting makes one just as easy as the
other to code, and possibly the original code is a bit more clear and
harder for a maintenance programmer to mess up.
 
> ... and secondly... how did that manage to get into Prod?  Wasn't there
> *anyone* in that shop who could design things to use a Proper, Decent,
> Righteous MOVE CORR?

Possibly.  But it is possible that that field was named the same because
of the way it was defined in a data dictionary or copy member.  MOVE
CORRESPONDING may not be appropriate (or it may be disallowed in that
shop).

I work in an IDMS shop and keep thinking on how data dictionaries are
almost real nice tools!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qg4C5.2682$2Q4.15242@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<Dc0B5.10929$bD5.68217@news3.atl> <20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com> <d_lB5.107$Hs3.49381@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
> Ms. Preston, some of us (e.g., moi) prefer explicit qualification like this
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>   MOVE field-4 of record-v3  to field-4 of record-v4

What I prefer is (simplified example):

    01  MASTER-RECORD.
        03  MR-FIELDA    PIC ..
        03  MR-FIELDB    PIC ..
        03  MR-FIELDC    PIC ..
        ...

    01  SECOND-RECORD.
        03  SR-FIELDA    PIC ..
        03  SR-FIELDB    PIC ..
        03  SR-FIELDC    PIC ..
        ...

    MOVE MR-FIELDA TO SR-FIELDA.
    MOVE MR-FIELDB TO SR-FIELDB.
    MOVE MR-FIELDC TO SR-FIELDC.
    ...

This gives the consistency of data names without the over-wordiness of
qualification or the confusion and uncertainty of CORRESPONDING. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D8D78F.BA728497@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<Dc0B5.10929$bD5.68217@news3.atl> <20000930002451.00837.00000246@ng-fu1.aol.com> <d_lB5.107$Hs3.49381@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Qg4C5.2682$2Q4.15242@news1.atl>`

```
The only time I use a Move Corresponding is when I write an
unload/restructure/reload.  The new structure has a couple of fields
which are different (maybe only their picture), but the rest of the
fields get moved the same.

And then I throw away the program when I am done.
```

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>

```
yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:

> The first 3 positions indicate the shipping plant and the next 4
>are a counter.  The counter is going from 0 thru 9999 to A000 thru Z999.

>The crux of the matter is in a lot of programs this field is defined as numeric
>9(7) and in others it is packed!  

thats gonna bite some programmer in the ass in a couple of decades.
it'd be much better to just increase it to 20 digits numeric. yea, you
lose some space, but what happens when you hit ZZZZ? add another
alphanumeric? and then you gotta do a master update anyways.

>We are also taking this oportunity to 'clean house'.  That is remove obsolete
>code etc and make the jobs more efficient etc.

>Today she received a program where the programmer used the same variable name
>in several file layouts and coded by reference in the Procedure Division.  That
>is:

>MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OUTPUT
>MOVE INVOICE OF THE-RECORD TO INVOICE OF THE-OTHER-OUTPUT 

you mean the code said it copied from a file to an output spec? or an
object of the file to an object of the output?

>and so on.  She was aghast.  She had heard of it but never seen it.  Welcome to
>the real world.  You ain't seen notin yet.


reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T02:27:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001002222731.04492.00000090@ng-fn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bdcctsceqa690ic7sriosoo2uhnn7et1ke@4ax.com>`

```
Hmmm - several comments to answer.

First - when we hit Z999 we go back to A000 - that gives us about 26,000
invoice numbers per plant.  If we do 26,000 shipments from each plant in a year
then look out Bill Gates!  Our largest warehouse ships about 10,000 a year. 
All we have to do is keep the number from rolling in a 12 month rolling
calendar (Jan to Dec, Feb to Jan etc).

Sorry - don't quite understand the 'copied from a file to an output spec? or an
object of the file to an object of the output'.  INVOICE OF THE-RECORD is input
INVOICE OF THE-OUTPUT is the output file layout (one of several output files)
and I can't off the top of my head tell you if it is in WS or the FD.

Expand the field size - ok - add 6 months to the project as we rebuild all the
VSAM files that use it as a key (ugh).

CORRESPONDING.....
Hey - I didn't write the code - just copied as it was written :)  I think the
program was written sometime in the middle 80's and at that time they didn't
have any review, heck - they don't now.

We have a lot of DB2 programs where you see a lot of Move field1 of database1
to field1 of database2 as the shop standard is to name columns on the databases
the same if they contain the same data in each database such as plant number or
part number etc.  In such cases I'd rather see CORRESPONDING - but....
::sigh::.

Sorry Michael M - your response hasn't shown up yet either here or at work :(
Only saw a snippit in someone else's response.

Judson - you and I are on the same page - much prefer that style of coding. 
But we're talking maintenance of someone else's code and we live with what
we're given :)

Of course there's the programs written by one guy HH (his initials) that have
no 'white space' in them.  Legend has it that when he was told to reduce the
size of his programs (I'm assuming load modules) he put all the code into one
continuous line within each paragraph.  Has periods etc but try and read the
dang things!  Must have been a contemporary of another illustrious programmer
we've heard of on the group.  Fortunately I've not had the pleasure of working
on one of them at 2am, they are scary enough at 2pm :).

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```

>
> To be brief ( too late )  I would appreciate your help in identifying
> the top 10 ( or more, or less ) tasks that occupy maintenance
> programmers the most.


Hi:

I haven't followed this thread so others may have said this:

When maintaining a program, be sure you have backups of the
original before starting work.

Never change an existing line of code. Instead, comment the
old and insert the changed line. Put the date of the change
in column 73. My editor and compiler allow me to use a 256-
character line, so I can put comments in beyond column 72.
If code is to be no-longer-used, do not remove, but make
it comments instead. You never know when they will change
their minds and want to go back to the old way.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
For once, I agree with Tony - a little.

I agree that you DEFINITELY should make certain that you have a backup of
previous versions of your programs.  Certainly the "last" production version
(which MOST programmers keep) - but I also think that it is a good idea to
make certain that you have "several" generations of production code.  (On
IBM mainframes, such products as Endevor, Panvalet, Librarian, etc - make
this "easy" and pretty much  required.)

I would NOT, however, say that I would want to keep ALL variations of code
as comments within production source code.  (I might WELL want to keep the
LAST version there - but that depends on the nature and extent of the
change.)  With "comparison" products on the PC, it would seem to me that
saving the source code in "back-up" versions - rather than comments - is
probably a better solution there too, but I wouldn't "object" if someone
actually kept it in the production source code itself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8r7unj$rm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> For once, I agree with Tony - a little.
>
> I would NOT, however, say that I would want to keep ALL variations of
code
> as comments within production source code.  (I might WELL want to
keep the
> LAST version there - but that depends on the nature and extent of the
> change.)  With "comparison" products on the PC, it would seem to me
that
> saving the source code in "back-up" versions - rather than comments -
is
> probably a better solution there too, but I wouldn't "object" if
someone
> actually kept it in the production source code itself.
>

Hi:

I know what you mean. I sometimes feel like doing spring-cleaning
and getting all the old stuff out.

But then there is always the thought that, someday, sometime,
you will be really glad that it is there.

The reason I like having the old code in the current version is
primarily because it does not require looking at older versions.
Naturally, there are generations of backups.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1x4C5.2703$2Q4.15371@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8r7unj$rm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>
> I know what you mean. I sometimes feel like doing spring-cleaning
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Naturally, there are generations of backups.

Look at it this way.  If you were smart enough to change it from what
it was into what it is, why wouldn't you be smart enough to change
it back, if necessary?  If you keep code backups, you can always look
to see what the old code was, if necessary, and even copy it over.  No
offense to those who do this, but I believe it simply indicates a lack
of confidence in one's ability.  Remember, the chances of using that
commented code again is small, but the chances of having to eventually
locate and remove it is 100%. :-)  Looking over programs with lots of
commented code can cause you to overlook a few lines of uncommented
code surrounded by commented code, and vice-versa.  And when you have
ample backups available, there is no point. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D8D961.63B4A88A@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8r7unj$rm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1x4C5.2703$2Q4.15371@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> > The reason I like having the old code in the current version is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> to see what the old code was, if necessary, and even copy it over. 

Some times it is not at all easy to look through generations of listings
to find the one with the code you thought you might use again.  In fact,
I have never worked in an environment where this was anything less than
a royal pain, so much so, that the main reason it's done at all is to
determine when a bug was entered into the code.

Fortunately, it is very rare that I have code which gets deleted in
production which I expect might be needed again.

> Looking over programs with lots of
> commented code can cause you to overlook a few lines of uncommented
> code surrounded by commented code, and vice-versa.  And when you
> have ample backups available, there is no point. :-)

Of course, it isn't too hard to make sure your comments stand out. 
Don't just put in one asterisk in column 7, but isolate it and say why
you're saving that code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D9906A.C544E92E@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <39D88FC6.73A0771F@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> keep it depending on what he thinks the future will bring.

My personal opinion, no more & no less, is that if you're using a
product like Endevor, lose the commented out code & the dates in 1-6 (or
73-80). Endevor maintains software versions more consistently than I've
ever been able to do manually and I'm always looking at the *current*
source (unless I want otherwise, of course) with no clutter (the clutter
bothers me no end). Endevor will list all the versions it has, it will
list the deltas between versions, etc. If you're still dealing with 029
key punches and cabinets full of punched cards, may God have mercy on
you.

LiamD
```

had a few old mechanical calculators (actually tabulating machines
dating from the 50's).  You should have seen those things multiply!

calculators had gas plasma displays, which looked cool at the time. :-)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DD78C3.71C6DA4F@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <39D88FC6.73A0771F@brazee.net> <39D9906A.C544E92E@optonline.net> <8rechv$i47$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <39DBEF34.62042F5C@optonline.net> <8rhc2f$9nv$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <q7%C5.490$5l.2925@news1.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> When I was at the University of Alabama in the mid 60's, they still
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> calculators had gas plasma displays, which looked cool at the time. :-)

They were, too. I worked for IBM 1965-1970 and was trained in their
punched card equipment. The electronic calculator was a 604 (the 602 was
mechanical). I was in a new business office and we didn't have any
customers with 604 punches (not many with 602's either). By 1965, if a
customer was spending that much on tab equipment we'd sell them a
computer. It was kind of fun, actually, wiring the boards and putting
punched card applications together. I went to computer training in the
spring of '66 and that was the last I saw of the tab equipment. I don't
think I've seen a punched card in 20 years or more.

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:41:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39da7cb1.1074686@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8r7or2$5rn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sun, 1 Oct 2000 11:30:22 -0500, William M. Klein wrote:

>I would NOT, however, say that I would want to keep ALL variations of code
>as comments within production source code.  (I might WELL want to keep the
>LAST version there - but that depends on the nature and extent of the
>change.)  

Usually, I find that keeping one (the most recent) group of changes
still in the production source is a good compromise.  I have
occasionally needed to re-insert some code that was removed several
generations back, and for that I can always copy-and-paste from a
previous source file, and insert a comment like "The following code
for validating xxx was restored from Version 3.26" or some such.  This
way, the revision history still makes sense, reinventing the wheel is
kept to a minimum, and the production source is kept relatively clean.

Obviously, the scales can tilt if you "know" that some change you are
making will later be reversed (several times?); or if a routine was
particularly complex to write and debug, in the first place.

Again, company coding standards may also drive this decision.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g1s24k0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sun, 01 Oct 2000 12:33:45 GMT, Foodman wrote:

>
>>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I agree with the backup, but I don't agree with commenting out removed lines.
Use a librarian system to keep the old versions around, document the changes
but don't let the old code in. I've seen many times people thinking that
commented-out lines were executed. If it isn't there, it can't confuse you.

Just my opinion

Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maintenance - top 10 tasks

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:42:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39da7cd8.1114057@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<39d221bf.421691787@news1.qc.sympatico.ca> <8r7av9$isc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g1s24k0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
On Sun, 01 Oct 2000 19:13:37 GMT, Willem Clements wrote:

>[...] I've seen many times people thinking that
>commented-out lines were executed. If it isn't there, it can't confuse you.

If situations arise where commented code needs to be kept in the
source, it can be set off by using multiple asterisks or other
identifying features, such as:
   Instead of:
      *    MOVE X TO Y.
   One of:
      *****MOVE X TO Y.
      *--->MOVE X TO Y.
      ********MOVE X TO Y. ******** <DELETED BY PATCH 5-003>

While this can look messy, it can avoid confusion.
```

```

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-32f75cd3d7"></a>
## Thread: DOS COBOL to COBOL/VSE migration — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### DOS COBOL to COBOL/VSE migration

- **From:** "Leon" <Leon.in.Newport.News@att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xuLB5.11204$s76.780837@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
The company from which I am retired contracted me in 1998 to get their
systems ready for Y2K.

Although the company had an IBM S-390 they decided against migrating to the
new COBOL at that time because they did not want to add complexities to
getting ready for Y2K.

The company now wants to contract me to migrate their DOS COBOL programs to
COBOL/VSE.  The old compiler was the '74 standard but the company used the
LANGLVL(!) CBL option for all compiles so that programs written during the
sixties and early seventies would not have to be changed.

I have been reading the IBM migration manuals and have not seen anything
that looks overwhelming.

I would appreciate hearing about any unexpected problems encountered by
anyone who migrated from DOS COBOL to COBOL/VSE.

Thanks,
Leon
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS COBOL to COBOL/VSE migration

- **From:** "Glenn A. Mitchell" <g.mitchell@computer.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FNSC5.5518$z66.156254@newsr1.maine.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<xuLB5.11204$s76.780837@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
None of the problems are particularly overwhelming, especially if the shop
had some reasonable standards and adhered reasonably well to them.   Getting
the LE and COBOL default parameters established properly early on is
important (e.g. the way you specify parms on the execute card is, by
default, NOT the way you're used to doing it but the default can be
changed).  Many programs should simply require a recompile (YMMV).

You may wish to consider the CCCA product from IBM if you find you have a
lot of code to convert which requires manual intervention.
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS COBOL to COBOL/VSE migration

- **From:** Jeffry Kennedy <jakcert@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC7491.19127026@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<xuLB5.11204$s76.780837@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
We did the migration over the last few years and there didn't seem to be any
"show stoppers".  I did find IBM COBOL for VSE/ESA Migration Guide
(GC26-8070-00) invaluable.  Our biggest problem was since we use Report Writer
extensively we had to purchase the Report Writer add on.  That and just
browsing a lot of lines of code. More grunt work than thought inducing though.

Pat H

Leon wrote:

> The company from which I am retired contracted me in 1998 to get their
> systems ready for Y2K.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Leon
```

<a id="t-507b72c62e"></a>
## Thread: File Buffers on MVS — 2 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2000-09](2000-09.md#t-507b72c62e)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D749E8.E8958B3F@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <39cecaeb.21977805@207.126.101.100>`

```
I saw this problem during the Y2K panic.  The client company decided to
recompile all COBOL programs with a "certified Y2K compliant" compiler.  In this
case, an OS/VS COBOL program was recompiled using the VS COBOL II compiler and
it died with a S0C7 when tested.

It appears that VS COBOL II doesn't acquire the I/O buffers for a file until the
OPEN is executed.  With that mindset, the I/O buffers are probably released when
the CLOSE is executed.

Has your shop recently recompiled and relinked everything to move applications
above the 16 megabyte line?  That would explain the problem.

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> I've seen something like this suddenly bite (even though it should
> have long ago) from a simple change in the amount of memory the region
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Buffers on MVS

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rb4l9021gj@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8qhe420ba3@enews3.newsguy.com> <39cecaeb.21977805@207.126.101.100> <39D749E8.E8958B3F@sprynet.com>`

```
I don't know of anything about changing the line or whatever...  The 
compiler didn't change.  Our systems group does change things now and then
w/o telling us...

Thanks for the response.

Jeff

----------

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

---

Sounds like your missing a bunch of steps here. For starters you have to
compile the program and run the executable, not the source code. Further this
program is missing some important sections, your book probably assumes your
familiar with Cobol constructs and just gave you a partial code extract. Read
up on the Environment Division as a start, that will tell you how Cobol equates
internal file structures with physical disk files. etc etc.

have fun
```

In article <39cd6d92.1167854@news.sprint.ca>,
  email@address.com wrote:

Helps to know what you typed actually means and is used for.

What errors did you get?

What compiler are you using?

Did it compile alright and then crash when you ran it?
  If it did it is a file access problem!

On a PC you have to give a directory address.  How does your program
know where the file is at?  Maybe you have to define a default
directory or give the dirctory address in quotes single or double
quates could be required depending on the compiler.

    Select student-file assign to "C:\temp\COBLOL\test.dat".

Access is line sequential may or may not work, it depends on the file
in question that you are reading?

Why do you have to say a print file has an organization?  That must be
specific to pc operations.

My instructor always said to never use an end-if!

IF COBOL SEES A PERIOD IT KNOWS THE STATEMENT IS AT THE END ALREADY.

Why would you use an end-read and at-end at the same time?  This
program is designed to run till the end of the file then you stop it by
changing the switch for the end of the file.  Maybe if you were reading
randomly or dynamically you would use something to trigger an end read
if you were doing reads in an endless loop.

> Here it goes.. I just typed from the book and run it. It's should
> work, but it didn't. any idea? Thanks.
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Before you buy.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D8F861.E614E90D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39cd6d92.1167854@news.sprint.ca> <8ralag$24l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> My instructor always said to never use an end-if!
> 
> IF COBOL SEES A PERIOD IT KNOWS THE STATEMENT IS AT THE END ALREADY.

I use periods and END-IFs.  I suppose your instructor believes neither
you nor the people who maintain your programs will ever nest IF
statements.

If you come across this code in a program which you are debugging, do
you know the programmer's intentions?


IF A=B
   IF MYSWITCH
      NEXT SENTENCE
   ELSE
   IF OLD-FILE
      PERFORM READ-OLD-FILE
ELSE
   ADD 1 TO COUNTER.

If he had three END-IFs, you are pretty sure you knew what was intended.

Evaluate your coding style as if you were maintaining someone else's
code in the middle of the night!!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T02:45:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NIbC5.1112$UP5.18742@news6.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<39cd6d92.1167854@news.sprint.ca> <8ralag$24l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

On  2-Oct-2000, lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com wrote:

> My instructor always said to never use an end-if!
> IF COBOL SEES A PERIOD IT KNOWS THE STATEMENT IS AT THE END ALREADY.

Wow.  Another example of "those who can't, teach".

Do this without an END-IF:

IF condition-1
    PERFORM THIS-1
ELSE
    IF condition-2
        PERFORM THIS-2
    END-IF
    PERFORM THIS-3
END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00

```

    IF condition-1
        PERFORM THIS-1
        GO TO EXIT-PAR.
    IF CONDITION-2
        PERFORM THIS-2.
    PERFORM THIS-3.
EXIT-PAR.
    EXIT.

    It can be done, but personally I prefer to use an END-IF for every IF,
CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE, and one period per paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RwgC5.308$bI6.43327@news1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<39cd6d92.1167854@news.sprint.ca> <8ralag$24l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <NIbC5.1112$UP5.18742@news6.giganews.com> <8rbj07$6qt2$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
> > Do this without an END-IF:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > END-IF.

On  2-Oct-2000, "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:

>    IF condition-1
>         PERFORM THIS-1
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     It can be done,

My point exactly.  Why do some people use GO TO?  Because their limited
COBOL vocabulary requires it.

>  I prefer to use an END-IF for every IF,
> CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE, and one period per paragraph.

CONTINUE is a necessity if you're using one period per paragraph.  NEXT
SENTENCE means to begin re-executing the instructions following the next
period, while CONTINUE simply acts as an imperative placeholder and lets the
logic continue.

I like END-IFs for every IF, no GO TOs, no SECTIONs, no THRUs.  SECTIONs and
THRUs imply the possibility of an alternate entrance into a performed block
of code.

I'm still unconverted on the idea of one period per paragraph.  I end every
construct that's not within an explicit scope delimiter (END-IF,
END-PERFORM, etc.) with a period.  Probably causes me more pain than not,
but it just feels more like my old COBOL (been writing it for 25 years) and
some things are hard to give up.  Besides, this looks weird to me, and I see
some people do it a lot:

PARA-1.
    PERFORM THIS
    PERFORM THAT
    PERFORM THE-OTHER
    .                      <--period by itself
PARA-2....

{shiver...}

Cliff
```

```

On  2-Oct-2000, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:

> >But... I'm still a missionary for no GO TO   ;-)
> Mr Peterson, with all due respect... your using words like 'missionary'
> and 'converted' regarding matters of, essentially, zeroes and ones gives
> at least *me* a bit of pause.

That's like saying that Rembrandt's craft was just, essentially, cloth and
colored oil.

Everyone here has their own programming belief system.  Many are willing to
defend those beliefs with their, um, life.

I've seen scholars ("my experience with 185 COBOL compilers convinces me
you're an idiot"), zealots ("let's unionize"), the angry mob ("and you're
ugly, too"), evangelists ("I post to this NG because I want newbies to see
that my way is best"), atheists ("COBOL? You write in COBOL?"), agnostics
("it's all just zeroes and ones"), and the recently converted ("my
instructor said to never use END-IF").

Me?  I'm a seeker who is convinced that COBOL is good for my soul, but is
seeking to improve his chances in the afterlife (post-Y2K) by consulting
with the gurus of CLC.  Perhaps you're one.

Cliff
```
