###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D9D926.4EFC47D4@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39cd6d92.1167854@news.sprint.ca> <8ralag$24l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <NIbC5.1112$UP5.18742@news6.giganews.com> <8rbj07$6qt2$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <RwgC5.308$bI6.43327@news1.giganews.com>`

```
Boy do we agree!

Cliff Peterson wrote:
> 

> My point exactly.  Why do some people use GO TO?  Because their limited
> COBOL vocabulary requires it.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> logic continue.

I can think of code where NEXT SENTENCE would work but CONTINUE wouldn't
- but I certainly would not like to see such code in a program I am
maintaining.  So I have stopped writing NEXT SENTENCE in my code.

When I do maintain programs with EXIT paragraphs, and my compiler
complains because the Exit paragraph is empty, I stick in a CONTINUE
there instead of an EXIT.  This allows someone to add a debugging
DISPLAY without commenting out the EXIT/CONTINUE.

There is no reason for new code to have EXIT paragraphs, unless it is a
shop standard.
 
> I like END-IFs for every IF, no GO TOs, no SECTIONs, no THRUs.  SECTIONs and
> THRUs imply the possibility of an alternate entrance into a performed block
> of code.

Agreed.  I have to have SECTIONS because I have to use some copy members
which contain them.  If it were my shop, I would gradually replace them
with new copy members and make your likes above into a shop standard.
 
> I'm still unconverted on the idea of one period per paragraph.  I end every
> construct that's not within an explicit scope delimiter (END-IF,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> some people do it a lot:

I agree again.  Furthermore, in an IDMS shop which has the pre-compiler
insert IF logic without END-IFs, we have to have full stop periods to be
safe.

What's the gain?  That you can cut and paste logic to within an IF
statement without having to erase a period?  Big deal, you're not going
to indent and review the pasted logic anyway?
 
> PARA-1.
>     PERFORM THIS
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cliff

This is against standards here, as people think that is hard to see, and
certainly find it ugly and counter-English.  Give me my periods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: any good programmer here to find the problem for me, please?

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com

```
I think this will work and ... no goto's!

IF condition-1
    PERFORM THIS-1
ELSE
    IF condition-2
        PERFORM THIS-2
    	PERFORM THIS-3
    ELSE
    	PERFORM THIS-3.
```

## Thread: Group vs element — 3 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [2000-09](2000-09.md#t-622f192874)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Group vs element

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D88D41.67E609BD@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net> <L0aB5.55$ux3.668@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Certainly that is why they had the original code.  I am trying to figure
out why they made the "fix", to include the BIG-STRING-REDEFINED code.

I.E. what is the difference between:
MOVE SPACES TO BIG-STRING.
STRING A,B,C INTO BIG-STRING DELIMITED BY SPACE.

and
MOVE SPACES TO BIG-STRING
STRING A,B,C INTO BIG-STRING-REDEFINED DELIMITED BY SPACE.

The only reference the maintenance fix had to the new
BIG-STRING-REDEFINED was in that STRING command.

"Lucas, Todd" wrote:
> 
> Maybe to access each individual character in the array, as well as to
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > spot where a STRING went into.  Why is this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group vs element

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39D8BF35.81A72032@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net> <L0aB5.55$ux3.668@newsfeed.slurp.net> <39D88D41.67E609BD@brazee.net>`

```
It's possible that the maintenance fix originally used BIG-STRING-REDEFINED
elsewhere but the other use(s) got removed by a later maintenance fix.

Howard Brazee wrote:

> Certainly that is why they had the original code.  I am trying to figure
> out why they made the "fix", to include the BIG-STRING-REDEFINED code.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > spot where a STRING went into.  Why is this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Group vs element

- **From:** Don Hill <dhill@sc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E087C1.9A5AE0AF@sc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<39D51856.22F4D33E@brazee.net>`

```
Group move treats source and target as PIX X strings and there is no test
for picture
mismatch.

Element move will test move for mismatch.  Example - do not move
non-numeric
field to a numeric picture field.
```

<a id="t-fb65c85d37"></a>
## Thread: Speaking of 'Programmer Shortages'... — 26 msgs

### Speaking of 'Programmer Shortages'...

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<4KZB5.8116$l35.166604@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Another view from
<http://news.cnet.com/news/0-1007-200-2880309.html?tag=st.ne.1002.bgif.ni>

--begin quoted text:

To rectify the perceived shortage of workers, some employers would like to
see colleges churn out more IT-trained people in less time; other
companies want the government to expand immigration to attract foreign IT
workers. Congress is currently weighing legislation that would boost the
number of skilled high-technology workers permitted to immigrate into the
United States each year. The bill, supported by the high-tech industry,
would raise the number of visas for IT workers to 195,000 annually. 

But Cappelli says these actions are premature until other, more obvious,
issues are addressed. These include understanding why turnover is so high
in IT jobs, especially in programming; why employers rely so heavily on
college graduates to supply these key jobs; and why it appears difficult
to recruit experienced workers.

--end quoted text

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Speaking of 'Programmer Shortages'...

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39d8ced4.9101477@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<4KZB5.8116$l35.166604@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
We have had some turnover reciently.  I don't think it has been
directly caused by salary or people problems.  I think the primary
cause has been opportunities opening up for people to get away from
dead-end, no-fun work positions.  Then we have to recruit from outside
for these jobs because no one in house wants them, and it becomes very
hard to find someone qualified for the unique skills required, so we
appear to have a staff shortage.

I think our industry has a shortage of fun jobs.  And people with the
desirable skills are shying away from the difficult jobs.

Another significant problem with our industry is too many  unique
skill requirements.  What new language or technology will we have to
manage in the next 6 months?  Give me a break!

Strictly one mans opinion :-)
Pete


On Mon, 02 Oct 2000 10:48:32 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:

>
>Another view from
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Speaking of 'Programmer Shortages'...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<39D8DC0F.57C626F5@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<4KZB5.8116$l35.166604@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39d8ced4.9101477@news.transport.com>`

```


Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> We have had some turnover reciently.  I don't think it has been
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> manage in the next 6 months?  Give me a break!

So management needs to look at work conditions other than salary to keep
happy, productive employees.  Sometimes it needs to remember the promise
it made to the prospective CoBOL programmer that he would have an
opportunity to learn Java or Sun or something, and give him the chance
to try it here instead of quitting to try it.  Even though the company
needs his CoBOL skills very badly.  Better a half time CoBOL programmer
who knows your system than to lose him.

Maybe he doesn't really want Java - he wants the applications which the
Java programmer gets to work on.  

Furthermore, if you have 3 programmers who spend 1/3 of their time on a
new technology, the company isn't up the creek when one leaves.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Speaking of 'Programmer Shortages'...

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s59its49hb08ssg06tnd9pm1m5lra4jskl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4KZB5.8116$l35.166604@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39d8ced4.9101477@news.transport.com> <39D8DC0F.57C626F5@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> What new language or technology will we have to
>> manage in the next 6 months?  Give me a break!
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>opportunity to learn Java or Sun or something

i agree here, but in a different fashion. the employer should try a
little harder to ensure that new technologies do not avail themselves
if older ones exist. think about it, why should an employee *want* to
learn java if cobol has the same functionality? the employer should
have been investing the extra bucks in new cobol technologies (like
cobolrpc or relativity or cobolhtml or whatever) instead of finding
out some new company got the jump on them.

ok, from my small and pithy experience, we recently got windows me,
without even a customer purchasing it yet. that's a $100 risk plus the
costs of learning the software which they fronted for probably a 2
year product. what would the equiviliant risk be for cobol? hmm. $100
every 2 years is at least using the newest compiler. period. you may
not have the newest OS, but you got the newest compiler.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

```
are finding a welcome response.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Speaking of 'Programmer Shortages'...

- **From:** stephenpon@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T05:15:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<8rbq24$13p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<4KZB5.8116$l35.166604@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39D8D726.93D8E79B@gte.net>`

```
In article <39D8D726.93D8E79B@gte.net>,
  flak@gte.net wrote:
> This percieved shortage isn't just happening here either. Several
> European countries are trying to pass legislation making more work
visas
> available for foreign workers (mostly eastern European targeted, but
> anybody will do). I think those Americans interested in working abroad
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You're right.  There is NO shortage.  The real problem is ageism.
Those experienced programmers over 40 are a real problem.  Some of them
expect to make a living wage.  For instance, there probably is a
shortage in certain unaffordable areas like the Silicon Valley.  Well,
there may be a shortage of workers who would move to a place where you
can't buy a home for under $1,000,000 or rent for under $3,000/mo.  But
I suppose you could hire a bunch of young foreigners who would be
willing to live together so they could afford their housing.  Sort of
like the farm workers.  But even this doesn't make sense since the H1B
Visa consulting agencies charge more than 3 times what I make.  Even
though the foreign workers get less than 50% of what their agencies
charge, I still don't see where the companies are saving money.

But they are lowering the standard of living since so many experienced
programmers are put out to pasture.  If your skills don't stay current,
your ability to gainful employment are down the drain.  I suspect that
lowering the wages of American programmers, getting the over 40
programmers out of the workforce, and kickbacks from the H1B consulting
agencies are all part of the scam.

What are your thoughts?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
expect to make a living wage.  For instance, there probably is a
shortage in certain unaffordable areas like the Silicon Valley.  Well,
there may be a shortage of workers who would move to a place where you
can't buy a home for under $1,000,000 or rent for under $3,000/mo.  But
I suppose you could hire a bunch of young foreigners who would be
willing to live together so they could afford their housing.  Sort of
like the farm workers.  But even this doesn't make sense since the H1B
Visa consulting agencies charge more than 3 times what I make.  Even
though the foreign workers get less than 50% of what their agencies
charge, I still don't see where the companies are saving money.

But they are lowering the standard of living since so many experienced
programmers are put out to pasture.  If your skills don't stay current,
your ability to gainful employment are down the drain.  I suspect that
lowering the wages of American programmers, getting the over 40
programmers out of the workforce, and kickbacks from the H1B consulting
agencies are all part of the scam.

The Senate is expected to pass new legislation this week that would
double the number of H1B Visas.  Check out the Programmers Guild below
and contact your Senators.

http://www.programmersguild.org/american.htm



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
```

"Ron Todd" <rltodd@ix.netcom.com
> >
> > But Cappelli says these actions are premature until other, more
obvious,
> > issues are addressed. These include understanding why turnover is
so high
> > in IT jobs, especially in programming; why employers rely so
heavily on
> > college graduates to supply these key jobs; and why it appears
difficult
> > to recruit experienced workers.

The article came from the Wharton School of Business and proposes the
typical ivory-tower solution: more studies. The academic(s) want
answers to three questions:

1. Why is turnover so high? (Duh. Because the grass is greener
somewhere else.)

2. Why college graduates? (Duh. Because most IT jobs require a term of
disciplined study, proof that goals can be set and met, and the
broader liberal education inherent in the degree.)

3. Why  is it difficult to attract experienced workers? (Duh. Because
the grass is not as green.)

Bozos.
```

```
the barrel, and why not as HR is only overhead it doesn't produce any
income.  These people can't understand anything farther than matching
words on list one, the requirement, with list two, the resume.  "Oh it
says UNIX instead of Unix, it doesn't match, reject....."  Dumb way to
run a business, but I'm sure everyone else has seen worse......
```

```

<eskwired@SPAMBLOCK.shore.net
>
> You seem to think that specific deterrence is the only reason for
> subjecting someone to the criminal justice system.  There are at
least two
> other models of penology, general deterrence and retribution.  You
need to
> do a more thourough analysis before you come to any firm
conclusions.

Actually, most jurisdictions (and the Federal System) define three
purposes for punishment:

1. Protection of society from the further unlawful acts of the
criminal.
2. Rehabilitation of the offender.
3. By example, deterrence of others similarly inclined.

I'm not aware of 'retribution' being codified in any Code of Criminal
Procedure. But we all know retribution is the primary motivation of
the entity determining the sentence (wink-wink, nudge-nudge).
```

```
The other reason to lok at visual age is price.  Meratn seems to be
busily pricing themselves right out of the cobol developers market.  For
all the parts it sounds like you need, merant is goning to cahrge
something like $5000.00 per PC, plus $18,000.00 per mainframe node.  IBM
will charge something like $1700.00 per PC.  Because you run most of you
session ont eh mainframe, there are no real problems (or added cost)
with mainframe db/2, IMS, or subroutines....

I like the look, feel, and ease of use of mainframe express much better
than visual age.  Unfortunately I don't like them enough to justify the
much higher cost...
```

## Thread: GO TO and THROUGH — 1 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2000-09](2000-09.md#t-2ecb1f198d)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO and THROUGH

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s54C5.2667$2Q4.15249@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<8qvg1d$mr9$1@ins21.netins.net> <39D461C9.7B57D5D6@mescoma.com> <39D4944C.8E9D155C@brazee.net> <QV1B5.11071$bD5.69303@news3.atl> <8r3tq8$7ju$2@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> > "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>     What checker?  Can we get one?

I wrote mine (they're not that hard to write), but it is so tailored
to my own coding style that it would be of limited or no value with
other coding styles.  For example, I use PERFORM THRU <exit> with
paragraph numbers.  The paragraph numbers are always ascending front
to back in the source, the numbers of a paragraph and its matching
exit paragraph are equal, and there can be no paragraphs between the
two (no duplicate numbers).  The exit paragraphs must end with -EXIT.
 Unlike some others here, I use periods after each sentence instead
of one period at the end of a paragraph.  My checker verifies that
any code line that follows a period must begin in column 12, but
other code must begin in column 16 or greater.  In this way, I can
catch missing and extra periods as well.  Any GO TO can only be to
the paragraph or exit it is within.  My clients, who all adopted my
coding style in their programming standards, purchased my checker,
but I don't sell it to anyone else.  I have added a few other little
features, such as flagging paragraph names over 30 characters long,
etc.

If you don't use a similar paragraph numbering scheme, you may need
to make two passes, one to build a paragraph table and the other to
check for overlapping performs, and GOTO outside the paragraph if
you permit GOTO.  If you use periods after each sentence, and follow
standard indentation rules, implementing the missing/extra period
test should be easy.  You really need COBOL 85 to avoid periods
after each sentence, and my two largest clients still use COBOL 74
because their COBOL 85 compiler is far slower.
```

- **References:** `<39CE892A.5EE9DFC5@juno.com> <39cea52c.4820961@news.globetrotter.net> <0cJz5.2345$103.16203@news1.atl> <8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <Kd9B5.1468$QF1.222186@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39D2B62D.8F2515E@kg.hsanet.net> <be7dts8se32jkkevpar77teoh89a1h48ek@4ax.com> <barbara-knox-0210001708380001@192.168.0.10>`

```
On Mon, 2 Oct 2000 04:08:38, barbara-knox@iname.com (Barb Knox) wrote:

> The problem with such "free" agreements is disparity in bargaining power
> between a single worker and a large business.  Consider the abuses that
> occurred in "company towns" (such as "company stores" and debt-slavery). 
> Also, would you consider it fair for a boss to require "consensual" sex as
> a condition of continued employment?

Donno if I buy it, Barb.

A lot of the abuses were the result of poor business sense on both 
sides.   

I once worked for a beltway bandit.  They negotiated rates, terms,
conditions, and such with the client.  Their role is strangely similar 
to the role of the union.  

If the client got uppity, mouthed off to a worker, the beltway bandit 
would shuck and jive but in reality, would pull the worker, swap them to
another contract, and life would go on. 

Sadly, while I have contracted to women bosses, none of them have 
required "consensual" sex as a condition of continued employment.  I 
don't think it's fair either way though. 
  
> Collective bargaining tends to equalize the disparity.

Maybe, but when you're the best and you know it, and they know it, it 
doesn't matter.  The problem isn't, as Clint says, a man's gotta know 
his limitations.   For some of us, the message is, a man's gotta know 
when he has the upper hand.  Or.  You gotta know your value.

If you know in your heart of hearts what you're worth, ask for it.  If 
you know that you're priced appropriately for the market, that's the 
number and there's no negotiation.

You also have to know when they're playing games.

You people over there in a.c.c and c.l.c. Know this, the market rate for
a geezer who fixes small briggs and stratton engines is $49.95/hour, 
the Acura dealer charges $79/hour across the board for mostly bolt-off, 
bolt-on work.  

(Please, don't bring up the mechanic's tools and shop overhead.  I pay 
rent on an office and have a mainframe that runs MVS.)

Programming, software engineering, computer science, and general geekin'
is difficult, complex and takes aptitude, experience, academics and 
$100/hour is dirt cheap for a technical specialist. 

I believe the access clickers, Lan boys, and web-weenie's generally 
get a fair rate, somewhere around a hundred an hour.   The rest of us 
don't charge enough. 

I'd like to see journeyman programmers, COBOL types especially, continue
to get the Y2K consulting rates.  
```

---

- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <39D98EAF.348DF48E@optonline.net> <6atC5.435$sL2.70157@news.shore.net> <FDuC5.1542$v06.76691@nnrp1.sbc.net> <PgvC5.12192$l35.180889@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net>

> >> You should learn a little bit about criminal law before you go
> >spouting
> >> off about hate laws being bad.  You seem to understand neither
the
> >> underlying subject matter, or even what hate laws are all about.
> >
> >Well, I gotta admit something. I know a lot about criminal law and
I
> >don't understand what hate laws are all about - from a
> >philosophy-of-law perspective.
>
> Ummmmm... in another posting didn't you recognise the effect of
intention
> on a criminal act?

Yes.

>
> >
> >I understand what hate laws are about from a visceral, emotional,
> >level, but, fact is, hate crimes clutter up the legal process and
make
> >the administration of justice more complicated and less effective.
>
> Hatred is a matter of intention; the law seems to take that into
account
> for a variety of other activities.  What reason have you for
singling it
> out in this instance?

Hatred is not intent.

Classic common law ranks a criminal act according to the mental state
of the perpetrator. This mental state has been defined according to
the degree of cognition, not the mental state's cause. The four
classic mental states (pre-meditated, willful, reckless, and
negligent) exist independently of the emotional state leading to their
existence. That a Pre-meditated mental state exists is sufficient for
the criminal law irrespective of what emotion (if any) gave rise to
that state.

As the law stood for centuries, all pre-meditated murderers were
prosecuted under the same law, no matter whether the emotion (if any)
leading up to the decision was hate, lust, envy, anger, fear, greed,
or lack of toilet paper.

So, in my view, adding an emotional trigger to the definition of a
crime is an unnecessary (legal) complication.

That's my story, and I'm sticking to it (unless beaten, of course).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<bU8D5.508$PU4.78695@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <FDuC5.1542$v06.76691@nnrp1.sbc.net> <PgvC5.12192$l35.180889@iad-read.news.verio.net> <%QPC5.1256$rw.69289@nnrp2.sbc.net> <2hRC5.12934$l35.189936@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net>

> >
> >Hatred is not intent.
>
> How interesting... might you be able to provide a citing or source
of some
> kind to support that assertion?  According to something rather
simple,
> such as found at http://www.m-w.com , intent is :
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ... so hatred, in that it is a state of mind, is an intent.  You are
more
> than welcome to bring forward other sources, of course, but if all
you
> have is your own say-so on the matter then it seems you've left the
realm
> of Reasoned Discourse and gone off into areas of Mere Opinion.
Decades
> back my Drill Sergeant taught me something about Opinions.

Your conclusion (hatred = state of mind = intent) is wrong. The
distinction and definition is subtle, but I bet you can puzzle it out.
Further, you seem to have a propensity to challenge someone to bolster
their assertions by appealing to outside sources. Well, here goes:

Witters v U.S. 106 F.2nd 837, 840, 70 App.D.C. 316 (et al)
(Intent equals design, resolve, or determination with which a person
acts).

Intent and motive are not in law one and the same thing. State v Logan
344 Mo 351

'Intent' in the legal sense is purpose to use particular means to
effect a certain result; whereas 'motive' is reason which leads minds
to desire that result. United Fidelity v Adair, Tex.Civ.App 29 SW2nd
940

'Intent' expresses mental action at its most advanced point, or as it
actually accompanies an outward, corporal act, which has been
determined on. Intent shows the presence of will in the act which
consummates a crime. Burrill, Circ.Ev. 284

Hatred can engender intent, but it is the INTENT that is the necessary
element of the crime. Many other considerations can create the intent,
and hatred by itself may NOT give rise to intent. Whether hate (or
anger, lust, envy, greed, compassion, etc.) gives rise to the
requisite intent is (until recently) irrelevant. You can have hate
without intent and intent without hate. The two are not the same.
```

```
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

That was not my conclusion; you seem to have made the same mistake that Mr
Smith did... and when it was pointed out to him he felt the need to
killfile me.  I did not say that they were equal; I cited the definition
from a common source and concluded that 'hatred, in that it is a state of
mind, is an intent'.

'... in that it is' is not the same as 'is'; please be so kind as to
address what I've written, not what you wished I had written.

DD
```

```
Main Entry: 1in�tent
Pronunciation: in-'tent
Function: noun
Etymology: Middle English entent, from Old French, from Late
Latin intentus, from Latin, act of stretching out, from intendere
Date: 13th century
1 a : the act or fact of intending : PURPOSE; especially : the
design or purpose to commit a wrongful or criminal act <admitted
wounding him with intent> b : the state of mind with which an act
is done : VOLITION
2 : a usually clearly formulated or planned intention : AIM
3 a : MEANING, SIGNIFICANCE b : CONNOTATION 3
synonym see INTENTION

http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary


Tom Beckner
```

```
http://www.m-w.com/

Main Entry: ha�tred
Pronunciation: 'hA-tr&d
Function: noun
Etymology: Middle English, from hate + Old English r[AE]den
condition -- more at KINDRED
Date: 12th century
1 : HATE
2 : prejudiced hostility or animosity <old racial prejudices and
national hatreds -- Peter Thomson>

There is no reference to the element of action in this
definition, nor is there reference to intent or state of mind.

The only similarity between hatred and intent is one of your
invention, expressed in your opinion.

Tom Beckner
```

```
then a citing would be appreciated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<AW%C5.13064$l35.193206@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <6R_C5.810$aS6.2623@newsfeed.slurp.net> <E9%C5.13056$l35.192605@iad-read.news.verio.net> <Lo%C5.838$aS6.2575@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
In article <Lo%C5.838$aS6.2575@newsfeed.slurp.net>,
Barry Smith <barry@lincolnplatingNOSPAM.com> wrote:
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
>news:E9%C5.13056$l35.192605@iad-read.news.verio.net...
…[89 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or, does 'is' not mean 'is'?

No, but as stated above, 'in that it is' does not equal 'is'.  Consider:

'A square, in that it is a two-dimensional, closed figure with parallel
sides and equal angles totalling 360 degrees (and a few other
qualifications which I have forgotten), is a rectangle.'

'So you are asserting that a square is a rectangle?'

'No, I am asserting that a square, in that it is... (etc.)'

'In that it is' may be seen as a consideration of aspect(s) or an
acknowledgement of a subset of qualities; it is not the equivalation of an
unqualified 'is'.

DD
```

```
On Sun, 5 Oct 3900 14:09:02, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:

> In article <Pp%C5.842$aS6.406@newsfeed.slurp.net>,
> Barry Smith <barry@lincolnplatingNOSPAM.com> wrote:

> >This newsgroup is all 4 newsgroups that this is cross-posted to.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Yes, it is tough but us pollies are tough and we can handle it.

It's wonderful to see that all is normal, super-normal even.  HP stock 
is up, way up today, no problems at HP.  None at Dell, Apple, 
Sun, Priceline, Xerox, EDS, Kodak, United, AOL, Procter and Gamble,
Alcoa, Microsoft even. These companies are soaring, step right up and 
put your money down, you can't win if you don't play. The market is up,
way up and we're all making millions.

Gas is plentiful, the station down the street didn't have their pumps 
"bagged" because their fuel service firm hasn't been answering their
calls for help.  This didn't go on for a week.  I'll check this weekend
to see how much longer this non-problem continues.

Worshington Gas, my default natural gas provider, didn't send me a bill 
for September with a P.G.C. (the cost of the gas itself) that was 25% 
higher than August.  I'm not expecting a bill this winter that's 2X or 
3X previous highs.

The early October weather won't be blustery and windy, nope, it's Indian
summer and there's nothing about the West Nile Virus in the news.

Speaking of HP, I'm buying lots of their stuff.  Just got an 895Cse for 
a client who wants to print proposals, I also picked up a bunch of 
ink carts. They're cleaning up there.  The printer was $230 but the 
extra ink was $170.

A local store is advert'ing Laserjet 4si's for $399 including Lan cards.
I'll be over there next week to get a second one.  I've gotten pretty
good at cleaning up 4si's, I know how to reset the page counter even. 
Maybe I'll service them and resell them.

Now if I could just figure out how to get affordable toner.  The best 
price I've found on toner is $125 (for genuine HP brand)

Oh, and Anna Nicole Smith didn't lose 50 lbs and gain a half billion 
dollars.

Frank's in town but I have to spank naughty cornputers for some clients.

I'll give him a shout on 147.300 this weekend, that is, if I can figure 
out how to invert my HTX-202 and set the PL.

All is well.
```

```
It's not melting down, this is a buying opportunity. This is it, your 
chance to score big-time.  Borrow every cent you can and head over to 
that day-trading room in New Jersey.

Anyway, none of the Doom-sters predicted a 6-9 month delay between the 
IT problems (which not only didn't happen, but couldn't happen.) and the
external manifestation.  Nor did they predict a rolling, growing wave, 
lasting months, years.

If they did, we'd be in the beginning stages of stock market problems as
the 3Q reports trickle in and problems with distribution, inventory,
records escape the containment.  

Since there are no manufacturing or distribution problems, Palm Pilots,
Visors, and Sony Playstation IIs are plentiful, no reports of shortages
of resistors and capacitors (I verified this by visiting Radio Shack,
yep, lots of resistors and capacitors on the walls), not only is
everything fine but poised to shoot to the sky.

Bet big and live big, my man (if you are a man, on the internet, no one 
knows.)

Since nothing happened at midnight, December 31, 1999, there are no 
problems and any slight dip in share values will soon be regained.  Keep
the faith, hold steady at the helm, put your money on the table, you've 
already won.

Oil, electricity, natural gas, pipelines, inventory control, markets, 
distribution, manufacturing, the good times are rolling, Anna Nicole 
Smith lost 50 lbs and won a half billion dollars.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)

- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers

- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <%QPC5.1256$rw.69289@nnrp2.sbc.net> <2hRC5.12934$l35.189936@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8rhs8l$144$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
In article <8rhs8l$144$1@bob.news.rcn.net>,
Tom Beckner <becknert@erols.com> wrote:
>
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>intent with two things, state of mind and an act.

Equates?  Mr Beckner, the definition provides three criteria: state of
mind, an act and doing.  (To speak of 'an act' without speaking of its
'doing' can get rather... involved.)  There is no equating of state of
mind and act; the definition reads 'intention = ((state of mind) with (is
done))
 >
>> ... so hatred, in that it is a state of mind, is an intent.
>
>Nope. State of mind alone does not equal intent, according to
>your reference.

Mr Beckner, the 'act' associated with the state of mind is the crime...
the one associated with 'hate crime'.  My apologies for not having made
that more specific for you.

DD
```

<a id="t-82fab71dd4"></a>
## Thread: Multiple Key-Strokes after Receive(s) — 5 msgs

### Multiple Key-Strokes after Receive(s)

- **From:** Bill Gaphardt <bill_gaphardt@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rd9d6$7b7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
  I have a CICS program that requires me to receive data from the
terminal when the trans-id is typed in by the operator: XALS AUTO or
XALS STOP (or XALS with no data).  I do an ASSIGN STARTCODE to
determine that I was started with Terminal Input, then I do a CICS
RECEIVE INTO an area that's 9 characters long.  AUTO tells it to run as
a background task.  STOP, of course, tells it to stop.  And, the trans-
id with no data tells it to show the screen (SEND the MAP).

  The problem occurs when I type the trans-id with no data and the
screen comes up.  From that point on, if I hit enter I have to hit it
twice.  The same goes for PF-keys - hit 'em twice before they take
affect.  It appears the RECEIVE of the trans-id and data (or no data)
happens the first time I press a key and the RECEIVE of the MAP happens
on the second key-stroke.

  The question is... How do I get my program to react to the first key-
stroke ?





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple Key-Strokes after Receive(s)

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F53B88EDBB43C977.F0A4BB091409D482.F77F02280F7EB286@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<8rd9d6$7b7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

>  I have a CICS program that requires me to receive data from the
>terminal when the trans-id is typed in by the operator: XALS AUTO or
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I expect that you have something wrong in your SEND MAP command.  Post
what you have.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Why does Sea World have a seafood restaurant? I'm halfway through my

http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Steve
```

```
:>screen comes up.  From that point on, if I hit enter I have to hit it
:>twice.  The same goes for PF-keys - hit 'em twice before they take
:>affect.  It appears the RECEIVE of the trans-id and data (or no data)
:>happens the first time I press a key and the RECEIVE of the MAP happens
:>on the second key-stroke.

:>  The question is... How do I get my program to react to the first key-
:>stroke ?

Sounds like a bug.

What does CEDF show as happening?
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple Key-Strokes after Receive(s)

- **From:** Bill Gaphardt <bill_gaphardt@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rvpul$jm7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8rd9d6$7b7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


  I found someone at work who answered my question...

  I only need to receive the data with the startcode once (and then
send the map).  From that point on, I need only receive the map (no
start data).  So, I check EIBCALEN and only receive the start data when
it's equal to zero.  That way, I never do 2 receives in the same
cycle.  Simple !




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple Key-Strokes after Receive(s)

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T07:25:06+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hf95uskqlfmiqb7dmqueji06358f4f49b8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8rd9d6$7b7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 03 Oct 2000 18:43:52 GMT, Bill Gaphardt <bill_gaphardt@my-deja.com> wrote:

>  The problem occurs when I type the trans-id with no data and the
>screen comes up.  From that point on, if I hit enter I have to hit it
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>stroke ?


This looks to me like a bug in your program.  Run CEDF to see the sequence of events.   I
would guess that you would see something like this:


EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP

EXEC CICS SEND MAP

EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP

EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP

EXEC CICS SEND MAP

EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP

EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP

EXEC CICS SEND MAP

etc.

Either in one task, or in a sequence of tasks.  Regardless, this would be a bug.  Two
receives in a row don't make sense, and two receives in one task violate the concept of
pseudo-conversational design


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Who is General Failure, and why is he reading my drive?
```

## Thread: Please help. Error R6003 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Please help. Error R6003

- **From:** "Derek" <prg@bambi.com.hk>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39d98d97$1@newsgate.hknet.com>`

```
Hello,

    I have an cobol programme with wrote five years ago, but when i try to
run it on computer with cpu over 200MHz. It doesn't work. And have an error
message-
Run Time Error R6003
Interger divide by 0
    How can i solve this problem>

Best regards.

Derek
```

#### ↳ Re: Please help. Error R6003

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OalC5.24980$WK6.522589@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<39d98d97$1@newsgate.hknet.com>`

```
I think you are running Realia cobol.
There was indeed such a bug, when the compiler tries to
compute how many lines per minute it compiles. The
bug happens if that number exceeds 65,535. It is easy
to fix by hacking the binary compiler. You can download the
patched compiler module (REALCOB.P) from
http://www.etk.com/products/etkr16/index.htm
then click on fixes.zip, unzip the file into your Realia directory.
If you like, rename the old REALSCOB.P first to
something else.

Derek <prg@bambi.com.hk> wrote in message
news:39d98d97$1@newsgate.hknet.com...
> Hello,
>
>     I have an cobol programme with wrote five years ago, but when i try to
> run it on computer with cpu over 200MHz. It doesn't work. And have an
error
> message-
> Run Time Error R6003
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001002225946.18411.00000183@ng-ct1.aol.com>`

```
Sorry about the non COBOL post but i was thinking of buying one and would like
to know if anyone likes em or hates em before i shell out my $, is it worth it
for a college student?  I would have posted this somewhere else but i aint sure
where else there is.

Thanks,
Deek
```

#### ↳ Re: Palm Pilot question

- **From:** David Bretz <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DA1816.94040A78@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<20001002225946.18411.00000183@ng-ct1.aol.com>`

```
Well....I love mine.   But, I'm not a college student!   I use it every
day.

If you're going to invest in the Palm, a couple of things you may want
to consider.

First, you'll probably be spending (if you're honest) some money for
after market software.  I've replaced my calendar and To Do programs
with more sophisticated ones.  I've also added a program called BugMe,
which allows you to write hand written (non-graffiti) notes to yourself
and set alarms, etc.   There are a couple of other pieces of software
that I've added over the years, but may not be useful to you.

Second......if you're thinking of a Palm Pilot, you may want to consider
the same sort of unit from Handspring (check www.handspring.com).  It
was started by the same folks as developed the Palm, but with some
interesting differences.   

Dave Bretz
```

<a id="t-3107890d3d"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and CORBA — 1 msgs

### COBOL and CORBA

- **From:** "news.iona.com" <stacey.matinale@iona.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rdk46$m6$1@bvweb.iona.com>`

```
We typically don't post product announcements to newsgroups, but genuinely
thought many of you would want to try this product.  You can test your
existing COBOL applications with IONA's OrbixCobol for NT, FREE 90 day
evaluation, which can be run stand-alone on your PC.
http://www.iona.com/OS390

Orbix is a software environment that allows you to build and integrate
distributed applications. Orbix is a full implementation of the Object
Management Group's (OMG) Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA)
specification.  CORBA objects are standard software objects implemented in
any supported programming language. CORBA supports several languages,
including COBOL, PL/I, C++, and Java.

The first step in developing a CORBA application in COBOL is to define the
interfaces to objects in your system, using CORBA IDL. You then compile
these interfaces using an IDL -> COBOL compiler - which is available from
IONA as part of their COBOL solution.  This IDL compiler generates COBOL
code from IDL definitions, and produces the following:

1) Server skeleton code, which allows you to develop the mainframe COBOL
server programs which can accept CORBA requests from off-platform clients,
and

2) COBOL copybook files that define the program attributes and operations in
COBOL format based on the IDL interface for this service

CORBA Clients and servers communicate their request/ replies using the IIOP
protocol which runs over TCP/IP -  i.e. a C++ or Java client on Windows or
Unix can access a COBOL program running in Batch mode, IMS or CICS on OS/390
using IONA's Orbix, iPortal and iPortal OS/390 Server products.

Click on the url below for a Free 90 day Evaluation Copy of OrbixCobol for
NT:

http://www.iona.com/OS390

Note: You require the following to install OrbixCobol for NT 3.0.1:

- Microsoft Visual C++ 6.0 (Service Pack 2).
- A Cobol compiler for NT.  The supported compilers are:
 * NetExpress 3.0
 * Micro Focus (www.microfocus.com).
 * Cobol Workbench V4.0 Micro Focus (www.microfcus.com).

Kind Regards,
Stacey Matinale

IONA Technologies
staceym@iona.com
781 902 8000
```

<a id="t-5f86ca708d"></a>
## Thread: Merant Mainframe Express Evaluation — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Merant Mainframe Express Evaluation

- **From:** David Bretz <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DA97BF.8D181BA8@mescoma.com>`

```
I know you're all sitting on the edge of your office chairs, waiting to
hear about my evaluation of MFE from Merant.   So, here goes.....

First, I'm not finished with it yet.   I've had it installed for 5 days,
and I'm working my way thru the tutorials (I have to balance this with
my job, you know!)   

Second, I'm both pleased and disappointed with MFE.  I'm pleased because
they have created a total development environment which seems to work
well.  Combine MFE with Revolve (which I think is a great analytical
tool) and I believe you could have one heck of a set of development
tools!   The debugging tool in MFE I find to be very intuitive, and
simpler to use than the Workbench Animator (which I have been using for
over 6 years).

I'm disspointed though because even while working thru the very simple
projects presented in the tutorial, I've encountered MFE abends and page
fault errors.  I suppose my thoughts are what happens to MFE when I try
to use it with some of the more complex programs I've written and plan
to write.

Overall though, I'm still very interested in the capabilities of MFE.  I
still have to get thru the regression and testing tools, but I've been
moderately impressed so far.  If the environment becomes even more
stable with future releases, it will be great.

Dave Bretz
```

---

<a id="t-64b2f53ad2"></a>
## Thread: Set switch to true — 31 msgs

### Set switch to true

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net>`

```
A coworker spent several hours debugging a program today which had
multi-valued switches set and checked as part of its logic flow.

I'm not sure it is a good idea to have two-valued switches - I don't
like forcing people to check WORKING-STORAGE to follow the logic flow. 
This will change when we can SET MONTH-SWITCH TO FALSE.

Three way logic flow switches take up too much of the unfamiliar
maintenance programmer's time, and are more likely to be in error at
some time.

A valid-value switch is a different animal:

Saying IF BOULDER or DENVER or LONGMONT is nice.
```

#### ↳ Re: Set switch to true

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T01:32:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SW9D5.7209$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net>`

```

On  4-Oct-2000, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> A coworker spent several hours debugging a program today which had
> multi-valued switches set and checked as part of its logic flow.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Saying IF BOULDER or DENVER or LONGMONT is nice.

Howard,

By valid value, are you referring to the ability to set a value based on the
88?
On the IBM, with this working-storage:

    05 DATA-RECORD-TYPE               PIC X.
               88 RECORD-IS-ACTIVE           VALUE "A".

I can say:

     SET RECORD-IS-ACTIVE TO TRUE

...or am I off the track?

Cliff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DDE0CC.5DB6C4F5@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <SW9D5.7209$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com>`

```


Cliff Peterson wrote:
> 

> Howard,
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cliff

You got it.

A single, obviously named value isn't bad - e.g.: 
	AT END SET SW-PAY-FILE-EOF TO TRUE.

But a maintenance programmer shouldn't have to look at Working storage
to find out whether SET ABC-SWITCH TO TRUE turns DEF-SWITCH off.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <SW9D5.7209$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <39DDE0CC.5DB6C4F5@brazee.net>`

```
This is a good argument.  One way to solve this problem, is use a very 
structured naming convention for your switches.

01  WS-SWITCHES.
    05  FILLER            PIC X(1)  VALUE 'N'.
        88  INPUT-EOF               VALUE 'Y'.
        88  NOT-INPUT-EOF           VALUE 'N'.

If programmers use a decent little convention like this, then many things
during maintenance are 'given' at that point.

I am still a young COBOL programmer.  The most complex program I've written
is 6,200 lines, uses 10 internal tables, does an internal sort without the
SORT verb, has 30+ loops in it, has about 40 hold variables, 15+ switches,
only 3 input files and one output file.  It has a lot of logic in it.  It
runs fast and does alot of work.  It is very fragile.  One incorrectly coded
line in a program like this can have devastating effects to the business.

In a program like that, it helps to have a uniform method to the code.  Many
things in a program like this aren't apparent to a later, maintenance
programming task.

I know this is all off topic, but I picked up a 4 pack of Murphy's Irish
Stout at the store tonight.  It is quite good.

Jeff

----------
In article <39DDE0CC.5DB6C4F5@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:


>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> to find out whether SET ABC-SWITCH TO TRUE turns DEF-SWITCH off.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T03:06:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<buRD5.11637$UP5.225244@news6.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```

On  7-Oct-2000, "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

> This is a good argument.  One way to solve this problem, is use a very
> structured naming convention for your switches.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> during maintenance are 'given' at that point.

Problem is that most programers use the FILE STATUS fields.  EOF is 10, Good
read is 00, and there are about a hundred other possibilities.  NOT EOF
becomes problematic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Glenn A. Mitchell" <g.mitchell@computer.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hs5E5.6558$z66.184919@newsr1.maine.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com> <buRD5.11637$UP5.225244@news6.giganews.com>`

```
I tend to use multiple 88's on FILE STATUS a lot.  A(n almost) typical usage
might be roughly:

01  WS-FILE-STATUS.
        05    MASTER-IN-FILE-STATUS                     PIC XX.
                88  MI-IO-SUCCESSFUL                          VALUE "00".
                88  MI-END-OF-FILE                                 VALUE
"10".
                88  MI-DUPLICATE-KEY                          VALUE "22".
.
.
.
        READ MASTER-IN-FILE
        PERFORM 1000-PROCESS-MASTER-RECORD
                 UNTIL NOT MI-IO-SUCCESSFUL
        END-PERFORM
        IF NOT MI-END-OF-FILE
                    perform some error routine etc.

I've not had a problem with this approach to-date, but . . . there are file
statuses other than 00 that are OK in some instances/architectures.  Adding
those values to MI-IO-SUCCESSFUL might be the way to go, but . . . may not
in some circumstances.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E1C8AF.C3F1E4F9@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com> <buRD5.11637$UP5.225244@news6.giganews.com> <Hs5E5.6558$z66.184919@newsr1.maine.rr.com>`

```


"Glenn A. Mitchell" wrote:
> 
> I've not had a problem with this approach to-date, but . . . there are file
> statuses other than 00 that are OK in some instances/architectures.  Adding
> those values to MI-IO-SUCCESSFUL might be the way to go, but . . . may not
> in some circumstances.

I was using a called program which handled VSAM KSDS (keyed) file I/O
which made that assumption.  But in the OPEN command, '97' is also a
successful return code.  Whomever programmed that call program did not
realize that, and returned a generic error message to the calling
program.

In your case, you still have the return code available, so a maintenance
programmer can do an extra check by hand.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rse9n$insnv$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com> <buRD5.11637$UP5.225244@news6.giganews.com> <Hs5E5.6558$z66.184919@newsr1.maine.rr.com> <Pj9E5.15156$UP5.264903@news6.giganews.com>`

```

Cliff Peterson <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:Pj9E5.15156$UP5.264903@news6.giganews.com...
>
> On  8-Oct-2000, "Glenn A. Mitchell" <g.mitchell@computer.org> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > those values to MI-IO-SUCCESSFUL might be the way to go, but . . . may
not
> > in some circumstances.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cliff

I think that you missed what he was doing...

He had the end of file switch as a separate switch (away from the file
status).  As in:

01 WS-SWITCHES.
      05 INPUT-EOF-SW                PIC X   VALUE 'N'.
           88 EOF-INPUT                                VALUE 'Y'.
           88 NOT-EOF-INPUT                      VALUE 'N'.

01 WS-FILE-STATUS.
     05 INPUT-STATUS                  PIC X(2).

...

OPEN INPUT INPUT-FILE.

PERFORM UNTIL EOF-INPUT
   READ INPUT-FILE INTO WS-INPUT-RECORD
        AT END SET-EOF-INPUT TO TRUE
        NOT AT END
            IF INPUT-STATUS NOT = '00'
                  abend - or what ever
            END-IF
    END-READ
    do something else...
END-PERFORM.

CLOSE INPUT-FILE.

SET NOT-EOF-INPUT TO TRUE.

OPEN INPUT INPUT-FILE.

PERFORM UNTIL EOF-INPUT
   READ INPUT-FILE INTO WS-INPUT-RECORD
        AT END SET-EOF-INPUT TO TRUE
        NOT AT END
            IF INPUT-STATUS NOT = '00'
                  abend - or what ever
            END-IF
    END-READ
    do something else, but probably not the same thing you did last time  :)
END-PERFORM.

CLOSE INPUT-FILE.

I have used this type of assignment before, but can only see a purpose for
the NOT-EOF switch if you are going to reopen the file for some reason.  I
have drifted toward using this instead...

01 WS-FILE-STATUS.
     05 INPUT-STATUS             PIC X(2)  VALUE '00'.
          88 EOF-INPUT                                VALUE '10'.
          88 NOT-EOF-INPUT                      VALUE '00'.

Where the eof switch is set under the file status, and, again, the only use
for the NOT-EOF switch is to reset the status because I am using the file
again (I do a lot of dynamic allocation to the same DD and have a tendency
to name my file status switches by the DD name).  I also do all of my file
status checking (except EOF) in the same paragraph (so I don't have to keep
repeating the same code and messages).  Anyway...

Dave
```

```
That was used as an illustration.  But I am curious - which really is
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qYnE5.26019$bI6.914412@news1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com> <buRD5.11637$UP5.225244@news6.giganews.com> <39E1C68A.480C8305@brazee.net>`

```

On  9-Oct-2000, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> > > This is a good argument.  One way to solve this problem, is use a very
> > > structured naming convention for your switches.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> are keyed files.

See my other reply - I'm feeling particularly chastised about the NOT-EOF,
but I'm still feeling opinionated...

Mostly, I see AT END, but some of the code on old mainframe systems is 30
years old.  Personally, I have problems using AT END to set a flag when FILE
STATUS is a perfectly good flag itself.

So,
READ XXXX AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW
IF EOF-SW-TRUE....

Or, (assuming XXXX-EOF is part of the FILE STATUS stuff)
READ XXXX
IF XXXX-EOF...

I think that AT END lends itself to unstructured code.  Much of the time,
the "read" paragraph contains a READ, followed by some moves or decisions. 
AT END forces you to GO TO an exit to skip them (or set a flag), where the
file status allows you a nice, clean IF following the read..
But - your mileage may vary.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E2126C.C4CCDD8B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <8roh3s0cc8@enews2.newsguy.com> <buRD5.11637$UP5.225244@news6.giganews.com> <39E1C68A.480C8305@brazee.net> <qYnE5.26019$bI6.914412@news1.giganews.com>`

```
Cliff Peterson wrote:
> 

> > That was used as an illustration.  But I am curious - which really is
> > more common, using an AT END or checking file status?  With programs
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> STATUS is a perfectly good flag itself.

This is the main reason I use status codes.  I got used to writing that
way with database AT-END type status code checking (although even there
I found more often that not, other coders set external switches - with a
bit more reason as all database records use the same status code).

With my style of coding, where the READ comes at the end of the main
process perform, this works nicely.  When you read multiple files
though, an AT END PERFORM THIS-ROUTINE or NOT AT END PERFORM
THAT-ROUTINE can be nice.

You do want some kind of switch for match/merge logic - and that can be
with status codes.
 
> I think that AT END lends itself to unstructured code.  Much of the time,
> the "read" paragraph contains a READ, followed by some moves or decisions.
> AT END forces you to GO TO an exit to skip them (or set a flag), where the
> file status allows you a nice, clean IF following the read..

Or with my style, telling the PERFORM that you're done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t794s$n17$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
SET TO FALSE is part of the next Standard, but is NOT part of the '85
Standard.  MERANT (and possibly some others) already support it - but it is
not currently "portable".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t7hg6$qb8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <IsqJ5.21615$rD3.1393974@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <39f6fe95.173461264@news> <8t794s$n17$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <t8bevs4vaf92u675fsrk7ti9hgvk4rj70r@4ax.com>`

```
There is a NEW phrase for 88-levels that tells which value is set, e.g.

05 EOF-Switch  Pic X Value "N".
  88  EOF   Value "Y" "y" FALSE "N"

The full form of the phrase - including optional words is
   WHEN SET TO FALSE IS literal
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T07:50:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F7D42E.93F18375@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <IsqJ5.21615$rD3.1393974@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <39f6fe95.173461264@news>`

```
Kevin J. Hansen wrote:
> One thing, however, is I usually give my switches a name instead of
> FILLER so that I can look 'em up in my debugger!

I dislike 88 levels because they are hard to find in source code. 
Unless one uses a consitent prefix for the flag variable and all 88
levels belonging to it then it is necessary to do multiple searches or
greps to find where the flag is used.

Using MOVEs and IF = it is easy to find where these variables are set
and used with a grep.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39f743c9.255257@news>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <IsqJ5.21615$rD3.1393974@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <39f6fe95.173461264@news> <39F7D42E.93F18375@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Thu, 26 Oct 2000 07:50:22 +0100, Richard Plinston
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>I dislike 88 levels because they are hard to find in source code. 
>Unless one uses a consitent prefix for the flag variable and all 88
>levels belonging to it then it is necessary to do multiple searches or
>greps to find where the flag is used.

Richard,

I definitely agree that 88s can make life difficult, and we don't
always use 'em, it depends on the circumstances.

One of the really nice things about an 88-level is that it's
self-documenting when used properly.

A case in point:  

Our old character-mode screen manager didn't use 88-level items for
anything.  As a result, you needed to refer to the manual to figure
out what a given returned value meant, or to know what value to move
in to a field to cause desired behavior.

We fielded lots of phone calls from people who wanted to know how to
do certain things - to the extent that "it's on page 3-23" was the
number one answer to the most common tech support questions...

We resolved we'd figure a way to eliminate this sort of question with
our new screen manager, so GUI ScreenIO generates 88-level items
beneath (virtually) all control fields.  Now, instead of knowing that
you need to move 'X' to panel-whatever to cause something to happen,
you just set the desired plain-English 88-level item to TRUE.  You
don't need to know the value.  The names are self-explanatory so you
don't really need the manual anymore.

Also, since in GUI ScreenIO also predefine all of the events that
could cause a return to your program (and assign your own names to
them), we can also generate 88-level items for those - so all of the
stuff you need to handle in your program is clearly documented in each
panel's copybook.  It REALLY made it easier.

The "shouldn't-need-to-call-for-tech-support-on-this-subject" tech
support calls went way down.  Which is how everyone likes it.

-------
Incidentally, the other nice thing about an 88 level item is that no
code is generated for it unless it's referenced in a program.  So, a
big collection of codes defined as 88's is quite efficient in terms of
storage utilization.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T23:04:55+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F81DE6.A707B0B0@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <IsqJ5.21615$rD3.1393974@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <39f6fe95.173461264@news> <39F7D42E.93F18375@Azonic.co.nz> <39f743c9.255257@news>`

```
"Kevin J. Hansen" wrote:

> I definitely agree that 88s can make life difficult, and we don't
> always use 'em, it depends on the circumstances.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> storage utilization.

Great post,  worth repeating.   This is my experience as well.  I write
small utility functions for everyone to use and there is little need for
documentation apart from what module you want.

As a simple question:  Is zero a good value or bad?

Zero is good in Operating systems as a general rule.

Zero is considered as NO by a few programming languages, as in not set.

One 88 level clears this up directly in the copybook that they are
probably looking at anyway.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T05:50:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F7C5CC.D533164B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <IsqJ5.21615$rD3.1393974@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <39f6fe95.173461264@news> <39F7D42E.93F18375@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Kevin J. Hansen wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> and used with a grep.

Xpeditor is the debugger I am most familiar with.  I haven't tried
lately, but I suspect you can't set a KEEP to watch a data item if it
has no name.

In my shop standards a condition name is supposed to look like this:
88  88-100-ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED     VALUE 'Y'.

The 88- prefix tells me it's a condition name, the 100- tells me which
level 01 record owns it, and all the condition names sort together in
the OS/VS COBOL cross-reference listing.  Back then we actually filed
hardcopy listings.  I didn't write that shop standard, but it works well
enough for me. 

ISPF doesn't have grep, but you can do "X ALL;F
88-100-ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED ALL".  If you have the ISRONLY macro
installed it's even easier:  "ONLY 88-100-ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED".

But it's still hard to search the source code if the condition name is
SET sometimes, and other times a value is MOVE'd into its parent.  The
data-name usually varies enough from the condition name that
exclude-all-global-find-string-all won't locate both names.

That's why they pay you the big bucks.


http://arnold.trembley.home.att.net/
```

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> ISPF doesn't have grep, but you can do "X ALL;F
> 88-100-ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED ALL".  If you have the ISRONLY macro
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> exclude-all-global-find-string-all won't locate both names.

Yeah, I tend to x;all;f sw-bill-eof, then I show the line above and below
the switch to find out the variable name and any other 88 levels, then do a
find on them.

Of course, this is generally by doing an SE on the SDSF listing.  One
trouble with copy members is that I can't guarantee that I have found all
instances of that variable in the source code.  So often, my first step in
debugging is to do a compile, just so that I can view the listing.
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

L is correct.  F3, L4  display first 3, or last 4 excluded lines.  FLIP switches
excluded with non excluded.  Before making global changes, I often do a X ALL;F
MY-STRING ALL, then look at the fields, excluding every instance of MY-STRING-1,
and then doing a C MY-STRING YOUR-STRING NX ALL, to change just non-excluded
occurances.   This powerful exclude command is enough to counter the advantages
of PC based editors I have used.
```

```
i usually set the value to 1 for true and zero for false and skip the
set X to true / 88 statements. a real programmer will look up the
values, or look at the next comment line figuring out what does what.

or, my personal favorite

*somewhere in the storage section

02 some-data-item pic 99.

02 constants.
	03 false pic 99 value 0.
	03 true  pic 99 value 1.

*somewhere in the procedure division

move true to some-data-item

ok, doesn't work if you have two sets of the same settings name, but
you can improvise and make sdi-true and sdi-false variables

i could never get the hang of level 88's. comments, yes, but
non-existing data values?


reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set switch to true

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogrower@telocity.co>

- **Message-ID:** `<I_5K5.20819$Q8.4197743@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<39DBAC29.246F67A5@brazee.net> <IsqJ5.21615$rD3.1393974@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <39f6fe95.173461264@news>`

```
Get a better debugger! or the upgrade.  Viasoft current (and previous rel)
supports
it with 'KD' or 'ZD'  on the data def line!.

NO you CAN NOT SET  a condition-name (88) to FALSE.

You can in IBM COBOL set an UPSI switch to 'on value' or 'off value'.

Try names like this:

05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
    88  WS-MASTER-TRAN-FILE-EOF         VALUE "NO".
    88  WS-MORE-MASTER-TRANS            VALUE "YES".

BTW, when you have this:
05  WS-SOME-VARIABLE         PIC X(??).
05   REDEFINES WS-SOME-VARIABLE.

You can drop FILLER from any REDEFINES where you do not refer
to the group name.
```

<a id="t-595b426460"></a>
## Thread: GO TO exit — 74 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### GO TO exit

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>`

```
Here's my troll for today...

In a prior thread, we talked about CONTINUE, NEXT SENTENCE and the use of
one period per paragraph.  We have talked about programmers who code GO TOs
to an exit paragraph, like this:

PERFORM ENTRY-PARA THRU ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
...........................
ENTRY-PARA.
    DO THIS.
    DO THAT.
    IF SOME-CONDITION
        GO TO ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
    DO THE-OTHER.
    DO SOMETHING-ELSE.

ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
    EXIT.

Now, assuming you use one period per paragraph, couldn't you do this?

PERFORM ENTRY-PARA.
...........................
ENTRY-PARA.
    DO THIS
    DO THAT
    IF SOME-CONDITION
        NEXT SENTENCE
    END-IF
    DO THE-OTHER
    DO SOMETHING-ELSE
    .  <---(period)

NEXT SENTENCE moves you past the period, essentially exiting you from the
procedure.  Cute, but strange.

While this is not my cup of tea, it also points out the difference between
NEXT SENTENCE and CONTINUE when eliminating periods within a paragraph. 
Newbies beware!
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** David Bretz <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DBA981.46499CDE@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>`

```
sure you could.......until some bozo that doesn't understand what you
did comes along and adds code behind the period.  



Cliff Peterson wrote:
> 
> Here's my troll for today...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Newbies beware!
```

```
> In a prior thread, we talked about CONTINUE, NEXT SENTENCE and the use of

…[34 more quoted lines elided]…

This might be a good program to give to someone in a class to debug.  I
certainly don't want programmers using such "tricks" as there is too big
of a likelihood that during the life of the program, someone won't
understand it.

For this reason, I never type NEXT SENTENCE anymore - even though I use
full stop periods after every sentence.
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DBC235.6E938C7@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>`

```
My motto in programming is "clarity, not cleverness". It is certainly 
okay to be clever but not at the expense of making the program confusing. 
You should never use "next sentence" in this context without
very explicit comments. It could confuse even competent programmers.
Don't be cute just to be cute. It does however make a good question for
one of those "How well do you know obscure cobol syntax" quizzes.
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rh1j4$9ib$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>`

```
Actually, in an ANSI conforming program this is NOT legal code.  There is an
explicit rule that says that "thou shalt not" use a NEXT SENTENCE in an IF
statement that is terminated by an END-IF.  As I recall, you (Cliff) use an
IBM mainframe compiler.  They, like several others, do have an extension to
allow this - but it is NOT "conforming" source code and is not necessarily
portable.
```

- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rh1j4$9ib$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

> an explicit rule that says that "thou shalt not" use a NEXT SENTENCE in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> is not necessarily portable.

Outstanding.  BTW I didn't say I liked the construct, just found it
interesting. In fact, one of the reasons for my post was to warn newbies
(especially the big iron ones) that "here be dragons".
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:07:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>`

```
In article <TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>,
  cliff.peterson@spamcop.net wrote:
> Here's my troll for today...
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph. Newbies beware!


Hmm, that seems a bit dangerous; one misplaced full stop could bollix
the whole thing. I would be inclined to code it thusly:


    PERFORM ENTRY-PARA THRU ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.

    .
    .
    .


    ENTRY-PARA.

        DO THIS.

        DO THAT.

        IF NOT SOME-CONDITION
            DO THE-OTHER
            DO SOMETHING-ELSE
        END-IF

    ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.  EXIT.


People often cringe at the NOT operator, but I find the above much more
straightforward than using NEXT SENTENCE or CONTINUE.  I have often seen
long chains of:

    IF CONDITION-A
        NEXT SENTENCE
    ELSE
        PERFORM PROCESS-X.

    IF CONDITION-B
        NEXT SENTENCE
    ELSE
        PERFORM PROCESS-Y.

I cannot believe that people would code such simply to avoid the NOT
operator.  Is the above really clearer than the following?

    IF NOT CONDITION-A
        PERFORM PROCESS-X.

    IF NOT CONDITION-B
        PERFORM PROCESS-Y.

Or, to eliminate the full stops:

    IF NOT CONDITION-A
        PERFORM PROCESS-X
    END-IF

    IF NOT CONDITION-B
        PERFORM PROCESS-Y
    END-IF

The last example is my preference.


Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
cbass2112@my-deja.com wrote:
> (snip)  
> People often cringe at the NOT operator, but I find the above much more
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> operator.  (snip)

While I tend to agree you, I have to tell you I see this all the time in
my shop (except that we use CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE), because
it is a shop standard and it is enforced by peer review.

It does seem silly in the trivial example, but there can be logic
problems when combining NOT, AND, OR in the same condition:

IF EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "M"
OR EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "S"
    PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.

This example tends to cause newbies some problems in testing.  Suddenly
you find that "Married" and "Single" are invalid marital statuses. 
That's why we have the silly rule, and why it is enforced by peer
review.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rj4ll$2jm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net>,
  arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
> cbass2112@my-deja.com wrote:
> > (snip)
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> by peer review.

Well, my view is that the above problem should be explained; if
EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS is "M" then it is not "S", and vice-versa, ergo,
PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS will *always* be performed since at
least one of the sub-conditions will always be true. Simply replacing
the "OR" with "AND" will produce the desired result.

Am I the only one geeky enough to realize this??!? ;)

In normal English, this doesn't seem to be a problem:

"That person doesn't look female *or* male . . ."

     NOT ('Female' OR 'Male')

"That person doesn't look female and that person doesn't look male,
either . . ."

     NOT 'Female' AND NOT 'Male'

What gives, y'all?


Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
