```
In article <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

[snippage]

>> I cannot believe that people would code such simply to avoid the NOT
>> operator.  (snip)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>it is a shop standard and it is enforced by peer review.

Why, Mr Trembley... how's this, you have something which affects your work
and is not accounted for in the Dilworth Anomaly?

I've consulted in shops with the 'No Negative Conditionals' rule and it
has been... difficult, to say the least.  Yes, the archetypical 2-yr
maintenance jockey might, at 3:AM have problems with

IF NOT CONDITION-A AND NOT CONDITION-B

... but I would argue (and have, at times, even with success!) that this
is prohibited by a 'No NOT... AND' injunction while a simple negative
conditional might still be clear.

(the condition being checked was for something like a valid department
number; the code was something like

READ-N-EDIT.

    READ TRANSFIL
        AT END MOVE 'Y' TO WS-TRANSFIL-SW
               GO TO RNE-EXIT.

    IF DEPTNO = '0144' OR '3421' OR '3895' OR '3829'...
    (several lines of ORs later)
        NEXT SENTENCE
    ELSE
        PERFORM INVALID-DEPTNO-RITUAL THRU IDR-EX
        GO TO RNE-EXIT.

(the paragraph was invoked, of course by PERFORM READ-N-EDIT THRU RNE-EXIT
UNTIL NO-MORE-TRANSFIL)

I argued that in absence of a lookup-table or VSAM read it would be best
to code a copybook containing:

05  WS-DEPTNO PIC X(4).
    88  VALID-DEPTNO VALUES '0144', '3421', '3895'. '3829'... etc.

... and change the code to something like

    MOVE DEPTNO TO WS-DEPTNO.
    IF NOT VALID-DEPTNO
        PERFORM INVALID-DEPTNO-RITUAL THRU IDR-EX
        GO TO RNE-EXIT.

After the squawking about the negative conditional there came the
interchange:

'But... when we get a new department we'll have to change the copybook and
compile all the programs that use it!'

'That's right... what do you do now?'

'Well... we change all the programs themselves and recompile them.'

'So what's better... changing one source module, a copybook, and
recompiling... or changing a bunch of source modules, the programs, and
recompiling them?'

'Uhhhhhh..... that's a Good Question, I'll have to get back to you on
that.')

DD
```

```
I'd thought of that first time through... but, believe it or not, my goal
was not to get Standards to allow for negative conditionals...

... it was to get the tests for VALID-something (in this case DEPTNO, in
other cases BRANCHNO, etc.) out of PROCEDURE DIVISION hardcoding and into
88s and WORKING-STORAGE copybooks.

The negative conditional was, in effect, a smokescreen.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DD147C.4A385099@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <h0%C5.13054$l35.192837@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:

> I've consulted in shops with the 'No Negative Conditionals' rule and it
> has been... difficult, to say the least.  Yes, the archetypical 2-yr
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Actually, in such a case I argue for building a dynamic list from an external
file. When a new department is added, or an old one is deleted, update the
external file & don't bother recompiling anything. There're always tradeoffs
of course-if the list is pretty static I'd go along with embedding it.

On the original question, whenever I get confused by combined NOTs and ANDs or
ORs I start using parentheses. What's wrong with

    IF NOT (MARITAL-STATUS = 'M' OR MARITAL-STATUS='S')
        PERFORM INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS

?
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2jD5.25$Cb6.18730@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <h0%C5.13054$l35.192837@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39DD147C.4A385099@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote in message
news:39DD147C.4A385099@dced.state.ak.us...

> On the original question, whenever I get confused by combined NOTs and
ANDs or
> ORs I start using parentheses. What's wrong with
>
>     IF NOT (MARITAL-STATUS = 'M' OR MARITAL-STATUS='S')
>         PERFORM INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS
>

    EVALUTE MARITAL-STATUS
      WHEN "M"
       WHEN "S"
            CONTINUE
       WHEN OTHER
            PERFORM INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS
     END-EVALUATE

      --------------------------
05 MARITAL-STATUS-SW
   88 VALID-MARITAL-STATUS  VALUES ARE "M", "S".
   ....
    IF VALID-MARITAL-STATUS
         CONTINUE
     ELSE
         PERFORM INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS
     END-IF


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DDDCCF.D65E1C77@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <h0%C5.13054$l35.192837@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39DD147C.4A385099@dced.state.ak.us> <f2jD5.25$Cb6.18730@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <josephk-730A49.20243106102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
Another thing I do in the era of cut 'n paste is to code the whole
condition each time.

(Actually, I would use 88 levels, table lookups, and/or EVALUATE - but
since this is the example of the thread, let me make my point).

I say
    IF GENDER = 'M'
    OR GENDER = 'F'
    OR GENDER = 'U'
    OR GENDER = SPACE
        PERFORM XXXX

This allows one to comment out a condition, and it instantly correctly
readable.  Sure, I could have saved keystrokes by putting that condition
on one line without repeating the "A", but I want my intentions
instantly obvious to all.
```

```
Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-730A49.20243106102000@news.m.iinet.net.au...
> In article <f2jD5.25$Cb6.18730@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>, "Michael
> Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>     END-IF

Its maybe 60-40 I'd do it that way - today. When I first started working in
COBOL, my supervisor was very big on using *positive* tests (i.e., avoiding
NOT) . At the time, I didn't understand what the big deal was, but I do now.

I'm comfy with NOT in a simple condition, but NOT in combined conditions
still screws me up big time.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E740CC.ACAF1DAD@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <39DE06B9.83B938C8@dced.state.ak.us> <m6oD5.13297$l35.201931@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1WID5.94951$dZ2.39375579@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <9DOD5.13470$l35.215327@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9auF5.120911$dZ2.54325179@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Tim Hillock wrote:
> 
> Yes Common-law is a form of marriage and show have all the legal benefits of
> a regular marriage.   "C" could be used to distinguish between the two
> types.

In Colorado, if you represent yourself in public as married (and can
otherwise be legally married) - you are.

Which means, do your analysis before making any assumptions on how the
program should work.

I know that a century ago some people wanted to throw a visiting Sheik
in jail for polygamy - but that our law recognized his marriages as
being legal.  I suppose that hasn't changed, but I am glad I don't have
to modify a program which wants his spouse's name for insurance
purposes.
```

- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <1WID5.94951$dZ2.39375579@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <9DOD5.13470$l35.215327@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9auF5.120911$dZ2.54325179@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <LuuF5.201$B_2.15790@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:

> In article <9auF5.120911$dZ2.54325179@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>,
> Tim Hillock <timhillock@home.com> wrote:
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

As far as I know a common-law marriage does not require court sanction (divorce decree) to be
dissolved although it might require a court hearing to settle dissolution/support
disagreements. Nor, I believe, would it be considered bigamous if either or both parties were
either already married or later got married, to other people of course. To me, that implies
that the two statuses are not legally identical. Although they might receive equal benefits it
doesn't seem like they would receive equal protection. Or maybe I'm wrong-it's not my
specialty.
```

```
DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

```
> DD

I debated that because I too thing that legal protection is a benefit, but I couldn't find another
way of saying it. What I was thinking about was similar to what Robert Heinlein said in "The Moon
is a Harsh Mistress" (paraphrased because I can't remember it exactly).

Reporter questioning ambassador from Lunar government: "I understand that divorce is quite simple
in your society, you simply announce that you're divorced and that's it, right?"

Ambassador: "Simple? Divorce is never simple. You've got belongings to split, the couple might be
business partners, there might be children. No, we might not have much in the way of legal
requirements but that doesn't make it simple."
```

```
If you are a programmer in Vermont, you would have to code three marital
statuses: S for single, M for married, and P for domestic partnership.
Vermont is the only state so far that recognizes gay partnerships in any
way.
```

```
Well, what *I* learned was that Microfocus COBOL doesn't support the 2nd form as I have
it configured. Error message is 'illegal operand' and I'm afraid that I'm not interested
enough to track down whether or not I could reconfigure it to support it. The form I
proposed works fine, applying the NOT to the *entire* phrase within the parentheses.
NOT(A OR B) is the equivalent to NOT(A) AND NOT(B) but to me it is clearer (more
English-like).
```

```
I once worked on a system where the allowed values for 'GENDER' were Male,
Female, MaleWasFemale, FemaleWasMale, and Unknown. They were debating
MaleWasFemaleWasMale and FemaleWasMaleWasFemale. The really funny thing was
it was a gas utility and I couldn't image how this was any of their
business, or useful to know.

I guess this example also proves a point in one of the other threads about
maintenance being a reaction to changes rather than bug fixes. Who in 1965
would have predicted the need for 'GENDER' to have more than 2 values?

Karl
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

We always need to program for unknowns.  But the values for gender in a
business application should always include "UNKNOWN".  It might be a
good idea to include "Not Given" for people who say "None Of Your
Business".

Of course, I love the program which determined that a Joe Smith (M)
checked into a hospital and never came out.  While another program is
trying to figure out when Jane Smith (F), who checked out last week
entered the hospital.  That's why you have patient numbers.

I like the person who always put down "M" for sex, until she married and
switched to "F".  (you decide what those two initials stand for).
```

```
Why is it that COBOL shops have this ridiculous restriction.
I don't know of ANY other language were there are more artificial 
restrictions on what a programmer may or may not do.

No one tells a C++ programmer that they can't use some of the language 
constructs. Ditto for Java, Fortran, pascal, etc.

If a programmer gets confused on the above statement, then they should 
not be in the job.

As to the above why not code:

IF NOT (EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS EQUAL "M" AND
        EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS EQUAL "S")
    PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com

```
This won't work, EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS can't be "M" and "S" at the
same time. This would work if you change the "and" to an "or". There
are less confusing ways of doing this still, using continue and else
and ditch the "not".

>
> As to the above why not code:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** ttate@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rinvi$nh5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <8rimcv$lsl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Nope, the OR definately won't work.  The original is equivalent to

IF EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "M" AND
   EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "S"

an OR would translate the same way:
IF EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "M" OR
   EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "S"

so that 'S' becomes an invalid value (evaluation stops after the first
condition is met).

This is exactly why the simpler is the better.


> This won't work, EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS can't be "M" and "S" at the
> same time. This would work if you change the "and" to an "or". There
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Yes, the "or" will work:

    IF NOT (EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS EQUAL "M"     or
            EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS EQUAL "S")
        PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OCnI5.10279$Q8.2070645@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <8rimcv$lsl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rinvi$nh5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8s7h44$uth$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Well, Well, the original question was whether to PERFORM ABEND or
GO TO ABEND, was it not?

Really, if you take a goal of having no warnings when you compile (level
W/4) and as few
Infos (level 02/01 I) as possible, then you should try to avoid the
This statement can never be reached' or 'there is no return from performed
range'.

Therefore for an ABEND routine, you should make it CLEAR that you are GOing
TO ABEND
the program.

Now as for NEGATIONS and abbreviated conditions,  not all compilers support
abbreviated
conditions!

Also, Most problems with 'OR' and 'NOT' involve the coder's failure to
understand how they
are grouped:

AND is a BINARY relation (2 items x AND Y) whereas OR and NOT are UNARY (one
item :
X OR Y, if X, evaluation ENDS, if not X, Y is evaled)
Therefore, and to make it clear months / years later, I always use
Parenthesis and condition-names
that lend themselves to both True and False (e.g,
  05  FILLER  PIC X(3)                     VALUE SPACES.
        88  WS-NO-FILE-ERROR       VALUE 'NO '.
        88  WS-FILE-ERROR               VALUE 'YES'.
)
IF NOT-FIRST-RECORD  and WS-NO-FILE-ERROR
    do whatever

for more complex I use parentheses to EXPLICITLY order my evaluations so
that
should the compiler or language std change, my code executes the same after
compile.


<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8s7h44$uth$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Yes, the "or" will work:
>
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8st4gi$51e$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <8rimcv$lsl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8rinvi$nh5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8s7h44$uth$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <OCnI5.10279$Q8.2070645@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```

"mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.com> wrote in message
news:OCnI5.10279$Q8.2070645@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net...
 <snip>
>
> Now as for NEGATIONS and abbreviated conditions,  not all compilers
support
> abbreviated
> conditions!
>

All "standard conforming" compilers support them.  (This is/was true for
'68, '74 *and* '85 Standard compilers).  Given your reference (in a part of
the snipped code) tor RC=4, my guess is that you are "used to" IBM mainframe
compilers.  It is true that OS/VS COBOL supported *some* extension
abreviated conditions that are not supported by other vendors or more recent
IBM compilers.  HOWEVER, all Standard conforming compilers support "not"
with combined abreviated conditions - when they meet the Standard
requirements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jb5D5.6709$Ly1.76538@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```

On  5-Oct-2000, Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

> Why is it that COBOL shops have this ridiculous restriction.
> I don't know of ANY other language were there are more artificial
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> constructs. Ditto for Java, Fortran, pascal, etc.

Depends upon the shop, not the language.
"Nobody tells me how to code" gets you shown the door in any organization
that values their code.  It's not your code - it's theirs.

> As to the above why not code:
>
> IF NOT (EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS EQUAL "M" AND
>         EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS EQUAL "S")
>     PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.

How about:

05  EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS     PIC X.
      88  VALID-MARITAL-STATUS     VALUES "M" "S".
------------
IF NOT VALID-MARITAL-STATUS
    PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rird1$ftl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au...
> In article <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net>,
> arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>
 <snip>
> >
> > It does seem silly in the trivial example, but there can be logic
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The reason that some (not all - possibly not most) COBOL shops have such
standards for COBOL - but not other languages is that COBOL has a (rather
unusual) construct called "combined abreviated conditional" expressions.
Furthermore, how "NOT" worked in such expression has actually CHANGED over
the history of COBOL.  I have trouble "resurected" all the stange cases -
but can if someone really asks me to.  The bottom-line is that if you ALWAYS
use "full" expression with NOT and AND/OR, then you shouldn't have too may
problems, however, if you (sometimes) leave out parts of the expression (as
COBOL allows) you may WELL end-up with difficult to maintain programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Change in NOT (was: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rlbr5$plf$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <8rird1$ftl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <39dddc3c.85373910@news1.qc.sympatico.ca>`

```
1) The "classic" change in code semantics was between the '68 and '74
Standards.  (For older IBM mainframe compilers, this was "continued" with
via the difference between LANGLVL(1) and LANGLVL(2)).  Consider the code
statement:

     IF A = B AND NOT LESS THAN C OR D

In the '68 Standard, this was "equivalent" to:

     IF ((A = B) AND NOT (A < C) OR (A < D))

In the '74 (and '85) Standard, this is equivalent to:

     IF ((A = B) AND (A NOT < C) OR (A NOT < D))

2) The other example was a "clarification" in the '85 Standard of what was
and was not allowed:

I'll have to "dig out my notes" to find an example - but the bottom-line is
that you are (now) NOT allowed to use "NOT" before the "extended relational
operators" "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO" and "LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO".  This had
to do with whether or not you were trying to say "(NOT GREATER THAN ...) or
(EQUAL TO ...)" or NOT (GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO) ...
```

```
And if we were allowed to do so - I would shoot down any such code I saw
in a walk-thru.

Put in the parenthesis.
Repeat both sides of the comparison operator.
Leave no doubt in your successor's mind about what your intentions were.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e09e07.661111134@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <8rird1$ftl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Here is one "strange case" and maybe a hint that will make things
clearer when using NOTs.

When combining conditions the NOTS must be repeated.  For example.

01  DATA-ITEM PIC X.

MOVE "X" TO DATA-ITEM.

IF NOT DATA-ITEM = "A" AND NOT DATA-ITEM = "B"
    DISPLAY "TRUE
ELSE
    DISPLAY "FALSE"
END-IF

MOVE "B" TO DATA-ITEM

IF NOT DATA-ITEM = "A" AND NOT DATA-ITEM = "B"
    DISPLAY "TRUE
ELSE
    DISPLAY "FALSE"
END-IF

Will result in a display of:

TRUE
FALSE

But if you do this:

MOVE "X" TO DATA-ITEM.

IF NOT DATA-ITEM = "A" AND  "B"
    DISPLAY "TRUE
ELSE
    DISPLAY "FALSE"
END-IF

MOVE "B" TO DATA-ITEM

IF NOT DATA-ITEM = "A" AND  "B"
    DISPLAY "TRUE
ELSE
    DISPLAY "FALSE"
END-IF

You will get:

FALSE
TRUE

Repeating the NOT's however, will yield the proper results.

Ie:

MOVE "X" TO DATA-ITEM.

IF NOT DATA-ITEM = "A" AND NOT "B"
    DISPLAY "TRUE
ELSE
    DISPLAY "FALSE"
END-IF

MOVE "B" TO DATA-ITEM

IF NOT DATA-ITEM = "A" AND NOT  "B"
    DISPLAY "TRUE
ELSE
    DISPLAY "FALSE"
END-IF

gives:

TRUE
FALSE

On Thu, 5 Oct 2000 16:21:03 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
>news:josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au...
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<HQ6D5.87372$dZ2.34333448@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```

"Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au...
> In article <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net>,
> arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>

> Why is it that COBOL shops have this ridiculous restriction.
> I don't know of ANY other language were there are more artificial
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

> Joe Katnic


Of course people tell C++ programmers that they can't use certain language
constructs, or that they must use certain classes or functions in preference
to others. There's also a whole slew of coding conventions that are used by
C++ programmers governing variable and method naming, indention, use of
brackets, etc. One such rule is "thou shalt use CString in MFC programs".
Other rules govern the use of strncpy() versus strcpy(), hungarian notation,
declaring method variables as private rather than public, then providing
methods to alter those variables. Code has get through 'lint' and
'boundschecker'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC8ECD.8F06A12A@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```


Joseph Katnic wrote:

> Why is it that COBOL shops have this ridiculous restriction.
> I don't know of ANY other language were there are more artificial
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> not be in the job.

Probably because CoBOL programs tend to stick around for decades with
lots of different programmers doing maintenance on them.  A standard
which says "Keep it as clear and simple as possible", tends to be more
valuable in this environment.

It is not the language which lead to the standards, it is the business
environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:19:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001005201948.14652.00000555@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
>Subject: Re: GO TO exit
>From: Joseph Katnic josephk@iinet.net.au 
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I dislike "NOT" conditionals because they inevitably lead to programs that say:

IF NOT <error condition1>
     IF NOT <error condition 2>
          PERFORM NORMAL-STUFF.

As the set of all possible errors is infinite, it is better to say:

IF <good stuff>
        PERFORM NORMAL-STUFF
ELSE
        PERFORM BAD-STUFF
END-IF.

And if <good stuff> means "keep going", that's why we got CONTINUE.
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
> arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > it is a shop standard and it is enforced by peer review.

NEXT SENTENCE is also invalid in conjunction with END-IF in the Cobol
standard, this should be enforced by your compiler.


> > IF EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "M"
> > OR EMPLOYEE-MARITAL-STATUS NOT EQUAL "S"
> >     PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.

 
> If a programmer gets confused on the above statement, then they should
> not be in the job.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     PERFORM PROCESS-INVALID-MARITAL-STATUS.

Interesting. You were confused by the statement to the point that you
both missed the point and reproduced the bug.  Should you be in your job
?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5X6D5.6978$Ly1.79009@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <39DD80AC.86396B6D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On  5-Oct-2000, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> NEXT SENTENCE is also invalid in conjunction with END-IF in the Cobol
> standard, this should be enforced by your compiler.

From the IBM VS COBOL II Language Reference:
------------------------
    NEXT SENTENCE
        If the END-IF phrase is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE phrase must
        not be specified.

        (IBM Extension)
        NEXT SENTENCE may be specified with END-IF.  Note, however, that
        if the NEXT SENTENCE phrase is executed, control will not pass to
        the next statement following the END-IF, but instead will pass to
        the statement after the closest following period.
-------------------------
Not even a warning.  Scary, huh?

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<8riv53$nbc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Run with the FLAGSTD(H) compiler option - and you WILL get a compiler
"warning".  However, you may not want to see some of the other things that
it warns you about.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-A7DD71.20152006102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39DC2B3F.BA9FD192@worldnet.att.net> <josephk-07A056.21413805102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <39DD80AC.86396B6D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <39DD80AC.86396B6D@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston 
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> > arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
> > If a programmer gets confused on the above statement, then they should
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> ?
Yes I understand the bug, I was merely stating that there is a better 
way of writing the code so as to only have one negative (NOT) in the 
condition. This tends to make it easier for people to see the 
consequences of the logic.

As to the bug, the way I have written the logic, can be used to 
immediately test the condition. STATUS can NEVER be S AND M 
simultaneously. So even if it were a S or a M, the condition would 
always be false and thus the PROCESS-INVALID... paragraph would always 
be executed. Thus this shows us that the connecting AND should be an OR.

Then the condition would work as planned.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

```

> I cannot believe that people would code such simply to avoid the NOT
> operator.  Is the above really clearer than the following?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>         PERFORM PROCESS-Y.

I agree completely.  NOTs can be useful, but some people abuse them, like
this:

IF NOT NOT-FOUND  or IF NOT NO-FILE-ID

I hate that...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T01:54:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HbRC5.1752$uB4.310144@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <8rggo4$tk3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<cbass2112@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8rggo4$tk3> > of one period
per paragraph.  We have talked about programmers who
> > code GO TOs to an exit paragraph, like this:
> >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> long chains of:

Keep if simple:

 ENTRY-PARA.
    DO THIS.
    DO THAT.
    IF SOME-CONDITION
         CONTINUE
    ELSE
          DO THE-OTHER.
          DO SOMETHING-ELSE
    END-IF.

 ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
    EXIT.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T08:32:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cu7ots8dvpacnsf38ridrl6nccnreg133b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com>`

```
On Wed, 4 Oct 2000 20:58:43 GMT, "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net> wrote:

>Here's my troll for today...
>

>Now, assuming you use one period per paragraph, couldn't you do this?
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No you can't, because NEXT SENTENCE is not allowed in the ANSI standard inside an
IF...END-IF.

Yes, you can, in IBM COBOL (at least), which allows this monster as an extension.  In my
opinion, this is a bug, not a feature.  Just imagine someone adding a line

DO SOMETHING-NEW.  (note the dot)   before the DO SOMETHHING-ELSE
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO exit

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wUVC5.6664$wx5.231703@news2.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<TPMC5.5054$Ly1.57248@news5.giganews.com> <cu7ots8dvpacnsf38ridrl6nccnreg133b@4ax.com>`

```

On  4-Oct-2000, Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:

> Yes, you can, in IBM COBOL (at least), which allows this monster as an
> extension.  In my
> opinion, this is a bug, not a feature.  Just imagine someone adding a line
> DO SOMETHING-NEW.  (note the dot)   before the DO SOMETHHING-ELSE
Again, let me emphasize that I'm pointing out an anomaly, not showing you
the Peterson Coding Standard :-)
IBM often leaves programmers on the wire without a net.  But.. considering
this code...

PERFORM ENTRY-PARA THRU ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
...........................
ENTRY-PARA.
    DO THIS.
    DO THAT.
    IF SOME-CONDITION
        GO TO ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
    DO THE-OTHER.
    DO SOMETHING-ELSE.

ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.
    EXIT.

...forgetting the period on GO TO ENTRY-PARA-EXIT is disastrous, as is
adding a line DO SOMETHING-NEW.  (note the dot) before the GO TO
ENTRY-PARA-EXIT.  Lots of places in to go wrong, even without ANSI standard
protecting us....  I use CONTINUE and END-IF religiously (sorry, DD).
```

<a id="t-e6bb5051c1"></a>
## Thread: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!! — 20 msgs

### Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

- **From:** "SuperStudd" <jamal_alam@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
I'm a college student here in Canada, and one of the classes that I have to
take is Cobol....

As bad as Cobol is, the demand for those who can program in this language is
HUGE here in Canada.......the highest paying jobs are Cobol related.  That
opened my eyes a bit :-)

I'm currently learning tables.......not as brutal as control/page breaks,
LOL!

Later,
Jamal
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DBAD77.3E445487@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


SuperStudd wrote:
> 
> I'm a college student here in Canada, and one of the classes that I have to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> LOL!

Business programs handle money.  It is a safe bet to be able to work
with business needs such as accounting, payroll, taxes, inventory,
finance, etc..   If a recession hits, glitzy applications may cut back,
but they still need to control their inventory and pay their taxes.

This means tables, reports, databases, and established business
procedures.  Very often those are designed with the language with
"Business" as its middle name.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T04:50:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DMTC5.6492$wx5.225334@news2.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On  4-Oct-2000, "SuperStudd" <jamal_alam@hotmail.com> wrote:

> As bad as Cobol is, the demand for those who can program in this language
> is HUGE here in Canada.......the highest paying jobs are Cobol related. 
> That
> opened my eyes a bit :-)

COBOL is real work.  For every programmer coding twirling icons under Java,
there's a COBOL programmer working on your paycheck or your car loan or your
tax return.

Pascal last week, C a few days ago, C++ yesterday, Java today, something new
tomorrow.  Why?  Because they build applications that don't matter as much.


Games that last a month, defrag programs that last until the next hard drive
model comes out, web pages that last ten minutes.

I built an accounting system in dBase II, upgraded to dBase III, ran it
under dbCompiler till they tanked, ported to Clipper.  They were bought by
Computer Associates and reduced to rubble, so I ported to FoxPro.  DOS was
dying, so I ported to FoxPro for Windows.  They were bought by Microsoft, so
I ported to Visual FoxPro to keep up.  I've upgraded to version 3, 5 and 6.
Now, with the advent of Visual Studio, I hear rumors that VFP's days may be
numbered.  Pisses me off.

The largest banking software package was written in COBOL in 1977 to run on
370s with ISAM.  It has a common file handler.  By changing the handler, the
system now runs on the fastest machines under VSAM, IMS and DB2, unheard of
when the system was written.  But, by changing 25 of 11,000 modules, it's
handling the new environments.  Many of the modules (interest calculation,
posting, etc.) haven't been changed in twenty years, because their functions
haven't changed.  Upgrades are made to handle new banking features or IRS
compliance, not to dazzle or impress.  It has green screens, but if you want
a nice windows front end, you can buy a screen scraper and build your own
with three kids with PCs and Java - just don't let them near the money.

Will that hold true of the latest 3D game?  Probably not.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-4DF5AD.20373706102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <DMTC5.6492$wx5.225334@news2.giganews.com> <8rin24$mh7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8rin24$mh7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, shine98@my-deja.com wrote:

> Games are written in C because it is fast and quick to write, IMHO. If
> COBOL were faster, the game writers would use that.
>
You can write games in COBOL, you must remember to make all number 
variables BINARY to speed the computations.

Generally, games are not written in COBOL, because COBOL requires much 
more code than C or C++. This often gets in the way of the game builders 
creative processes.

Much easier to write:
     a := c;
than
      MOVE A TO C.

Other than this minor problem, I don't think there is anything inherent 
in the COBOL language that prevents games being written in COBOL.

Oh yes!
COBOL generally requires a runtime environment. This is usually specific 
to the COBOL compiler and is NEVER provided by the operating system as 
is the case for C. C's runtime environment is generally linked into the 
ending module. So there is usually no need to ship a runtime environment 
with the application.

IBM have got around this restriction by providing a language runtime 
environment for all High Level Languages on the Mainframe. This comes 
with the OS and all languages use it. COBOL, FORTRAN, PL1, C, C++.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DDCAE3.B0E8FB8B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <DMTC5.6492$wx5.225334@news2.giganews.com> <8rin24$mh7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <josephk-4DF5AD.20373706102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> Generally, games are not written in COBOL, because COBOL requires much
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> in the COBOL language that prevents games being written in COBOL.

A typist can type them both as fast as we should be thinking through our
code.

Game writing typically wants quickly compiled modules - CoBOL has been
moving in that direction, but these were available in C when game
programming started.  Also, early games were very time dependent.  Even
if optimizing compilers were available for DOS PC's, sometimes they USED
delays.  Programmers wanted control over the machine code.  It made a
bigger difference then.  So the skills and libraries were built in C.
 
Switching languages later would require a significant advantage in
making the switch.  Such does not exist for CoBOL.  (nobody maintains
games - they get replaced)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DDFE20.AE0924D7@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <DMTC5.6492$wx5.225334@news2.giganews.com> <8rin24$mh7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <josephk-4DF5AD.20373706102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <39DDCAE3.B0E8FB8B@brazee.net>`


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rr6km0n3j@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <DMTC5.6492$wx5.225334@news2.giganews.com> <8rin24$mh7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <josephk-4DF5AD.20373706102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <39DDCAE3.B0E8FB8B@brazee.net>`

```
I have to disagree.

One example of a game being maintained is Street Fighter by Capcom. I think
it's debut was back in about 1989 or so.  It is still being produced.  I
doubt the programmers of this game started with a clean sheet everytime a
new version comes out.  All the Street Fighter games share a distinct feel,
and I suspect some of this is due to reused code.  Correct me if I'm wrong.

My definition of maintenance includes enhancements; yours may not.  There
are many other games, like Doom, that were around using the same basic game
engine.  I think Myst was written on a Mac using Hypercard.  I think Riven,
the sequel, was also.

Just my $.02

Jeff

>
> Switching languages later would require a significant advantage in
> making the switch.  Such does not exist for CoBOL.  (nobody maintains
> games - they get replaced)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e09aed.660316853@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <DMTC5.6492$wx5.225334@news2.giganews.com> <8rin24$mh7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I disagree entirely.

C was used as a replacement for ASM which at the time of initial game
programming, was the only way to get at the video memory.  BASIC had
some ability and some games were written in Basic.

COBOL did not (and does not) have direct hardware interface.  However,
now that many games are created via calls to DirectX and the like,
there's no reason one could not be created using COBOL.

However, COBOL programmers are business programmers and the games
programmers are Games programmers, and the two cultures have diverged.

As to maintenance, there IS maintenence with games.  One version is
based on the previous version -- to a great degree.  C programmers
agree that C code is hard to maintain, and that manitainable C code is
hard to create -- but possible.  

On Thu, 05 Oct 2000 20:07:39 GMT, shine98@my-deja.com wrote:

>Games are written in C because it is fast and quick to write, IMHO. If
>COBOL were faster, the game writers would use that.
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

## Thread: please help — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### please help

- **From:** "Matt Wieben" <mw5@dana.ucc.nau.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rel3l$r19$1@usenet.nau.edu>`

```
This program doesn't separate the coloumns and it repeats numbers, I've also
included the data file for this at the bottom.. I can't get any help at
all...my prof pretty much told me to f*ck off when I went to her for help on
why the output is STILL funky.
Thank you VERY VERY MUCH.


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

       PROGRAM-ID.    PROJECT1.
       AUTHOR.        WIEBEN.
       DATE-WRITTEN.  10-3-00.

      *****************************************************************
      *                                                               *
      *  ASSIGNMENT NUMBER                                            *
      *  DATE ASSIGNED                                                *
      *  DATE DUE                                                     *
      *  PURPOSE:  THIS PROGRAM READS INVENTORY RECORDS AND           *
      *            PRINTS THEM OUT WITH NO HEADINGS OR SPACING        *
      *                                                               *
      *****************************************************************

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT 100-INVENTORY-FILE
                  ASSIGN TO "inventry.dat"
                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.


           SELECT 200-REPORT-FILE
                  ASSIGN TO "inventry.rpt"
                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.

       FD  100-INVENTORY-FILE
               RECORD CONTAINS 50 CHARACTERS.
       01  100-INVENTORY-RECORD PIC X(50).
               01 IN-ITEM-NUMBER              PIC 9(5).
               01 IN-ITEM-DESC                PIC X(25).
               01 IN-ITEM-QUANTITY            PIC 9(4).
               01 IN-ITEM-COUNT               PIC ZZ9(2).
               01 IN-ITEM-COST                PIC ZZZZ99v99.
               01 IN-ITEM-REORDER             PIC ZZ9(2).
               01 IN-SUPPLIER-CODE            PIC 9(2).
               01 IN-BUYER-CODE               PIC 9(2).

       FD  200-REPORT-FILE
               RECORD CONTAINS 133 CHARACTERS.
       01  200-REPORT-LINE PIC X(133).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS    PIC X(3)       VALUE 'YES'.
           88 MORE-RECORDS                          VALUE 'YES'.
           88 NO-MORE-RECORDS                       VALUE 'NO'.

       01  400-TITLE-LINE.
           05 FILLER   PIC X(15).
           05 FILLER   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'MATTHEW WIEBEN'.

       01  500-RECORD-IN                     PIC X(50).

       01  600-OUT                           PIC X(50).

       01  HEADING-LINE.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(6)    VALUE'ITEM #'.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(10)   VALUE 'ITEM DESC.'.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(7)    VALUE 'ON HAND'.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(9)    VALUE 'COST/UNIT'.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(13)   VALUE 'REORDERLIMIT'.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(13)   VALUE 'SUPPLIERCODE'.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(10)   VALUE 'BUYER CODE'.

       01  INVENTORY-DATA.
           05 ITEM-NUMBER              PIC 9(5).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 ITEM-DESC                PIC X(25).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 ITEM-COUNT               PIC 9(2).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 ITEM-COST                PIC ZZZZ9(4).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 ITEM-REORDER             PIC ZZ9(2).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 SUPPLIER-CODE            PIC 9(2).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05 BUYER-CODE               PIC 9(2).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       A100-MAIN-PARA.
           PERFORM B100-INIT-PARA
           PERFORM C200-READ-PARA
           PERFORM B200-PROCESS-PARA
                   UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS
           PERFORM B300-CLOSE-PARA
           STOP RUN
           .

       B100-INIT-PARA.
           OPEN INPUT 100-INVENTORY-FILE
           OPEN OUTPUT 200-REPORT-FILE.
           WRITE 200-REPORT-LINE FROM 400-TITLE-LINE
              BEFORE 1

           MOVE HEADING-LINE TO 200-REPORT-LINE.
              WRITE 200-REPORT-LINE.
           MOVE SPACES TO 200-REPORT-LINE.
              WRITE 200-REPORT-LINE.
           *>MOVE 500-RECORD-IN TO 600-OUT.




       B200-PROCESS-PARA.
           PERFORM C100-WRITE-REPORT-PARA
           PERFORM C200-READ-PARA
           .

       C100-WRITE-REPORT-PARA.
           *>MOVE 500-RECORD-IN TO 600-OUT
           MOVE IN-ITEM-NUMBER     TO      ITEM-NUMBER.
           MOVE IN-ITEM-DESC       TO      ITEM-DESC.
           MOVE IN-ITEM-COUNT      TO      ITEM-COUNT.
           MOVE IN-ITEM-REORDER    TO      ITEM-REORDER.
           MOVE IN-SUPPLIER-CODE   TO      SUPPLIER-CODE.
           MOVE IN-BUYER-CODE      TO      BUYER-CODE.

           MOVE INVENTORY-DATA TO 200-REPORT-LINE.
               WRITE 200-REPORT-LINE.

           *>WRITE 200-REPORT-LINE FROM 600-OUT
             *>   AFTER 1

       C200-READ-PARA.
           READ 100-INVENTORY-FILE INTO 500-RECORD-IN
                AT END
                   MOVE 'NO' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
           END-READ
           .


       B300-CLOSE-PARA.
           CLOSE 100-INVENTORY-FILE
           CLOSE 200-REPORT-FILE
           .

02222ANTI-GLARE SCREEN        00100004000000050102
03001TURTLENECK SWEATER       00250000800000150101
08234SPARK PLUGS 1 DOZ        00400000120000500103
11653LEATHER JACKET           00070001500000050104

21100PRINT SILK SCARVES       00030000180000100301
21133T-SHIRT DRESSES          00080000210000050302

22165DESIGNER SWEATER         00080000500000050301
22298ST COTTON SHORTS         00200000190000150304
22324BLUE JEANS PLEATS        00300000300000200202

22700LG COTTON SHORTS         00090000140000100202

22988DESIGN SWEAT SHIRT       00220000450000200103

24345ACID WASHED JEAN JR      00130000180000100204
24350ACID WASHED J SKIRT      00080000220000150204
24355ACID WASHED MINI-SKIRT   00100000240000080203
```

```
Hint:  there's WAY too many 01 levels on your input file.
Another hint:  your input fields don't match your output fields.

Also, you should either use periods by themselves at the end of paragraphs,
or not.
```

#### ↳ Re: please help

- **From:** "Charles W. Cribbs II" <CHARLESCRIBBS@prodigy.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```


Matt Wieben <mw5@dana.ucc.nau.edu> wrote in message
news:8rel3l$r19$1@usenet.nau.edu...
> This program doesn't separate the coloumns and it repeats numbers, I've
also
> included the data file for this at the bottom.. I can't get any help at
> all...my prof pretty much told me to f*ck off when I went to her for help
on
> why the output is STILL funky.
> Thank you VERY VERY MUCH.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>

your file description is wrong.
when you define an 01 level all levels below it are subornate to it so must
not be an 01 level.  you can use any other number up to 50 i believe but it
is usually done line this:

    FD  100-INVENTORY-FILE
>         RECORD CONTAINS 50 CHARACTERS.
>        01  100-INVENTORY-RECORD.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                05 IN-BUYER-CODE               PIC 9(2).

you can not have numeric edited fields in a record layout

>        FD  100-INVENTORY-FILE
>                RECORD CONTAINS 50 CHARACTERS.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

if you use an 88 level of yes you don't want to give the initial value as
"YES"
>        01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS    PIC X(3)       VALUE 'YES'.
>            88 MORE-RECORDS                          VALUE 'YES'.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.

do you really want a number that is 8 numeric characters long with the first
4 zero supressed?
>            05 ITEM-COST                PIC ZZZZ9(4).
>            05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.

this applies here too.
you usually don't need to zero suppress until you have moved the field to
your report field.  your report field would have a picture using zero
suppression.
>            05 ITEM-REORDER             PIC ZZ9(2).
>            05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> you have got to more consistent in the use of periods.  if you are going
to use them do them at the end of statements and paragraphs just like
english.  don't use them within an if statment but after the end-if.  the
scope terminator functions as a period but most programs will use both
because it helps to block logic off in sections for the eye to see.
>        A100-MAIN-PARA.
>            PERFORM B100-INIT-PARA
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>               BEFORE 1
what are you doing here??  all you need to do is say write from report line
and it will write one line and advance to the next.

>            MOVE HEADING-LINE TO 200-REPORT-LINE.
>               WRITE 200-REPORT-LINE.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 AT END

you have an 88 level of no more records don't make a move of it set the 88
level to true

that would be said:    set NO-MORE-RECORDS to true
by doing this you have set the condition once the at end has been reached.
>                    MOVE 'NO' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
>            END-READ
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: please help

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001007153046.04122.00000022@nso-cb.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<39DF2D0C.656D5705@brazee.net>`

```
In article <39DF2D0C.656D5705@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
writes:

>"Charles W. Cribbs II" wrote:
>> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I know for a fact the you can have numeric edited as input or sending 
fields under OS\390/COBOL for OS\390.  We had a couple input sources 
that were basically trial balance reports on electronic media that had to be
reformatted into usable fields.  I did however find that the numeric edited 
fields have to move thru a display numeric field before being moved to 
packed or binary fields.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: please help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rnv4e$p8a$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39DF2D0C.656D5705@brazee.net> <20001007153046.04122.00000022@nso-cb.aol.com>`

```
There is no restriction in any current OR PAST Standard that says that you
can't have edited (numeric-edited or alphanumeric-edited) fields in any
"record layout" (under FD or otherwise).  Now, the problem in using this in
an "input" record layout - is that the data may NOT come in that way.  For
example, if you have:

  05 NumEdit  Pic  ZZ9.

in your input field - and your data comes in as "001" instead of "  1"
(leading blanks), then you TECHNICALLY have "incompatible data" - and
results are "undefined".  However, it is EQUALLY true that if your data does
come in with leading blanks, then it would cause incompatible data if you
defined it as:

  05 NumEdit Pic 999.

Therefore, the answer is to define your input fields the way the data
actually exists in the files.
```

---

## Thread: COBOL, DB2 Position — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COBOL, DB2 Position

- **From:** chris@infosys.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rg35k$hqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


If you or anyone you know would be interested in the following
consulting opportunity with our Fortune 500 client, please contact me
via phone or email at your earliest convenience.

Job Title: COBOL, DB2 Programmer
Starting: ASAP
Type: Contract
Length of Job: 6 months
Location: Northbrook, IL

Required Skills:  COBOL, DB2, DB2 Utilities, MVS/TSO/ISPF, Batch
Processing, JCL, Endevor, Programming, Testing

Description:  DB2 Programmer with experience using DB2 utilities,
specifically the Load and Unload functions on large and sophisticated
databases.  This project is fast paced and candidate must be able to
work within aggressive target dates, and produce high quality
deliverables.  Candidate will also be required to follow established
standards, procedures, and processes.

Thank you for your interest and consideration.

If you do not feel that this job is a good match for you, feel free to
visit our website to see all of our open jobs.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates,
associated with the position(s) offered; doing otherwise leads many to
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to
determine rates.
```

<a id="t-a8c0067d1c"></a>
## Thread: Linux and MF COBOL. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Linux and MF COBOL.

- **From:** Hemant Shah <shah@typhoon.xnet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rfn41$1bq$1@flood.xnet.com>`

```

Folks,

  Recently we purchased MF COBOL for Linux to port our product to linux. We
  already use MF COBOL on AIX, HP-UX and Unixware.

  Here is my problem.:

  I could compile and run the COBOL code, but if I create a executable
  that is combination of C and COBOL I get following error:

  error in loading shared libraries: liboops.so: cannot open shared object
  file: No such file or directory.


  Our application is complex, it is a combination of COBOL and C programs,
  it is also exec'd from a SUID C program. At first I thought that the
  LD_LIBRARY_PATH was not being exported to the SUID program, so I set the
  environment variable in the SUID program, but I got same result. To get
  more information it exec'd strace from a SUID program. 
  
  Following is the C code for the SUID program:

---------cut-------------cut-------------cut-------------cut----
#include "src/sub_gen/sysswtch.h"
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>


int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
   char *path;
/*
 *  setenv("LD_LIBRARY_PATH", "/lib:/usr/lib:/opt/MfCobol_V4.1_8d.H3.13.03/coblib", 1);
 */
 
   putenv("LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/lib:/usr/lib:/opt/MfCobol_V4.1_8d.H3.13.03/coblib");
   path = getenv("LD_LIBRARY_PATH");
   printf ("LD_LIBRARY_PATH = %s\n", path);

   /* argv[0] = "region";
    * argv[1] = NULL;
    * execv("./cob/run/bin/region", argv);
    */
   argv[0] = "strace";
   argv[1] = "./cob/run/bin/region";
   argv[2] = NULL;
   execv("/usr/bin/strace", argv);
   printf("region not started, buildreg probably not done\n");
   exit(1);
}
---------cut-------------cut-------------cut-------------cut----

The above program is called startreg and is an SUID root program.

In above "./cob/run/bin/region" is my application which is combination 
of C and COBOL code.

When I run the application I get following error:
---------cut-------------cut-------------cut-------------cut----
[apdev39@lidp20]/c1/apdev39>
-> startreg
LD_LIBRARY_PATH = /lib:/usr/lib:/opt/MfCobol_V4.1_8d.H3.13.03/coblib
execve("./cob/run/bin/region", ["./cob/run/bin/region"], [/* 52 vars */]) =
0
brk(0)                                  = 0x83104ec
open("/etc/ld.so.preload", O_RDONLY)    = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/etc/ld.so.cache", O_RDONLY)      = 3
fstat(3, {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=17997, ...}) = 0
mmap(0, 17997, PROT_READ, MAP_PRIVATE, 3, 0) = 0x40013000
close(3)                                = 0
open("/lib/i686/mmx/liboops.so", O_RDONLY) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/lib/i686/mmx", 0xbffff29c)       = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/lib/i686/liboops.so", O_RDONLY)  = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/lib/i686", 0xbffff29c)           = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/lib/mmx/liboops.so", O_RDONLY)   = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/lib/mmx", 0xbffff29c)            = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/lib/liboops.so", O_RDONLY)       = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/lib", {st_mode=S_IFDIR|0755, st_size=3072, ...}) = 0
open("/usr/lib/i686/mmx/liboops.so", O_RDONLY) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/usr/lib/i686/mmx", 0xbffff29c)   = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/usr/lib/i686/liboops.so", O_RDONLY) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/usr/lib/i686", 0xbffff29c)       = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/usr/lib/mmx/liboops.so", O_RDONLY) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/usr/lib/mmx", 0xbffff29c)        = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
open("/usr/lib/liboops.so", O_RDONLY)   = -1 ENOENT (No such file or
directory)
stat("/usr/lib", {st_mode=S_IFDIR|0755, st_size=12288, ...}) = 0
write(2, "./cob/run/bin/region", 20./cob/run/bin/region)    = 20
write(2, ": error in loading shared librar"..., 37: error in loading shared
libraries: ) = 37
write(2, "liboops.so", 10liboops.so)              = 10
write(2, ": ", 2: )                       = 2
write(2, "cannot open shared object file", 30cannot open shared object file)
= 30
write(2, ": ", 2: )                       = 2
write(2, "No such file or directory", 25No such file or directory) = 25
write(2, "\n", 1
)                       = 1
_exit(127)                              = ?
[apdev39@lidp20]/c1/apdev39>
-> 

---------cut-------------cut-------------cut-------------cut----
As you can see, it never searches /opt/MfCobol_V4.1_8d.H3.13.03/coblib 
directory, liboops.so is in that directory.

[apdev39@lidp20]/opt/MfCobol_V4.1_8d.H3.13.03/coblib>
-> ls -l liboops*
-r--r--r--   4 root     bin         94172 Sep 28 10:08 liboops.so
-r--r--r--   4 root     bin         94172 Sep 28 10:08 liboops.so.2.0


Following is the version of MF COBOL compiler.
[apdev39@lidp20]/c1/apdev39>
-> cob -V
version @(#)cob.c       1.254
PRN=OXCTX/AAA:8d.H3.13.03
PTI=NLS
PTI=LMF
PTI=ASLMF
PTI=Y2K
I see no work


Following is the version of gcc compiler.
[apdev39@lidp20]/c1/apdev39>
-> gcc --version
egcs-2.91.66
```

#### ↳ Re: Linux and MF COBOL.

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC3D12.6A36DD05@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<8rfn41$1bq$1@flood.xnet.com>`

```
Hi Hemant,

Do you have libstd++ installed?  If not please install in.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and MF COBOL.

- **From:** Hemant Shah <shah@typhoon.xnet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ri4qp$b07$1@flood.xnet.com>`
- **References:** `<8rfn41$1bq$1@flood.xnet.com> <39DC3D12.6A36DD05@merant.com>`

```
While stranded on information super highway Stephen Gennard wrote:
:)Hi Hemant,
:)
:)Do you have libstd++ installed?  If not please install in.
:)


  I figured out the problem, it has nothing to do with libstd++. The dynamic
  link loader (ld.so), for security reasons, will ignore LD_LIBRARY_PATH if
  if the program is USID root. 
  
  To solve the problem I had to edit /etc/ld.co.conf file and add the path for
  ${COBDIR}/coblib, then run ldconfig command to build the cache
  (ld.so.cache).

  The only problem I can see right now is that I cannot run multiple version
  of COBOL compiler at same time (i.e. test newer version along with old
  version).


:)--
:)Stephen Gennard (mailto:stephen.gennard@merant.com),  Merant International Ltd,
:)ACT Development, The Lawn, Newbury, UK.
:)URL: http://www.merant.com
:)
:)
:)
:)
:)
```

<a id="t-2f8cbdc04b"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Win32 API Question — 1 msgs

### NetExpress Win32 API Question

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001004115542.04202.00000150@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I have a computer that is acting as a server.  When I use the "ExitWindowsEx"
API to shutdown the computer the system comes up and tells me that there are
users connected.  I have tried the various options that are available with the
"ExitWindowsEx" API, but they all seem to come up with this message.  Is anyone
aware of an option that I can use on this API to bypass this message or does
anyone know of an API that will allow me to logoff all users ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol and grids from other vendors. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Cobol and grids from other vendors.

- **From:** Millenium Business Solutions <mbs@labyrinth.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T11:43:03+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DA8B27.663DCAA0@labyrinth.net.au>`

```

Does anyone who is using Fujitsu PowerCOBOL with a grid from a
supplier such as Janus, Green tree, Developer Express, Sheridan, etc
know how to use these in PowerCobol??

If you have a powerful grid that you are using, and it is not one of the

above, we would like to know also.

NB: This application is not using a DB, but ISAM files.

Thanks
```

## Thread: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos — 1 msgs

### Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-04T03:35:31+00:00

<a id="t-9df8b01fa5"></a>
## Thread: Show me your code... — 27 msgs

### Show me your code...

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T01:32:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com>`

```
Anyone interested in showing their coding style?

Here's a complete procedure division circa 1993 - a vendor package.  How
would you change it, or is it perfect-o just like it is?

Yes, students, you'll see a ton of this at your first job...

Cliff
--------------------------------------------
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-OF-PGM.
           OPEN INPUT I04.
           OPEN INPUT P07.
           OPEN OUTPUT I00.
       INITIALIZE-OUTPUT-REC.
           MOVE SPACES TO I00-P.
           MOVE 'DCI' TO SYS-ID-I00-P.
           MOVE 'I00' TO FILEID-I00-P.
           MOVE 'P' TO REC-ID-I00-P.
           PERFORM READ-I04 THRU READ-I04-EXIT.
           PERFORM READ-P07 THRU READ-P07-EXIT.
       COMPARE-KEYS.
           IF KEY-I04 IS EQUAL TO 99999
             AND KEY-P07 IS EQUAL TO 99999
               PERFORM WRITE-I00 THRU WRITE-I00-EXIT
               GO TO END-OF-PROGRAM.
           IF KEY-I04 IS EQUAL TO KEY-P07
               IF PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P IS GREATER THAN SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
                   MOVE PROJ-ID-I04-P TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
                   PERFORM READ-I04 THRU READ-I04-EXIT
                   PERFORM READ-P07 THRU READ-P07-EXIT
                   GO TO COMPARE-KEYS
               ELSE
                   PERFORM WRITE-I00 THRU WRITE-I00-EXIT
                   MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
                   MOVE PROJ-ID-I04-P TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
                   PERFORM READ-I04 THRU READ-I04-EXIT
                   PERFORM READ-P07 THRU READ-P07-EXIT
                   GO TO COMPARE-KEYS.
           IF KEY-I04 IS GREATER THAN KEY-P07
               IF PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P IS GREATER THAN SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
                   MOVE PROJ-ID-P07-P TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
                   PERFORM READ-P07 THRU READ-P07-EXIT
                   GO TO COMPARE-KEYS
               ELSE
                   PERFORM WRITE-I00 THRU WRITE-I00-EXIT
                   MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
                   MOVE PROJ-ID-P07-P TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
                   PERFORM READ-P07 THRU READ-P07-EXIT
                   GO TO COMPARE-KEYS.
           MOVE 1 TO DUMP-CODE.
           GO TO DUMP-ROUTINE.
       READ-I04.
           READ I04
           AT END
               MOVE 99999 TO KEY-I04
               GO TO READ-I04-EXIT.
           IF SYS-ID-I04-P EQUAL TO 'EOF'
               GO TO READ-I04.
           MOVE REC-ID-I04-P TO LTR-HOLD.
           PERFORM SEARCH-FOR-LTR THRU SEARCH-FOR-LTR-EXIT.
           MOVE LTR (LTR-NDX) TO REC-ID-I04-P.
           IF REC-ID-I04-P IS NOT EQUAL TO 'P'
               GO TO READ-I04.
           IF DELETED-I04-P EQUAL TO 'Y'
               GO TO READ-I04.
           MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO KEY-I04.
       READ-I04-EXIT.
           EXIT.
       READ-P07.
           READ P07
           AT END
               MOVE 99999 TO KEY-P07
               GO TO READ-P07-EXIT.
           IF REC-ID-P07-P IS NOT EQUAL TO 'P'
               GO TO READ-P07.
           IF DELETED-P07-P EQUAL TO 'Y'
               GO TO READ-P07.
           IF EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P IS EQUAL TO 1
               GO TO READ-P07.
           MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO KEY-P07.
       READ-P07-EXIT.
           EXIT.
       WRITE-I00.
           MOVE SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO TO EDP-SEQ-NO-I00-P.
           MOVE SAVE-PROJ-ID TO PROJ-ID-I00-P.
           WRITE I00-REC FROM I00-P
               .
           IF  I00-STATUS NOT EQUAL TO '00'
               MOVE I00-STATUS TO DUMP-STATUS
               MOVE 'STATUS RETURN FROM WRITE' TO DUMP-TEXT2
               MOVE 2 TO DUMP-CODE
               GO TO DUMP-ROUTINE.
       WRITE-I00-EXIT.
           EXIT.
       SEARCH-FOR-LTR.
           MOVE 1 TO LTR-NDX.
       SEARCH-LTR-LOOP.
           IF  LTR-HOLD NOT EQUAL TO LTR (LTR-NDX)
               IF  LTR-NDX NOT EQUAL TO 26
                   ADD 1 TO LTR-NDX
                   GO TO SEARCH-LTR-LOOP
               ELSE
                   MOVE 1 TO LTR-NDX.
           COMPUTE LTR-NDX = 27 - LTR-NDX.
       SEARCH-FOR-LTR-EXIT.
           EXIT.
       DUMP-ROUTINE.
      * REMOVED THIS CODE FOR BREVITY AND REPLACED WITH...
           CONTINUE.
       END-OF-PROGRAM.
           CLOSE I00
                 I04
                 P07
                 .
           STOP RUN.
-----------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Show me your code...

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com>`

```
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-OF-PGM.
           OPEN OUTPUT I00
           MOVE SPACES TO I00-P
           MOVE 'DCI' TO SYS-ID-I00-P
           MOVE 'I00' TO FILEID-I00-P
           MOVE  'P'  TO REC-ID-I00-P

           OPEN INPUT I04, P07
           PERFORM READ-I04
           PERFORM READ-P07
           PERFORM COMPARE-AND-READ
              UNTIL KEY-I04 = 99999
                AND KEY-P07 = 99999

           PERFORM WRITE-I00
           PERFORM END-OF-PROGRAM
           .

       COMPARE-AND-READ.
           IF KEY-I04 = KEY-P07
               IF PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P NOT > SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   PERFORM WRITE-I00
               END-IF
               MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
               MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
               MOVE PROJ-ID-I04-P    TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
               PERFORM READ-I04
           ELSE
           IF KEY-I04 > KEY-P07
               IF PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P NOT > SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   PERFORM WRITE-I00
               END-IF
               MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
               MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
               MOVE PROJ-ID-P07-P    TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
           ELSE
           IF KEY-I04 < KEY-P07
               MOVE 1 TO DUMP-CODE
               PERFORM FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP
           .
           PERFORM READ-P07
           .

       READ-I04.
           MOVE ZEROES TO KEY-I04
           READ I04
           PERFORM READ-MORE-I04
             UNTIL KEY-I04 > ZEROES
           .

       READ-MORE-I04.
           IF I04-STATUS > ZEROES
               MOVE 99999 TO KEY-I04
           ELSE
           IF SYS-ID-I04-P = 'EOF'
               READ I04
           ELSE
               MOVE REC-ID-I04-P TO LTR-HOLD
               PERFORM SEARCH-FOR-LTR
               MOVE LTR (LTR-NDX) TO REC-ID-I04-P
               IF REC-ID-I04-P NOT = 'P'
               OR DELETED-I04-P    = 'Y'
                   READ I04
               ELSE
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO KEY-I04
           .

       READ-P07.
           MOVE ZEROES TO KEY-P07
           READ P07
           PERFORM READ-MORE-P07
             UNTIL KEY-P07 > ZEROES
           .

       SEARCH-FOR-LTR.
           PERFORM SEARCH-LTR-TABLE
             VARYING LTR-NDX FROM 26 BY -1
               UNTIL LTR-HOLD = LTR (LTR-NDX)
                  OR LTR-NDX  = 1

           COMPUTE LTR-NDX = 27 - LTR-NDX
           .

       SEARCH-LTR-TABLE.
           EXIT
           .

       READ-MORE-P07.
           IF P07-STATUS > ZEROES
               MOVE 99999 TO KEY-P07
           ELSE
           IF REC-ID-P07-P NOT = 'P'
           OR DELETED-P07-P    = 'Y'
           OR EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P =  1
               READ P07
           ELSE
               MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO KEY-P07
           .

       WRITE-I00.
           MOVE SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO TO EDP-SEQ-NO-I00-P
           MOVE SAVE-PROJ-ID    TO PROJ-ID-I00-P
           WRITE I00-REC FROM I00-P

           IF  I00-STATUS > ZEROES
               MOVE  I00-STATUS                TO DUMP-STATUS
               MOVE 'STATUS RETURN FROM WRITE' TO DUMP-TEXT2
               MOVE  2                         TO DUMP-CODE
               PERFORM FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP
           .

       FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP.
      * REMOVED SOME CODE FOR BREVITY
           PERFORM END-OF-PROGRAM
           .

       END-OF-PROGRAM.
           CLOSE I00, I04, P07
           STOP RUN
           .


Cliff Peterson <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com...
> Anyone interested in showing their coding style?
>
…[120 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----------------------------
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
   I've only been programming in COBOL for 2 years, but I have one pet peeve
with those trying to eliminate all GO TOs:
   Do "good" programmers think it is proper to PERFORM a paragraph which
always executes a STOP RUN or GO BACK? IMHO, a GO TO is much more clear,
especially if the paragraph name does not make it obvious that there is no
return. If I see PERFORM ERROR-RTN, I assume (without looking at ERROR-RTN)
that an error will be handled, ant the line following the PERFORM will be
executed next. If ERROR-RTN always stops the processing, shouldn't it be
accessed with a GO TO, so the reader knows it will never return?
   At least the FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP and END-OF-PROGRAM names make it clear
there is no return. However, PERFORM still _implies_ a return.

Leif Svalgaard <leif@leif.org> wrote in message
news:GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
>        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>        START-OF-PGM.
…[119 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0SsD5.26501$WK6.609416@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```

William Bub <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com...
>    I've only been programming in COBOL for 2 years, but I have one pet
peeve
> with those trying to eliminate all GO TOs:

it is not that we are 'trying' to avoid a GO TO. The GO TO simply never
is needed in the first place. A 'disaster bail-out' is part a structured
programming anyway. Now, I tried to stay close to the spirit of the
original program, but if I had to design (not re-design) the program
my way, I would have worked the logic differently. As it is, the program
is too simple-minded about errors.

>    Do "good" programmers think it is proper to PERFORM a paragraph which
> always executes a STOP RUN or GO BACK? IMHO, a GO TO is much more clear,
> especially if the paragraph name does not make it obvious that there is no
> return.

but the paragraph name DOES make it clear that there is no return:

> At least the FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP and END-OF-PROGRAM names make it clear
> there is no return.

> However, PERFORM still _implies_ a return.

that is debatable.  the phrases PERFORM QUIT or PERFORM STOP do not
suggest a return. Conversely GO TO may not always means that you are
assured of no return:

A.
   GO TO B.
C.
  ....
B.
  ....
  GO TO C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
