- **Message-ID:** `<39DF2DDA.747DB025@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```


William Bub wrote:
> 
>    I've only been programming in COBOL for 2 years, but I have one pet peeve
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> there is no return. However, PERFORM still _implies_ a return.

I agree with you.  However, there are some compilers which create more
efficient code if they do not find any GO TOs in the code, and some
shops which have no GO TO standards.

I code GO TO only to get to a paragraph which exits the program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JkHD5.43983$3y3.828831@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <39DF2DDA.747DB025@brazee.net>`

```
> >    I've only been programming in COBOL for 2 years, but I have one pet
peeve
> > with those trying to eliminate all GO TOs:
> >    Do "good" programmers think it is proper to PERFORM a paragraph which
> > always executes a STOP RUN or GO BACK? IMHO, a GO TO is much more clear,
> > especially if the paragraph name does not make it obvious that there is
no
> > return. If I see PERFORM ERROR-RTN, I assume (without looking at
ERROR-RTN)
> > that an error will be handled, ant the line following the PERFORM will
be
> > executed next. If ERROR-RTN always stops the processing, shouldn't it be
> > accessed with a GO TO, so the reader knows it will never return?
> >    At least the FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP and END-OF-PROGRAM names make it
clear
> > there is no return. However, PERFORM still _implies_ a return.
>
> I agree with you.  However, there are some compilers which create more
> efficient code if they do not find any GO TOs in the code, and some
> shops which have no GO TO standards.


I did not want to start a new GO TO war. I have no problems with
GO TO FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP, it is just that I didn't use the
GO TO out of pure habit. Howard's point is also true, my main
compiler (Realia) is one of them. In tight loops (such as the one
in the example: SEARCH-FOR-LTR) this can really make a
difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e64428.7836029@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
On Fri, 06 Oct 2000 22:45:43 GMT, William Bub wrote:

>Leif Svalgaard <leif@leif.org> wrote in message
>news:GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
[snip most of program]
>>        FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP.
>>       * REMOVED SOME CODE FOR BREVITY
>>            PERFORM END-OF-PROGRAM
>>            .

>>        END-OF-PROGRAM.
>>            CLOSE I00, I04, P07
>>            STOP RUN
>>            .

>[...]If ERROR-RTN always stops the processing, shouldn't it be
>accessed with a GO TO, so the reader knows it will never return?

This has merit, but the reader could also know the program will not
return by comments in the error procedure, or in the procedure name
(such as the FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP, in this case).

>   At least the FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP and END-OF-PROGRAM names make it clear
>there is no return. However, PERFORM still _implies_ a return.

Another thing to consider is the disposition of the aborted program.
If, for example, in the area indicated by * REMOVED SOME CODE FOR
BREVITY, a program dump is produced, the usability of the dump could
be affected.   In this instance, if the FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP procedure
is PERFORMed, rather than accessed by GO TO, the stack information in
the dump may provide a clue as to where in the program the error
procedure was PERFORMed.  (There would be no such clue if the routine
was accessed via GO TO.)  This becomes particularly important if the
FATAL-DUMP-AND-STOP procedure could be PERFORMed from any of several
places.


Just my $0.02 (speaking as someone who occasionally must use such
techniques in program failure analysis).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Jeff York <jeff@jakfield.xu-netx.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00

- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <39e64428.7836029@news1.frb.gov>`

```
WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS) wrote:

>On Fri, 06 Oct 2000 22:45:43 GMT, William Bub wrote:
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>places.

I must admit that I don't like the idea of PERFORMING a procedure that
has no return..  Old habits die hard I suppose and as an assembler
programmer I was taught that "the stack is like a bathroom..  Please
leave it in the same condition as you entered it.."..   :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QVYG5.7210$Fe4.142306@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <rCsD5.37693$355.5232822@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <39e64428.7836029@news1.frb.gov> <j04ousocivf145mb1rhos9ponrtu57l7ia@4ax.com>`

```
If you PERFORM something, then inside the PERFORM make
a GO TO, what does that correspond to in your bathroom
analogy? Even a STOP RUN is at some lower level a call
with no return. GO TO DUMP is just as 'damaging' to the
stack (if there is one - not all compilers generate stack
oriented code). But as I've said before, if you MUST have
a GO TO instead of PERFORM DUMP, then do so.
That was not the main point of the original exercise
(which seems to have been forgotten as the thread
degenerated into the 10 millionth GO TO debate).


Jeff York <jeff@jakfield.xu-netx.com> wrote in message
news:j04ousocivf145mb1rhos9ponrtu57l7ia@4ax.com...

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

clear
> >>there is no return. However, PERFORM still _implies_ a return.
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> jeff@jakfield.xu-netx.com  (remove the x..x round u-net for return
address)
>
> ... "There are few hours in life more agreeable
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

well, the fact is (IMNSHO), if your code is about to transfer control to a
routine that
STOPS all processing prematurely, then it is YOUR responsibility to setup
whatever
indicators / values to describe the error condition BEFORE transferring
control.

To do otherwise is futile, wasteful and unprofessional.

```
My current abort method prints the name and version of the module
that it is in, the date compiled, and the file name of the source code
module.  All those are set by each module before they can access
system parameters.  To do otherwise forces and immediate invocation
of the module containing the code, and that is then debugged first.

There is only one "stop" in the entire code, and that is also in the abort
method.  Whether or not it is a normal or abnormal exit is determined
by presetting a flag in the system object to allow a normal exit.

Excellent.  I noticed your handling of the SEARCH-FOR-LTR - I came up with
the same answer... You want to end up with 1 if nothing is found?  Start at
26 and work backward.

By the way, the LTR is 26 bytes long (obviously) containing ABCDEF... you
get the idea.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Show me your code...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LsKD5.14092$Ly1.198061@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com> <GclD5.26421$WK6.599529@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <qWwD5.11216$Ly1.156202@news5.giganews.com> <52ED5.43964$3y3.820966@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```

On  7-Oct-2000, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org> wrote:

> what I do not get is why you do the final compute:
> LTR-NDX = 27 - LTR-NDX

----------------------------------
From the original code:

 MOVE REC-ID-I04-P TO LTR-HOLD.
 PERFORM SEARCH-FOR-LTR THRU SEARCH-FOR-LTR-EXIT.
 MOVE LTR (LTR-NDX) TO REC-ID-I04-P.
 IF REC-ID-I04-P IS NOT EQUAL TO 'P'
     GO TO READ-I04.
---------------------------------

They search LTR-HOLD's position in LTR, then turn it around.  If it's 1 it
becomes 26, if it's 26 it becomes 1.
Since LTR contains 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ', A becomes Z, B becomes Y,
etc.  Their desired outcome is "P", the 16th letter, so LTR has to
originally contain K, the 11th letter.  27-11=16, or "P".

All I can figure is that the vendor developed a "trick" to decode
REC-ID-104-P, or they came up with the "P".  It was named the "P" record in
their documentation, perhaps, but contains a "K" in the record ID.

Maybe it's code.  Maybe it's a trademark doo-da.  Pretty strange.  It could
have been eliminated and check for "K".

Cliff
```

>Btw what is the definition on 'New Programmer' and in what way does it
>differ from 'Old Programmer' especially in coding techniques?

I cannot speak for anyone but myself, Mr Meister, but I'd imagine that
someone trained under COBOL74 would code a bit differently than someone
trained under COBOL85... for example, it seems to me that, as of late
('late' being defined as after 1983) there is more encouragement of the
'one period/full stop per paragraph' school.

DD

#### ↳ Re: Show me your code...

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliff.peterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T03:30:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sNwD5.11339$bI6.544780@news1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<%W9D5.7211$Ly1.83712@news5.giganews.com>`

```
      ***********************************************
      * I'M ASSUMING THESE "FILE STATUS" FIELDS, BUT
      * DIDN'T ADD FILE STATUS CHECKING TO THE CODE.
      ***********************************************
       01  FILE-STATUS-FIELDS.
           05  I00-STATUS   PIC 99.
               88  I00-STATUS-OK        VALUE 00.
           05  I04-STATUS   PIC 99.
               88  I04-STATUS-OK        VALUE 00 10.
               88  I04-STATUS-EOF       VALUE 10.
           05  P07-STATUS   PIC 99.
               88  P07-STATUS-OK        VALUE 00 10.
               88  P07-STATUS-EOF       VALUE 10.
*************************************************************
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           OPEN INPUT  I04.
           OPEN INPUT  P07.
           OPEN OUTPUT I00.

           PERFORM READ-I04.
           PERFORM READ-P07.
           PERFORM UNTIL KEY-I04 = 99999 AND KEY-P07 = 99999
               EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN KEY-I04 = KEY-P07
                   IF PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P NOT > SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                       PERFORM WRITE-I00
                   END-IF
                   MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-I04-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
                   MOVE PROJ-ID-I04-P    TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
                   PERFORM READ-I04
               WHEN KEY-I04 > KEY-P07
                   IF PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P NOT > SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                       PERFORM WRITE-I00
                   END-IF
                   MOVE PROJ-LEVEL-P07-P TO SAVE-PROJ-LEVEL
                   MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO
                   MOVE PROJ-ID-P07-P    TO SAVE-PROJ-ID
               WHEN OTHER
                   MOVE 1 TO DUMP-CODE
                   PERFORM DUMP-AND-END
               END-EVALUATE
               PERFORM READ-P07
           END-PERFORM.
           PERFORM WRITE-I00.
           PERFORM END-OF-PROGRAM.

       READ-I04.
           MOVE ZEROES TO KEY-I04.
           PERFORM UNTIL KEY-I04 > ZERO
               READ I04
               EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN I04-STATUS-EOF
                   MOVE 99999 TO KEY-I04
               WHEN SYS-ID-I04-P NOT = 'EOF'
                   PERFORM VARYING LTR-NDX FROM 26 BY -1
                       UNTIL REC-ID-I04-P = LTR (LTR-NDX)
                          OR LTR-NDX  = 1
                   END-PERFORM
                   COMPUTE LTR-NDX = 27 - LTR-NDX
                   MOVE LTR (LTR-NDX) TO REC-ID-I04-P
                   IF REC-ID-I04-P      = 'P' AND
                      DELETED-I04-P NOT = 'Y'
                       MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-I04-P TO KEY-I04
                   END-IF
               END-EVALUATE
           END-PERFORM.

       READ-P07.
           MOVE ZEROES TO KEY-P07.
           PERFORM UNTIL KEY-P07 > ZERO
               READ P07
               EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN P07-STATUS-EOF
                   MOVE 99999 TO KEY-P07
               WHEN REC-ID-P07-P = 'P'
                   IF DELETED-P07-P    NOT = 'Y' AND
                      EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P NOT = 1
                       MOVE EDP-SEQ-NO-P07-P TO KEY-P07
                   END-IF
               END-EVALUATE
           END-PERFORM.

       WRITE-I00.
           INITIALIZE I00-P.
           MOVE 'DCI'           TO SYS-ID-I00-P.
           MOVE 'I00'           TO FILEID-I00-P.
           MOVE 'P'             TO REC-ID-I00-P.
           MOVE SAVE-EDP-SEQ-NO TO EDP-SEQ-NO-I00-P.
           MOVE SAVE-PROJ-ID    TO PROJ-ID-I00-P.
           WRITE I00-REC FROM I00-P.
           IF NOT I00-STATUS-OK
               MOVE I00-STATUS TO DUMP-STATUS
               MOVE 'STATUS RETURN FROM WRITE' TO DUMP-TEXT2
               MOVE 2 TO DUMP-CODE
               PERFORM DUMP-AND-END
           END-IF.

       DUMP-AND-END.
      * REMOVED THIS CODE FOR BREVITY
           PERFORM END-OF-PROGRAM.

       END-OF-PROGRAM.
           CLOSE I00
                 I04
                 P07.
           STOP RUN.
```

---

## Thread: RMCOBOL 98 Error? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RMCOBOL 98 Error?

- **From:** dbmartin@wt.net (David)
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39dcd892.84693882@news-west.newscene.com>`

```
Does anyone have a list of the meaning of the RMCBOL 98,xx errors?
Eg. what does the XX mean?  I can not find the info. in the standard
RMCOBOL manual.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: RMCOBOL 98 Error?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T04:23:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DEA52B.A66BCEB4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<39dcd892.84693882@news-west.newscene.com>`

```


David wrote:
> 
> Does anyone have a list of the meaning of the RMCBOL 98,xx errors?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

David,

My sympathies. I have a copy of RM/COBOL '85 V 5.3 - which I haven't
loaded and don't use. For the life of me I can't find in the manuals a
reference to what you are after using keywords like "File Status", "File
Errors" etc.

Just continue plugging away at your reference material - or if all else
fails, contact Liant. (I'm willing to bet there is a table somewhere
specifying file status 1,2, 3 etc. and regarding the 'xx' associated
with the '9' - which like Micro Focus, is probably an extension of the
'9' spelling out the error more specifically).

If interested in a status-checking routine, (when you've found them
out), I could send you a copy of a called program/invoked class, showing
what I do with NetExpress using OO - but you could adapt it to
structured. Plus, for what it's worth, my RM '74 code using Declaratives
and calling FileErrors to display the errors.

(Still use old RM/COBOL '74 and one of its nasties with file status 98 -
implying crashed index - was in fact too many files in the program !).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RMCOBOL 98 Error?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WEmE5.15$cg5.432@client>`
- **References:** `<39dcd892.84693882@news-west.newscene.com> <39DEA52B.A66BCEB4@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:39DEA52B.A66BCEB4@home.com...
>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> fails, contact Liant.  [snip]

Jimmy, why "if all else fails" ??  Liant support is just an e-mail away...
http://www.liant.com/support/email/

David,

As documented in Appendix A of the User's Guide, the XX (where XX not = 01
or 02) means that file has an inconsistent structure.  The reason that you
cannot find further information these codes is that they detail exactly
which internal check failed  (for example:  I think I just read a data
block.  Does the block code = data block? No...98,XX!!).  But even if you
knew which internal check failed, it is most likely not a diagnosis of the
actual failure, since the failure is in the history of the file, the present
inconsistent state being only a symptom of the failure.

The cure does not depend upon which internal check failed, but it is
sometimes useful for our tech support analysts to know which internal
failure occurred.  Furthermore (though this has not happened in several
years) we might see a trend to a particular type of internal failure, which
might suggest a problem in our algorithms.  And, of course, if the internal
failure can be reproduced, we definitely want to know about it!

If you you have trouble submitting your problem at the URL supplied above,
let me know by email.

Best regards,

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation  www.liant.com
```

## Thread: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos — 3 msgs

### Re: Mark Johnson -- Programmers Guild racist yahoos

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<R%8D5.515$PU4.80788@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<pFcA5.6865$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <k7b5tssdb0rclbaqam5ran5104ngtl1adl@4ax.com> <3URA5.1964$HO3.141800@news1.onlynews.com> <5usftssjdcs1j11oklhd99ucoatcj2oe5l@4ax.com> <lrZB5.8115$l35.166385@iad-read.news.verio.net> <015F414633359FBF.81B77F6CD020EC93.061D9A34A2174CE5@lp.airnews.net> <pnpptsg6omedu2385r21a5ml6ufactshpt@4ax.com>`

```

<fixxit@bright.net>
>
> If you committed a crime against a person based on their race, that
> shows "intent". Does it make a difference if I kill a person for
their
> wallet, or kill them because of their race?

Not under the law (until recently). "Intent" is not dependent on
'motive.' If I intend to murder someone, it matters not (usually) what
led me to that conclusion (the obvious exception being murder for
hire). If I have a motive - say, hatred - I may or may not have
intent. If I have intent, I may or may not have a motive (e.g., a
crime of passion). The two are not the same.

The law is an ass.
```

## Thread: My apologies......... — 28 msgs

### My apologies.........

- **From:** "SuperStudd" <jamal_alam@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bv6D5.295364$Gh.8451424@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
It appears that I have gotten some people in here upset over my comments
towards Cobol......

I don't like it.  People I know who make a living programming in Cobol,
don't like it too.  However, there are those that DO like this out-dated
language......and "woohoo", I'm so happy for you.

The fact is, you people take criticism way too hard.  It's like I'm
insulting a family member or something!!  Now I understand that Cobol brings
much joy to your lives, and me disliking this language makes with the
anti-Christ.....but freedom of speech is such a wonderful thing.

Granted, most of you guys are in your 40s and still preach the gospel that
is CoboI....... I'm well aware that you folks are hardly in touch with
today's society.  I'll let that slide, however, since most of you are
already contemplating suicide and I don't want to do further damage to your
already fragile minds.

I apologize.....
```

I hate to prolong this "discussion" - but did you ever tell us what you do
NOT "like" about COBOL?  It certainly is "different" from many other
programming languages - but if we understood what you "liked" about other
programming languages and disliked about COBOL, we might have a better
understanding of your posts.  Such information would also help us know which
other languages you are familiar with.  (For example, if you "like" APL -
but don't like COBOL <G> - then that would certainly tell us something about
your programming preferences.)  If you "like" visual Basic but don't like
COBOL, that too would tell us something (especially if you told us what type
of thing about visual Basic you preferred over COBOL).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: My apologies.........

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DEFF25.E261ED87@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<Bv6D5.295364$Gh.8451424@news20.bellglobal.com> <8ritm4$j0n$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <8rlib3$ifh2o$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`

```
2000-10-06 12:44:21 MEST

DBuck wrote:
>
> ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably just saw a show on TV or something.....

The point is, often enough, an insufficient identification
of likes and dislikes. Somebody may want to write technical
applications and dislikes commercial applications. (This
is entirely legitimate.) The dislike then diffuses towards
the tools of the job. Maybe a teenage-programmer does not
make this distinction.

Then, Cobol tends to be used in larger institutions, for
longer-lasting applications. In such programming shops,
there is a higher probability of encountering bureaucracy
and inefficiency. No competent programmer likes that, and
again, this may affect the attitude towards the tools
used at such workplace.

Maybe that SuperStudd does not have a clear idea what
he likes and what not.

However, our "fragile minds" may not fit at all, what a
bright teenager is thinking.

Herwig
```

whisperin' like there's somethin' ta hide!  Now when *I* was a lad we
SPOKE RIGHT UP, we did, loud 'n clear, no mumblin' there... 'cept when we
was in the liberry, then ol' Mizz Dustnersnatch... now *there's* a name i
ain't thought of in a *dawg's* age, Mizz G. B. Dustnersnatch... Gotta
Buncha, her name was, an' she was so dried an' withered-up an'
prunery-like that we thought that she got a bunch a' dust in her... well,
it was *someplace*, ya never know who's lis'nin'... anyhow, we was in the
liberry when ol' Bilfred... we *called* 'im Bilfred but his name really
was Wilfred, 'cept he thought that was kinda sissyfied an' wanted us all
ta call him Bilfred... poor boy, left town after they caught him in the
men's room at th' Greyhound Bus Station, in one a' th' stalls with...
great big hurkin' fella, too, never woulda thunk that he'd be in there...
well, last anyone heard he was in New York City, runnin' some kinda
decoratin' service... ya never know 'bout some folks, not up until th'
very end... leastwise we didn't with ol' Bilfred, we didn't... anyhow,
there was was in th' liberry an'...


zzzzzzzZZZZZzzzzzzz..... zzzzzaaaaaAAAWWWWWwwww....
ssssnnnnkkkkKKKHHHHOOOORRRRRKKKKKKHHHHhh.....

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: My apologies.........

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K18D5.495$PU4.67319@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<Bv6D5.295364$Gh.8451424@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"SuperStudd" <jamal_alam@hotmail.com
>
> Granted, most of you guys are in your 40s and still preach the
gospel that
> is CoboI....... I'm well aware that you folks are hardly in touch
with
> today's society.  I'll let that slide, however, since most of you
are
> already contemplating suicide and I don't want to do further damage
to your
> already fragile minds.
>
> I apologize.....

Your apology and contrition accepted - although your technique could
use a little work.

One observation, though. Your view of the world (perhaps based on your
experience with, oh, say 50 co-workers and the internet) is, if I may
say so, a bit parochial. I, myself, have been all over the world and
parts of south Georgia. Most in this group have had experiences and
adventures about which you can only dream. Because of your limited
experience, your disparagement of COBOL may come from confusing the
tool with the task.

To many (I think they're called 'nerds'), the tool is the end in
itself and often they have to create a problem so they can apply their
latest nifty solution. To most in the COBOL world, the tool is but a
means to an end (like tracking stock certificates or oil field royalty
payments or compilation of census tract data or tracking spare parts
for the space shuttle or, well, you get the idea).
```

#### ↳ Re: My apologies.........

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dPkD5.12563$bg6.69846@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<Bv6D5.295364$Gh.8451424@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
You miss the point entirely.  Most of us are quite in touch with what
is going on in today's world.  The problem is that you don't have the
experience to realize that there is a niche where COBOL is simply the
best tool there is.  Just because a tool is newer doesn't make it
better, or inferior if it is older.  When a tool is very well suited
to its purpose, it may never be improved upon.  We still use common
tools which have not changed in hundreds of years, simply because
they haven't been bettered.  For example C/C++ may be fine for writing
operating systems, but it is crummy for writing programs that handle
money.  Writing a payroll program in C/C++ is equally as foolish as
writing a word processor in COBOL.  The point is that there are many
different tools, and none of those tools are well suited for every
kind of task there is.  The proper question is, "Is this tool best,
or at least very good, for this particular application?"  And COBOL
is the best tool available for certain kinds of applications.
```

<a id="t-a550949a07"></a>
## Thread: Cobol browser for Windows — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Cobol browser for Windows

- **From:** "Eugenie & Remco van Drie" <nieuws@oochytoe.demon.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<970780350.28825.0.pluto.c3adfc4b@news.demon.nl>`

```
Hi everybody,

I am a Cobol programmer on the Compaq NSK (Tandem) mainframe and would like
to have a Cobol browser. When I have FTPed the Cobol source I would like to
see the program structure in an easy way. For instance that it is
automatically indented. Narrative can be easily toggled on/off. Jump to
subroutines with a mouse click or key and backwards.

Does anybody know a program that can handles the described functionality?
Preferably a program in the public domain (shareware or freeware)

Thanks

Remco
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol browser for Windows

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uE6D5.87354$dZ2.34306535@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<970780350.28825.0.pluto.c3adfc4b@news.demon.nl>`

```
Visual SlickEdit (http://www.slickedit.com) can provide some of this
functionality 'out of the box'. It'll give you a list of paragraph/section
headings that you can click on to navigate around, colorize COBOL syntax,
de-emphasize comments and the like. It also has integrated ftp so you can
save and restore to the Tandem box via ftp from within the editor. It also
supports a scripting language that will allow you to write macros for
selectively hiding comments, or load the source from a called subroutine,
but I think you'll find the colorization negates the need to hide comments.

Merant Revolve (http://www.merant.com) can be used if you want to be able to
navigate through a full system, but I don't know if it's parser can handle
Tandem COBOL.

Neither is free though.

Karl

"Eugenie & Remco van Drie" <nieuws@oochytoe.demon.nl> wrote in message
news:970780350.28825.0.pluto.c3adfc4b@news.demon.nl...
> Hi everybody,
>
> I am a Cobol programmer on the Compaq NSK (Tandem) mainframe and would
like
> to have a Cobol browser. When I have FTPed the Cobol source I would like
to
> see the program structure in an easy way. For instance that it is
> automatically indented. Narrative can be easily toggled on/off. Jump to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol browser for Windows

- **From:** John Burgess <John.Burgess@home.se>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DF07AC.570ED71B@home.se>`
- **References:** `<970780350.28825.0.pluto.c3adfc4b@news.demon.nl>`

```
I resolved a similar requirement using emacs (from http://www.emacs.org/) -
xemacs (from http://www.xemacs.org/) is an alternative - together with "COBOL
mode" (from http://www.azundris.com/hacks/lisp/).

John

```
2000-10-05 12:14:16 MEST

Bearhair schrieb:
>
> ...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> you're on support? Ha! I'm turning MY ringer off when I'm asleep!

Sire,

working in a language is one thing, liking it an
entirely other thing. Everybody is entitled
not to like the programming language in which
he works. Likes and dislikes are human rights.

For what is the next step? People disliking
Cobol, people with other skin color, people
with other religion?

I kindly suggest that the attitude towards
a programming language is a private thing.

There ARE arguments for and against Cobol,
as there are for any other language, and
these should be discussed. That's what
newsgroups are made for.

Herwig

#### ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC7AE6.B8A54A42@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <dqmntsk2eu4aa6bnbrpffm4f191gnh8lv6@4ax.com> <39DC642E.E0F8A970@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Herwig Huener wrote:
> 
> I kindly suggest that the attitude towards
> a programming language is a private thing.

On the other hand, it is never too early to develop attitudes which will
serve one well in a workplace.

When you interview - you LIKE the type of work being offered - if you're
smart.

When you talk with co-workers, you are enthusiastic about your work and
theirs.  You don't tell them how disgusting their work (and chosen
profession) is.  

People do resent being told that their chosen profession is too boring. 
So if you don't want coworkers to resent you - practice tact until it
becomes natural.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DCE6C6.84F75B01@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <dqmntsk2eu4aa6bnbrpffm4f191gnh8lv6@4ax.com> <39DC642E.E0F8A970@fujitsu-siemens.com> <39DC7AE6.B8A54A42@brazee.net> <39DC8DDF.FF10F6DD@fujitsu-siemens.com> <39DCDD7E.9CA827DE@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
2000-10-05 22:33:44 MEST

Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> > BTW: ...
> 
> Hooo! Now I'll be dazed the rest of today after just thinking about
> money as a vector quantity.

What about a wave-function? Copenhagen interpretation
of quantum mechanics seems to apply to money: You do
not know whether you have some, and if you need it,
you find out that you don't. Collapse of the
wave-function of the money, or, in that case, of yourself.

The more modern intepretation of QM is the
many-worlds-interpretation: Might be that you have
money in all other parallel universes - unfortunately,
not in your own.

It is a philosophical question whether Cobol is
best suited for expressing all methods and
aspects of not having money.

> I think the original nasty response was less due to SuperStud's dislike
> of COBOL as to the rudeness of his expressing that dislike in a COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> SuperStud should have done.

Maybe he is a teenager, and as we all know, teenagers
are always right.

Let me, for instance, express my own attitude
towards Cobol: If I want to program a cruise missile,
I use Ada. If I want to program an accounting system,
I use Cobol. Unfortunately, as I was married to an
accountant, I do not like accounting systems, and that
shapes my attitude towards Cobol forever. That is
not the fault of Cobol.

Also, as for getting divorced from an accountant,
a cruise missile is more useful, and Ada has
an advantage there.

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol = Money.......but God, does it suck!!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T01:16:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fK9D5.13185$l35.197507@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<NvMC5.316028$1h3.7625050@news20.bellglobal.com> <39DC8DDF.FF10F6DD@fujitsu-siemens.com> <39DCDD7E.9CA827DE@dced.state.ak.us> <39DCE6C6.84F75B01@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
In article <39DCE6C6.84F75B01@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>,
Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton 
  <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> wrote:
>2000-10-05 22:33:44 MEST

[snippage]

>It is a philosophical question whether Cobol is
>best suited for expressing all methods and
>aspects of not having money.

Nothing is best suited for all (including this sentence) but I have found
that in my personal experience expressing things in COBOL prevents the
aspects of me not-having money.

DD
```

<a id="t-344b012541"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu cobol V3 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu cobol V3

- **From:** icu15452@euronet.nl (CoMag)
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39dc5756.2033524@news.euro.net>`

```

Hi,

I'm an aplication designer, who's interested in cobol aplicatons on
the www.

In my own time I'm trying to realize this whith Fujitsu 3. Fujitus 3,
because I first want to know if it's working and it is easy to use.

It does work, but, when I'm trying to run the program's, I'm getting a
dialog box, for environment settings!

How do I get rid of this dialog box (also appearing on normal
GUI-based progams). 

I have to be certain that the programs, compiled with F3, are working
without the dialog box, before I can recommend cobol programming and
the use of Fujisu (verson 5?) for programming on the web.

regards,

CoMag.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu cobol V3

- **From:** "Brad Prothero" <brad.prothero@clarica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PM5D5.28$1p2.5602@news.corpcomm.net>`
- **References:** `<39dc5756.2033524@news.euro.net>`

```

CoMag <icu15452@euronet.nl> wrote in message
news:39dc5756.2033524@news.euro.net...
>
> Hi,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
V5 uses a different way of handling the runtime so the window does not come
up. If you enter '@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE' in the runtine file(COBOL85.CBR),
that window won't come up either.

Brad Prothero
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu cobol V3

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-10-06T01:28:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001005212817.02202.00000127@nso-fl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<39dc5756.2033524@news.euro.net>`

```
In article <39dc5756.2033524@news.euro.net>, icu15452@euronet.nl (CoMag)
writes:

>
>How do I get rid of this dialog box (also appearing on normal
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Try adding 
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@WinCloseMsg=ON
to the COBOL85.CBR file under the 
[program name] section.

Edit cobol85.cbr
[MyProg]
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@WinCloseMsg=ON


The EnvSetWindow=UNUSE parameter turns off the displaying of 
the Run-Time Environment Window.
The WinCloseMsg=ON parameter turns off the displaying of the
'Console Window Closed' message.

The runtime Environment window allows setting variables that are 
needed by your program in order to run.  This is if you are referencing
files without having some form of internal name string area that has
the physical file name that would be found in Explorer.

Hope that this helps.
```

<a id="t-172e71bb16"></a>
## Thread: "SS" ridle now solved — 1 msgs

### "SS" ridle now solved

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39DC7A7F.B2B769D4@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
2000-10-05 13:55:31 MEST

As I understand J4/00-0645, the
equivalence of "SS" and "ß" (German
"sharp" s) is now dead.

Very good. Otherwise, the following
would have been equivalent:

       move 17 to Gußstahl
       move 17 to GUSSSTAHL
       move 17 to Gusßtahl

So Gusßtahl == Gußstahl!

Now, as we have a general revision
of orthography in Germany anyway,
we would have taken this!

(BTW "Gußstahl" == "casting steel")

Herwig
```

---

## Thread: Call for Papers: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2001) — 1 msgs

### Call for Papers: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2001)

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 2000-10-05T00:02:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<uspdi-0410002000310001@3cust99.tnt35.tco2.da.uu.net>`

```
                            
                  CALL FOR PAPERS and PRESENTATIONS
                               for the

           18th International Conference and Exposition on

                TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2001)

            Theme: Meeting the New Challenges of Testing
 
             June 18-22, 2001 * Washington, D.C. * USA
       (Conference: June 20-22; Workshops/Tutorials: June 18-19)

In cooperation with:

   ASSOCIATION FOR COMPUTING MACHINERY (ACM) SIGSoft
   AMERICAN SOCIETY FOR QUALITY (ASQ) Software Division
   IEEE RELIABILITY SOCIETY
   SOFTWARE TECHNOLOGY SUPPORT CENTER (STSC)

            PLAN NOW TO ATTEND ON JUNE 18-22, 2001!

  We are soliciting 45 to 90 minute presentations on software testing!

Participate in the leading international conference on software testing.
Learn how testing professionals and experts are using advanced methods and
testing applications to improve the effectiveness of their testing, reduce
testing costs and meet schedule deadlines. Share your own experiences in
testing with others. Learn: 
     
* The impact of increasingly complex software on testing
* Ways to improve the testing process     
* Practical experience with the various techniques     
* Which efforts have been most successful     
* Which efforts have been least successful
     
While we encourage  presentations that directly relate to the theme, other
topics relevant to improving the testing process are encouraged and will
be accepted.

* CONFERENCE FEATURES:
  * International speakers
  * Presentation of current and emerging best testing practices
  * Presentations based on the experiences of actual testing
    practitioners     
  * Opportunities to share experiences and lessons with your peers in
    other organizations     
  * Vendor show of products and services

* PLANNED TRACKS:
  * Management
  * Technical
  * Automation
  * Productivity
  * Internet/Web
  * Advanced Topics
  * Case Studies

* PRE-CONFERENCE WORKSHOPS/TUTORIALS:
Two-day and one-day technical sessions for software testing practitioners.

* VENDOR SHOW:
Providers of test support services and software will display and explain
their products.

* SUBMISSION INFORMATION:
     
Proposals for presentations should be 1 to 2 pages long, with enough
detail to give an understanding of the presentation, including an outline
of its contents. Indicate if the most likely audience is technical or
managerial, and please make clear the topic's relevance to software
testing.
           
The attached contribution cover sheet is to be completed and returned with
your submission.
           
Submissions can also be made through the Conference website at:
http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2001call/
             
                PRESENTATION PROPOSALS ARE DUE:

                      October 31, 2000

We are interested in hearing from you. If you want to submit a proposal
for a presentation, or explore an idea for a presentation, please contact
the Program Chair:
     
                      Denis Meredith
                   Meredith Consultants
                  3008 Churchville Road
               Churchville, MD 21028-1808
                Phone: [+1] 410-734-4590
               E-mail: DCMeredith@ACM.org
     (preferred submission method, ascii text only please)

IMPORTANT DATES:

Presentation Proposals: October 31, 2000
   Notice of Selection: January 31, 2001
    Materials Deadline: April 15, 2001
            Conference: June 18-22, 2001

If you are interested in attending the TCS2001 Conference, contact USPDI.

                  Conference Management by:

          U.S. PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT INSTITUTE
             612 Ethan Allen Avenue, Suite 100
                  Takoma Park, MD 20912
                 Phone: [+1] 301-270-1033
                   Fax: [+1] 301-270-1040
                  E-mail: admin@uspdi.org
           Web: http://www.uspdi.org/conference

========================================================================

          18th International Conference and Exposition on
 
                TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2001)

            June 18-22, 2001 * Washington, D.C. * USA
                   Hyatt Regency (Bethesda)

                   CONTRIBUTION COVER SHEET
      
          * Note: This form is available on the Web: *
               http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2001call/

within schedule, (3) How testing theory meets reality.

- **From:** barbara-knox@iname.com (Barb Knox)

- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000,us.issues.occupations.computer-programmers
- **Message-ID:** `<barbara-knox-0710000043540001@192.168.0.10>`
- **References:** `<8qo24n$vj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eRMz5.2758$103.18067@news1.atl> <39D01744.93177590@net.gr> <JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <Kd9B5.1468$QF1.222186@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39D2B62D.8F2515E@kg.hsanet.net> <be7dts8se32jkkevpar77teoh89a1h48ek@4ax.com> <barbara-knox-0210001708380001@192.168.0.10> <f47qts463hkh7h1u21qaojlgclmi0evlft@4ax.com>`

```
In article <f47qts463hkh7h1u21qaojlgclmi0evlft@4ax.com>, fixxit@bright.net
wrote:

> barbara-knox@iname.com (Barb Knox) wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> way, once he gets hired by a company.

Do I?  That's a pretty wild inference from what I wrote.  My experience is
rather the converse:  once an employer has hired an employee, the employer
often acts as though the employee owes them much more than just a fair
day's work for a fair day's pay.  That's one of the main reasons I prefer
to work as a contractor rather than a wage slave.


> If all those 'single workers'
> would refuse to work for a company with policies they don't like,
> they'd be better off.

If a large employer believes that treating the employees like mushrooms is
good for the bottom line (which many do), and if there is a pool of
potential workers suitable for the job, then whether or not a particular
single employee quits will have little effect on the employer (even though
losing the job may easily have a significant negative effect on the
employee).  But if, as you say, ALL the suitable workers can together
credibly threaten to withdraw their labor power, then that does have a
significant effect on the employer.  That's what unions try to do.


> >                                         ...Consider the abuses that
> >occurred in "company towns" (such as "company stores" and debt-slavery). 
> 
> We're talking about the present.

Sadly, the Robber Baron mentality is not just a historical relic.  An
interesting footnote is that in New Zealand law (and presumably other
Commonwealth countries), the employer - employee relationship is termed a
"master - servant" one.  For example, the crime of embezzlement is called
"theft as a servant".


> >Also, would you consider it fair for a boss to require "consensual" sex as
> >a condition of continued employment?
> 
> No, and I wouldn't expect anyone to work for that boss, unless they
> agreed with their rules.

But what then can a lone employee do if such practices are pervasive among
the employers in their industry (e.g. the "casting couch")?


> >Collective bargaining tends to equalize the disparity.
> 
> Agreed. I'm not arguing against employees trying to change working
> conditions; I'm arguing against *government* involvement.

Ah, I thought this thread was about unions.  But even so, appropriate
government activities (e.g. in civil law) are necessary to allow employees
to form unions in the first place.  For a long time in the US unions were
prima facie illegal as "conspiracies in restraint of trade".  Debt slavery
was legal.  And of course prior to that even chattel slavery was legal.

> Note the URL for email corrections--Visit 
> my web page to see what I do... www.bright.net/~fixit

T+280 days
```

Are you saying the starting hourly for a 3 year rookie is 65/PH ? Then
riddle me this. What would be the rate for a 25+ year person ? BTW,
you're excluding veteran people by indicating the rate "could go up".
It should be "it will go up, substantially".

When you say you prefer 1099, are you also including corp-to-corp (no
benefits for either scenario) ?

If 1099 is your preference, then the rate should go up to a minimum
75/PH for the rookie, because you've eliminated much of the overhead
associated with a W2.

What's your high hourly for 1099/corp-to-corp ?

What percentage is CICS related ? What percentage is BATCH ? What
percentage is neither ? Are you on a fishing trip ?

Bill

## Thread: trainee programmer — 7 msgs

### trainee programmer

- **From:** "whitehouse_s_j0" <whitehouse_s_j0@tinyonline.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T02:48:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39de80b9_2@news1.vip.uk.com>`

```
Simply put, I am looking for a trainee position for myself who has passed
the City and Guilds 424 in COBOL and has gone onto the 425.  My only
programming experience is this course which provided me with a project
(supposedly) equivalent to 6 months commercial experience.  I am fed up with
agencies which only try to match exactly what is asked for and do not look
at  the bigger picture of trainability.
  I have a Forces background of 9 years in Administration which involved the
upkeep of a database of personnel.  I also have 3.5 years as an electrician
and 2 years teaching experience.  The highest level of education is at HNC
level with two modules, Mathematics and Electrical/Electronic principles at
distinction level.  I live in Birmingham, England but am willing to relocate
anywhere having no ties.
```

#### ↳ Re: trainee programmer

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rn0jf$h2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39de80b9_2@news1.vip.uk.com>`

```
In article <39de80b9_2@news1.vip.uk.com>,
  "whitehouse_s_j0" <whitehouse_s_j0@tinyonline.co.uk> wrote:
> Simply put, I am looking for a trainee position

Hi:

Good luck with your search.

I suppose it is the same in England as in America and elsewhere
that most body shops or whatever they call themselves these days
are nothing more than skill-set-matching automatons.

Since you are a beginner, your skill-set will be less than
adequate and therefore will fail the match.

Although a resume may give one some idea of what a person knows,
it will not reveal how well they know what they know nor how
good they are.

Anyway, in the olden days when I hired programmers, I would never
speak to anyone who would not bring in samples of their work.
Programs and stuff which they had written.

So, people got hired based on an interview and the samples of
their work.

It probably might not make any difference whatsoever, but I
always suggest creating a portfolio of your work to show to
people who might be capable of appreciating it. Show them
what you have done. All sorts of other professions do this,
but not programmers, I wonder why?

You might also want to create an HTML version of your
portfolio with screen shots and sample code which you could
email to people. As a beginner, it might not be much, but
it is better than nothing.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-d199450c9d"></a>
## Thread: CALL statement - for non cobol progs? — 3 msgs

### CALL statement - for non cobol progs?

- **From:** "alan jones" <ob2@freeuk.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TQAD5.19278$L12.381833@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
Can the CALL statement be used to call non cobol programs and if so how does
cobol differentiates between cobol sub-programs which are called by program-id
and regular programs which are called on path/name?
```

#### ↳ Re: CALL statement - for non cobol progs?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8roinl0edc@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<TQAD5.19278$L12.381833@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```


----------
In article <TQAD5.19278$L12.381833@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com>, "alan
jones" <ob2@freeuk.com> wrote:


> Can the CALL statement be used to call non cobol programs

YES.  I would GUESS that any program available to the operating system could
be called.


 and if so how does
> cobol differentiates between cobol sub-programs which are called by program-id
> and regular programs which are called on path/name?

COBOL wouldn't care.  When CALL is exectued, program control goes to the
called program.

In a OS390 environment, there are many ways to CALL an external program.
US7VC and REMCMND come to mind.  Even an FTPed file can cause an external
program to 'tap off' from the scheduler software.

So it kinda depends on what you are talking about exactly.

Jeff

>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: CALL statement - for non cobol progs?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crDD5.17$rR1.18549@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<TQAD5.19278$L12.381833@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
alan jones <ob2@freeuk.com> wrote in message
news:TQAD5.19278$L12.381833@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com...
> Can the CALL statement be used to call non cobol programs and if so how
does
> cobol differentiates between cobol sub-programs which are called by
program-id
> and regular programs which are called on path/name?
>

Well, the short answer is that in COBOL "CALL" is used to load (if
necessary) and execute pre-compiled code which was *designed* to be CALLed
rather than executed 'standalone.'

That is, a 'regular' program designed to be executed from the command-line
is likely not a suitable object for a COBOL 'CALL' verb.

Most COBOL systems have a separate mechanism to "Execute an external
'regular' program and return".

In either case - CALLed or 'executed' - the (sub-)program can be written in
any language.

MCM
```

---

About your #1..Chrysler is still under contract with UAW, so that
didn't work.  

About your #2..They don't have to reopen in another country.  They can
reopen in another state.  Many states have right-to-work laws which
means an employer cannot dismiss or not hire a person that does not
want to be part of the union.  Kentucky and Arizona are two examples
that come to mind.  In Arizona, unions are rare even among the trades
like plumbers and electricians.  They do exist, but they are rare.
And they are rare because employees are continuously voting to keep
them out.

>

    With a company as large as Chrysler, unionization is a
matter of degree.  Many of the plants that supply parts to
chrysler
are non union.  Not all to be sure, but many.  On the other
hand, far more of GM's suppliers are union, and the union is
not about to let that change.


> About your #2..They don't have to reopen in another country.
They can
> reopen in another state.  Many states have right-to-work laws
which
> means an employer cannot dismiss or not hire a person that does
not
> want to be part of the union.  Kentucky and Arizona are two
examples
> that come to mind.  In Arizona, unions are rare even among the
trades
> like plumbers and electricians.  They do exist, but they are
rare.
> And they are rare because employees are continuously voting to
keep
> them out.
>
    Georgia is another.  But once a company is union, getting rid
of the
union (assuming that the workers want to keep the union) can be
legally difficult.

    You are right though - it can happen.  I believe that
Caterpillar did
this at one time.  Of course they had to operate with picket
lines
in place for years.  I do not know Caterpillar's story, but most
of
the stories of companys that got rid of unions involved unions
that
were strangling the company.  It is a lot like politics.  If you
lose the
hearts and minds of the people, you are in trouble.  It does not
matter
if you are union or managment or president for life.

<a id="t-e64a0bd374"></a>
## Thread: Update an INDEXED FILE using SEQ FILE — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Update an INDEXED FILE using SEQ FILE

- **From:** Sarah Mellings <sarah@whoofmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<su117bh7msij30@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi, this is a student question..please guide me in the right direction.

The code below (MAIN-ITERATION) is to update a LINE SEQUENTIAL MASTER FILE 
using a LINE SEQUENTIAL TRANSACTION FILE.

************************************************
MAIN-ITERATION.

     EVALUATE TRUE

       WHEN TRANS-CODE < MASTER-CODE

         PERFORM SOME-PROCESS
         PERFORM READ-TRANSACTION-FILE

       WHEN TRANS-CODE > MASTER-CODE

         WRITE NEW-RECORD FROM MASTER-RECORD
         PERFORM READ-MASTER-FILE

       WHEN TRANS-CODE = MASTER-CODE
         
            PERFORM UPDATE-PROCESS

            PERFORM READ-TRANSACTION-FILE
            PERFORM READ-MASTER-FILE

     END-EVALUATE.

READ-TRANSACTION-FILE.
     READ TRANSACTION-FILE AT END
          MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO TRANS-CODE
     END-READ.

READ-MASTER-FILE.
     READ MASTER-FILE AT END
          MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO MASTER-CODE
     END-READ.
************************************************

There is nothing wrong with the above program and it works perfectly ok
and is already made passed by my tutor.

Now my problem is I have another similar program to update an
INDEXED SEQUENTIAL MASTER FILE using LINE SEQUENTIAL TRANSACTION FILE.
The output is modified INDEXED SEQUENTIAL MASTER FILE.

************************************************
For this I have coded the following:

MAIN-PARA.
     PERFORM INITIAL-PROCESS
     PERFORM MAIN-ITERATION UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "Y"
     STOP RUN.

INITIAL-PROCESS.
     OPEN INPUT TRANSACTION-FILE

     PERFORM READ-TRANSACTION-FILE.

READ-TRANSACTION-FILE.
     READ TRANSACTION-FILE AT END
          MOVE "Y" TO EOF-FLAG
     END-READ.

MAIN-ITERATION.
     OPEN I-O MASTER-FILE

     MOVE TRANS-CODE TO MASTER-CODE

     READ MASTER-FILE

     INVALID KEY

          PERFORM SOME-PROCESS

     NOT INVALID KEY

          PERFORM UPDATE-PROCESS

     END-READ

     CLOSE MASTER-FILE

     PERFORM READ-TRANSACTION-FILE.

************************************************

Now my questions are:
(a) Is this the correct way to update an INDEXED SEQUENTIAL FILE
using LINE SEQUENTIAL FILE ?

(b) Can this be done by using HIGH-VALUES clause at all ?

(c) In the MAIN-ITERATION above: Am I opening and closing the
MASTER-FILE too many times due to the loop ?

(d) I have ALSO to code the UPDATE-PROCESS as:

UPDATE-PROCESS.

IF TRANS-REC-TYPE = 'D' THEN

         DELETE MASTER-REC

END-IF.
( I have to remove the record from master file if it matches and if 'D' )

The Compiler (Fujitsu V3 - win 95) gives me the compilation error exactly 
as:
"JMN2516I-S  'MASTER-REC' IN DELETE STATEMENT MUST BE FILE OTHER THAN SORT-
MERGE FILE."
Please help..

**Files are**********************************

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

FILE-CONTROL.

     SELECT TRANSACTION-FILE ASSIGN TO "A:\TF.DAT"
            ACCESS MODE  IS SEQUENTIAL
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

     SELECT MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "A:\MF.DAT"
            ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
            ACCESS MODE  IS DYNAMIC
            RECORD KEY   IS MASTER-CODE.
*******************************************

This is a homework question but I am also not expecting any REAL CODING
from anyone..Please just give some hints on the above..I am trying this
program since last 4 weeks and came up with the above solution..Please
guide me in the right direction. Thank you.

Regards

Sarah
```

#### ↳ Re: Update an INDEXED FILE using SEQ FILE

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zE9E5.532$D81.146611@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<su117bh7msij30@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Sarah Mellings" <sarah@whoofmail.com>
> ************************************************
>
> Now my problem is I have another similar program to update an
> INDEXED SEQUENTIAL MASTER FILE using LINE SEQUENTIAL TRANSACTION
FILE.
> The output is modified INDEXED SEQUENTIAL MASTER FILE.
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> using LINE SEQUENTIAL FILE ?

Pretty much. Styles may differ.

>
> (b) Can this be done by using HIGH-VALUES clause at all ?

What's a HIGH-VALUES clause? If you mean should the program detect
HIGH-VALUES as a sentinel record (meaning no-more-data), sure, you can
do that.
>
> (c) In the MAIN-ITERATION above: Am I opening and closing the
> MASTER-FILE too many times due to the loop ?

It won't hurt, but you can take a severe performance hit and
inefficiency is frowned on by puritans (I'm one). Depending on the
size of the file, the operating system, and speed of the machine,
several seconds may be required just to open or close a file. The more
common way is:

OPEN TRANSACTION-FILE.
OPEN MASTER-FILE.
PERFORM UPDATING-STUFF.
CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE.
CLOSE MASTER-FILE.

>
> (d) I have ALSO to code the UPDATE-PROCESS as:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> ( I have to remove the record from master file if it matches and if
'D' )
>
> The Compiler (Fujitsu V3 - win 95) gives me the compilation error
exactly
> as:
> "JMN2516I-S  'MASTER-REC' IN DELETE STATEMENT MUST BE FILE OTHER
THAN SORT-
> MERGE FILE."

Let's decode:
JMN2516I-S 'dataname' in DELETE statement must be FILE (other than
sort-merge file).

Is MASTER-REC (in your example) the name of a file?
```

#### ↳ Re: Update an INDEXED FILE using SEQ FILE

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T01:11:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001008211149.21115.00001196@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<su117bh7msij30@corp.supernews.com>`

```
>From: Sarah Mellings sarah@whoofmail.com 
>Date: 10/8/00 10:30 AM Eastern Daylight Time

The only 'error' I see in the indexed update is that you don't have to
open/close the indexed file each time.  Open it once in the initialization and
close it at the end of the program.

HIGH-VALUES checking isn't needed in indexed updates.  It is usually only used
in the sequential updates that you have already coded.

As for the compiler error I'm a mainframe programmer so it doesn't make sense
to me.  I don't see a SORTFILE in a SELECT statement and you didn't post the
FDs so I can't make an educated guess.  Have to wait on the PC bodies on this
one.

The only other thing you should have is a FILE-STATUS on the indexed file. 
This should be checked to see if the file opened correctly and be checked to
see if the DELETE worked properly, and last after the CLOSE.  I don't know if
you have any DECLARATIVES in your program for this or not so I'm giving my
preference as to technique.  I think DECLARATIVES still needs a FILE-STATUS in
the SELECT though (which may be giving you your compiler error??? - just a wild
guess).

Good post :)  Come back again.

Eileen


>Hi, this is a student question..please guide me in the right direction.
>
…[137 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sarah
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Update an INDEXED FILE using SEQ FILE

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:27:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rrjeo$b6j$2@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<su117bh7msij30@corp.supernews.com> <20001008211149.21115.00001196@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```

"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20001008211149.21115.00001196@ng-ce1.aol.com...
> >From: Sarah Mellings sarah@whoofmail.com
> >Date: 10/8/00 10:30 AM Eastern Daylight Time
>
> The only 'error' I see in the indexed update is that you don't
have to
> open/close the indexed file each time.  Open it once in the
initialization and
> close it at the end of the program.
>

    But remember to close the file anytime an actual person
is involved in the transaction.  You never know what a person
will do.

    You did not post the selects, but depending on the platform,
small details of record and file locking can have huge impact on
speed.
An exclusive open can speed things up a lot, and eliminate
many possible types of problems.

<snip>
```

YukonMama wrote:
> 
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> guess).
Sarah,

Eileen's comment about using file-status - if you don't believe her, try
this one :-

	close CustomerFile	*> Yes "CLOSE" or just plain don't do 				*>
anything to OPEN it

	set FileNotFinished to true
	perform until FileFinished
	  read CustomerFile next record
		at end set FileFinished to true
		not at end display CustomerRecord
	  End-read
	End-perform

Try it and see what happens <G>. If you are using a compiler that has an
animated debugger - check out the file-status and the flag for
FileFinished/FileNotFinished.

Jimmy
```

## Thread: status 46 abend --- help! — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### status 46 abend --- help!

- **From:** "Seekermike" <seeker@dwx.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e12ff3@news.dwx.com>`

```
I program in mainframe COBOL for MVS/VM.  Last week a nightly batch program
that had been running fine for 6 weeks started giving me problems.  The
program reads a sequential DASD file, GS.MA.SRTD.AUDIT.AGTMF.UPDT(+1), and
keeps abending for status code 46, trying to do a READ NEXT but there is no
next record.  The odd thing is, the job runs fine when I run it the next
morning!  I copy the production input files to test, so as far as I can
tell, everything is exactly the same.  I even copied the production JCL
(which abends at night) to test and ran with it.....and it runs fine!
Something is different at night that makes the job abend, but I can't figure
out what.  Can anyone help me?  My home e-mail is seeker@dwx.com.

Here is the program code and JCL I'm using.


        SELECT AUDIT-FILE
            ASSIGN TO UT-S-AG232S1.
::
::
::

 FD   AUDIT-FILE
      RECORDING MODE IS F.
      COPY AGWIQO10.
::
::
::

 000100*W AGWIQO10  AGENT MASTER AUDIT TRAIL RECORD
 000200****************************************************************
 000300**   THIS RECORD LAYOUT IS FOR THE SEQUENTIAL AUDIT TRAIL FILE
 000400**   THAT IS WRITTEN TO AS CICS TD QUEUE 'AGPR'.
 000500****************************************************************
 000600 01  AGWIQO10.
 000700     05  AGWIQO10-KEY.
 000800         10  AGWIQO10-PROC-GRP        PIC XX.
 000900         10  AGWIQO10-REC-TYPE        PIC XX.
 001000         10  AGWIQO10-AGENT           PIC X(18).
 001100         10  AGWIQO10-COMP            PIC XX.
 001200         10  AGWIQO10-LINE            PIC XX.
 001300     05  AGWIQO10-SUB-KEY.
 001400         10  AGWIQO10-DATE            PIC S9(7) COMP-3.
 001500         10  AGWIQO10-TIME            PIC S9(7) COMP-3.
 001600         10  AGWIQO10-UPDT-CODE       PIC 9.
 001700         10  AGWIQO10-OPER-ID         PIC XXXX.
 001800     05  AGWIQO10-DATA                PIC X(1674).



  3450-READ-AUDIT.
      INITIALIZE AGWIQO10-KEY.
      MOVE '01' TO AGWIQO10-PROC-GRP.
      MOVE 'AA' TO AGWIQO10-REC-TYPE.
      MOVE AAF-AGENT-KEY TO AGWIQO10-AGENT.
      READ AUDIT-FILE NEXT
        AT END
          MOVE 'Y' TO AUDIT-EOF.
      MOVE AGWIQO10 TO WS-AGWIQO10.
 ****
 **** THIS MAKES SURE THE RECORD WE JUST READ FROM THE AUDIT FIL
 **** MATCHES THE RECORD WE READ ON THE TFG AGENT MASTER FILE
 **** (BOTH FIELDS ARE 18 BYTES LONG)
      IF (AGWIQO10-AGENT = AAF-AGENT-KEY)
            MOVE 'Y' TO AUDIT-EOF.
  3450-READ-EXIT.
      EXIT.

::
::
::

//STEP0080 EXEC PGM=AGB0170,COND=(0,NE)
//NA100I1  DD  DSN=GS.NA.MASTER,DISP=SHR                     *READ*
//NA100I2  DD  DSN=GS.NA.INDEX,DISP=SHR                      *READ*
//NA100I4  DD  DSN=GS.NA.HISTORY,DISP=SHR                    *READ*
//NA100I5  DD  DSN=GS.NA.CLIENT,DISP=SHR                     *READ*
//TALIVLB  DD  DSN=GS.TA.LIVE.TABLES,DISP=SHR                *READ*
//AGSI010  DD  DSN=GS.MA.AGENTS.MASTER.AA.DASD,DISP=SHR      *READ*
//AG232S1  DD  DSN=GS.MA.SRTD.AUDIT.AGTMF.UPDT(+1),DISP=SHR  *READ*
//AGMISC   DD  DSN=GS.MA.AGENTS.MISC,DISP=SHR,               *READ*
//             AMP=('BUFND=8,BUFNI=8')
//AMVU020  DD  DSN=AM.P.XX.P.AGTMF,DISP=SHR                  *UPDATE*
//AMVU030  DD  DUMMY    *,DSN=AM.P.XX.P.AGTRANS,DISP=SHR     *UPDATE*
//AMRO040  DD  SYSOUT=3,FLASH=SL3Y             *TFG RECS*
//AMRO050  DD  SYSOUT=3,FLASH=SL3Y             *IMT AGTMF UPDATED*
//AMRO060  DD  DUMMY                           *IMT TRANS UPDATED*
//AMRO070  DD  SYSOUT=3,FLASH=SL3Y             *IMT AGTMF NOT FOUND*
//AMRO080  DD  DUMMY                           *IMT TRANS NOT FOUND*
//MIN9001  DD  DSN=AM.P.XX.P.AMDATE,DISP=OLD   *DATE OF LAST RUN*
//SYSUDUMP DD  SYSOUT=D
//SYSDBOUT DD  SYSOUT=D
//SYSOUT   DD  SYSOUT=*
```

#### ↳ Re: status 46 abend --- help!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E1E50F.22EC87FA@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<39e12ff3@news.dwx.com>`

```


Seekermike wrote:
> 
> The odd thing is, the job runs fine when I run it the next
> morning!  I copy the production input files to test, so as far as I can
> tell, everything is exactly the same.  I even copied the production JCL
> (which abends at night) to test and ran with it.....and it runs fine!


When I get something like that, I ask myself the following:

Do you have the same compiler options for test and production?

Do you have same security options for the test and production files?

Are you running the same version of the program?

Are the files what I think they are?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: status 46 abend --- help!

- **From:** "Seekermike" <seeker@dwx.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e24294$1@news.dwx.com>`
- **References:** `<39e12ff3@news.dwx.com> <39E1E50F.22EC87FA@brazee.net>`

```
As it turned out, one of the files was NOT what I thought it was.   I have a
date input dataset that was set to 20001003 instead of 2001004, so the
program was searching for data from more than one date, then looking for its
counterpart in the audit file.  When it couldn't find the record it was
looking for, it abended for status code 46.  Thanks!


Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:39E1E50F.22EC87FA@brazee.net...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: status 46 abend --- help!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

Howard Brazee wrote:

> Seekermike wrote:
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are the files what I think they are?

SSRANGE compiler option will tell you whether you have a memory overwrite
problem.   This causes strange results like think to happen, optimise
reorganises the working storage.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: status 46 abend --- help!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FsDE5.347$tC5.17529@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<39e12ff3@news.dwx.com> <39E1E50F.22EC87FA@brazee.net> <39E2949D.FEC39563@zip.com.au>`

```
I have seen this a number of times. Last time it happened it was on an IBM
mainframe on a VSAM file which had not been re-org'd
(REPRO/DELETE/DEFINE/REPRO) for while and all the CI's were full.

Time before that it was when the OPEN failed, but never interrogated, and
the FILE STATUS variable was shared across files, as in:

OPEN INPUT
          FILE-1    << Open fails, FILE STATUS not '00'
          FILE-2    << open succeeds, file status is '00'
          FILE-3.
IF File-status NOT = '00'
  (error procedure)...

This code will only trigger an error if the OPEN of FILE-3 fails.

Just some things to keep in mind....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: status 46 abend --- help!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```

> 
> Time before that it was when the OPEN failed, but never interrogated, and
> the FILE STATUS variable was shared across files, as in:

I like having a company standard stating that all FILE STATUS variables
are unique to particular files, with names which make it obvious which
files they refer to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: status 46 abend --- help!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rvqth$ich$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39e12ff3@news.dwx.com> <39E1E50F.22EC87FA@brazee.net> <39E2949D.FEC39563@zip.com.au> <FsDE5.347$tC5.17529@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I suspect that Michael understands this, but others may not.  Including
multiple files in the same OPEN statement is NOT the problem with the
"attached" example.  The problem is that the different files all use the
same field to store the file status-code.  If each of the files DID have a
different file-status field and all 3 fields were queried after the OPEN,
then this would be "OK" code. (Although, you would need to think about the
question of whether or not you really WANT to open the 2nd and 3rd files -
if the 1st file's open failed.  Sometimes you do - sometimes you don't)
```

## Thread: COBOL programming standards — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL programming standards

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rpl4j$raj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Anybody interested in elaborating on the standards you employ?
Are they formal? Are they enforced? Are new employees trained
in their use? What do they cover? Do you like them? Any
general comments about standards?

COBOL only please.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL programming standards

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rr7lc02pt9@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8rpl4j$raj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
----------
In article <8rpl4j$raj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
wrote:


> Hi:
>
> Anybody interested in elaborating on the standards you employ?

Why not.



> Are they formal?

The rules for writing programs in our application are WELL documented, and,
sigh, badly out of date.



Are they enforced?

No.  They are not enforced as much as they should be.  But any good, new
programmer will quickly realize the value of such standards, the first night
they are on-call and the business is on the line.




 Are new employees trained
> in their use?

On a case by case basis.  It doesn't take long to realize how good a new
programmer is going to be.  The sloppy ones are watched closely and their
code is reviewed.




 What do they cover?

Our standards cover things from on-call procedures to coding style.  We have
standards for called modules, copybooks, tables, databse coding, etc etc.  I
could go on and on.




 Do you like them?

Even a loosely enforced set of standards is better than none.  As I said
earlier, standards allow certain things to be 'given'.  This is important at
3:00AM when the business is at risk.


 Any
> general comments about standards?
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

Position: EDI Analyst
Location: Houston, Tx
Salary:65K-72K
Requirements: Minimum 3-5 years EDI X12 standards experience.
Respond To: tcblanch@ghg.net
Respond With Subject Line: EDI Analyst <newsgroup you found posting>

Preferred Knowledge Of:
GENTRAN MVS workflow, mapping, and operation.
CICS and COBOL programming
TSO, ISPF, and JCL

- **References:** `<JAWz5.5095$583.191033@news1.onlynews.com> <39D0EA87.E38B34B8@sectech.com> <oFcA5.6864$583.293343@news1.onlynews.com> <msa5tsginfnbt2os919vfbf89lheduaa15@4ax.com> <39D1B61E.DC5B78FF@kg.hsanet.net> <Kd9B5.1468$QF1.222186@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39D2B62D.8F2515E@kg.hsanet.net> <be7dts8se32jkkevpar77teoh89a1h48ek@4ax.com> <barbara-knox-0210001708380001@192.168.0.10> <f47qts463hkh7h1u21qaojlgclmi0evlft@4ax.com> <barbara-knox-0710000043540001@192.168.0.10> <s9jvtsk3mmimbpek6vhqvnft7f65fd51ps@4ax.com>`

```
In article <s9jvtsk3mmimbpek6vhqvnft7f65fd51ps@4ax.com>, fixxit@bright.net
wrote:

> barbara-knox@iname.com (Barb Knox) wrote:

[snip]
> >That's a pretty wild inference from what I wrote.  
> 
> My apologies if I mis-inferred, ma'am.  :)

That's very gracious of you, suh. <g>

[snip]
> >> If all those 'single workers'
> >> would refuse to work for a company with policies they don't like,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> have another employer in line *before* taking drastic action.

Being able to have an effect on the employer improves one's bargaining
position.  A union can in many cases strike a better bargain for a worker
than the worker could on their own.

> >      ...But if, as you say, ALL the suitable workers can together
> >credibly threaten to withdraw their labor power, then that does have a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> a job, because they thought they deserved more.

Indeed, power corrupts.  Unions as well as businesses can do damage.

[snip]
> >But what then can a lone employee do if such practices are pervasive among
> >the employers in their industry (e.g. the "casting couch")?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> joblessness notwithstanding :)

In principle I agree.  But what constitutes unacceptable harm is
determined by the legal system, so that is another area of labor relations
where government activity seem unavoidable.

[snip]
> >                                            ,,,But even so, appropriate
> >government activities (e.g. in civil law) are necessary to allow employees
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> free country!

Supposed by whom?  It's said that the US has the best government money can
buy...
 
> >                                   ...For a long time in the US unions were
> >prima facie illegal as "conspiracies in restraint of trade".  Debt slavery
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> around'?

That law is the Sherman Antitrust Act.  Here are some details (from
http://longman.awl.com/garraty/glossary_s.htm):
> The 1890 Sherman Antitrust Act, the nation's first antitrust act, made any
> concentration (monopoly) in restraint of trade illegal. This already weak
> law was emasculated when the Supreme Court ruled in "U.S. v. E. C. Knight"
> (1895) that manufacturing was excluded from the antitrust law.
> The Sherman Act was often used to break up labor unions.

I believe the Act is still in force, and that the varying uses of it over
time have been due to varying judicial decisions.

T+282 days
```

A lot of this termonology is just a result of the fact that the
English legal system originated in feudal times. And lawyers keep
using the same terms because they have a known precise meaning
that works. I wouldn't read too much into this. 

Tim Josling

## Thread: Need to access data in AcuCobol Isam Files — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Need to access data in AcuCobol Isam Files

- **From:** Adam Williams <adam@lnx01688.morrison.iserv.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%hkE5.56435$sB2.1123113@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
I have a large application written in PHP running on a
RedHat Linux/Apache web server.  Up till this point I
have used an Informix database (either through PHP's
native Informix support or UnixODBC).  

Now I need to access data residing in AcuCOBOL ISAM
(or are they called Vision files?) running on an
IBM RS/6000 AIX 4.2.1.  AcuCOBOL does support data
access from Win32 ODBC drivers, but I need to acces
this data from my web server.  The version of
AcuCOBOL is:

ACUCOBOL-GT version 4.0.0
Vision version 4 file system

I've inquired at both Informix (Data Blades) and
AcuCOBOL and neither has been able to offer any
solutions. 

And pointers would be greatly appreciated
```

>RedHat Linux/Apache web server.  Up till this point I
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

---

#### ↳ Re: Need to access data in AcuCobol Isam Files

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadim-cbl@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-14T05:27:31+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<39E7EF48.B641E978@siber.com>`

```
Looks like we have an answer.

Siber Systems has a C++ library that can read 
AcuCobol data files and turn the records 
into sets of fields that C++ can understand.

We can license the library to you in source or binary format.

For more details see http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need to access data in AcuCobol Isam Files

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <smithr@ix.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T09:07:46+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OP4G5.349$bq1.801@news.syd.ip.net.au>`
- **References:** `<%hkE5.56435$sB2.1123113@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39E7EF48.B641E978@siber.com>`

```
> Adam Williams wrote:
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> > And pointers would be greatly appreciated

I find it a little surprising AcuCorp couldn't help you.
Acu's Vision files are created and accessed by a C routine
which is linked into their cobol runtime.
We use a call to "I$IO" (which is the C routine) to access Vision
files without use of the standard Cobol commands.

What Acu must be saying then is that they won't allow their C
routine to be linked into / or used by any other program.

It is probably worth asking Acu again but specifically ask if you
can use their I$IO routines from a different language (PHP).

Sometimes the person you ask the question to doesn't
have all the answers!

"I" thought it would have just been a licensing issue.

Regards,

Ray Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need to access data in AcuCobol Isam Files

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@NoSpamPlease.online.no>

```

capable of using a C library, and that this person has an agreement with
Acucorp this should be a doable task.

Gisle Forseth
