<a id="t-f6924be7c2"></a>
## Thread: Cobol on my PC — 26 msgs

### Cobol on my PC

- **From:** "Mark Jepson" <mark.jepson@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
Hi,

I am a Cobol developer on OS/390 mainframe, but I would like to work from
home. Could anyone reccomend a PC based compile link editor that is close to
the Cobol 370 (Cobol for MVS) version?

Thanks

Mark Jepson
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LFoE5.4255$ML5.21215@news4.atl>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
"Mark Jepson" <mark.jepson@btinternet.com> wrote:
>
> I am a Cobol developer on OS/390 mainframe, but I would like to work
> from home. Could anyone reccomend a PC based compile link editor that
> is close to the Cobol 370 (Cobol for MVS) version?

Mark, I suspect the closest PC based COBOL would be MERANT Micro Focus
Net Express 3.1; you can even do CICS.  But it is expensive.  You can
get version 3.0 of Fujitsu COBOL free from www.adtools.com, which is
not as compatible with IBM COBOL.  Or you can buy Thane Hubbell's book
"Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", which comes with the Fujitsu COBOL
compiler on CD.  All the examples in the book are on the CD and work
with the Fujitsu compiler.  Thane also frequents this newsgroup.  You
should be able to get help with either compiler in this newsgroup.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>

too (but not as expensive as Merant, I believe.)

Ok - I'm confused (so what else is new - nu?)

Howard, to whom were you directing the comment about editing on a PC and
porting to a mainframe?  Not being snooty - just the order of these replies
isn't making any sense :)

Like Mark I wish to learn a PC version of COBOL so I got Thane's book.  One of
these days I may get to use it!  Next on my list after this blankity blank CBT
class I'm taking on ANSI C and C++.  I'm sure Thane's book is much better
written and at the least I can 'snarf' directly at the author :D.

Having a mainframe like compiler would be nice - smaller learning curve - but
hey you don't always get what you want.

As for editors - at work I have 2 choices - ROSCOE or TSO (ISPF).  Neither
highlight, neither cut/paste.  Change control is Librarian.

Eileen

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E47506.B5760627@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<aa64us8et46q8422b92cbv320ru9sog422@4ax.com> <20001010222408.12870.00000967@ng-fw1.aol.com>`

```


YukonMama wrote:
> 
> Ok - I'm confused (so what else is new - nu?)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> isn't making any sense :)

I am making general conversation - my first post was a question, I was
curious.  Subsequent have been responses with quotes.
 
> Having a mainframe like compiler would be nice - smaller learning curve - but
> hey you don't always get what you want.

It just seems like much more work to me, especially in testing.
 
> As for editors - at work I have 2 choices - ROSCOE or TSO (ISPF).  Neither
> highlight, neither cut/paste.  Change control is Librarian.

ISPF highlights, if you have a color terminal set to 80 columns.  Set
profile COBOL.  I use a COBOL PDS so that it gets set automatically.  I
notice though, that when I use it at home where I have a color printer,
my terminal emulator prints in black and white.

For cutting and pasting, I use it two ways:

If I want words, then I use standard Windows cutting and pasting
(Doesn't everybody use terminal emulating programs these days?).  The
limit here, is that I can't cut more than the visible page at one time. 
But when I want to do that, I can use the SPF editor commands.

But CoBOL seems very line oriented to me.  The SPF editor is also very
line oriented.  I do know this editor well, and use the exclude command
quite a bit.  Copying a bunch of lines from one program to another can
be done by macros (such as CUT and PASTE), by saving the lines in a
DUMMY file (actually PDS member), or by knowing their line numbers.  Of
course within a program it is even easier.

I have used a wide variety of editors on various mainframes and PC's to
edit CoBOL programs.  I prefer the ISPF editor for my coding.

Where SPF falls short is in hypertext links (not counting the ZOOM and
SHOWPROC commands).  But to get this to work on the PC, would involve
copying every module you might want to your PC to get this to work.  I
don't like counting on copies of modules to be current.  When I work on
the mainframe, I can quickly look at the real McCoy - not via hypertext,
but I know where to look anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)

I think I mistated - we use Librarian and therefore edit directly in the test
source library not from a 'flat' file (Librarian files have their own funky
format).  To edit we go into something called Elipse.  That is the ISPF people
do. I'm a Roscoe body so I just pull the text into my library and work with it.
 Store it there too although when I compile it is copied into the test
Librarian file.  I can sorta edit my programs in Roscoe by keying the command
COB or COB2 but the editor isn't all that good.  It does find the MVOES and
PERFROMS and missing periods etc but that's about it.

Eileen

I'll see if the COBOL works in Elipse.

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
I have read both Judson's and Volker's replies.  I would like to stress that
IBM VisualAge COBOL *is* closer to IBM's COBOL for OS/390 & VM - because
they actually use the same "front-end" source code.

Having said that, IBM does NOT offer any (PC) solution for older OS/VS COBOL
code or even newer code compiled with CMPR2.  Therefore, if you have either
of those in your "mainframe environment" - the MERANT solution is "closer to
what you want".

There is also an issue of whether or not you have either Assembler or PL/I
modules in your mainframe environment.  If you have Assembler, then MERANT
provides a better solution; if you have PL/I, then IBM probably does.

Another option to look at - is CA-Reallia which also provides a "mainframe
emulation" environment. Like MERANT and IBM, it is NOT cheap.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I don't really understand the advantage in having compatible CoBOL
compilers on the PC and Mainframe and porting the code across.

I suppose it would be nice to have a PC editor which had the correct
syntax highlighting, but when you are done typing, and want to compile
and test - don't you want to be back on the mainframe?

I guess it is a pain to dial in to a terminal emulator.  If you have
broadband access though, that problem is solved.  I can get into my
mainframe via the internet, so I will be sitting pretty whenever
broadband access is available for my house. (although I still would have
to dial in to get my work's e-mail as they don't have a VPN).

Why do you want to compile on a PC code which will run on a mainframe?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** "Brad Prothero" <brad.prothero@clarica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z5GE5.47$1p2.8326@news.corpcomm.net>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net...
> I don't really understand the advantage in having compatible CoBOL
> compilers on the PC and Mainframe and porting the code across.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why do you want to compile on a PC code which will run on a mainframe?
We had this setup at our shop. We ported all of our test files down to the
PC to give people a choice as to where they could do their development. When
a new file was introduced or a file format was changed, it was part of our
change control that that file would be ported down. We have people that work
on our Agent software that we send out to the field. All of this is done on
the PC. With all of the Mainframe test files ported down to the PC, they
could use the same Development tools that they use on the PC software. We
even had a CICS emulator with which we could test. This was an incredible
help since you did not need to bother a Sys. Programmer to add a
Transaction, Map, Program,...  Granted we did not have a big department at
that time, maybe 20 in IS total, so managing this was easier.

Also, I know the puritans will have their hair on their neck stand up about
this, with the way that most people learn about programming and computers
being on PC's, some people are more comfortable on the PC that trying to
learn how to use the Mainframe. The learning curve can be less on a PC tool
than it can be on a Mainframe editor. If you want to do your editing on the
PC, you really do not want to port your code up to the Mainframe each time
you test and need to change something. Obviously, there will need to be
final testing on the Mainframe but hopefully, most of the *simple* logic
error will be handled before it gets up and you would be mostly testing the
JCL.

Brad Prothero
Clarica Life Insurance
Fargo, ND

No, we do not talk like the movie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E34440.A784F19F@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> I don't really understand the advantage in having compatible CoBOL
> compilers on the PC and Mainframe and porting the code across.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why do you want to compile on a PC code which will run on a mainframe?

Speaking from personal experience I found it more frustrating to upload my
source code to the mainframe, compile it, switch back to the PC to fix the
inevitable bugs, upload it again, compile it again, repeat until all
compilation bugs are fixed, test run it, switch back to the PC to fix the
inevitable logic bugs, upload it again, compile it again, switch back to the
PC to fix the new bugs, upload & compile it again (usually only took 1 loop
this time), test it again, etc. Than to download the data files onto the PC,
test the logic there, then upload to the mainframe and re-test.

Sure, there were a few occasions where something worked differently on the
mainframe than on the PC, but those were pretty rare because I use pretty
standard code. 'Course we ended up switching entirely to PC & don't do
anything on the mainframe any more. We still have some remnants of JCL
because using a JCL emulator for job control made the porting easier but not
necessarily cleaner. I'd like to look at other job control options, but I'm
having all I can do to convince the newcomers that COBOL's still worth
learning. They seem to think the reason I can write a program in half the
time it takes them is because of my experience, not because I'm using COBOL
while they're using VB or C++.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E35B44.CAD39C9D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net> <39E34440.A784F19F@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> Speaking from personal experience I found it more frustrating to upload my
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> test the logic there, then upload to the mainframe and re-test.

So my question continues - why is your source code on your PC in the
first place?

My mainframe editor has CoBOL highlighting, and it is very easy to cut
and paste code from other programs which are already on the mainframe. 
Furthermore, if I get run over by a lottery, other workers will find my
current source.

Every editor has its advantages and disadvantages.  But for code, I love
having a line oriented editor with a powerful exclude command.

I like working on the machine which contains the current copy members
(or Object source code).  I figure that having copies of code on my PC
leaves me vulnerable when I assume it is up to date.  I like being able
to look at real data instantly instead of what I pulled off for my early
tests.  I like moving my disk space threshold up for testing.
```

I did work in one environment where we stored all of our source code on

Howard Brazee wrote:

> Brad Prothero wrote:
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> access there.

I can see where you're coming from now, and this *is* a problem here, but not a
big one because we're basically a small town. The furthest anyone lives is about
20 minutes drive from the office when the weather is nice. (When the weather's bad
we might not be able to drive here at all.) We don't have dialup access to our
LAN, but we do to one of our PC's. When we want to work at home we check out the
source code we'll be using and store it on that PC. If we forget to check out all
of the code, or if the work is due to an emergency, well, we just have to come
back into the office. No big deal since we live so close & we've all got keys.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E37B47.DF87914A@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net> <39E34440.A784F19F@dced.state.ak.us> <39E35B44.CAD39C9D@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> tests.  I like moving my disk space threshold up for testing.

First, something I apparently didn't make clear is that this was implemented as a
shop standard. *All* developers pulled source code (including copy members) from
libraries on our LAN and returned the updated source code (including copy members)
when they were done. I don't think this kind of system could be implemented very
successfully as a personal idiosyncracy.

Second, the reason we moved the source code to the PC is because the PC-based
editor was more efficient and it was enough more efficient that it offset most of
the extra effort involved in keeping the source code current. (Yes, we had a few
occasions where the debugging changes were made on the mainframe and not returned
to the library, but people learned. Oh, yes, those who stayed did indeed learn.) I
don't know how much the expectation of moving processing to the PC drove moving
the source code or whether we moved the source code and then realized we could
also move processing. I wasn't involved in the higher level planning at that time.
I just know that we moved the source code first then, after a couple of years,
moved the processing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E496E5.51689FD4@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net> <39E34440.A784F19F@dced.state.ak.us> <39E35B44.CAD39C9D@brazee.net> <39E37B47.DF87914A@dced.state.ak.us> <8s06cv0lkk@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Possibly-the environment was called ROSCOE. The worst part was performance. I got
tired of using the search function to find text that I knew was only a few screens
down, but unless I *knew* that what I was looking for was within 4 screens it was
faster to search than to page down. On the PC I can page thru most entire programs
faster than invoking the search.

There were similar problems all over the mainframe, both in performance and
featureset. Change control's about as easy on the PC as on the mainframe, you just
need to implement it. The biggest difference is that we're in charge of our LAN
whereas we were 'users' on the mainframe.

Jeff Baynard wrote:

> That must have been one horrible editor on your mainframe.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> learn.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol on my PC

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rvqfo$gbv$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8rt341$b18$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <8ru5ko$72t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39E31A95.FA6A3F40@brazee.net>`

```
Howard,
  I don't think ANYONE would (today) suggest editing on the PC - but doing
all testing on the mainframe.  As someone who worked for BOTH a mainframe
debugging vendor (ADS - who developed and distributed Xpediter) and a "PC
vendor" Micro Focus (developer and distributor of Workbench - and then
Mainframe Express), I can tell you "pros and cons" of both environments -
for development and maintenance.

Note1:  Both companies have changed names/owners since I worked for them.
Today, see Compuware and MERANT.

Note2:  There are now products that do "remote debugging" which allows you
to use a PC "front-end" to testing of programs running on an IBM mainframe.
This note doesn't (directly) deal with those options.

The bottom-line is that it varies based on company environment and
individual preference as to WHICH is a "better" environment for you/your
company.

Doing ALL development up to and thru "unit testing" on the PC *may* be more
productive if you shop either has significant resource constraints or
bureaucracy.  As mentioned in another note, working on the PC means that you
don't have to go thru "systems programmers" to set up CICS, IMS, or DB2
"tables".  You can do them for yourself and modify them - quickly and
(semi-)easily as requirements change - or as your "design" needs
modification.  If you are in an environment where "turn-around" for compile
and tests can be slow - or where "foreground debuggers" are prohibited
during "normal work hours" - then doing development on the PC can REALLY
save time and frustration.

OTOH, doing development/testing/maintenance on the PC requires SOMEONE to
set up a "mirror" environment for your mainframe.  In some shops, this is
more of a problem than the benefits that you gain.  If your shop does NOT
have "good" test data - but instead uses copies of multi-gig files
(databases) for testing, then you will probably have problems getting them
on the PC (LAN) much less multiple copies for each developer or group of
developers. Similarly, if you don't have a "good" source control system, it
is possible that your PC copies of copylib members can cause you serious
problems.  There are also issues of "converting" lengthy/complex JCL streams
to PC equivalents; some products include JCL emulators or converters - but
certainly NOT all PC products.

It is ABSOLUTELY true (IMHO) that you MUST do your "final testing" on the
mainframe itself.  However, for shops that work with and gain experiences
with PC development environments, it is possible to gain and maintain
confidence in doing all your development THRU unit testing on the PC.
```

### Animating Dialog system

- **From:** "Fernando Kvistgaard" <fek@lr.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T08:15:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rrnpn$7qd$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
I use Net ExpressIit 3.1 and Dialog. Is it possible to animate/debug a COBOL
program which is called from a Dialog screenset with CALLOUT? and how ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Animating Dialog system

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rs1q8$r5u$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8rrnpn$7qd$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Fernando,

If you animate the main calling program the CALLOUT modules will also be
animated when you get to them.

You could also try adding a CALL "CBL_DEBUGBREAK" to your code.  When
this is executed you are asked if you want to start the animator.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

### i need help to modify my cobol program can someone help soon please

- **From:** gz11375@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rssrd$72v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have written a cobol program, but it needs changes by looking at the
question, i don't know how to add those changes in it, can someone
please help me make these changes in the program, by looking at the
question, and email me soon i need this by thursday or so.
here is the question:
Given two related dates,(eg: start and end of the World War !!) find
the number od days, the number of months and the number of
years/months/days between the two dates.

*If it is interactive, submit your disk with the correct .int file on
it.
If you are using files, include a copy of both the input and output
files

here is my program:
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  Y2K.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DATE-DATA.
           02  DATE-INTEGER        PIC 9(6).
           02  DATE-INPUT.
               03  DATE-Y          PIC 9(4).
               03  DATE-M          PIC 99.
               03  DATE-D          PIC 99.
           02  DATE-RDF REDEFINES DATE-INPUT PIC 9(8).
           02  LAST-DATE-SW        PIC X.
               88  LAST-DATE       VALUE 'n' 'N'.
           02  DATE-E              PIC 9(8).
           02  DATE-EX.
               03  YY              PIC 9(4).
               03  MM              PIC 99.
               03  DD              PIC 99.
           02  DATE-EDIT.
               03  MM              PIC Z9/.
               03  DD              PIC Z9/.
               03  YY              PIC 9999.
           02  NUM-DAYS            PIC S999.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       CONVERT-DATE.
           MOVE 'Y' TO LAST-DATE-SW
           PERFORM GET-DATE
               PERFORM UNTIL LAST-DATE
                   COMPUTE DATE-INTEGER =
                       FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(DATE-RDF)
                   END-COMPUTE
                   DISPLAY DATE-INTEGER
                   DISPLAY 'HOW MANY DAYS TO MATURITY ?'
                       WITH NO ADVANCING
                   ACCEPT NUM-DAYS
                   ADD NUM-DAYS TO DATE-INTEGER
                   END-ADD
                   DISPLAY DATE-INTEGER
                   COMPUTE DATE-E = FUNCTION
                       DATE-OF-INTEGER(DATE-INTEGER)
                   DISPLAY DATE-E
                   MOVE DATE-E TO DATE-EX
                   MOVE CORRESPONDING DATE-EX TO DATE-EDIT
                   DISPLAY DATE-EDIT
                   DISPLAY 'CONTINUE ?  (Y/N)' WITH NO ADVANCING
                   ACCEPT LAST-DATE-SW
                   IF NOT LAST-DATE PERFORM GET-DATE
                   END-IF
               END-PERFORM
            STOP RUN
            .

       GET-DATE.
           DISPLAY 'ENTER YEAR IN YYYY FORMAT  '
               WITH NO ADVANCING
           ACCEPT DATE-Y
           DISPLAY 'ENTER MONTH IN MM FORMAT   '
               WITH NO ADVANCING
           ACCEPT DATE-M
           DISPLAY 'ENTER DAY IN DD FORMAT     '
               WITH NO ADVANCING
           ACCEPT DATE-D
           .

If someone can help please email me gz11375@aol.com
thanks


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: COBOL Help- not a hw question — 8 msgs

### COBOL Help- not a hw question

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T03:05:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001009230544.21103.00001424@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
As i have stated many times before i am still new to COBOL, but would like to
try to write my own program, but i am not sure where to start, i dont have a
major working concept of COBOL yet, but I understand what a medium portion of
code does. my question is does anyone know of something that would be kinda
simple to start for a newbie?

Thank you kindly,
Deek
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Help- not a hw question

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ru3t5$16r0$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<20001009230544.21103.00001424@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```

I would start simple to build confidence, then gradually add more functions,
therefore complexity. For instance, start by creating an input file with 1
record consisting of just 3 fields; a, b, and c.  Write a program to open
the input file and an output file, read the input file, add a to b giving c,
move the record to an output record area, write the record, close the files
and end the program.  Look at your output file to see if you got what you
expected.  If not, use displays in your program to debug where you went
wrong.  After success, add more logic like using perform statements,
evaluate statements, checking your input fields for valid values, or valid
ranges, etc.  Keep adding COBOL verbs until eventually, you've used ALL of
them.  As you add each new statement, study it's syntax in the programming
reference manual to fully understand what it does.  The key is to start
simple and gradually add complexity.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Help- not a hw question

- **From:** William Zhang <uniware@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8rujh5$jji$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20001009230544.21103.00001424@ng-ce1.aol.com> <8ru3t5$16r0$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```

functions,

with 1

open

giving c,

files

you
> expected.  If not, use displays in your program to debug where you
went

valid
> ranges, etc.  Keep adding COBOL verbs until eventually, you've used
ALL of
> them.  As you add each new statement, study it's syntax in the
programming
> reference manual to fully understand what it does.  The key is to
start
> simple and gradually add complexity.
>
> --
>

When I began to learn COBOL, I did almost the same thing as above.
And another way is to read small program from others, then modify
it and add your own. Any discussion with your classmate, friend, or
colleage is helpful.
```

> As i have stated many times before i am still new to COBOL, but would
like to
> try to write my own program, but i am not sure where to start, i dont
have a
> major working concept of COBOL yet, but I understand what a medium
portion of
> code does. my question is does anyone know of something that would be
kinda
> simple to start for a newbie?
>
> Thank you kindly,
> Deek

Hi:

Try doing something you will have to do in the real world like a
simple report program. "Hello world" is not the sort of program
you would get paid to write. Besides, I think you are beyond that
stage.

Here are the specs.

You have a sequential file (which you can create with your editor)
in sequence by employee number PIC 9(03) in cols 1-3. Columns 4-8
contain the hours-worked PIC 999v99. (I would not suggest
creating a file with an editor in a production system, for your
purposes you can).

The program has to read the file and print totals by employee. There
can be many records per employee. This will introduce you to the
classic control break problem.  Total lines contain employee number
and the total hours. Grand total at the end. Page number on the
reports - skip to new page after 55 lines. You will have to break
control on employee number. You might as well sequence check the
file to make sure it is in sequence - put this in the control break
part of the program. This is real world stuff.

I also recommend drawing a flow chart before you start to code. I
don't know if they teach you flowcharting in school or not. I am
inclined to believe it is a dead art - try it to see what you might
learn.

Things to consider are: a file with no data in it; a file with only
one record in it; no file in existence, etc. These things will
happen in the real world and you should prepare for it.

Be sure to include file-stats checks, you never know what will
happen next in the real world.

If you use this, feel free to ask further questions.


Thanks

Tony dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Deek

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Help- not a hw question

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T04:37:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f0xE5.23108$Ly1.315234@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20001009230544.21103.00001424@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```

On  9-Oct-2000, deek40@aol.com (Deek40) wrote:

> As i have stated many times before i am still new to COBOL, but would like
> to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> simple to start for a newbie?

Try the classic "hello world".

Write a program to display "hello world".  When that's working, change the
program to print "hello world" on a printer or in a file.  Then, just keep
embellishing on the code until you have Quicken!  Simple, really...

Seriously, as Terry said, do one line, then keep at it!

Cliff
```

I always recommend The CICS Application Programming Primer as a good
place for a COBOL programmer to start.

For discussion forums (fora?) there's either the newsgroup
bit.listserv.cics-l or the CICS-L mailing list at  CICS-L@VM.MARIST.EDU.
To subscribe send an email to LISTSERV@VM.MARIST.EDU with SUBSCRIBE
CICS-L YOURNAMEHERE as the subject.  If you'd like a flavour of the
discussion list the archive is held at
http://www.marist.edu/htbin/wlvindex?CICS-L

## Thread: How do I support IClassFactory2 (NetExpress) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I support IClassFactory2 (NetExpress)

- **From:** "Neil Hayes" <NeilH@syspro.co.za>
- **Date:** 2000-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e2c74c$0$229@helios.is.co.za>`

```
I've created a few COM components using NetExpress 3.1 and one important
issue is licensing of dll's to prevent pirating etc.

Reading several articles the recommended method is supporting IClassFactory2
preventing the user creating the object if no license is found, if a license
is found but not valid the user will receive CLASS_E_NOTLICENSED.

How do I implement this 'feature', if someone has a sample I would be
grateful.

If anyone has any other good suggestions for protecting the dll, I'd be
happy to hear from you.

Regards

Neil
NeilH@syspro.co.za
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: Edit VSAM? — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Edit VSAM?

- **From:** petert@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T06:22:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Is there any easy way to edit/update a VSAM KSDS without writing a cobol
program?  I just want to change 1 byte.......
Thanks
Peter


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** tscoffey@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s1j66$p8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  petert@my-deja.com wrote:
> Is there any easy way to edit/update a VSAM KSDS without writing a
cobol
> program?  I just want to change 1 byte.......
> Thanks
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you have FileAid, you can use it.

You could perhaps do it with SyncSort.
Use 1 sort to separate the record(s) you want to change from the ones
you don't. Use a second to edit the changing records. Use a third
to re-combine the file by key. Then do an IDCAMS reload.

To use a VSAM KSDS as SyncSort input, you need to tell SyncSort
what the record type and length are.
RECORD TYPE=F,LENGTH=xxxx   for a KSDS with fixed record size
RECORD TYPE=V,LENGTH=(max,,,min,average) for a KSDS with variable
recsize.


Tim


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UKYE5.587$8k7.19177@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
File-Aid can do this (this is the easy way).

An EzTrieve Program to change one byte wouldn't be too bad.

I don't know REXX, but I would not be surprised if a 'change one byte'
script is pretty easy.

Or, you could always IDCAMS/REPRO the KSDS to a QSAM file, edit that with
the TSO Editor, and reload the KSDS with another IDCAMS/REPRO. (DFSORT and
SyncSort could be used to replace IDCAMS/REPRO in this scenario).

Probably some other ways, too.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T02:29:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E521DC.4D8A3B69@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <UKYE5.587$8k7.19177@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> File-Aid can do this (this is the easy way).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> script is pretty easy.

While REXX is a terrific language (IMHO, anyway), it doesn't have a VSAM
interface.

Someone suggested REPROing a record into a flat file, editing it (in
ISPF), then REPROing it back into the file. Sounds like a workable
solution in a low volume situation.

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m8hF5.25$7%6.21225@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <UKYE5.587$8k7.19177@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39E521DC.4D8A3B69@optonline.net>`

- **From:** dallasgard@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-31T18:46:53+00:00

- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <UKYE5.587$8k7.19177@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39E521DC.4D8A3B69@optonline.net>`

```
In article <39E521DC.4D8A3B69@optonline.net>,
  LiamD@optoffline.not wrote:
> Michael Mattias wrote:
> >
> > File-Aid can do this (this is the easy way).
> >
> > An EzTrieve Program to change one byte
wouldn't be too bad.
> >
> > I don't know REXX, but I would not be
surprised if a 'change one byte'
> > script is pretty easy.
>
> While REXX is a terrific language (IMHO,
anyway), it doesn't have a VSAM

Here is something I did using SYNCSORT to copy a
flat file to an IAM file (VSAM replacement)
converting ZD to PD with a workaround, using CASE
commands, to pack a 16 character Cardholder
number when neither File-aid nor SYNCSORT would
handle a numeric field greater than 15 bytes. The
CASE feature could easily be used to translate
one value to another.

 SORT FIELDS=COPY
 OUTFIL FILES=1,
 OUTREC=(1,10,ZD,PD,12,5,ZD,PD,18,1,
         CHANGE=(1,C'1',X'01',
         C'2',X'02',
         C'3',X'03',
         C'4',X'04',
         C'5',X'05',
         C'6',X'06',
         C'7',X'07',
         C'8',X'08',
         C'9',X'09',
         C'0',X'00'),
         19,15,ZD,PD)

> interface.
>
> Someone suggested REPROing a record into a flat
file, editing it (in
> ISPF), then REPROing it back into the file.
Sounds like a workable
> solution in a low volume situation.
>
> LiamD
>



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

petert@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Is there any easy way to edit/update a VSAM KSDS without writing a cobol

Third party tools allow you to edit KSDS.  But you can REPRO the file to
a flat file, edit it, then REPRO it back.

#### ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** "Seekermike" <seeker@dwx.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<39e4e801@news.dwx.com>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Be very careful if you REPRO a VSAM file to DASD and use ISPF option 3.4 to
edit it!  Truncation of the rightmost bytes can occur!  I got bit by this
last week.  From now on, I'm using Fileaid to edit my files.


<petert@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Is there any easy way to edit/update a VSAM KSDS without writing a cobol
> program?  I just want to change 1 byte.......
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

done by now.

if..then is fine for me.

Jeff

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E5BB3E.17EB828A@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8s2v4s017sv@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> It takes all of what, 20-30 minutes to code, compile, and run a COBOL
> program to do something like this.

Good point but...
 
> In the time it took you to post the message, get a response, etc. you'd be
> done by now.
> 
> We have File-Aid.  Used it once, will never use it again.  Displays and
> if..then is fine for me.

When I had it, I used it a lot.  It takes a minute to look at and/or
change a file, instead of the 20-30 minutes to create a program.  And
when I want to adjust my test data a different way, it takes me another
minute, instead of another 10-15 minutes to modify my program.

So he uses a minute writing his request (and if he's reading the
newsgroup anyway, the only other cost is having a couple of replies he's
interested in).  And from now on, he might know a way to modify his data
in 5 minutes instead of the 20-30 minute way he knows how to do now. 
(Next time he may want a more complicated edit/change)
```

get.  This assumes you know the key of the record you want to change
and you have security to run CECI.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T02:16:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E6706F.FE4CAA5A@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<8s10vf$j0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8s2v4s017sv@enews4.newsguy.com> <39E522C3.C711993A@optonline.net> <04C9F2FA10AA671A.228EDE83C35E31D3.B331D205D7A2C450@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 12 Oct 2000 02:33:00 GMT, Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> and you have security to run CECI.

It's also possible to write a utility program which performs certain
simple & common repairs, e.g., marking the control record for an async
transaction that abended "complete", so it can be restarted. I'd much
rather do this than mess around with CECI and be a byte off, or
something. If you feel like it, you can also make your utility a little
more general purpose (which I have not done - the compliance people
aren't happy with the above, but we can justify it and demonstrate that
we do not have the ability to modify any financial data - if it were
general purpose they'd be in orbit). 

As always, YMMV,
LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Edit VSAM?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T03:14:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001011231444.03174.00002183@ng-fh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<39E522C3.C711993A@optonline.net>`

```
We have Fileaid - and just had a 3 hr free tutorial from CA on it as well - and
lots of free neato manuals.  I love it.

In any event....

If that 1 byte is part of the key (assuming a KSDS file) then you'd be better
off doing the IDCAMS REPRO to a flat file, change the byte, SORT it, and the
REPRO it back.

VSAM KSDS gets very upset if you don't have things in the proper order.


As for the DISPLAY question with CICS.  I believe it will work, think we had a
few programs with that in it (gotta check on this) and it goes to the console
assuming that's how the region is configured.  Definately not to any printers. 
Usually use the write to console command instead.  We have some very strange
CICS programs.

Eileen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: XML Parser

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T01:13:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CktF5.120750$dZ2.54174156@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<bMACFHALSK55Ew3Q@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <WG2F5.113991$dZ2.50699178@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <zkNIfAA$7i55EwQ2@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Since you won't tell me where you're running, the answers are:

For OO COBOL you can probably use Microsoft's XML parser, assuming your
compiler can interact with C++ objects.
(http://msdn.microsoft.com/xml/default.asp). There are also Java parsers
available. from Apache, Sun and others if that helps.

There's a product for the mainframe called XML4COBOL
(http://www.xml4cobol.com) which creates assembler code to parse specific
documents based on a DTD. I haven't looked at it in a while so I don't know
if it has kept up with development.

I mucked with a SAX-compliant parser for COBOL for a while before I decided
it was easier to parse the XML in some other language, like Java, and
restructure it into a form that was more readily digestible by COBOL.

Karl


"Alistair Maclean" <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:zkNIfAA$7i55EwQ2@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> Cobol (I think)
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> you find out how strong they are.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBMCOBOL and LE ABEND

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<39E48387.2EE194F4@generali.it>`

```

TRACE, DUMP, UADUMP or UATRACE. If not, try rerunning with one of those options
and tell us what you get.

When I try to compile it, I receive an avalanche of error?

Alain.

#### ↳ Re: Using the Fujitsu COBOL v 3

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)

Take a look at Thane Hubbell's Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 hours.  In the
early chapters he goes through the process of editing, compiling, linking, 
and executing a program using Fujitsu v. 3.  Later chapters add more
complexity - including the debugger and multiple compilation units.

Floyd Johnson

#### ↳ Re: Using the Fujitsu COBOL v 3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Other responses have suggested other problems.  My guess is that you
downloaded/created your source code withOUT "CRLF" characters.  All (???) PC
compilers require source code to be
 A) ASCII (not EBCDIC)
 B) "Line sequential" files (files with CR-LF characters separating lines)

How did you create/get your source code?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using the Fujitsu COBOL v 3

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Bill might just be right.  Go to a DOS prompt and use EDIT on your
file:

EDIT OBSERVE.COB 

Then save it.  Don't just exit - make sure to SAVE.

This will insert the CR LF's where only LF's existed before.  (Or is
it only CR's, I can't recall).

I believe that it might be helpful to look at the compile option
SRF to specify the source format.  by default it is SRF=VAR,VAR
and works for most situations.
I have encountered some instances where I use SRF=FIX,FIX
for code downloaded from the mainframe.
```

## Thread: C++ is way better than Cobol — 21 msgs

### C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Barry" <bazza44@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T10:26:36+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
I've read that C++ is superior to Cobol. Is it?
```

#### ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fvwF5.24652$UP5.397663@news6.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```

"Barry" <bazza44@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au...
> I've read that C++ is superior to Cobol. Is it?

I've heard that Volvo station wagons are superior to RCA televisions.

My point is that while both are items that can be compared, they are not
interchangeable.  I would not be interested in writing a compiler in COBOL.
Neither would I be interested in writing a business application in C++.

Cliff
```

A WhatEver?    ...

Herwig

> programmers would prefer Cobol, the other

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s70d3$fol$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <fvwF5.24652$UP5.397663@news6.giganews.com>`

```
Your comparson is just a little exagerated. You could write a business
application in C++ and you could write a compiler in COBOL, but you
could not drive a RCA television cross-country and you can't watch
Seinfield on the winshield of a Volvo. IMHO, COBOL would be better if
it adopted some of the advantages of (effective pointer manipulation
and user defined functions come to mind) C++, but I don't feel the
reverse is true. COBOL is old (although very effective for applicatin
work) and has much crust on it, but this may change in the next 50
years.

UMMV.


In article <fvwF5.24652$UP5.397663@news6.giganews.com>,
  "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net> wrote:

not

COBOL.
> Neither would I be interested in writing a business application in
C++.
>
> Cliff
>
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ayEF5.13580$ML5.84299@news4.atl>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <fvwF5.24652$UP5.397663@news6.giganews.com> <8s70d3$fol$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8s70d3$fol$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Your comparson is just a little exagerated. You could write a business
> application in C++ and you could write a compiler in COBOL, but you
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> years.

In general, C/C++ is very poor for handling money, because it has no
native scaled decimal math.  True, objects could be written to do
this, but even then it would be clumsy, probably inefficient, and
may not be reliable, compared to the built in and well tested native
compiler routines.

It is my opinion that people who want to add features like explicit
pointers to COBOL misapprehend one of COBOL's primary strengths:
its simplicity.  Using pointers can be very subtle and fraught with
danger.  I suspect there are probably more bugs in C/C++ code related
to pointers than all other things put together.  While subscripts and
indexes aren't as widely flexible as pointers, they are far safer to
use, and much less subtle.  I've yet to write an application in COBOL
(and I have written quite a few widely varied COBOL applications,
including a compiler and p-code interpreter) where I felt the need for
explicit pointers.  Simplicity = fewer bugs = less cost + better
reliability.  Windows is written mostly in C/C++, and many heavy duty
Windows applications are written in C/C++.  What is your own personal
experience with the reliability of those, versus applications written
in COBOL?  How many 25 year old C applications do you know of that are
still running? ;-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<phNF5.366$Zb7.86928@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <8s5uqc$ja$1@usenet.nau.edu>`

```

"JamesK" <aout@jamesk.net> wrote in message
news:8s5uqc$ja$1@usenet.nau.edu...
> What would be some functional advantages that COBOL has over C++.

1. The time it takes to complete a job execution. Here I'm thinking of
processing the 20-million record Reader's Digest subscription list or
generating 50 million Social Security checks.
2. Ease of program maintenance.
3. A potential trained workforce maybe 50 times greater to draw upon.


There is a need for C++ programmers, though. Many legacy C++ programs
are being converted to COBOL and bi-lingual programmers are in demand.
```

#### ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39e71401.2472590@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
"Barry" <bazza44@netscape.net> wrote:

>I've read that C++ is superior to Cobol. Is it?
>
>

Barry:

I've read that a North American Aerospace F-14 Tomcat is superior to
an Aerospatial Airbus 340.......except when the task is to haul
several hundred people across the North Atlantic.

I've also heard that a wrench (spanner to my British pals) is superior
to a hammer.......except when the task is to drive nails into wood.

Barry....think about it....doesn't it make sense that perhaps certain
programming languages were designed for specific purposes?  To say
that one language is superior to another is like saying that red is
superior to green.  If you're painting a fire truck, perhaps that's
true, but I wouldn't want my lawn to be red.

...Use the right tool for the task at hand.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener!@!fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s7551$3be$1@news.mch.sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <39e71401.2472590@news.epix.net>`

```
2000-10-13 16:04:01 MESZ

Bob Wolfe schrieb:
>
> ...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> true, but I wouldn't want my lawn to be red.

Ada was designed to replace some 600 languages used in
military applications. About every area of programming
was taken into consideration. Also, one has to love Ada
because the Army says you have to.

However, there are clearly areas of application where I
would recommend other languages. And always keep in mind
that existing training and existing skills may influence
the choice of language.

I know that Microfocus implemented their Cobol-Compiler
in Cobol. I myself would never consider Cobol for
compiler-construction - on the other hand, writing
a compiler in its own language provides a major testcase.
Might be that writing the thing in Cobol was a great idea
- or wasn't it? Anyone here from MF who can comment
on that? - Just curious.

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

DOD now says that you don't have to use Ada or at least the Air Force the
last time I heard.
One solution for every situation normally proves to be wrong in the long
run.  That is why we have options everywhere.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** mangogrower <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F1F440.DD494B25@telocity.com>`
- **References:** `<8s5rt0$jsc$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <39e71401.2472590@news.epix.net> <8s7551$3be$1@news.mch.sbs.de>`

```
I always laugh when I hear the claim that 'we wrote our compiler in the new
xxx language'.

That's like saying, This chicken hatched incubated itself as an egg!

It just aint so.
BTW, its not the Army but  the Department Of Defense that CREATED ADA
in 1985 (+/- 2rys).  However they did screw up one thing:  the standard
does not address
what to do with the cursor after a 'put.text'.  It is up to the compiler
vendor to decide
whether the cursor stays at the end of the line or performs a CR / CR/LF.


Herwig Huener wrote:

> 2000-10-13 16:04:01 MESZ
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Herwig
```

I'm reminded of a question asked by a student at the *end* of an
"Introduction to Computers" class given at a local vendor's sales office by
a friend some twenty years ago.  The class was geared to the owners and
high-level managers of small companies about to automate some aspect of
their accounting for the first time.  As I recall it, the class ran at least
a couple of days.

The question was "Is COBOL faster than disk?".

       -Chuck Stevens

- **Message-ID:** `<8s7fli$n0j$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

in response to the question asked by another

<<"Is COBOL faster than disk?">>

Alas, 'twas not I that was asked.  I believe his response was a stunned

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Comparison

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E73B7B.C0163B5E@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8s39m7$90g$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <8s52pa$4du$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> The question was "Is COBOL faster than disk?".
> 
>        -Chuck Stevens

Sounds funny at first.  (I am assuming they are not thinking of being
I/O bound)

But as I think more it is a good question - whether to those managers or
to ISS people.  A business has two primary measurements for its
decisions:

1.  True/False:  We are complying with the law and government
regulations.
2.  What are the monetary costs and benefits of this choice.

Sometimes the choices are to make very efficient code, efficient use of
hardware, efficient maintenance, efficient upgrading, flexibility in
upgrading, ability to find good people, ability to keep good people,
ability to get a working tool to the money makers quickly, etc.

Measure your choice to use CoBOL in $$$ with criteria such as those in
mind.  Measure upgrading your disks the same way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Comparison

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s7kmj$sg1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8s39m7$90g$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <8s52pa$4du$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <39E73B7B.C0163B5E@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:39E73B7B.C0163B5E@brazee.net...

> Sounds funny at first.  (I am assuming they are not thinking of being
> I/O bound)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> decisions:  ...

Ummm ... As I indicated earlier, this class wasn't geared to a very high
level of expertise in either business management or in IS technologies.
This was way before the Personal Computer revolution, and these
businessfolks were attempting to begin the process of considering the
possibility of automating *some* part of their accounting mechanisms, and to
figure out "What's a computer?  What does RAM mean?  What's a utility (and
how does one buy stock in it)?  How does a printer work?" and so forth.

Back then, the Personal Computer hadn't really taken off.  If you were
beyond the pen-and-ink stage, you either used a service bureau or you had
mag-stripe ledger machines from Burroughs or Monroe Computometer or NCR.
The folks in question were migrating from the mag-stripe level to the
files-on-disk level of automation to gain better control of their business
and better access to cash flow information.

These were shops like a family-run wholesale novelty distributor (daddy
bought the computer at sonny's insistence), a local long-haul truck
distributor, a construction equipment rental firm (also family-run, being
dragged into the Automation Age by two sons of the owner), and so forth.
The larger ones among them might have had as many as twenty or thirty
employees total ...

But those were different times.   Today, a similar-size business could
probably do just as much (and probably a lot more) on a minimum-price PC
running something like QuickBooks than they were able to accomplish with
home-grown (or even store-bought) code on a smaller mainframe back then.
And a WHOLE lot less expensively.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

Daryl,

Erm, not sure how to answer this one - it is a bit like asking "how long
is a piece of string" <g>.

You may be better off by defining what you want to write with your
computer language.  You can write almost anything is most languages
but that does not mean it is the thing to do!

Modeern COBOL is a very feature rich language and supports things
like pointers, OO, SQL etc. etc.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Since version 5, that is not a problem any more. When I as with version 4,
what I used to do was to write whatever words I needed with accented
characters in Notepad, then copy/paste them into the cobol editor. That way
it worked fine, since the compiler accepts them. It is o+nly the editor that
has the problem.

Regards,

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft

<a id="t-6ac0e031f4"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 3 error — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu 3 error

- **From:** "Daryl" <rs2000@bestofperth.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T11:18:55+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s3aj2$95m$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
When i try to link the object file i get this error
NMake: fatal error V1077: 'C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x68'
 also
LINK: fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
Can anyone help?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 3 error

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2000-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s4d25$7e1$1@ins21.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<8s3aj2$95m$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
You will need to set a compiler option that states that your program is
MAIN.  This is to distinguish it from supporting files that are compiled
separately.  To do this add the following line to your code beginning in
column 8:

	@OPTION MAIN

This must be the very first line of your program and must be all
caps.  Because this a compiler directive, and not a COBOL statement, case
does matter.

I think this is in the fact; but if not, it is covered in Chapter 2 of
Thane Hubbell's book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours".

Good luck.

Floyd Johnson

:  also

: Can anyone help?

compile
a subroutine, which will not link as a main program. I have not used Version

problem. If
you are using Pstaff, it should be in the menus somewhere.

<a id="t-13ba6b6448"></a>
## Thread: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol — 55 msgs

### A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Matt Wieben" <mw5@dana.ucc.nau.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu>`

```
What would be some functional advantages that COBOL has over C++?
```

#### ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu>`

```

Matt Wieben <mw5@dana.ucc.nau.edu> wrote in message
news:8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu...
> What would be some functional advantages that COBOL has over C++?
>

How about:

1)  Anything invloving financial calculations, especially a great deal of
precise calculations...
2)  Processing time in large runs (runs involving millions of records)...
3)  A lot easier to read/maintain...
4)  Larger work force...
5)  Easier to learn...

C++ has it's functions and so does COBOL.  Anymore, there are actually more
and more systems converting back to COBOL...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T05:04:58+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`

```
Is anyone aware of *evidence*, as opposed to opinion, that COBOL
programs are easier to comprehend than C programs, for someone
who knows the language well. 

I know both COBOL and C well and I find no difference at all. To
me the key things that make programs readable are:

- meaningful data and procedure names.
- documentation on interfaces and data structures. 

But is "ADD A TO B GIVING C" better than "c=a+b"? 

In C you can see more on the screen at one time because it is
usually more concise, but some things are hard in C like
financial calculations, etc. 

Anyone who thinks COBOL is automatically self documenting should
have a look at the code at 
http://www.nist.gov/itl/div897/ctg/cobol_form.htm as an example.
Some of it is well and truly obfuscated.

Tim Josling

DBuck wrote:

> 3)  A lot easier to read/maintain...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2ga920.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au>`

```
On Sun, 15 Oct 2000 05:04:58 +1100, Tim Josling wrote:

>Is anyone aware of *evidence*, as opposed to opinion, that COBOL
>programs are easier to comprehend than C programs, for someone
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think that in general a COBOL program is easier to understand than a C
program. Of course, you can write COBOL programs that are difficult to
understand, but the average C program is more difficult to read.
I use both COBOL and C, and I have never seen a COBOL program I couldn't
figure out what it was doing, but C programs..... Just to illustrate, here is
a little gem

#include<stdio.h>
main()
{
   
      long a,b,c,d,e,f,g[8401];
for(a=10000, b=0, c=8400; b-c;g[b++]=a/5);
   for (e=0,f=0; d=0, f=c*2; c-=14,printf("%04d",e+d/a),e=d%a)  {
      for (b=c;d+=g[b]*a,g[b]=d%--f,d/=f--,--b;d*=b ); {
      } /* endfor */
   } /* endfor */

Without running it, I don't think it's easy to find out what this program
does
Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8saind$ni6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2ga920.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
One of the issues that you need to "think about" when comparing
maintainability/readability of COBOL vs C (or C++) is what happens when you
(or someone else) reads a program written by ANOTHER programmer - not when
reading your own code (although even that can be hard to understand after a
while).  I believe (but can't provide references at the moment) that both
IBM and MERANT have some statistics (available to customers) supporting what
happens when "equally experienced" COBOL and C (C++) programmers are given
"equally well written" programs created by another programmer - and asked to
make a "minor" enhancement.

As stated elsewhere, it is certainly possible to write "bad" COBOL programs
and "good" C (C++) programs - but there is (I believe) some evidence that
the "self-documenting" constrains in COBOL *tend* to make equally
"qualified" COBOL programmers create "better" (as far as maintainability
goes) programs.  ON THE OTHER HAND,  I believe that there is *also* evidence
that these same "equally qualified" programmers tend to write "more
efficient" code in C (C++) than in COBOL (especially when "low-level"
machine interfaces are required - or you are talking about quick-running -
not long batch oriented programs).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EAFD15.B87370D5@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2ga920.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8saind$ni6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I believe that there is *also* evidence
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> not long batch oriented programs).

That has always been my assumption - but optimizers make a big
difference.  With C, the programmer is closer to the machine language. 
How does an optimizer know what you really want and what can be futzed
with?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EC2BDD.D7641512@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2ga920.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8saind$ni6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <39EAFD15.B87370D5@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> with?

It is truly amazing what they do with it.  You can write a program and it can
almost totally rewrite the code to make it more efficient.   For example
knowing that a variable does not change within the code and you have coded an
if statement inside a tight loop it moves it outside the loop.

They unroll loops,   so perform 3 times might be coded as three sequential
processes if that is more efficient.

C has some many more clues on what you are doing that it makes optimisation
easier.  If you pass a variable that is defined as a constant argument in the
interface definition then the optimiser knows that it can still work out it's
value because it has not and cannot change.

I do not think that you can beat the high end C optimisers.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<ih8G5.30041$Ly1.425627@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au>`

"Tim Josling" <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au...
> Anyone who thinks COBOL is automatically self documenting should
> have a look at the code at
> http://www.nist.gov/itl/div897/ctg/cobol_form.htm as an example.
> Some of it is well and truly obfuscated.

I've downloaded the COBOL you've mentioned.  Can you give me a specific
reference to what you find obfuscated?  While it isn't particularly well
structured (a matter of taste), I've seen a lot worse.

Some of the code would not pass formatting standards at most shops - things
like multiple sentences per line, paragraph names on the same line with
sentences, other things.  That's undisciplined programming.  But obfuscated?

COBOL may be self-documenting if you're a programmer, not a keyboard
plinker.   It will not, however, provide instant comprehension of a program
whose function you don't understand.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T04:31:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FqaG5.29667$tl2.1911442@bgtnsc07-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <ih8G5.30041$Ly1.425627@news5.giganews.com>`

```
This discussion left out some significant
information about the program linked to.

The program was developed over the life of COBOL by Navy Sailors working
for Hopper, and for a team in the National Bureau of Standards, and
Standards people in France and Germany to do what?

Make sure that COBOL compilers were STANDARD.  They did use what could be
considered bad practices because  a
COBOL compiler does not standardize the writing of programs, but the tools
with which the writing is done.  Obfuscated
in this case was a part of the system design.   Think about it.

BTW, I believe that there are standards for other languages, but I don't
know if they are verified in this manner.

IMHO, this group is highly qualified to
create a new program that does the same job, OR contribute to the
maintenance of this program.   I was impressed by the code. I've seen it in
earlier days. I hope someone(s) continue to update and run this program.

Warren Simmons

"Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:ih8G5.30041$Ly1.425627@news5.giganews.com...
>
> "Tim Josling" <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Some of the code would not pass formatting standards at most shops -
things
> like multiple sentences per line, paragraph names on the same line with
> sentences, other things.  That's undisciplined programming.  But
obfuscated?
>
> COBOL may be self-documenting if you're a programmer, not a keyboard
> plinker.   It will not, however, provide instant comprehension of a
program
> whose function you don't understand.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T07:49:43+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EA1867.C0721B28@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <ih8G5.30041$Ly1.425627@news5.giganews.com>`

```
Well as an example of obfuscated code, how about this (see at the
end)? I especially like WZ-MISCELLANEOUS. Perhaps one reason
others did not find it obfuscated and I did was that I was
actually trying to work out what it did so I could use it.
Superficially much of it is not obfuscated if you just scan
through it.

To understand that code, you are reduced to finding every
reference to all the switches to try and reverse engineer what
they mean. This is in the test driver, not one of the test
programs themselves, so the excuse of doing a torture test of
COBOL is not relevant. 

I believe that COBOL has a number of major advantages over other
languages, in that you have good suupport for common business
functions like financial calculations on large numbers, big
sorts, precise control over numeric accuracy, etc, plus you have
standard ways to use indexed files, etc. That and the fact that a
lot of talented and experienced programmers only know COBOL well
makes it worth writing a free compiler for it. 

But I am not convinced about the claim that COBOL is more
readable. The claim is often made but seldom backed up by
evidence. Bill Klein referred to studies that Merant etc have and
I will follow that up. But my own experience was that I found
COBOL harder to learn than Fortran and C, which may be in part
why I am sceptical that it is more readable, once you are past
the learning curve. And you do need to get past the learning
curve to understand all the rules like truncation, scope of
performs, what the magic level numbers mean etc.

Also why did I say "add a to b giving c" and not "compute c  =
a+b"?

A good question, although I would suggest that the 'add'
statement is more in keeping with the spirit of COBOL and the
'compute' is more C-like in that it uses symbols rather than
words to describe the operation.

I don't use compute because early on in my programming career, I
found that the precision of compute statements was very
unpredictable. IBM do have a description of the intermediate
precision in the back of the manual but I found often the
intermediate precision was not what I wanted and it was hard to
get a clear idea in your head of what was going to happen. And
the results are compiler dependent. So, no compute for me unless
precision was not an issue eg when dealing with whole integers. I
belive this issue is addressed in the later IBM compilers where
they have increased the intermediate precision, and also COBOL
2002 has new way to specify a higher degree of precision in
intermediate results, so it may be time to have another look. 

Another programming habit I picked up a while ago is to always
have "-1" on the end of data names, to avoid clashing with
reserved words. How do other people avoid the ever growing list
of reserved words?

Tim Josling

The compiler directive in the new COBOL standard allows this:

It does not allow

   >> add 17 to 4 giving bloob

Do I see a deviation from the old COBOL spirit here?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9GtG5.31715$Ly1.463753@news5.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <ih8G5.30041$Ly1.425627@news5.giganews.com> <39EA1867.C0721B28@melbpc.org.au> <39EA5875.CAB9D46A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```

"Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton"
<webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> wrote in message
news:39EA5875.CAB9D46A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de...
> 2000-10-16 03:22:30 MESZ
> I shall forward a heretic question.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do I see a deviation from the old COBOL spirit here?

Pile ADD-GIVING on the trash pile with DEPENDING and ALTER, the cornerstones
of COBOL's original spirit.  Good riddance.

BTW, the IBM VS COBOL II Application Programming Guide says:

"Symbolic Constants - If you want the optimizer to recognize a data item as
a constant throughout the program, initialize it with a VALUE clause and
don't modify it anywhere in the program."

So, "bloob pic 99 value 23" is a constant in the compiled code if you don't
change the value in the procedure division.  Damn, us COBOL folk were using
constant directives and didn't even know it!

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A better way to ask C++ is way better than Cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3uBG5.442$3U.16445@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8s6fkq$1f8$1@usenet.nau.edu> <8s8m9f$jooir$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <39E8A04A.A91CAF97@melbpc.org.au> <ih8G5.30041$Ly1.425627@news5.giganews.com> <39EA1867.C0721B28@melbpc.org.au> <39EA5875.CAB9D46A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <9GtG5.31715$Ly1.463753@news5.giganews.com> <39EAD38E.FD73564D@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:39EAD38E.FD73564D@zip.com.au...
> This might work but is not useful because it is not 'guaranteed'.  What
happens
> when boofhead programmer uses your constant as a temporary variable?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

With IBM COBOL/390, the optimizer treats it as a constant.

You can tell because this happens:

01  FILLER
     05 Foo           PIC X VALUE SPACE.
...

     IF Foo NOT EQUAL SPACE
        PERFORM whatever
     END-IF

...
Whatever.

Whatever-exit.


IGYxxxxx - I (W?)  Code from Whatever to whatever-exit cannot be reached and
was discarded.

No such message if Foo is changed in the program or is part of a READ or
READ INTO buffer.

That is one SMART optimizer!

MCM
```

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>

```
When I started programming I already had a math degree so
symbolic notation did not bother me (part of what seems to put a
lot of people off C). You can go too far with symbolic notation -
I can't understand Haskell programs even ones I wrote recently. 

I think the issue with COBOL was that COBOL is very different
from the other languages I new by then (Fortan, Algol). Lots of
new things like PIC, perform, and (in those days) a lack of
structured programming constructs (this was cobol 68).

Tim Josling
