program logic is never reached.  In fact, I have a one line procedure
division -- GOBACK -- and get the same abend/return code.

Curiouser and curiouser. No clues yet. Well, can you enter (or, hopefully,
cut-and-paste) some lines from your compile and link and a sample CALL from TSO
ready?

In particular, it would help to see:

* the //SYSIN DD statements from your compile step - where is the source coming
from
* the //SYSLMOD DD statement from your link step - where is the load module
going
* the //SYSLIB DD statement(s) from your link step - what link library /
libraries are being used
* any //SYSIN DD statement from your link step - did you have any linkedit
control statements?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 & COBOL: What's the secret to ...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001016142552.07742.00002178@ng-cf1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<39EB3634.907EFFE2@da.zoo.com>`

```
Hmmm. Be careful what you ask for - you might get it.

Anyway, I don't spot any problems in your source, your JCL, your compile
options, or your bind.

So then, I presume you tested something like this (assuming for the moment, you
are at TSO READY prompt):

alloc fi(sysout) da(*) reu
tsolib activate da('cee.sceerun')
call pds(tstprog)

is that true? Is this when you get the S0C4?

Out of curiosity, have you tried running this program in batch?

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 & COBOL: What's the secret to ...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Panda Bear <pb@da.zoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EB54FB.6EF5774C@da.zoo.com>`
- **References:** `<39EB3634.907EFFE2@da.zoo.com> <20001016142552.07742.00002178@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Steve

You hit the nail on the head!  Thanks.

If I do the TSOLIB in TSO, everything works OK.  If I try to allocate it
in ISPF using the LIBEDF, I get the abend.  So it has to do with the
search order ISPF is using when there are any dynamically called modules
(such as the LE runtime modules).

Our systems guys still have the COBOL-II runtime libraries allocated in
our logon proc instead of SCEERUN.  I've asked for it to be upgraded,
but was using the LIBDEF in an attempt to get around the problem. 
Obviously the LIBDEF is not doing the trick.

S Comstock wrote:
> 
> Hmmm. Be careful what you ask for - you might get it.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> USA
```

<a id="t-ccd257bb4b"></a>
## Thread: Variable Rcord Length — 3 msgs

### Variable Rcord Length

- **From:** "NICHOLAS CREMIDIS" <NICK.CREMIDIS@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sd8nf$6hmg$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Hi folks;

I need help in reading and writing a variablke record length file....

Thanks

Pedro
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Rcord Length

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sdamp$4eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8sd8nf$6hmg$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <8sd8nf$6hmg$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>,
  "NICHOLAS  CREMIDIS" <NICK.CREMIDIS@prodigy.net> wrote:
>
> Hi folks;
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In your FD, specify RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM "Integer-1"
to "Integer-2" DEPENDING ON WS-FILE-LGTH.

03 WS-FILE-LGTH PIC 9(08) BINARY.

Note that WS-FILE-LGTH must be an UNSIGNED Numeric Variable. I prefer
to use BINARY Fullwords. You can use whatever you'd like.

After issuing a READ, WS-FILE-LGTH will be set to the length of the
actual record just read.

When issuing a WRITE, WS-FILE-LGTH must be set to your desired record
length. The value of WS-FILE-LGTH must be NOT LESS THAN "Integer-1" and
NOT GREATER THAN "Integer-2".

HTH....

Bill


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Rcord Length

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sj11v06ol@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8sd8nf$6hmg$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <8sdamp$4eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Also, in OS390 at least, that length takes up 4 bytes, so in the JCL be sure
to make the LRECL 4 bytes larger than it is in the FD.

Jeff

----------
In article <8sdamp$4eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, WOB Consulting
<wobconsult@sprynet.com> wrote:


> In article <8sd8nf$6hmg$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>,

…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

<a id="t-86e17818c8"></a>
## Thread: Agencies' Y2k taxpayers' costs — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Re: Agencies' Y2k taxpayers' costs

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37iG5.361$B81.15003@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8sbcnj$oov$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8sbcnj$oov$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
Info Junkie  <bondrock@ifx.net> wrote:
>(Fair use for education and research)
>
>"Agencies' Y2k haste makes waste"
>
>http://www.cleveland.com/news/index.ssf?/news/pd/cc08comp.html

This is *most* interesting... notice:

--begin quoted text:

"What really bothers me is that we bought an early '90s design," said
Michael R. Jacobson, administrative assistant at the Ohio Board of
Registration for Professional Engineers & Surveyors and an amateur Web and
database programmer. "We're going to roll it out in the early 2000s 
sometime and we're going to be stuck with it until 2020.

"When you're talking computer systems, this thing's going to be, in my
opinion, obsolete. It has no Web connectivity. It doesn't have all kinds
of things we've requested."

[snippage]

"I'm starting to hear the contractor's three favorite words - 'Not in
scope,'" Jacobson wrote in a memo titled "The Peanut Gallery Speaketh"
last July. "For the newbies, that phrase translates to, 'You want it, you
pay extra.'"

--end quoted text

So... when 'we buy an early '90's design' did 'we' request 'all kinds of
things' from the start... or were these requests made later?  To have a
request for mods included with a purchase agreement is one thing, to
request mods *after* contracting is, the last time I looked, a classic
definition of 'increasing the scope'.

For the newbies... this siutation is *most* annoying when it comes from an
amateur, as Mr Jacobson was described.  An 'amateur', as the word's root
indicates, does things out of love (amor)... 'So what if you have to
rewrite all the file descriptors to include a new field or three... just
recompile the system and run the test-scenario again, you can do that
overnight... and besides, system response is better then, too!'  

A professional, on the other hand, has the nasty habit of breaking things
down into time and money... this might come from the professional's love
having been taken advantage of two or three times too many in the past,
one never knows.

DD
```

```
Brad Prothero wrote:
> 
> > Common-law marriage was seen as encouraging fraud and condoning vice
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> obstain from sex before marriage.

But that applies to civil marriage as well.  Common law or civil is all
the same.

But I went to a Roman Catholic wedding last year and heard the Priest
mention that marriage is the only sacrament which a Priest doesn't
perform.  He just witnesses it.  That makes sense - the Church has to be
flexible enough to not condemn people who marry when a Priest is not
available (shipwrecks).

I knew someone once in the Air Force.  When he was in Korea, she went to
Hawaii, and they were married (civil ceremony) across the international
date line.  She set up house in the married quarters, and they got their
married pay.  When he returned, they had a church wedding.  And ever
since, they celebrated his, hers, and their anniversaries!!
```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

That is

>
> But that applies to civil marriage as well.  Common law or civil is all

No. Civil marriage does not require living together to be married.

>
> But I went to a Roman Catholic wedding last year and heard the Priest
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> available (shipwrecks).

We are not talking about people being shipwrecked. Cohabitation by choice is
the element that is frowned upon by the Church. That was my point. I did not
bring up the complete belief of the Church since I do not know it.


> I knew someone once in the Air Force.  When he was in Korea, she went to
> Hawaii, and they were married (civil ceremony) across the international
> date line.  She set up house in the married quarters, and they got their
> married pay.  When he returned, they had a church wedding.  And ever
> since, they celebrated his, hers, and their anniversaries!!

The church wedding is not when they got married. They were married when they
had the civil ceremony. After that, they were not cohabitors outside of
marriage, they were a married couple. That is the difference.

Brad Prothero

<a id="t-303540693e"></a>
## Thread: The Big Picture — 27 this month / 40 total

### The Big Picture

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Some days ago, I made a casual comment about paragraph-names
and the meaningless ones I use. Of course, this unleashed
a torrent of criticism. Most of the comments I make
also unleash vitriolic gushings. Most of the people
in the CLC think I am some mindless dimwit living in the
past - this is only partially true. Anyway, it dawned
on me that some of my comments have been out of context
and could require explanation.

So, here is an explanation.

My minimalist paragraph-names are just a reflection of a VERY
comprehensive COBOL methodology I use for the development of
commercial application systems. Perhaps, taken out of
context, they seem unattractive and undesirable. In context,
they become attractive and desirable.

Everything I ever say in CLC pertains to the development
of new applications. It is has nothing to do with maintaining
legacy systems, interfacing with existing systems or
anything to do with existing systems. It is purely about
developing new (or rewritten) business applications in COBOL.

Anyone who has worked for a while on ordinary COBOL
commercial applications starts to realize that there is
a certain sameness about business programs. After you
have written your tenth report program, it will
occur to you that you are rewriting the same program
over and over again, just changing the parts pertaining
to the application, report format, changing control
breaks, etc.

The essential logic of all report programs is the same
without regard to the nature of the report. The essential
nature of processing data in all business applications is
the same, only the data and business rules change.

If this were not true there would be no REPORT-WRITER
or Report Program Generator (RPG), or, even, if you
go back that far, FARGO. Going further back, IBM
would not have been able to manufacture tabulating
equipment had not the essential logic of the hardware
applied to every type of business allowing them to
sell identical equipment to everybody.

In another recent thread, a student complained about
the difficulties he had learning control breaks.  Every
programmer has to learn about these elementary things
at the beginning of their career and, usually, each
devises a solution which satisfies them. The solutions
may differ by individual. This is one of the reasons
maintenance is so difficult.

As time goes by, the programmer realizes that most of
the programs they write contain the same stuff. How many
times do you have to code the same fields for dates, the time,
subscripts, page and line counts, etc., before you get sick
and tired of it and make a general-purpose working-storage
copy and have done with it?  Doesn't everyone do this?
How long does it take the average newcomer to start creating
their own copies? Do they? Is this suggested in the COBOL courses?

How many times do you have to write an error routine,
or a routine to validate states before making a general-
purpose routine which you can use forever?

Like everybody else, I have my standard copies. There is one
general-purpose working-storage copy which is in every program
even though only one or two fields may be used in a particular
program. This copy has 300 items in it. Some of the things in
it are: program-id, special-purpose literals like a smiley-face,
13 subscripts (X1 thru X13), 13 subscripts to be used by
subroutines (X1-SR thru X13-SR), printer fields like: page size,
line count, page count, formatted date and time fields (filled
in by subroutine), the system date and time (with Y2K provisions),
standard fields for file status (WS-FSTAT), return-key (WS-FKEY),
a bell character, a standard error-message area, the alphabet and
special characters, print areas for seven printers, handy edit
fields with pics like: Z9, ZZ9, ZZZ9, Z,ZZ9, and so on, fields
for general-purpose touchscreen routines, a couple of single-
character fields for simple responses (WS-OPT and WS-ANS),
standard US State and Canadian Province codes, escape
sequences for opening cash drawers, data fields used by
common subroutines, device addresses for printers, and so on.
These are the things which every programmer needs. If anybody would
like a copy, email me.

Effectively, the names in the working-storage copy are
extensions to the reserved-word list for the compiler.
After a short while you don't even think about them
anymore, you just use them naturally and, they are short
and sweet and easy-to-remember and easy-to-type.

In the olden-days, it would not have been practical to have
a 300-line copy stuck in every program, especially on an 8k
1401. These days when memory is virtually unlimited and speeds
are ridiculous, who cares whether most of the 300 lines are
unused in a particular program. Since most programs compile in
less than one second, excluding the 300 and replacing them
with only those required, would be a pointless exercise.
Taking the time to code your own version of the things you
needed and excluding the w-s copy would be really dim.

The names in the working-storage copy are short and sweet
because they are used over and over and over.

Subscripts, as mentioned, are named X1 thru X13. Calling
them SUBSCRIPT-ONE wouldn't help a lot. The X1 terminology
was invented, I think for 1410 Autocoder. I liked it then
and I like it now. Short, sweet, effective. Why change?
Saying PERFORM BLAH VARYING BLAH-SUBSCRIPT Just makes the
BLAH statements ugly - MOVE HAT-SIZE (BLAH-SUBSCRIPT)
vs MOVE HAT-SIZE (X1). (The 'X' stood for Index-Register).

Names for frequently-used items like PAGCNT, LINCNT,
PAGSIZ are short and self-explanatory. Everybody knows
them and EVERYBODY uses them, or else!

Like everyone else, I have a bunch of sub-routines. Most
were written for MS COBOL on a Tandy 2000 (80186) in 1982
when I abandoned the mainframe world to concentrate on
the PC. They worked then, they work now - why change them? - for the
sake of fashion? Their data fields are in the general-purpose
working-storage copy.

I have routines which: Print to any of seven printers, get
the date and time and format both of them, standard all-
purpose error routine, validate State/Province, upcase
a string, upcase first character of words within a string,
Julian/Gregorian conversion, and so on. I am sure everyone has
their own collection. In accordance with my naming convention
subroutines are performed thus: PERFORM S9908 THRU S9908X.
S9908 is the name of the standard error routine. Everyone
knows this, changing it to PERFORM STANDARD-ERROR-ROUTINE
THRU STANDARD-ERROR-ROUTINE-EXIT wouldn't make it any better.
The standard print-area is called PA. To print, you
move your stuff to PA and then PERFORM W1A, W2A, W3A, or WTOP
(print after advancing 1, 2, 3 lines and top-of-page). As you
know, some programs have dozens of print statements,
surely PERFORM W1A is preferable to PERFORM PRINT-AFTER-ADVANCING-ONE,
yes?

In this case, short names make sense, especially if you
look on them as extensions to the reserved-word list.

Anyway, enough about standard copies and subroutines which
everyone uses anyway, don't they?

What I have which hardly anybody else has are two skeleton
programs (File-Maintenance and Report). Virtually any application
can be built using just these two things. Before you light your
flame-thrower, let me just say that I have used the methods
I am talking about and have built systems using these two
skeletons for companies as diverse as Estee Lauder,
Time-Warner, Exxon Research & Development, Bunge Corporation,
National Shoes, American Insurance Association, IBM, etc.
Because of the methodology, I was easily able to develop
applications on my PC and then port them to a VAX system
or anything else in no time flat.

The skeletons contain the general-purpose working-storage
copy and the subroutines.

My own POS software was created from these two skeletons. You may
occasionally come across the need for a special program
but not often. I don't claim to have invented skeletons
and I am sure there are other people who do what I do.
You can write your own, structured if you want.

Going back to the sameness of report programs which allowed
things like REPORT-WRITER to be invented, most people could
spend time and come up with a skeleton program which covered
the most essential logical elements of a general-purpose
report program. In my case, I spent a considerable amount of
time creating my skeletons until they were fool-proof and
considered every eventuality. The skeletons are functionally
perfect, programs made from them are too. How nice.

Because the skeletons are general-purpose, you can't have
application-specific paragraph-names. Because everyone
using the methodology is throughly familiar with the skeletons,
it becomes unnecessary to use long paragraph-names. The
names are single-letter with a small suffix and are in
alphabetical order. For example, in the file-maintenance
skeleton, the 'E' paragraphs accept an Action code from
the user which tells the program what the user wants to
do; the 'K' paragraphs are where the program does screen-io
in response to the user; the 'L' paragraphs edit user-entries
and construct records, and so on.  Since all programs are
made from the same skeleton, these rules hold forth
throughout and the technicians know them well.

The technician would know that a new screen field would
require new code in the 'K' paragraphs where the data is
accepted, and in the 'L' paragraphs where records are
edited and constructed.

I don't design for the mouse (except in touchscreen stuff)
and use the usually-ignored function keys for navigation and
control.  For this reason, I explicitly accept fields and
test the return-key after each accept. Any frequently-used
business application which forces the user to go from
keyboard to mouse over and over is infuriating.

In the File-Maintenance skeleton, the groups of related
fields on a screen are given a function-key value; for
example; to jump to a name and address area press F2,
to jump to another area, press F3 and so on.
When finished, the user presses F8 to file the record.
The ESC or up-arrow is used to backup to the preceding
field. F1 is used if the user changes their mind and
returns them to the Action in paragraph 'E'. This gives
the user COMPLETE control and convenience WITHOUT the
use of the mouse.

In the 'K' paragraphs, the paragraph-names reflect the
field numbers on the screen. It isn't necessary to have
a meaningful name for the paragraphs since the first
statement in each one moves the NEXT-FLD-ID to SP2 so
you know what field it is. CONVERSE is the SP2 'ACCEPT'.
K99, which is in every program, tests the RETURN-KEY from
SP2 and sets WS-FKEY to an appropriate value representing
the function-key pressed.

The code below contains the ONLY use I make of DEPENDING ON.
It just works very nicely for responding to function-keys.

The code shown is that which accepts the three
parts of the social-security number. The user can type
the number, or press F1 to forget-about-it, or ESC to
backup to the preceding field, or function-keys to leap
to other parts of the screen.

If you look at the code out of context then you might
say that it is not well-documented or easy to understand.
Look at it in context however, and it is pure and simple.
Being familiar with the model, you just know that all user
I-O is done in the 'K' paragraphs and that each field
on the panel has its own paragraph and that they are
executed in a specific sequence. So, the short names are
appropriate in this context. XFM60 is the program name
and primary panel name. Changing the names to something
like 600-ACCEPT-SOCIAL-SECURITY-PART1 really would be
pointless. Imagine the GO TO if long names were used.

In the future, if you needed to add new fields between
K10 and K11, for example, the names would be K10A, K10B, etc.
The following is taken from one of my POS systems:

       K10.
           MOVE XFM60-SS-1-I TO XFM60-NEXT-FLD-ID.
           PERFORM CONVERSE.
           MOVE XFM60-SS-1 TO WS-SS-1.
           PERFORM K99.
           GO TO K02A, E, K01, K09, K14, L L L L DEPENDING ON WS-FKEY.
       K11.
           MOVE XFM60-SS-2-I TO XFM60-NEXT-FLD-ID.
           PERFORM CONVERSE.
           MOVE XFM60-SS-2 TO WS-SS-2.
           PERFORM K99.
           GO TO K10, E, K01, K09, K14, L L L L DEPENDING ON WS-FKEY.
       K12.
           MOVE XFM60-SS-3-I TO XFM60-NEXT-FLD-ID.
           PERFORM CONVERSE.
           MOVE XFM60-SS-3 TO WS-SS-3.
           PERFORM K99.
           GO TO K11, E, K01, K09, K14, L L L L DEPENDING ON WS-FKEY.
           (Corresponding to ESC, F1, F2, F3, F4, F5, F6, F7, F8)

		*	*	*	*	*

I have a Customer Manager program which maintains my customer/
contact file. It was made from the File-Maintenance skeleton in 1982.
In addition to the basic Actions for adding, changing, deleting
records, etc., the program has about 20 additional Actions for
things like printing envelopes, customer directories, backup,
and so on.  Anyay, I recently needed to add a new Action, one
which would read my customer file and write an ASCII file
containing email addresses. Since everything I needed was
already in the main program, all I needed to do was code
the procedure - about 30 lines. The code appears below.

Again, if you were to look at this code out of context,
you could heap scorn upon it. (Of course, some people
are going to heap scorn anyway, but they are the ones
who don't get the Big Picture.)

The routine is executed if the user types Action '6';
IF WS-ACT = '6' PERFORM EMAIL-LIST THRU EMX. EMAIL-LIST
is used for documentary purposes and is an example of how
meaningful names ARE used - as required. Since all the
letters of the alphabet are used up in the main
program (made from skeleton), the paragraph names
in the subroutine borrow from their purpose. So,
the names are EMA thru EMX. The routine which prints
envelopes is called EVA thru EVX, and so on.

The data names in the code would be known to the technician
via the directory and reference to the appropriate copies.
For clarification, FFP01A is the name of the customer file
(in accordance with naming convention), RFP01-B is the
second alternate key for that file. To know what the key
consists of the technician refers to the record-description
copy (RFP01). S9912 is the routine which upcases a string.
W-WORK is in the standard working-storage copy, as is
the subscript X1. S9908 is the standard error-message
routine. W-WORK and W-STRING are in the standard working-
storage copy. W-FSTAT is the standard file-status code. My
technician would know all of this by heart.

This code will horrify people because it is upper-case,
not structured, uses punctuation on every line, has
meaningless paragraph-names, it contains a GO TO
in a performed paragraph, etc. If you are overly
sensitive to this type of code, have a quick drink
before continuing. . .

On the other hand, I see some advantages:

1. It is very easy to read.
2. It is compact and contains no superfluous code as
   would be found in a structured program.
3. Within the context of the methodology it is easy to understand.
4. It is easier and faster to type.
5. It is highly portable.
6. Anyone can understand it.
7. Because it is merely another Action in a larger program which
   contained everything it needed, no data division entries were
   required (the usual case).
8. Functionally, I don't think it can be improved.
9. No time is spent licking one's metaphorical pencil while
   dreaming up unique long paragraph-names and then, later on, wondering
   how you spelt them. . .
10. It is written quickly and efficiently.

(NB: File FFP01A is opened before the routine is used.)

      *
      *    CREATES FILE WITH EMAIL ADDRESSES IF '@' IN FP-CONTACT.
      *
	EMAIL-LIST.
		OPEN OUTPUT ASCIIFILE.
		IF W-FSTAT NOT = '00' GO TO EMB.
	EMA.
		MOVE 'I' TO RFP01-B.
		START FFP01A KEY IS NOT < RFP01-B INVALID KEY GO TO EMB.
		IF W-FSTAT NOT = '00' GO TO EMB.
		GO TO EMC.
	EMB.
		MOVE 'Status Error in EMB.' TO W-ERR.
		PERFORM S9908 THRU S9908X.
		GO TO E.
	EMC.
		READ FFP01A NEXT AT END GO TO EMX.
		IF W-FSTAT   NOT = '00' GO TO EMB.
		IF FP-STATUS NOT = 'I'  GO TO EMX.
	EMD.
		MOVE FP-CONTACT TO W-WORK.
		PERFORM S9912 THRU S9912X.
		MOVE W-WORK TO ASCII.
		MOVE W-WORK TO W-STRING.
		PERFORM EME VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > 40.
		GO TO EMC.
	EME.
		IF W-CHAR (X1) = '@'
       	   GO TO EMF.
	EMF.
		WRITE ASCII INVALID KEY GO TO EMB.
		GO TO EMC.
	EMX.
		CLOSE ASCIIFILE.
		IF W-FSTAT NOT = '00' GO TO EMB.

Would it help any if EMA were changed to EMA-START-CUSTOMER-FILE?
Would it be any better if it were structured?

Admittedly, the sample given is small. However, large programs are
divided into discrete functions where each is coded as above.

The problem with this sort of methodology is that 'real programmers'
hate it and will fight it whenever possible. 'Real programmers' want
to exercise their creativity, they do not want to be bound to standards.
'Real programmers' want to enlarge their skill-set with as many things
as possible.

In the olden-days, when I hired people, a lot of them would lose
interest when told that they would have to adhere to strict standards.
So, I hired raw trainees. People who had completed any COBOL course.
Prior experience undesirable. Computer Science degree undesirable.
In those days, as today, newcomers found it hard to get a job
because of their limited skill-set/experience (Although skill-sets
hadn't been invented then).

So, goodbye, 'real programmers'; hello COBOL technicians.

The people I hired were grateful to get the job. My employer was happy
because they only wanted $12,000.00 to begin. They were happy to
get the training and they were universally delighted to see that
they could actually create real programs which actually worked
with so little effort and so soon after starting work. My boss
loved it because we were so productive and never missed deadlines.
Now, programs are manufactured, using screens designed with Flexus
SP2, by taking a copy of the skeleton, making global changes
to file and record names, and inserting the application-specific
code. Technicians work at the DOS prompt (see thread 'Only for
COBOL newbies').

On day one, hour one, my new employee (if I needed one) would be
presented with the following:

-An explanation of the methodology.
-Naming convention for files, copies, routines, record and data names
 and everything else.
-A printed directory listing all programs, copies, etc.
-Printed copies of all subroutines, standard copies, etc.
-Copies of the File-Maintenance skeleton and the Report skeleton.

They would be locked in their room until they became very familiar
with the whole thing. Then they would be given simple programs
to manufacture until they gained experience.

The advantages to using an extensive and mimimal methodology
are sort of overwhelming, here are some of them:

a. Limited skill-set - only COBOL and SP2 required.
b. Lower-cost employees.
c. Faster (cheaper) development.
d. Predictable development time (enables actual planning).
e. Reduced testing time - only the application-specific code needs test.
f. Low-cost maintenance - all the programs are the same.
g. Several people can write code for one program all at once without
   talking to each other.
h. Unparalleled dependability - all programs are made from the perfect
models.
i. Very high degree of portability.
j. Lower software-tool expense.
k. Easy to find technicians.
l. Short training time for technicians.
m. Standardized mode of operation for all programs.
n. Training of users virtually eliminated due to self-documentation of
   software and standard mode of operation.
o. Mouse virtually eliminated.
p. Printed documentation eliminated.
q. Distribution costs less because of compact size of systems.
r. It is staggeringly synergistic.

These are sound business reasons for adopting such a methodology,
aren't they?

Yes, I've been doing things the same way for years. No, I don't
stay with the fashion. No, I am not going to change. This methodology
worked in the sixties, it works now and it will work 'til the
end of time. To change it would be to change it for the sake of
change.

Sure, I could (actually, I couldn't) rewrite the skeletons to
be structured but I would still need those paragraphs where
the technician inserts the application code. Why bother?

You could write your own structured skeletons and do everything I do
but in a structured way.

The objective must be to stop people from writing programs from
scratch in their own style and to make them all the same, as they
should be.

If you are interested in seeing a program made using the methodology
go to foodman123.com/tcs.htm.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth












Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: The Big Picture

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T05:08:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<29RG5.57352$bI6.1996851@news1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi:

Hi, Tony.  I want to make my comments about your post, but let me make a few
statements up front.  First, as I've said in a previous post, I have the
highest respect for an entrepreneur who is successful.  Regardless of the
style or content of your programs, your ability to make a living from your
own ability and resourcefulness is admirable.

> Some days ago, I made a casual comment about paragraph-names
> and the meaningless ones I use. Of course, this unleashed
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and could require explanation.

As I said, I would not be one of those who considers you a "mindless
dimwit".  I reserve those comments for those who say "my teacher told me
never to do it like that".

> So, here is an explanation.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> they become attractive and desirable.

After reviewing your attached code, I agree with your use of minimalist
paragraph names.  Your style is unstructured and very assembler- (or
AUTOCODER-) like, requiring numerous jumps to other sections of the code.
The resulting blizzard of paragraph names are impossible to name - most of
them don't title a section but just provide a place to land from a GO TO.

> Everything I ever say in CLC pertains to the development
> of new applications. It is has nothing to do with maintaining
> legacy systems, interfacing with existing systems or
> anything to do with existing systems. It is purely about
> developing new (or rewritten) business applications in COBOL.

Today's new development is tomorrow's "legacy system" and tomorrow's
"existing system".  New applications need the most attention, since they
establish the framework for decades of enhancements.

> Anyone who has worked for a while on ordinary COBOL
> commercial applications starts to realize that there is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> breaks, etc.

Reusing code is a great idea.  Most shops do it.  Personally, I can't
remember the last time I wrote a program from a blank screen.

> The essential logic of all report programs is the same
> without regard to the nature of the report. The essential
> nature of processing data in all business applications is
> the same, only the data and business rules change.

Agreed, as a veeerrrrry general statement...

> If this were not true there would be no REPORT-WRITER
> or Report Program Generator (RPG), or, even, if you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> sell identical equipment to everybody.

Also agreed.  In addition, the COBOL language finally removed the tyranny of
rewrites when moving from one platform to another.  I made a lot of money
converting AUTOCODER and SAAL to COBOL.

> In another recent thread, a student complained about
> the difficulties he had learning control breaks.  Every
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintenance is so difficult.

Most development shops have standards for this.  Most good programming
courses teach this.  It's hard to learn because it's hard.  We soon learn to
do it in a standardized way, masking the complexity.

> As time goes by, the programmer realizes that most of
> the programs they write contain the same stuff. How many
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> their own copies? Do they? Is this suggested in the COBOL courses?

Reuseable code.  Taught just about everywhere today.

> How many times do you have to write an error routine,
> or a routine to validate states before making a general-
> purpose routine which you can use forever?

Most development shops address this: "standard error codes" or "standard
error routines".

> Like everybody else, I have my standard copies. There is one
> general-purpose working-storage copy which is in every program
> >snip.....................
> These are the things which every programmer needs. If anybody would
> like a copy, email me.

I'd love to get a copy, Tony.  Thanks!  I'm always in "beg mode"...

> In the olden-days, it would not have been practical to have
> a 300-line copy stuck in every program, especially on an 8k
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> needed and excluding the w-s copy would be really dim.

Personally, I prefer to place these things in a called program, then provide
instructions for using the call.

> The standard print-area is called PA. To print, you
> move your stuff to PA and then PERFORM W1A, W2A, W3A, or WTOP
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> yes?

No problem.  I might prefer PERFORM PRINT-AFTER-1, but no quibbling...

> In this case, short names make sense, especially if you
> look on them as extensions to the reserved-word list.

Like I said, I prefer using a called program for this stuff.  Then, the
working-storage variables are hidden from view.


> ...............(snip)
> The skeletons contain the general-purpose working-storage
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You can write your own, structured if you want.

Again, I have my stuff in a called program.  That way, if I need to change
the read routine because of a file format change, I just change the called
program.  Skeletons usually contain repeated program code, unless you mean
that it contains exclusively DIVISION and COPY statements.  Repeated code
means that corrections to the underlying procedures requires searching
hundreds
of programs for a specific structure.

> Going back to the sameness of report programs which allowed
> things like REPORT-WRITER to be invented, most people could
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> perfect, programs made from them are too. How nice.

Well...
READs are functionally perfect, but programs made from them aren't.  READ is
an assembly language "skeleton" - using READ triggers a ton of assembler
steps in the compile..  What I'm trying to say is that a "skeleton" program
is useless unless you change it, and there's the rub.  Once you change it,
it's not your skeleton any more.

Come to think of it, a skeleton program is just legacy code adapted to a new
purpose, isn't it?

> I don't design for the mouse (except in touchscreen stuff)
> and use the usually-ignored function keys for navigation and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> keyboard to mouse over and over is infuriating.

With the narrow exception of POS, you're probably the only one who's
infuriated.  Most business applications provide for keyboard entry of
repetitive tasks, but it's amazing how seldom they're used.  In POS, since
there's usually no mouse, the issue is moot.

> the names are EMA thru EMX. The routine which prints
> envelopes is called EVA thru EVX, and so on.

Again, absolutely correct in an unstructured program, since many of the
paragraph names don't describe a function, but simply provide a GO TO
target.

> The data names in the code would be known to the technician
> via the directory and reference to the appropriate copies.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> technician would know all of this by heart.

Some of this is necessary, but some just increases the training time of your
new programmer.  S9908 could be ERR-MESSAGE-RTN or some such.  Conciseness
can go too far.

Also, I notice in your code that you have a routine for uppercasing a string
(essentially a one-statement operation), but you don't have one to START and
READ your master file.  What's up with that?

> This code will horrify people because it is upper-case,
> not structured, uses punctuation on every line, has
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> before continuing. . .

I use uppercase and punctuation...  But you GO TO out of a performed
paragraph that could have been ended simply.  It took me a minute to figure
out what you were doing.

> On the other hand, I see some advantages:
>
> 1. It is very easy to read.

Stumped me at first until I applied some indentation.

> 2. It is compact and contains no superfluous code as
>    would be found in a structured program.

What's superflous?  END-IF?

> 3. Within the context of the methodology it is easy to understand.

I would differ with you.

> 4. It is easier and faster to type.

Don't care.  I'll touch-type.  Shortening names just to speed development is
just folly.

> 5. It is highly portable.

What makes it more portable than other COBOL code?

> 6. Anyone can understand it.

I couldn't, at first.

> 7. Because it is merely another Action in a larger program which
>    contained everything it needed, no data division entries were
>    required (the usual case).

One line in a copybook makes a data division.

> 8. Functionally, I don't think it can be improved.

ANY code can be made to work, even if it has one letter variables and no
indentations.  The Appollo missions ran on large nests of FORTRAN code.
Nasty. Unreadable.  Functionally, though, it probably couldn't be improved.

> 9. No time is spent licking one's metaphorical pencil while
>    dreaming up unique long paragraph-names and then, later on, wondering
>    how you spelt them. . .

Structured code requires LESS paragraphage.  Paragraph names under
structured programming denotes SIGNIFICANT sections of the code.

> 10. It is written quickly and efficiently.

Like I said, your "skeleton" is done by most shops every day.

> (NB: File FFP01A is opened before the routine is used.)
>       *
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> IF W-FSTAT NOT = '00' GO TO EMB.

My equivalent (eyeball compiled only):

    OPEN OUTPUT ASCIIFILE.
    IF W-FSTAT NOT = '00'
        PERFORM ERROR-END
    END-IF.

    MOVE 'I' TO RFP01-B.
    START FFP01A KEY IS NOT < RFP01-B.
    IF FFP01-NOT-OK
        PERFORM ERROR-END
    END-IF.

    PERFORM READ-FFP01.
    PERFORM UNTIL FFP01-NOT-OK OR
                  FP-STATUS    NOT EQUAL 'I'
        MOVE FP-CONTACT TO ASCII
        INSPECT ASCII CONVERTING
              "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz" TO
              "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
        MOVE ZERO TO X1
        INSPECT ASCII TALLYING X1 FOR '@'
        IF X1 > 0
            WRITE ASCII
            IF W-FSTAT NOT EQUAL '00'
                PERFORM ERROR-END
            END-IF
        END-IF
        PERFORM READ-FFP01
    END-PERFORM.

    CLOSE ASCIIFILE.
    IF W-FSTAT NOT = '00'
        PERFORM ERROR-END
    END-IF.

READ-FFP01.
    READ FFP01A NEXT.
    IF FFP01-NOT-OK
        PERFORM ERROR-END
    END-IF.

ERROR-END.
    MOVE 'Status Error in EMB.' TO W-ERR.
    PERFORM S9908 THRU S9908X.
    GO TO E.

.....admittedly a few lines longer than yours, but contains two paragraph
names instead of eight.  I would, however, probably change all the reads,
writes, opens and closes to performed paragraphs.

> Would it help any if EMA were changed to EMA-START-CUSTOMER-FILE?

Nope.

> Would it be any better if it were structured?

IMVHO, yes.

> The problem with this sort of methodology is that 'real programmers'
> hate it and will fight it whenever possible. 'Real programmers' want
> to exercise their creativity, they do not want to be bound to standards.
> 'Real programmers' want to enlarge their skill-set with as many things
> as possible.

You didn't even use ELSE to simplify your code.  Do you require that of your
programmers?   Would you consider that to be an enlargement of their
skill-set?  Using a subset of a language doesn't denote skill.  Tiger Woods
has a whole bag of clubs and uses them all.  Your skill at COBOL is not
measured by how few verbs you can use.  More likely, it denotes a lack of
vocabulary.

> In the olden-days, when I hired people, a lot of them would lose
> interest when told that they would have to adhere to strict standards.

My background is banking.  We required strict standards and unbelievably
tight security.  We kept people without any problems.

> So, I hired raw trainees. People who had completed any COBOL course.
> Prior experience undesirable. Computer Science degree undesirable.

My assumptions:
1. You never involve the programmers in the development of your standards.
2. You never consider new styles of programming and how they would affect
new development.
3. You don't attempt to read up on new programming approaches, adopting the
good and tossing the bad.

> So, goodbye, 'real programmers'; hello COBOL technicians.

You want people who code in your style.  By your definition, you're a 'real
programmer', everyone who works for you is a 'COBOL technician'.

> Technicians work at the DOS prompt

I used to be a DOS guy.  I still go to DOS to do mass renames and stuff, but
my aversion to the mouse is not that strong.

> The advantages to using an extensive and mimimal methodology
> are sort of overwhelming, here are some of them:

(snippage of lots of "staggeringly" and "unparalleled")

> These are sound business reasons for adopting such a methodology,
> aren't they?

Well, yes and no.  Yes, they are great goals, but No, I can't imagine
adopting some of them in a 100 programmer shop.  Newbies without depth of
education or experience being thrust into development with 60s tools?
Newbies given the task of maintaining 60s code?  I reiterate, your skeleton
approach is essentially taking program A and modifying it until it becomes
program B.  Old stuff.  At least use BASIS or called modules to avoid
cloning code all over the place.

> Yes, I've been doing things the same way for years. No, I don't
> stay with the fashion. No, I am not going to change. This methodology
> worked in the sixties, it works now and it will work 'til the
> end of time. To change it would be to change it for the sake of
> change.

I'm probably as old as you.  I've worked 1401, Univac 1004, stood in front
of a sorter until I developed corns, wired collator boards.  But - I realize
that some of the new ways of doing things are actually better ways.  New
cars last longer than old cars because their engines are better.  New
products and methodologies are sometimes actually, well, better.

> Sure, I could (actually, I couldn't) rewrite the skeletons to
> be structured but I would still need those paragraphs where
> the technician inserts the application code. Why bother?

Tony, while I respect your ability greatly, you've let the world pass you
by.  You don't understand structured code, and worse, you condemn it because
you don't.

> You could write your own structured skeletons and do everything I do
> but in a structured way.

It's pretty much a standard in many shops today.  We call it reusable code.
We use loose coupling and information hiding, ideas generated from us 60s
guys influencing the education process.

> The objective must be to stop people from writing programs from
> scratch in their own style and to make them all the same, as they
> should be.

Did you notice how similar the programs were in "Show me your code"?
Precious little difference.  Structuring fosters that.

Tony, you're like the old man at the park who decries new cars, new houses,
new politicians, new hairstyles, new clothes.  Old isn't better, it isn't
worse - it's just old.  New isn't better, it's just new.

> If you are interested in seeing a program made using the
> methodology go to foodman123.com/tcs.htm.

I used to build programs that looked like this with Clipper.  Looks like a
DOS program sitting on my desktop.  Most users would say "where do I click"?
It's probably a good program, but since it's not standard windows, I
probably would look for another program by someone else.

Newbies should be encouraged to study the newer ways of coding.  Many are
better, some suck - anyone remember the Michael Jackson methodology?  Don't
expect people to stand idly by when you state, amish-like, that everything
after 1969 is evil.

Many of your detractors, being programmers, are somewhat socially
challenged, hence the vitriol.  Don't sweat it, and don't turn defensive.
Others in this NG can probably (gasp) actually teach you something.   I came
here to learn, and I certainly have succeeded.

Cliff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Big Picture

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EC2FA1.B84EFAFB@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <29RG5.57352$bI6.1996851@news1.giganews.com>`

```
Cliff Peterson wrote:

> > Anyone who has worked for a while on ordinary COBOL
> > commercial applications starts to realize that there is
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Reuse is about centralising the same logic in one place not having 50 copies of
the same logic having a potential time bomb.

Consider in Australia we use A4 paper which has 70 lines from top to bottom.
How many programs would that affect.   I would hasard a guess that 90% of the
programs out there need to change to cater for this.  I would also guess that
more than 20% would be non trivial to implement that change.  (wound up in
strange constants like  lines-left  equals 54).

The trick is to work out how to implement these things as subroutines.   This
is not nessecarily easy.  Report writer would be one place to start for this
specific problem.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Big Picture

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Merlin43PhD@netscape.net (Dwight Scott Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T00:00:00

I'm about to be blunt.

On Mon, 16 Oct 2000 12:17:45 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>The following is taken from one of my POS systems:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>           GO TO K10, E, K01, K09, K14, L L L L DEPENDING ON WS-FKEY.


This is meaningless gibberish.  COBOL should be self documenting.
This is not.  This is impossible to read and understand.  The 400
lines of explanation that preceded this code snippet should have been
unnecessary.

Move Quantity-ordered to edited-quantity
Compute total-price = quantity-ordered * unit-price
Move total-price to display-price, edit-price

That kind of stuff is READABLE, UNDERSTANDABLE and MAINTAINABLE.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: The Big Picture

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2p8wa0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 16 Oct 2000 12:17:45 GMT, Foodman wrote:

> Lots of stuff deleted

In the mid 70's, I worked as a programmer in a large shop with about 120
programmers. They had the most strict programming rules you can imagine. The
main part of the PROCEDURE DIVISION was a framework that was included with a
COPY statement and that took care of calling other sections for performing
various functions. The framework used a large COPY structure in
WORKING-STORAGE to save control fields to determine level breaks and so on.
The naming scheme of SECTIONS and PARAGRAPHS was abstract A for the mainline,
B for initialization, C1 for reading the first file C2 for the second file G1
for processing a record from the first file F1 for the highest level break
and Uxx for other routines. Every programmer was trained in using this method
and it was strictly prohibited to write a new program without using these
structures.
Even the position of the instructions was completely defined, for example a
MOVE statement should begin in position 21, the word TO in position 41 and
the receiving field in position 46.
Of course, there also existed a lot of programs that were written before they
invented these rules.

I worked quite some time with a small group of programmers who only had to
solve the production problems and there I had to modify programs written by a
lot of different programmers and I learned a lot of things about programming
that they won't teach you anywhere. 

My conclusions.

- If everybody uses the same abstract names in the procedures, it is not too
difficult to understand the program, but for somebody who doesn't know the
filosofy, it is very difficult to find out what the program is doing.
- Even using this rigid structure, a lot of people are able to produce
complicated, illogic, inefficient and impossible to maintain programs.
- Some of the "old" programs, without any pre-defined structure are much
easier to understand and to maintain than the so-called structured ones.
- A bad programmer will write a bad program using these structures, but
without them the result will even be worse
- A good programmer will write a good program anyway

- Any standard is better than no standard at all.

Just my opinion
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Big Picture

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sne5h$cpq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2p8wa0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
What I like about standards is that there are so many to choose from :)

I'll agree with the food dude: subscripts don't need a descriptive name
because the subscript or index just acts as a pointer to the nth
element of an array. a-table-item (a-table-item-index) is no more clear
than a-table-item (ndx). the important thing is to know that the index
or subscript will not be stepped on by another procedure in the
program. This is an area where local variables at the paragraph level
would really help. COBOL doesn't have them and C++ does. I'd love it if
you could say:

.
.
.
paragraph-name.

01 sub-x pic 9(05) value 1.

perform varying sub-x from 1 by 1 until sub-x > 10
    display "Item=" a-table-item (sub-x)
end-perform

and sub-x would be created and destroyed with entry to and exit from
paragraph name.

UMMV

On Thu, 19 Oct 2000 18:22:51 GMT, shine98@my-deja.com wrote:

>What I like about standards is that there are so many to choose from :)
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

Why don't you use the INDEXED BY clause to define an index for a table? It
increases performance, and the index field can only be used on its own table,
so it's not very likely it will be misused

Just my opinion
Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com

SET MY-INDEX DOWN BY 1.

Is soooo ugly!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Big Picture

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8snh01$q2t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2p8wa0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8sne5h$cpq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2pdwk0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
FYI,
  The ANSI/ISO Standard "requires" that an index (from the indexed by
phrase) ONLY be used in the table for which it was defined.  However, most
(almost all?) compilers allow it to be used for ANY table of the same
"size".  This is certainly allowed by IBM, MERANT, Realia - and I think
several others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Big Picture

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8snjd6$fo1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2p8wa0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8sne5h$cpq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2pdwk0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8snh01$q2t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:8snh01$q2t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...

<snip>

> However, most (almost all?) compilers allow it to be used for ANY table of
the same
> "size".  This is certainly allowed by IBM, MERANT, Realia - and I think
> several others.

None of the Unisys A Series COBOL compilers (COBOL(68), COBOL74 or COBOL85)
allow the use of an index against either a different table or a different
dimension of the same table.  An index may only be used as an index against
the table row of its declaration in those dialects.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Big Picture

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2000-10-29T14:06:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qD_K5.24874$Q8.5303656@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<8serl4$8cd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2p8wa0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8sne5h$cpq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2pdwk0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8snh01$q2t$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8snjd6$fo1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
IBM allows it:
5.3.10.3 INDEXED BY Phrase

The INDEXED BY phrase specifies the indexes that can be used with a table.
The INDEXED BY phrase is required if indexing is used to refer a this table
element.  See "Subscripting Using Index-Names (Indexing)" in  topic
1.5.1.8.2.

A table without an INDEXED BY option can be referred to through indexing.


1.5.1.8.2 Subscripting Using Index-Names (Indexing)

Indexing allows such operations as table searching and manipulating specific
items.  To use indexing you  associate one or more index-names with an item
whose data description entry contains an OCCURS clause.  An index associated
with an index-name acts as a subscript, and its value corresponds to an
occurrence number for the item to which the index-name is associated.

The INDEXED BY phrase, by which the index-name is identified and associated
with its table, is an optional  part of the OCCURS clause. There is no
separate entry to describe the index associated with index-name.  At run
time, the contents of the index corresponds to an occurrence number for that
specific dimension of the table with which the index is associated.

The initial value of an index at run time is undefined, and the index must
be initialized before it is used as a subscript.  An initial value is
assigned to an index with one of the following:

   The PERFORM statement with the VARYING phrase
   The SEARCH statement with the ALL phrase
   The SET statement

The use of an integer or data-name as a subscript referencing a table
element or an item within a table
element does not cause the alteration of any index associated with that
table.

As an IBM extension, an index-name can be used to reference any table.
However, the table element length of the table being referenced and of the
table that the index-name is associated with should match. Otherwise, the
reference will not be to the same table element in each table, and you might
get run-time  errors.


I have to agree with Thane, I personally and professionally dislike Dwight's
coding style.  You can
still have 'general purpose' copy routines (even if your compiler does NOT
support 'REPLACING'.

For instance :
UC-100-TO-UPPER
FIP-100-READ-MASTER-PRI-KEY
FOP-100-WRITE-MASTER-PRI-KEY
USB-100-SEARCH-TABLE-ALL

etc

BTW, Dwight, why have code all over the place to write a report, when you
can include
a WS Copy, and move variables (you have to do this part anyways),
set a value for Lines-Per-Page, Header-Lines, Data-Lines, Footer-Lines
and then call a 'ReportPGM'?

That's what I started doing after I wrote my 5th or 6th report program.  15
years later
when I encountered a report that needed more than 9 header / footer lines,

ALL
I had to do was add a table to convert A thru J to 10 thru 20, check whether
the input
header/footer-lines was numeric, if so, move it to a (change number 2) 2
byte header/
footer-lines, else look thru the table to match the letters, then move the
corresponding 2 byte
item to header/footer-lines.

All this used nice COBOL type names:  RPT-HEADER-LINES, RPT-DETAIL-LINES,
RPT-FOOTER-LINES, etc.




Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:8snjd6$fo1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com...
>
> William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

of
> the same
> > "size".  This is certainly allowed by IBM, MERANT, Realia - and I think
> > several others.
>
> None of the Unisys A Series COBOL compilers (COBOL(68), COBOL74 or
COBOL85)
> allow the use of an index against either a different table or a different
> dimension of the same table.  An index may only be used as an index
against
> the table row of its declaration in those dialects.
>
>     -Chuck Stevens

```

have written several programs where SEARCH ALL was the only way to go.  On
large amounts of data, a SEARCH ALL can make the difference between a 5
minute run time and un-runnable.

Jeff

shine98@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> What I like about standards is that there are so many to choose from :)
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph name.

But we don't have that.

Because of this, I use the following:

01  BIB-IDX                 PIC S9(5)  BINARY.
01  BIB-OCCURS              PIC S9(5)  BINARY  VALUE 100.
01  BIB-MAX                 PIC S9(5)  BINARY  VALUE ZERO.
01  IABRCIB-TABLE.
    05  IABRCIB-RECORD OCCURS 100.
        ...

If a junior programmer steps on THAT index, it won't be my fault.
```

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com

```
I do something similar to this and I also compute the size of the table
so that the value in a variable doesn't have to be changed to
correspond with the value in the occurs clause when the table size
changes. I also would set the value of BIB-OCCURS to 0 and put a note
in ws so that the next programmer would know not to worry about
changing BIB-OCCURS. Seems to work real well.


 COMPUTE BIB-OCCURS =
     LENGTH OF IABRCIB-TABLE / LENGTH OF IABRCIB-RECORD (1)

~shine

I thought about this and shine's idea was the first one I came up with.   I
then considered using search for first space with an at end condition.   No
maximum length values required.   I only did this at initialisation,  the
small overhead outweighed the risk of bad fixes later.   We use far to many
limitted internal tables due to the nature of our applications and the
growing data we are using.   We have a single customer with 5,000 mobile
phones for example,  they doubled due to sales push to 10,000 from one month
to the next.

I try to code out tables sometimes expediency is better than working out the
details.

Ken

Does anybody know how parameter passing is implemented in COBOL?

I'd like to have a COBOL program call C or C++ functions, and I need to
know, how to decode parameters passed by the COBOL program, and where
to put the return value.

Thanks in advance for any suggestions!

Marjeta

#### ↳ Re: Parameter Passing Implementation

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4B779F0BDB7FE814.2DC7DD5233207815.A18795AA05B02748@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<8seuor$b0o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 16 Oct 2000 13:10:55 GMT, marjeta@my-deja.com enlightened us:

>Does anybody know how parameter passing is implemented in COBOL?
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

In Cobol you would, of course, use the Call statement.  When you code:

CALL PRGMA USING PARAM-1, PARAM-2.

The address of PARAM-1 and PARAM-2 are passed to the called (PRGMA)
program.  In the mainframe world, standard IBM register convention is
used.  That is, the address of the first executable statement in PRGMA
is put into register 15; the address of the next statement after the
CALL is put into register 14.  The address of an area that contains
the address(es) of the paramters is placed in register 1 and the
address of an 18 fullword save area is placed in register 13.  

You can place a return code in register 15 which can then be checked
by the Cobol program.  I have no idea how you do this in C.

HTH

Regards,
 
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I Do Whatever My Rice Krispies Tell Me To.

Boycott Mitsubishi Industries
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parameter Passing Implementation

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001016121352.25152.00000681@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<4B779F0BDB7FE814.2DC7DD5233207815.A18795AA05B02748@lp.airnews.net>`

```
 SkippyPB swiegand@neo.rr.com writes ...

[snip]

>In Cobol you would, of course, use the Call statement.  When you code:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There's lots of new features in COBOL that make the above just the bare bones. 

In the latest OS/390 COBOL compilers you can :

* pass arguments BY REFERENCE, BY CONTENT, and BY VALUE. You can also receive
them this way. This was done to facilitate interlanguage communication between
COBOL and C

* set a subroutine to return a value, like a C function

* set a COBOL main to receive a value when invoking a C function (or even a
COBOL subroutine set up properly)

* specify an argument as OMITTED, and test if a parameter has been omitted in a
subroutine

All of these influence the way your COBOL subroutine access the parameters and
returns a value.

Here's a little summary of how COBOL passes parameters in OS/390:

R1 points to a parameter area (the same area is used for all CALLs in the
program and does not clear this out between CALLs)

The parameter area contains
* a(value to be returned) - if RETURNING was specified on the CALL
* a mix of
    - addresses of items passed by reference or by content
    - fullword zeros for omitted arguments
    - copy of any data passed BY VALUE (only certain data types are eligible)
* the last word has its leftmost bit set on (the end_of_list flag) unless the
last argument is being passed BY VALUE

Then, of course, there's the possibilities of simply sharing common storage
using dynamically allocated memory.

<ad>
For details on our course on how to do all this, and more, go to our website at
http://www.trainersfriend.com and click on the link to Topics; here select
Language Environment; on this page you will see "Inter-Language Communication
in OS/390"; click on this link for a course description; from this page you can
also view the course objectives and the detailed course outline.
</ad>

Regards,



Steve Comstock

#### ↳ Re: Parameter Passing Implementation

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39eb2baf.1909435@news>`
- **References:** `<8seuor$b0o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Marjeta,

COBOL generally passes the address of arguments, which is equivalent
to passing a pointer in C.  You can (to a degree) control how
arguments are passed by using modifiers in your call statement, like
this:

CALL 'subroutine' USING 
          BY REFERENCE A
          BY CONTENT B
          BY VALUE 2 SIZE 4
          GIVING returned-value.

Where the "BY REFERENCE' modifier passes a pointer, BY CONTENT passes
the value of the data found in "B", and BY VALUE creates a temporary
variable and then passes the value "2" as a 4-byte binary value.

The arguments are generally passed on the stack in the order
determined by the requirements of the called subroutine.    Returned
values are obtained using GIVING.  

For example, CA-Realia has you add a prefix to the actual subroutine
name that specifies the exact format of the call, e.g,, 

CALL 'S_windowsapi' USING ...

Where the "S_" prefix specifies the compiler should use the Windows
system calling convention.  

Other compilers use different ways of accomplishing this end.

Most COBOL compilers have a section in the documentation that
discusses how to call other languages  

Expect to struggle with this a bit until you get it figured out...

Kevin

On Mon, 16 Oct 2000 13:10:55 GMT, marjeta@my-deja.com wrote:

>Does anybody know how parameter passing is implemented in COBOL?
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parameter Passing Implementation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sfmpm$qpr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8seuor$b0o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39eb2baf.1909435@news>`

```
"Kevin J. Hansen" <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:39eb2baf.1909435@news...

>
> CALL 'subroutine' USING
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is BACKWARDS (sort-of) for BY VALUE and BY CONTENT.  BY VALUE passes
the "data" itself on the "stack" - while BY CONTENT creates a temporary data
items and passes the address of the temporary data item.

NOTE WELL:
  For this entire thread, as mentioned in one comment, it does HELP to know
which COBOL implementation and upon which Operating System, the question
applies.  Although the "semantics" of BY REFERENCE/CONTENT (VALUE) are the
same in all implemenations - the actual underlying structures may differ.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parameter Passing Implementation

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39eb724e.19987901@news>`
- **References:** `<8seuor$b0o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39eb2baf.1909435@news> <8sfmpm$qpr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi, Bill,

Darn, I hate it when I'm wrong...  You're correct for the general
COBOL case.  I've been working with Windows APIs too much for the last
4 years. 

The original post wondered how to call "C" APIs from COBOL.

In CA-Realia COBOL, if you specify the Windows system calling
conventions, BY CONTENT passes the value on the stack.  So, if you
want to call the WinExec Windows API:

UINT WinExec(
  LPCSTR lpCmdLine,  // command line
  UINT uCmdShow      // window style
);

You note that the lpCmdLine is a pointer, and uCmdShow is an unsigned
integer.

The CA-Realia call is:

CALL 'S_WinExec'USING 
          BY REFERENCE COMMAND-LINE
          BY CONTENT SHOW-COMMAND
GIVING RESULT-CODE.

In this case, the BY CONTENT is indeed passing the value, not a
pointer to a temporary data item.

Which only goes to show that interfacing to software written in other
languages requires fairly intimate knowledge of the target's calling
conventions as well as how the COBOL vendor implemented their
third-party language interface.

Kevin

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>the "data" itself on the "stack" - while BY CONTENT creates a temporary data
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

http://www.screenio.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vP_K5.24900$Q8.5308885@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<8seuor$b0o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39eb2baf.1909435@news> <8sfmpm$qpr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <39eb724e.19987901@news>`

```
Well, Kevin you were pretty close.  The prior posters just wasted Marjeta's
time!

I can tell you that on IBM (MVS / VM / OS-390), Wang, Honeywell, and IBM
PC's ('C')
the default method is that for CALL 'xxx' using var1, var2, var3, what it
does is:

the compiler takes 4 address sized words contigously in memory and
does this:
word0                       word1                  word2
word3
address_word1    addrress_var1    address_var2        address_var3

and passes one address:  word0.

Now, some systems add an address of all 00000 at the end (word4),  (32bit
addressing systems)
some systems (24/31 bit addressing systems) set Bit 0 of word3 'on'. (Since
an address can't use
32 bits on a 24/31 bit addressing system, no need to waste a word!)


So generally, the subroutine takes the pointer in R1 / TCB#1, (or parm), and
dereferences that address
, then 'bumps' the address in R1 by (usually 4) whatever is an address sized
int, and repeats
until bit 0 is on, or word = all_zeroes (NULL = invalid address)


Kevin J. Hansen <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:39eb724e.19987901@news...
> Hi, Bill,
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> >the "data" itself on the "stack" - while BY CONTENT creates a temporary
data
> >items and passes the address of the temporary data item.
> >
> >NOTE WELL:
> >  For this entire thread, as mentioned in one comment, it does HELP to
know
> >which COBOL implementation and upon which Operating System, the question
> >applies.  Although the "semantics" of BY REFERENCE/CONTENT (VALUE) are
the
> >same in all implemenations - the actual underlying structures may differ.
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parameter Passing Implementation

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-29T16:10:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ti77o$djd$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8seuor$b0o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39eb2baf.1909435@news> <8sfmpm$qpr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <39eb724e.19987901@news> <vP_K5.24900$Q8.5308885@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
NOTE WELL:
  The attached note EXPLICITLY is talking  about ONLY "BY REFERENCE"
parameter passing (even on the systems that it mentions).  This is (in deed)
the default if you don't specify anything else.  HOWEVER, on EVERY system
(including IBM - where such things as "TCBs" are meaningful) - if you use BY
CONTENT or BY VALUE - you get VERY different parameter passing "techniques".
```

This subject has me trying to remember something from 1960.  I think our
approach was "Declarative" (no subject to each sentence.)  There was an
implied "You" representing the computer, i.e.,
You move a to b.
You perform zilch.
You open file-1, file-2, etc.

do I have this screwed up in my mind?

Warren Simmons
P.S. Be kind.

Imperative programming:
"so called because it is based on commands that update variables held in
storage" Watt 1990

ANSI COBOL 85 is an imperative language...

It's better here to write in english, even if you write a bad english
like mine. That's the rule here.

If you want to learn Cobol in the french language, you can buy
"Programmer en Cobol" from Nino Silverio, Eyrolles, ISBN 2-212-9056-0.
The book comes with examples.

If you want to learn object cobol, the same author published a book
(same editor) named "Programmer en cobol objet".

Sue ce forum, il est pr�f�rable de s'exprimer en anglais. C'est la
r�gle tacite. C'est le seul forum - � ma connaissance - qui traite du
cobol et qui est suivi r�guli�rement par un nombre important
d'intervenants.

En fran�ais, je conseillerai deux bons livres de Nino Silverio aux
�ditions Eyrolles (avec code source des exemples) :
"Programmer en Cobol" pour la base 
"Programmer en Cobol objet" pour qui veut conna�tre les bases du cobol
objet.
Deux livres didactiques, bien faits qui ont le m�rite d'aborder le
cobol sous un angle moderne de programmation structur�e tordant le cou
aux reproches de vetust� du cobol.

J'esp�re que cela vous aidera.

Cordialement,

Alain Reymond

Any chance of letting us know which COBOL compiler it was that
created the files? Each compiler system tends to use its own
file handler for the files it creates. 

MERANT/Microfocus creates its data files with .dat and .idx extensions
and there are ODBC drivers available.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to access

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T02:34:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EFAF0F.395BE241@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8sfsvc$lq1$1@lola.ctv.es>`

```


Jonie wrote:
> 
> Hi group:
> 
>     I have some cobol data base files (*.idx + *.dat) and I would like to
> open them with Access. Can any one help me with this quetion. Thanks a lot.

A less attractive solution than Paddy Coleman's - and depends obviously
on frequency with which you want to do it.

Read your Micro Focus files sequentially and create ascii delimiteds for
Access. Some while since I did it, but it works. (One minor snag, my
end-user had Access V7 - the rich oil companies he was giving the data
to had Access V6 - no likee !)

Currently I use the same concept to transfer old RM/COBOL '74 data files
to Micro Focus NetExpress - RM comp fields are converted to M/F comps,
(using an M/F sub-routine), then output ascii delimiteds to create
NetExpress ISAMs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

set FK=C:\TEST
and in the working-storage:
copy "$FK\FILE-NAME.CPY"

With NetExpress 3.1 I have been trying doing this without success:

1. In the Project Properties menu item
2. In the Environment box klik on "Application"
3. write FK in the variable field
   and C:\TEST in the as the value field
4. klik SET
5. klik OK
6. I have a file named FILE-NAME.CPY in the C:\TEST folder

My NetExpress work area is "D:\work_express\skfs" but if in my source
programs have a line with COPY "$FK\FILE-NAME.CPY". I cann't edit the

new copyfile?

Thanks for help

Fernando

Fernando,

It sounds like you are placing your environment variables in the wrong
place.  The Application area is for settings which will be used at runtime.
As you are using FK in the source code you need to place it in the IDE
section.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman

MERANT International.
