<a id="t-776b8a4395"></a>
## Thread: Converting from ACUCOBOL 85 VISION to ACUCOBOL-GT with ORACLE — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Converting from ACUCOBOL 85 VISION to ACUCOBOL-GT with ORACLE

- **From:** M_champoux@yahoo.ca
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sia98$6qi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello !

We have a small application written with ACUCOBOL 85 with about 15
screens and we want to convert to a windows compatible application.
ACUCOBOL-GT sounds interesting because we can recover a lot of code
from the old application.

I would like to know what are the problems that I should look for !!

I think the conversion from Vision to ORACLE will be easy to do, but
the conversion from plain text screen to windows screen, that's the
part that's scares me the most.

	I would like to have some hints on the matter !!


	Thanks for the response, I appreciate
        Marty


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting from ACUCOBOL 85 VISION to ACUCOBOL-GT with ORACLE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sic1n$p9i$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8sia98$6qi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There are a VARIETY of other "solutions" to this problem, but AcuCOBOL is
quite well known for its "native language" support for GUI environments.
HOWEVER, the biggest issue (in my opinion) is allowing sufficient
time/effort for doing GOOD interface design.  Simply switching from
character based to GUI - without "re-thinking" the interface, is likely to
produce user UNfriendly results.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting from ACUCOBOL 85 VISION to ACUCOBOL-GT with ORACLE

- **From:** Millenium Business Solutions <mbs@labyrinth.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T08:51:55+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39ECD80B.845A5BCC@labyrinth.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8sia98$6qi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,

Suggest you look at Fujitsu PowerCOBOL at www.adtools.com.
This is a very stable system for you to create GUI Windows apps.
You can cut and paste your existing code into PowerCOBOL.
Also, there are no run time charges!

Regards.


M_champoux@yahoo.ca wrote:

> Hello !
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting from ACUCOBOL 85 VISION to ACUCOBOL-GT with ORACLE

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@NoSpamPlease.online.no>
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T03:36:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8V8H5.2911$_o5.13770@typhoon.san.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8sia98$6qi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<M_champoux@yahoo.ca> wrote in message news:8sia98$6qi$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hello !
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the old application.

There are numerous methods to accomplish this. Both using ACUCOBOL-GT and
via third party software.

I don't know third party software too good, but ACUCOBOL-GT provides various
paths to migrate from character to gui. We also provide guides in how to
accomplish this as simple as possible, as well as there is built in
mechanisms in the runtime and AcuBench to semi automate the task of
transferring a character application to GUI. Best of all, is that you can
choose to merge at your own pace, screen by screen.

I have myself been part of a migration of 40 Mb source from character to GUI
some years ago, the move did not introduce many problems.

> I would like to know what are the problems that I should look for !!

The most obvious question is whether you are a non English or not. For non
English applications one have to take into consideration ASCII versus ANSI
issues.

Regardless of approach you go for, I would strongly recommend you to use
much time on prototyping before you start actual implementation. Although
gui seem simple with modern design tools, it really is a challenge to make
an efficient graphical user interface.

You should also consider the fact that gui is event driven, you may pretty
much wrap up the event handling into traditional display/accept statements
in Acucobol, however, it does pay off to think it over a couple of times.
This again, must be seen in the context of what one wishes to accomplish.

> I think the conversion from Vision to ORACLE will be easy to do, but
> the conversion from plain text screen to windows screen, that's the
> part that's scares me the most.

Fear not, how about this:

    Character:
        ACCEPT MY-VAR LINE 10 COLUMN 20.

    Gui:
        ACCEPT ENTRY-FIELD USING MY-VAR LINE 10 COLUMN 20.

Or how about a message box?

    DISPLAY MESSAGE BOX "Hello world".

This is however the simplest approach utilizing the sequential display
accept method. I would recommend using SCREEN SECTION, which will greatly
pay off in later maintenance and enhancement. Actually, if your application
alread is in SCREEN SECTIONs, you're very close to have a running gui app.

> I would like to have some hints on the matter !!

It is such a vast area, I think you should start looking into it, and just
ship specific issues as you experience them, that would be much easier to
handle. There are several developers using Acucobol-GT that monitors this
NG, and I am pretty sure you will get a lot of good advises on your way.

Regards,

Gisle Forseth

I work for Acucorp, however, my opinions expressed in contributions to this
news group is my own and does in no way express opinions on behalf of the
company.
```

---

<a id="t-f883b169c4"></a>
## Thread: Efficiency of calls & invokes — 9 msgs

### Efficiency of calls & invokes

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EC50C1.E23A2A94@brazee.net>`

```
Has anybody done studies on how much it costs to replace in-program code
with calls and invokes?

I would guess that there is a cost, but I have seen programs do calls to
read each record of huge files, quite quickly.  (I guess it loads once,
and once in memory is almost as fast as optimized in-line code) 
Obviously, an optimizer won't look at both programs at compile time.

My biggest concern is where I create a call in order to access a
different subschema, but that could depend upon the database.  Call
efficiency in general can probably be tweaked by your system and system
programmers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Efficiency of calls & invokes

- **From:** "Scott A. Sirles" <scott.sirles@jrcg-inc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39ec6a08_1@corp.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<39EC50C1.E23A2A94@brazee.net>`

```
The answer to your question is critically dependent on your platform and
whether you are calling by reference (faster) or calling by value (slower).
```

A) Which COBOL product are you using?
B) Which operating System are you running on?
C) Are you using "dynamic" or "static" CALLs (or EXE vs dynamic link
libraries - depending upon environment)

I think that Howard was the original poster of this question - and he
usually posts related to OS/390 (z/OS ?).  If so, IBM did do some analysis
with VS COBOL II - but I don't think published anything after that.  Let me
know if you want me to research the status of that "stuff".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency of calls & invokes

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T21:57:12+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-CD53BD.21571218102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<39EC50C1.E23A2A94@brazee.net> <39ec6a08_1@corp.newsfeeds.com> <8sicai$v99$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8sicai$v99$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> To add to this, we would need to know:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Ok, first off this only applies to OS/390 nee z/OS Mainframe stuff as 
far as I know.

1. A "dynamic" call can be resolved ONCE on the first invocation.
   That is, it can be found on disk, loaded into memory and will stay
   there for the duration of the job.
2. Dynamically called modules can be cached into memory for every ones 
use.
3. The directory (of PDSes) that is search for a dynamic module can be 
cached into memory.

Net result.
First invocation - find and read module into memory.
All subsequent invocations - use to copy in memory.
Which is how different from static linkage?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency of calls & invokes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001018171333.10562.00000091@ng-fh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<josephk-CD53BD.21571218102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
[snip]

Joe Katnic writes ...

>1. A "dynamic" call can be resolved ONCE on the first invocation.
>   That is, it can be found on disk, loaded into memory and will stay
>   there for the duration of the job.

Well, the duration of the job step. Unless the calling program issues a CANCEL.

>2. Dynamically called modules can be cached into memory for every ones 
>use.

Well, loaded into the job step link pack area for use by all callers in the
jobstep. Or into the system link pack area (takes an act of God, more or less,
in most shops) to be shared by all callers in all address spaces. Module must
be RENT in these cases.

>3. The directory (of PDSes) that is search for a dynamic module can be 
>cached into memory.

Not explicitly.

>
>Net result.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, for a static call, you don't get the big performance hit on the first
call. And even on subsequent dynamic calls, there is a hit for the lookup of
where the loaded module is. On a static call, the linkage is a few instructions
resulting to a direct branch to the target. If a routine is called millions of
times in a run, this can add up significantly.

Dynamic calls are definitely the way to go in most cases. But for high
performance applications, static calls can still make a big difference.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency of calls & invokes (procedure pointers)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EEA475.65906EBB@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<josephk-CD53BD.21571218102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <20001018171333.10562.00000091@ng-fh1.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock wrote:
> 
> [snip]
> Dynamic calls are definitely the way to go in most cases. But for high
> performance applications, static calls can still make a big difference.

On May 24, 2000, I posted the following on comp.lang.cobol under the
title "Dynamic Call using procedure pointer":

*** original ***

I recently had a conversation with a programmer in another department,
and he tells me they are using dynamic calls with procedure pointers. 
This is using COBOL for MVS & VM, under 0S/390 release 2.8.

I've never seen this before, but apparently they also use EXTERNAL
working-storage items.  All programs that use exactly the same 01
EXTERNAL record definitions have them available in their working-storage
section without naming them in the call statement or procedure division
using clause.  The EXTERNAL 01 records cannot have VALUE clauses in
them, and they apparently reside in working-storage for both the caller
and callee.

The call is set up something like this:

01  SHARED-DATA-RECORD   EXTERNAL.
    05  WHATEVER-ELSE     ...

01  PTR-SUBPROG         USAGE IS PROCEDURE-POINTER.

SET PTR-SUBPROG TO ENTRY "PROGNAME"
CALL PTR-SUBPROG

My colleague tells me they've done extensive performance testing, and
this form of dynamic subprogram call with procedure pointers and
EXTERNAL data items is significantly faster than a standard dynamic call
(CALL MY-SUBPROGRAM USING PARM-1, PARM-2, et cetera).

Has anyone else used this technique?  Any comments on it? 

*** end quote ***

I had further conversations with my colleague, and the way he explained
it to me is that once you have obtained addressability to the
dynamically called subprogram using a procedure pointer, every
subsequent call to the same routine has no more overhead than a PERFORM
statement in your mainline.  A Standard dynamic call must check each
time to see if the contents of the program name variable have changed. 
He verified this by testing, proving to his satisfaction that there was
a measurable performance benefit to using procedure pointers for dynamic
calls (at least in an OS/390 environment).  In a large batch program
that may call the same routine ten million times, the performance gain
could be significant.  You can have all the benefits of dynamic calls
*and* the high performance normally associated with static calls.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency of calls & invokes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-1F3516.20001119102000@news.m.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<josephk-CD53BD.21571218102000@news.m.iinet.net.au> <20001018171333.10562.00000091@ng-fh1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20001018171333.10562.00000091@ng-fh1.aol.com>, 
scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote:

> [snip]
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
WELL, actually loaded into VLF for subsequent retrieval by anyone.

> >2. Dynamically called modules can be cached into memory for every ones 
> >use.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
See ABOVE!
 
> >3. The directory (of PDSes) that is search for a dynamic module can be 
> >cached into memory.
> 
> Not explicitly.
>
See VLF and LLA for STEPLIB's. 
> >
> >Net result.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Agreed.
> Regards,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Efficiency of calls & invokes

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T04:20:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001018002034.14708.00000191@ng-ct1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<39EC50C1.E23A2A94@brazee.net>`

```
Having worked at places that did both static and dynamic calls (we're talking
OS/390 here folks) I can state that static are more efficient because the load
module is right there with the other program - linked together.  Of course it
can be a maintenance nightmare because any change to the called program
requires a recompile of the calling programs and one can loose track of who is
calling whoml.

Back when we were running 256K mainframes things like this made a huge
difference, as did paging and so on.

Today the dynamic call is the call d'jour and when we upgraded to COBOL II then
to COBOL MVS we changed most of our calls in this shop to dynamic for what we
were told ease of maintenance and 'surprise' efficency!  Something to do with
LE and all that.  Yes I guess the sysprogs can tweak stuff to increase
efficency as well.

We have some packages that have static links and all that for I/O to IDMS
databases (which is the only IDMS we have - everything else is DB2 and VSAM)
and they are the worst system hogs we have.  The code in some of the programs
is enough to give one night terrors.

Eileen

As for z/OS - I think you're jumping the gun there Bill :)  Another 3 years
wasn't it?
```

---

<a id="t-53c5e6eaa5"></a>
## Thread: NT performance question clarified — 3 msgs

### NT performance question clarified

- **From:** lynne@nibordesigns.com (Lynne)
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:41:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4fNG5.20979$N%1.8497782@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
There were three test case scenarios.  In all three cases, the data read, 
manipulated and written to the database was identical.  In all three cases, the 
application code was essentially the same.

The application requires a large amount of run-time memory allocated to 
many large arrays.  In all three scenarios, the *structure* of each array was 
the same.  In one case, we minimized the length of the arrays to reduce the 
amount of run-time memory required to run.

The first case was run on the NT server with Oracle.  With full-size arrays, it 
ran at a deplorable speed.

The second case was run on the Win98 laptop.  With full-size arrays it ran in 
1/10th the previous case.

Trying to isolate the problem, we ran just the reads and writes to the database 
and the speed was more than acceptable.  So in the third trial, we minimized 
the array sizes *for* the test sample and it ran almost parallel to the time 
experienced on the laptop.

In order to run at full volume data, we need to bump the array sizes back up.  
But it won't run acceptably.  I do not see that it is a database problem 
because we've isolated those processes and found them to be sound.

I believe the problem to be that we've somehow run out of run-time memory and 
the application is caching or paging out.  I believe there is enough RAM, so 
wonder what we need to do to prevent the caching.

Thanks to all who have answered thus far!
Lynne
```

#### ↳ Re: NT performance question clarified

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39ec3edd.566869493@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<4fNG5.20979$N%1.8497782@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I missed the original message.  Three Questions:

What vendor's COBOL are you using, and what version?

Second How much RAM in the NT machine, and is it Server or
WorkStation?

On Tue, 17 Oct 2000 00:41:36 GMT, lynne@nibordesigns.com (Lynne)
wrote:

>There were three test case scenarios.  In all three cases, the data read, 
>manipulated and written to the database was identical.  In all three cases, the 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: NT performance question clarified

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39ec413f.567479487@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<4fNG5.20979$N%1.8497782@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I found the original message.  Can you change the database on the NT
machine to access an Access database instead of the Oracle (and stop
the Oracle service when you do it).

I'm assuming that Oracle is running locally on the NT box - you will
need to stop this service.  Actually, Oracle is going to take quite a
lot of memory overhead - it should run on a separate server from the
application.  You very well could be in a memory swapping situation on
NT and not on Windows 98 only because Oracle is running on NT, but not
on the Win98 box.  We are still pretty far from comparing apples and
apples.


On Tue, 17 Oct 2000 00:41:36 GMT, lynne@nibordesigns.com (Lynne)
wrote:

>There were three test case scenarios.  In all three cases, the data read, 
>manipulated and written to the database was identical.  In all three cases, the 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 - Dialog

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8slomk$10f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8sk46n$prh$2@news.inet.tele.dk> <8ska3n$kch$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <susgglsfurp923@corp.supernews.com>`

```
This is NOT true.  You need to use the correct directives to get support for
COMP-6 - but it is supported with some "dialect support" enabled.

P.S. Despite the other thread on the subject, VERY few implementations do
support unsigned packed-decimal fields - but this PROBABLY ties in with the
fact that this is not a "commonly used" definition.  (Where space is the
highest priority, BINARY is used - when Packed-Decimal is needed for some
reasons, the inclusion of a sign is most common.)
```

## Thread: COBOL and Dynamic SQL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COBOL and Dynamic SQL

- **From:** "Michel" <mm@glo.be>
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sk7og$3cc$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```
Does anyone knows how the SQL PREPARE statement works in COBOL ? I always
get the SQLCODE -104 (illegal symbol) when i execute the SQL
PREPARE-statement

Thanks,

Michel
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Dynamic SQL

- **From:** "Brad Prothero" <brad.prothero@clarica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s9iH5.71$1p2.12408@news.corpcomm.net>`
- **References:** `<8sk7og$3cc$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```

Michel <mm@glo.be> wrote in message news:8sk7og$3cc$1@news0.skynet.be...
> Does anyone knows how the SQL PREPARE statement works in COBOL ? I always
> get the SQLCODE -104 (illegal symbol) when i execute the SQL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My guess is the string that you are preparing is not in correct SQL syntax.
Here is a code snipet that I have working in my code. STMVAR is defined in
the SQL Declare section. This code is being passed the index in the Database
where the information is located. This will setup the cursor to grab that
information.

            01  SELECT-STRING.
                02 FILLER                            PIC X(34)
                    VALUE 'SELECT * FROM BENEFITS WHERE ID = '.
                02 SEL-IDX                         PIC 99.
            MOVE PASSINDEX TO SEL-IDX.
            MOVE SELECT-STRING TO STMVAR.
            Exec SQL
                Declare Cur2 Cursor for STMIDT
            End-Exec.
            Exec SQL
                Prepare STMIDT From :STMVAR
            End-Exec.

Brad Prothero
```

In article <8sl1ic$fit$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <shine98@my-deja.com> wrote:
>I like to round my fields with a file - a mill bastard file. It takes
>off those sharp edges and makes it all smoove! Although, in the old
>days the filings were hard to clean from the magnetic core.

Hmmmm... reminds me of an Olde Joke:

A Very Proper Woman enters a hardware store and asks the proprietor to
help her choose a file.  Without really thinking he says 'Well, you really
can't go wrong with an eight-inch bastard.'

The woman reddened and then replied, gamely, 'Thanks... but I think I'd
rather have a six-inch son-of-a-bitch.'

(and the Rather Odd Part is... this used to be a Man's Joke and now it can
be told on prime-time television in the USA; times have changed)

DD
```

<a id="t-275ab4994f"></a>
## Thread: Round Up — 18 msgs

### Re: Round Up

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T04:50:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<24vH5.1309$TO4.94404@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8skbgn$qrl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified>`

```
Round up is a bookkeeping term.

Only whole dollars and cents are used in the USA.  So if the remainder is
equal to or greater than .005, adding a half cent will round it up.  In
some applications the requirement is to ignore the rounding and use the
whole $ and perhaps cents.

Variations exist.  In my upbringing you could not get a job unless you
could round per instructions.

I ate enough to become round to prove I qualified.

Warren Simmons

"Dwight Scott Miller" <Merlin43PhD@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified...
> On Sun, 18 Oct 3900 14:19:12, ctowers <ctowers@my-deja.com> wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> RTFM!
```

#### ↳ Re: Round Up

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EEE96B.98B06580@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8skbgn$qrl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified> <24vH5.1309$TO4.94404@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> Round up is a bookkeeping term.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> whole $ and perhaps cents.

What is interesting is accounting & finance functions, where the
accountants have procedures to make sure all fractional cents are
accounted for.  We have to do it their way - which makes it nice that we
are using a language designed for business, and not a bunch of libraries
which haven't been designed around our particular accountants.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Round Up

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>

```
It is also interesting that because Accounting has to deal with product and
product can have more decimals, the ROUNDED option is optional.
Why else would the instructor ask them to
work out how to do it.  What amused me was apparently the question was not
asked in class.

Warren Simmons

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Round Up

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sof2i$kjnpf$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`
- **References:** `<8skbgn$qrl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified> <24vH5.1309$TO4.94404@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <39efa483.102460941@207.126.101.100>`

```

> On Sun, 18 Oct 3900 14:19:12, ctowers <ctowers@my-deja.com> wrote:
>
> Can anyone help me with this?
>

The obvious answer is to do your own homework.  I will help, though...


01 YOUR-ANSWER   PIC 9(9)V9(2).
01 THE-FIELD            PIC 9(9)V9(2).
01 THE-DIVISOR       PIC 9(3).

COMPUTE YOUR-ANSWER ROUNDED =
   (.5 + (THE-FIELD / THE-DIVISOR)
END-COMPUTE.

This piece of code would work....almost.  The basic idea is sound, but there
are two things wrong with it that make it unworkable.  Figure out what they
are, and you have your answer....

I'll give you a hint, one is quite obvious and deals with the basic
equation.  The other is less obvious, and is due to the fields used and the
way the ROUNDED directive works...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Round Up

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T16:16:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0gmK5.11488$oZ5.69915@news1.atl>`

```
    03  Number            PIC 999.999.
    03  Rounded-Number    PIC 999.

    ADD .999 TO Number GIVING Rounded-Number

Rounds up any non integer value in Number to the next higher integer.
```

of these round UP.  Function INTEGER (1.9) returns 1 and not 2.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Round Up

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-28T19:35:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tfr9r$9ap$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8skbgn$qrl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified> <24vH5.1309$TO4.94404@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <39efa483.102460941@207.126.101.100> <39F76CF5.9912C7D6@boeing.com> <0gmK5.11488$oZ5.69915@news1.atl> <MNzK5.987$xW6.28439@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39fae418.178993135@207.126.101.100>`

```
Using
 FUNCTION Integer (whatever) + 1

will ALWAYS round up (even for negative numbers) - if what you want is for
rounding up to the next highest integer.

If, however, you use INTEGER-PART, it will not work "correctly" for both
negative and positive numbers.

OBVIOUSLY, if you are trying to round "decimal points" - from 3 to 2 (for
example) - then this technique would NOT work - unless you used something
like,

  Compute Temp-Item = Original-Item * 100
  Compute rounded-up = (Function Integer (temp-item) + 1) / 100

which doesn't seem "cleaner or easier" (IMHO) than the original adding of
.999 - but would work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Round Up

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-30T08:11:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_jfL5.2034$M85.18832@news4.atl>`
- **References:** `<8skbgn$qrl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified> <24vH5.1309$TO4.94404@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <39efa483.102460941@207.126.101.100> <39F76CF5.9912C7D6@boeing.com> <0gmK5.11488$oZ5.69915@news1.atl> <MNzK5.987$xW6.28439@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39fae418.178993135@207.126.101.100> <8tfr9r$9ap$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <uannvsgqivc4q3ed6g2h6bb6jn0ijoro84@4ax.com> <vWZK5.10544$UL.630623@bgtnsc07-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net> wrote:
> I've always used...
> FUNCTION INTEGER (whatever) + .999999999999999999
> ...or as many decimals as the compiler will take.

Actually, using more decimals than is specified in the PIC of the
target field can get you into trouble.  If intermediate results are
rounded by the hardware or software at any point before storing the
final result, any 9's to the right of the current precision could
be rounded up, causing a number to be double rounded, incrementing
it too much. :-)  This type of rounding is best performed as one
step, using the precise number of .999's to match the PIC of the
data field being rounded up.  If you need to round negative numbers,
you can use either of the following methods.  Method 1 is far more
efficient. :-)

Method 1:

    03  WS-NUMBER    PIC S999.9999.
    03  WS-INTEGER   PIC S999.

    IF (WS-NUMBER < 0)
        SUBTRACT .9999 FROM WS-NUMBER GIVING WS-INTEGER
    ELSE
        ADD .9999 TO WS-NUMBER GIVING WS-ROUNDED
    END-IF

Method 2:

    03  WS-NUMBER    PIC S999.9999.
    03  WS-UNSIGNED  PIC  999.9999.
    03  WS-INTEGER   PIC S999.

    MOVE WS-NUMBER TO WS-UNSIGNED
    COMPUTE WS-INTEGER = WS-NUMBER + .9999 * (WS-NUMBER / WS-UNSIGNED)

(Note: I know the parenthesis are not required here, but I believe
       they make the calculation clearer.)

It is surprising to me that the 1989 Intrinsic Functions module did
not include the Absolute Value and Sign functions, either of which
would have simplified this and lots of other similar code [e.g.
COMPUTE WS-INTEGER = WS-NUMBER + .9999 * FUNCTION SIGN(WS-NUMBER) ].
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Round Up

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-30T08:38:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39FD95FC.868CDD9@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8skbgn$qrl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <B31Jl8xr32q4-pn2-6ywecvId6HGE@localhost.modified> <24vH5.1309$TO4.94404@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <39efa483.102460941@207.126.101.100> <39F76CF5.9912C7D6@boeing.com> <0gmK5.11488$oZ5.69915@news1.atl> <MNzK5.987$xW6.28439@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <39fae418.178993135@207.126.101.100> <8tfr9r$9ap$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Using
>  FUNCTION Integer (whatever) + 1
>
> will ALWAYS round up (even for negative numbers) - if what you want is for
> rounding up to the next highest integer.

So what is 3.00 rounded up?

And given a request like this, I would always double check to find out just
what the user wants when the number is negative.  Too many times, what I think
they mean is exactly what they say - but in a case such as this - it ain't
necessarily so!

One can always look for a fraction and then decide what to do.
```

### OT (sort-of) Re: Amdahl Exits Mainframe Market

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8snd4r$hi4$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ED3653126C90D311A0160000F84973610222E563@MSFCMSG4.MSFC.NASA.GOV>`

```
From the IBM-MAIN list - and of possible interest to some comp.lang.cobol
people.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT (sort-of) Re: Amdahl Exits Mainframe Market

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T02:49:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F3A714.862FB2FF@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ED3653126C90D311A0160000F84973610222E563@MSFCMSG4.MSFC.NASA.GOV> <8snd4r$hi4$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> From the IBM-MAIN list - and of possible interest to some comp.lang.cobol
> people.
<snip>
> > >
> > > "I thought I would have some negative reaction," Mr. Tajiri said. "But
> > > most
> > > of the e-mails I have received from employees say that my decision was
> > > right."
<snip>
Yeah, those of us that use to work there who tried to get
management to attack the up and coming markets were laid off
years ago...  As an example, I just wonder what would have
happened to Amdahl's market share had the Firebird (Intel 386
based S/370 emulation system -- 1989/90) been put on the market? 
Or, how about certifying the 5890 & 5990 boxes for DOS/VSE in
preparation for IBM having to make VSE use the XA I/O
instructions (since we all knew that the S/370 base
architecture's days were numbered -- 1988/90)?  What about Key
Computer -- what ever happened to that Unix Super computer
system?

When I use to work there, Amdahl had IBM worried.  Then came the
layoffs (began in 1989, 30 days after the big quake in the SF Bay
area).  Finally Fujitsu bought out the company and quietly (IMHO)
removed the top managers -- the ones that caused Amdahl to be so
indecisive -- no one that I knew there in 1995-6 could tell me
what direction the company would take.

Don't get me wrong -- I'm gonna miss those Red Boxes and folders
that use to get you a 10% discount from IBM (as soon as the IBM
sales person saw them, you got 10% off without saying a word
according to all the DP managers I've talked to).  I truly
enjoyed working there, and still miss it to this day.
```

<a id="t-df3706d24c"></a>
## Thread: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB? — 7 msgs

### Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** hollanjo <jay.holland@mailcity.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<suubsqglan4a77@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I have a Variable length file that I need to select records from, and I 
want the output to be fixed length.  

Using:
SYSIN :                                                      
 INCLUDE COND=((16,4,CH,EQ,C'SI35'),OR,(16,5,CH,EQ,C'SI395'))
 SORT FIELDS=COPY                                            
 OUTREC FIELDS=(5,16442)
I get 'Outrec RDW not included'.  I imagine this is a common task, so I 
hopefully don't need to write a pgm for this.  Appreciate any 
suggestions.                              
```

#### ↳ Re: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sncb9$alc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<suubsqglan4a77@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Jay,
  Can you show us the JCL for your "output file"? I certainly won't SWEAR
that this is where the problem is - but I think we should see it to give you
a good idea.  (If your output is an "existing" file, can you tell us what
its DCB attributes are?)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** hollanjo <jay.holland@mailcity.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<suuitufgi7fj11@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<suubsqglan4a77@corp.supernews.com> <8sncb9$alc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Here is everything.  Thanks.  The SORTLT2 is the select statement 
previously described.
//***************************************************************
//LISTSRT  EXEC PGM=SORT,TIME=10,REGION=4096K                    
//***************************************************************
//*ORTIN   DD  DSN=ISS.RPS17.XRPS11D3.HOLL2.SRTLST.T,DISP=SHR    
//SORTIN   DD  DSN=ISS.RPS17.XRPS11D2.HOLL2.LOGBKP(0),DISP=SHR   
//SORTOUT  DD  DSN=ISS.RPS17.XRPS11D3.HOLL3.SRTLTC.T,            
//             DISP=(,CATLG,DELETE),                             
//             UNIT=DEBUG,SPACE=(TRK,(50,10),RLSE),              
//             DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=16442)                        
//SORTMSG  DD  SYSOUT=*                                          
//SYSOUT   DD  SYSOUT=*                                          
//SYSUDUMP DD  SYSOUT=*                                          
//SORTWK01 DD  UNIT=DEBUG,SPACE=(TRK,(20,10),RLSE)               
//SORTWK02 DD  UNIT=DEBUG,SPACE=(TRK,(20,10),RLSE)               
//SORTWK03 DD  UNIT=DEBUG,SPACE=(TRK,(20,10),RLSE)               
//SYSIN    DD  DSN=RETHOLL.ISS.CTCLIB(SORTLT2),DISP=SHR          
//*******************************************************

you
> a good idea.  (If your output is an "existing" file, can you tell us what
> its DCB attributes are?)
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

#### ↳ Re: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2pbyj0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<suubsqglan4a77@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 19 Oct 2000 17:30:02 -0000, hollanjo wrote:

>I have a Variable length file that I need to select records from, and I 
>want the output to be fixed length.  
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You can't do this in one run, but it is easy in two.
In the first one use

RECORD TYPE=V,LENGTH=xxx
OUTREC FIELDS=(1,16447)

Then run the following on the output

SORT FIELDS=COPY
RECORD TYPE=F,LENGTH=16447
OUTREC FIELDS=(5,16442)

As long as there are no records shorter than 16442 in your file, this will
work

Hope this helps
Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sndpa$9dr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<suubsqglan4a77@corp.supernews.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2pbyj0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
Alternatively (according to one of my "usually reliable sources" <G>),

   ***

  To get this to work with DFSORT (and probably Syncsort), change the OUTREC
statement to:

     OUTFIL OUTREC=(5,16442),CONVERT

CONVERT tells DFSORT to change from VB to FB.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** hollanjo <jay.holland@mailcity.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<suumela20h8ha7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<suubsqglan4a77@corp.supernews.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.g2pbyj0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <8sndpa$9dr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Success!!!  Thank you both very much.  The 2 phase approach gave me an 
initial problem, which was probably due to the output DCB.  Prior to 
changing that, I tried the CONVERT suggestion and that did it.

Thanks again.

> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

OUTREC
> statement to:

I

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

I
> > >hopefully don't need to write a pgm for this.  Appreciate any
> > >suggestions.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > As long as there are no records shorter than 16442 in your file, this 
will
> > work
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

#### ↳ Re: Can Syncsort chg VB data to FB?

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8so421$dipvu$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`

```

hollanjo <jay.holland@mailcity.com> wrote in message

> I have a Variable length file that I need to select records from, and I
> want the output to be fixed length.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi,
Try a search on half.com and some of the other used-book stores
online.  At least you would have the agony of paying $50 for a text in
case you don't like the language.
If you are near a tech college go check out what they offer, and check
out what is in the text in their bookstore.
!!!  You can still get Fujitzu compilers for free at www.adtools.com.
This is about all you would need to do to get a great look at the
language. 
I was just there for a quick look, and the student section has the
manuals for download too !!!  Please go to,
http://www.adtools.com/student/index.htm
& check the site.  I remember that version 3 was free, but I'm not
sure after that one.  !!!
There is a freeware Cobol compiler in some of the download areas but
the last time I read the documentation it was something that was
started and never got finished( the guy developing the compiler passed
away).
You'd do best to work through Fujitzu.
I hope this helps,
GH

<a id="t-db269b6d0d"></a>
## Thread: What direction should I go now. — 3 msgs

### What direction should I go now.

- **From:** "slapper" <srahi@together.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PI1I5.14318$4u4.380562@nntp1.onemain.com>`

```
I need some suggestions. Next year I will have 1 year's of experience as
mostly a Cobol/JCL/Easytrieve/Native Call (my company does not have db2)
programmer.  I'm beginning to feel bored with cobol now and I've decided to
learn some more languages.
The problem is what language should I study. I probably won't learn anything
new in my company. What language would best go with cobol and will be valued
a lot my companies in the future.
Any suggestions ?
```

#### ↳ Re: What direction should I go now.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F0A9CA.89754D89@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<PI1I5.14318$4u4.380562@nntp1.onemain.com>`

```


slapper wrote:
> 
> I need some suggestions. Next year I will have 1 year's of experience as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions ?

Find out what kinds of jobs are available around where you want to
work.  Decide which of these look interesting, what they pay, and what
tools they use.  Learn the tools which get you the job you want.
```

#### ↳ Re: What direction should I go now.

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T10:01:26+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8svuvh$fvl@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<PI1I5.14318$4u4.380562@nntp1.onemain.com>`

```
There are two ways to respond to your question:

1.  Look in your local area to see what types of jobs "seem" to be highly
sort after.
I would suspect (if it's like Australia) to see lots of
* Microsoft jobs like Visual Basic, SQL Server Admin, MS C++ etc
* Internet technologies like Java, Javascript/HTML, ASP and with buzz words
like eCommerce experience.
Look in papers and online web sites and decide for yourself what seems to be
popular.

2.  The second way to answer your question is to say you should pursue
something your interested in.
Even if it doesn't look like there are lots of opportunities for direct
commercial applicatiosn for what you
do.  You will enjoy learning, it won't be as difficult to motivate yourself,
it will most likely still be beneficial
for future employement and it will be fun.

Ray Smith


slapper <srahi@together.net> wrote in message
news:PI1I5.14318$4u4.380562@nntp1.onemain.com...

<a id="t-ca8e379a6a"></a>
## Thread: Cobol to Natural - Bull to Unisys to Amdahl?? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Cobol to Natural - Bull to Unisys to Amdahl??

- **From:** <abe.lincoln@whitehouse.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
    I have almost 5 years of COBOL programming experience on a Bull and
Unisys mainframes.  Since this type of machines are limited in Texas my plan
was to go to a IBM mainframe with either PeopleSoft or Oracle applications
since I felt experience with the latter would be more marketable after
retirement (4 1/2 years away).  I am restricted by the fact that I need to
stay in state government until retirement.    Also, mainframe jobs are not
real plentiful in Austin at this time.
    At this time, I have a offer to go to a Amdahl shop which uses Natural.
I have heard that the JCL for Amdahl is very similar to the IBM.  Also, is
Natural a language that would be a good fit for someone who knows COBOL.
    Your comments are appreciated.
                                                                Susan
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to Natural - Bull to Unisys to Amdahl??

- **From:** akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent-2110000847420001@ip-64-38-141-116.dialup.seanet.com>`
- **References:** `<39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
In article <39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net>,
<abe.lincoln@whitehouse.nospam.com> wrote:

>     I have almost 5 years of COBOL programming experience on a Bull and
> Unisys mainframes.  Since this type of machines are limited in Texas my plan
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                                 Susan

Susan,

Up here in the Pacific Northwest, there are still a few organizations
which use Natural and COBOL, but typically the number of openings for such
opportunities are few and far between.  However, it never hurst to get
such additional experience.

As for working in an Amdahl shop, it is true that the "technical
environment" on an Amdahl machine is very similiar to an IBM machine.  You
can think of an Amdahl machine as if it were an IBM "clone".  When I
worked at Security Pacific Bank in the eraly '90s, I was working on an
Amdahl machine, and to me, there were no glaring differences between that
environment and working on an IBM machine.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol to Natural - Bull to Unisys to Amdahl??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sspoq$984$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net> <akent-2110000847420001@ip-64-38-141-116.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
Did everyone see the news report last week that Amdahl has explicitly stated
that they are GETTING OUT of the IBM compatible mainframe market?  If not,
check out the IBM-MAIN newsgroup and there have been a number of threads on
this topic.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to Natural - Bull to Unisys to Amdahl??

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SBfQ2EAnbH95Ewmv@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Natural and Cobol are similar in so far as they are both English-like
programming languages sharing much the same syntax. I went from Cobol to
Natural (and back again, and again...) with no problem and so should
you. Natural was designed to access the Adabas database from Software AG
in Darmstadt, Germany. There are two flavours of Natural, one and two.
Natural 1 is unstructured code and Natural 2 is structured only in so
far as you are forced to put your data items at the front of the
program. However, I like Natural chiefly because it allows a quicker and
easier way of writing online programs than Cobol/CICS. For the record,
you can do in a few lines with Natural what a 200 line Cobol program can
do. But there again you can do the same in two lines of Ramis. Go for
the Natural job. Don't worry about Amdahl doing away with IBM
compatibility. Even if there are few mainframe jobs using Natural you
can always find work on other boxes (I learned on an IBM mainframe and
now work on a Unix box).  

In article <39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net>, abe.lincoln@whitehouse.nospam.
com writes
>    I have almost 5 years of COBOL programming experience on a Bull and
>Unisys mainframes.  Since this type of machines are limited in Texas my plan
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to Natural - Bull to Unisys to Amdahl??

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T02:56:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F3A8C6.46271736@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<39f11ec9_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
abe.lincoln@whitehouse.nospam.com wrote:
> 
>     I have almost 5 years of COBOL programming experience on a Bull and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                                 Susan

Susan:

The Amdahl box runs the IBM operating system (VM or OS/390 --
MVS).  As an applications programmer you will not be able to tell
an IBM from Amdahl from HDS if someone doesn't show you
explicitly how.  Therefore, all the messages you will see will be
the same as if they were produced on the equivalent IBM system. 
JCL under OS/390 or MVS is JCL.  It is coded the same.

As for Natural and the others, I can't really address that.
```

## Thread: Win98 compared to WinNT 4.0 with NetExpress and Sp2 — 6 msgs

### Win98 compared to WinNT 4.0 with NetExpress and Sp2

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:51:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001019205141.21873.00000504@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

We have been selling our application on Win95 and Win98 terminals.  My boss
recently asked if we could also run under WinNT 4.0.  So I loaded two identical
terminals one with Win98 and one with NT.  Much to my surprise the NT terminal
appears to run much faster.  Has anyone else found this to be true ?  And could
anyone offer an expanation ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Win98 compared to WinNT 4.0 with NetExpress and Sp2

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<20001019205141.21873.00000504@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
File access and disk manipulation is much faster with NTFS file system
on NT than with the FAT32 on Win98.

Hi Jerry,

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Win98 compared to WinNT 4.0 with NetExpress and Sp2

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sp4fj$6bt$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20001019205141.21873.00000504@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

Firstly, I would ask if the "terminals" are of a similar specification?  If
so, then
in general terms you would expect to see this sort of result.  However, bear
in mind that many aspects can affect performance.

Windows 95/98/ME are a hybrid operating system which contains a great
deal of 16 bit code (for backwards compatibility with DOS etc.).  This means
that these operating systems run 16 bit code well.  They also have the
ability
to execute 32 bit code but the compromises made for backwards
compatibility mean that this is not the optimum solution.

Windows NT/2000 is the opposite.  NT's support for 16 bit software is
quite basic.  However, NT is 32 bit through-and-through (again,
generally speaking - lets not get in to the "old" OS debates) and this
yields better performance when running 32 bit code.

As Microsoft themselves say, if you want to run primarily 16 bit programs
and games then use 95/98/ME.  On the other hand, if you intend to run 32 bit
code in the main then NT should be your choice.

I hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20001019205141.21873.00000504@ng-cl1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
>
> We have been selling our application on Win95 and Win98 terminals.  My
boss
> recently asked if we could also run under WinNT 4.0.  So I loaded two
identical
> terminals one with Win98 and one with NT.  Much to my surprise the NT
terminal
> appears to run much faster.  Has anyone else found this to be true ?  And
could
> anyone offer an expanation ?
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Win98 compared to WinNT 4.0 with NetExpress and Sp2

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T01:55:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39efa5ec.102822427@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<20001019205141.21873.00000504@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
It runs contrary to the anectodal evidence posted here before... but

NT is a true 32 bit OS and 95/98 is not.  That might explain the
difference.

Have you tried Win2K?  I like it a lot more than NT 4.0.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-dd5c242e3a"></a>
## Thread: Hexadecimal literals — 3 msgs

### Hexadecimal literals

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <rosa.fischer@mch.sni.de>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T07:11:31+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39EFD403.D01D7A0@mch.sni.de>`

```
Today I need some advice about hexadecimal literals. IBM and Merant
support hexadecimal literals and I need to know what are they used
for and what length is normally used - shorter than 30 bytes or longer
than 100? Does anybody use them in copy replacing or replace statements?
Thank you in advance

Sincerely
Rosa Fischer
```

#### ↳ Re: Hexadecimal literals

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T01:12:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sonin$tpr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39EFD403.D01D7A0@mch.sni.de>`

```
There is no "general rule" that applies to all cases, but the MOST common
(usual) case for using hex literals is for "populating" (or comparing to) a
field that has characters in it that aren't in the "normal" print/screen
"displayable" set of characters (such as a X'00' which is used to interface
with those C programs that use "null-terminated strings")  Similarly X'0A'
is either a line feed or carriage control (I can never remember which) in
many systems.

The ONLY restriction on "length" is that the entire literal may not exceed
the "normal" length of an alphanumeric literal (when you divide by two).
Therefore X'00' counts as a "one-byte" alphanumeric literal.

In some systems it is QUITE common to see one and two byte hex-literals (to
fill in "attribute bytes" or similar not display characters) - but there are
also cases where you will see QUITE long ones (for example, you want to
"write" an EBCDIC string when running in an ASCII environment).

COPY/REPLACING is such "hornets" nest with  the "simplest" cases, that I
would not recommend adding hex-literals to the "stew".  However, they are
allowed where alphanumeric literals are allowed and if you want to make
source code maintenance a "challenge" - feel free to use them there <G>.

P.S.  Just noticing the ".de" in your email address, another semi-common
case for using them is when you want to use an "accented" or non-"basic
Latin" character in a report or screen - and the environment that you are
coding in/compiling in doesn't support that character.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hexadecimal literals

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DaXH5.17$ZX2.1444@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<39EFD403.D01D7A0@mch.sni.de> <8sonin$tpr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8sonin$tpr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>Similarly X'0A'
>is either a line feed or carriage control (I can never remember which) in
>many systems.

I remember this as a kind of chant... CRLF 0D0A ('SEE-ar-EL-eff
ZERO-dee-ZERO-ay' (where 'ay' = long 'a' sound as in 'may' or 'hay'))

DD
```

Hi,

I have a problem linking a small C program with microFocus runtime.
System is AIX 4.3, COBOL compiler is version 4.1.10.

Here's the main C program:
------------------------------------------------- loadlib.c
#define _ALL_SOURCE

#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>

void main() {
   eptest();
}
------------------------------------------------- loadlib.c


Here's the C program (library) that invokes mF RT:
------------------------------------------------- extlib.c
#define _ALL_SOURCE

#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>

extern void cobinit();
extern void cobexit( long );

void eptest() {
   printf( "eptest: trying cobinit...\n" );
   cobinit();
   printf( "eptest: cobinit complete.\n" );
   cobexit( 0 );
}
------------------------------------------------- extlib.c

Here's the makefile

-------------------------------------------------
TARGET: loadlib.c extlib.o
        ar qv scratch.a extlib.o
        ld -o shr.o scratch.a -bnoentry -bM:SRE -bE:extlib.exp \
        -bloadmap:map -bnoquiet \
        -L$(COBDIR)/coblib -lcobol.2.0 -lc
        ar rv libext.a shr.o
        cc -g -o loadlib loadlib.c libext.a
-------------------------------------------------
I generates extlib.o either by
    $ cc -c -o extlib.o extlib.c
or
    $ cob -x extlib.c
but it doesn't seem to affect the outcome.

And here's the output of make TARGET:

(ld): filelist 3 2

(ld): noentry

(ld): resolve

        The following symbols are in error:

Import-File
{Shared-object}

Symbol

----
---------------

xeinishr.c(/usr/lib/cobol/coblib/libcob
ol.2.0.a[xeinishr.o])

plication_config_name>

   eptest();
}
------------------------------------------------- loadlib.c


Here's the C program (library) that invokes mF RT:
------------------------------------------------- extlib.c
#define _ALL_SOURCE

#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>

extern void cobinit();
extern void cobexit( long );

void eptest() {
   printf( "eptest: trying cobinit...\n" );
   cobinit();
   printf( "eptest: cobinit complete.\n" );
   cobexit( 0 );
}
------------------------------------------------- extlib.c

Here's the makefile

-------------------------------------------------
TARGET: loadlib.c extlib.o
        ar qv scratch.a extlib.o
        ld -o shr.o scratch.a -bnoentry -bM:SRE -bE:extlib.exp \
        -bloadmap:map -bnoquiet \
        -L$(COBDIR)/coblib -lcobol.2.0 -lc
        ar rv libext.a shr.o
        cc -g -o loadlib loadlib.c libext.a
-------------------------------------------------
I generates extlib.o either by
    $ cc -c -o extlib.o extlib.c
or
    $ cob -x extlib.c
but it doesn't seem to affect the outcome.

And here's the output of make TARGET:
-------------------------------------------------
(ld): halt 4
(ld): savename shr.o
(ld): filelist 3 2
(ld): setopt noprogram
(ld): noentry
NOENTRY: There is no entry point.
(ld): i scratch.a
(ld): lib /usr/lib/cobol/coblib/libcobol.2.0.a
(ld): lib /usr/lib/libc.a
LIBRARY: Shared object libcobol.2.0.a[shr.o]: 858 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libcobol.2.0.a[shr_codeset.o]: 1 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libc.a[shr.o]: 2206 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libc.a[meth.o]: 2 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libc.a[aio.o]: 11 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libc.a[pse.o]: 78 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libc.a[dl.o]: 4 symbols imported.
LIBRARY: Shared object libc.a[pty.o]: 1 symbols imported.
FILELIST: Number of previously inserted files processed: 3
(ld): exports extlib.exp
EXPORTS: Symbols exported: 1
(ld): resolve
RESOLVE: 235 of 4043 symbols were kept.
(ld): addgl /usr/lib/glink.o
ADDGL: Glink code added for 48 symbols.
(ld): er full
ld: 0711-318 ERROR: Undefined symbols were found.
        The following symbols are in error:
 Symbol                    Inpndx  TY CL Source-File(Object-File) OR
Import-File
{Shared-object}
                              RLD: Address  Section  Rld-type Referencing
Symbol
 ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
----
---------------
 hashtb                    [30]    ER UA
xeinishr.c(/usr/lib/cobol/coblib/libcob
ol.2.0.a[xeinishr.o])
                                   00000104 .data    R_POS    [32]
<hashtb>
 mFt_rt_application_config_name [82]    ER UA
xeinishr.c(/usr/lib/cobol/coblib/l
ibcobol.2.0.a[xeinishr.o])
                                   00000138 .data    R_POS    [84]
<mFt_rt_ap
plication_config_name>
ER: The return code is 8.

So the linker can't locate the hashtb and mFt_rt_application_config_name
symbols that he founds in libcobol.a. I ran the nm utility on all microFocus
libraries to try to locate them, but didn't find them.

Can anyone help me solve this issue?

TIA,

Jacques

## Thread: Cobol 85 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Cobol 85

- **From:** Héctor Requeni Sáez <uji01252@uji.infomail.es>
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F211C8.D64B1315@uji.infomail.es>`

```
Hi!, I am working with RMCOBOL85 but I don't have a good manual. Do you
know where I can find a good manual for this language?

And, How Can I use asambler with Cobol?

And the last thing, When I am programming with Cobol (ACCEPT var ON
EXCEPTION var2) I can't read all the keys (left and right) How Can I do
it?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9SGI5.1772$914.117041@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<39F211C8.D64B1315@uji.infomail.es>`

```
H�ctor Requeni S�ez <uji01252@uji.infomail.es> wrote in message
news:39F211C8.D64B1315@uji.infomail.es...
> Hi!, I am working with RMCOBOL85 but I don't have a good manual. Do you
> know where I can find a good manual for this language?

IBM Bookmanager has a language reference manual for IBM COBOL on-line, but
that's not RMCOBOL, nor is it "pure' ANSI '85.

>
> And, How Can I use asambler with Cobol?

That's a function of the specific COBOL you are using. In _general_, you may
link or call dynamically modules written in any source language, provided
you respect the calling conventions. Check your RM manual for specifics for
the RM compiler.

Can't help you on the ACCEPT question, but that sure looks
compiler-specific. In your RM manual, you might want to look under
SPECIAL-NAMES and/or SCREEN SECTION to see if RM requires something special
to handle extended keys via the ACCEPT verb.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xpXI5.17$0Z2.161@client>`
- **References:** `<39F211C8.D64B1315@uji.infomail.es>`

```
H�ctor Requeni S�ez <uji01252@uji.infomail.es> wrote in message
news:39F211C8.D64B1315@uji.infomail.es...
> Hi!, I am working with RMCOBOL85 but I don't have a good manual. Do you
> know where I can find a good manual for this language?

Yes.  Please contact Cercosoft, the RM/COBOL distributor for Spain.

Cercosoft
Ctra de Sierra Alhamilla
Ed Comercial La Celulosa 2a Planta
Almeria, 04007
Spain
Phone: +34 (0) 9 50621166
Fax: +34 (0) 9 50621374
Email: cercosoft@cercosoft.com
Web Address: http://www.cercosoft.com/

>
> And, How Can I use asambler with Cobol?
>

This is explained in the User's Guide manual.  The exact method depends on
the operating system.  Also, this ability was greatly enhanced in the
version 7 releases.

> And the last thing, When I am programming with Cobol (ACCEPT var ON
> EXCEPTION var2) I can't read all the keys (left and right) How Can I do
> it?

This also is explained in the same manual.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

## Thread: Cobol compile can't locate db2 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Cobol compile can't locate db2

- **From:** iboyes@ibcs.co.nz (Ian Boyes)
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39f16b14.6927594@news.clear.net.nz>`

```
Hi

I have an AIX 4.3.3 system with ibm cobol using db2 connect 6.1 and
when I try to compile with this command

cob2  -c -x -o AICM020.o -I/app/rptst/db2adm4/sqllib/include/cobol_a
-I/app/rptst/src/copy -I/usr/mqm/inc -qpgmname\(mixed\) -qlib
-q"SQL('database "dsna" COLLECTION "AIX" datetime eur')"  AICM020.cbl

I get an error indicating db2 isn't there like this

 IGYDS0220-S   Unable to locate the DB2 product.  Install the DB2
 product then recompile. All "EXEC SQL" statements will be discarded.


If I use the db2 prep statement and bind it manually, the program
compiles fine. What is it looking for that gives the error?

 I have thousands of lines of code to change so using the bind
manually so it is not an option. The strange thing is all I am doing
is moving from an F50 system to an H80 with (supposedly) the same
versions of all the software. 

The only difference I can see is that the F50 has db2_06_01.xlic
(Extended license support for UDB) installed rather than the H80 which
has db2_06_01.clic (Connect license support). 

Any help would be much appreciated
Ian Boyes
```

<a id="t-94c42438c5"></a>
## Thread: MF Netexpress and ADIS — 1 msgs

### MF Netexpress and ADIS

- **From:** grahamr@cix.compulink.co.uk
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ss2n2$a9o$1@plutonium.compulink.co.uk>`

```
We've got a number of MF COBOL programs that use MF extended ADIS. It 
would be nice if we could hook into the code at a low level to trap the 
actual input and output streams and redirect them. The compiler is 
NetExpress 3.1

Any suggestions?

In fact we are converting the front end into using a browser (when 
available) and while some of the programs will require significant changes 
and we are content to rewrite the interface there are quite a number where 
the user interaction is limited (enter a date or two, Yes/No etc.), and 
there are quite a few of these. We would hope to find some way of avoiding 
the major recoding of these and we thought that if we could intercept the 
data streams then we could write utilities to redirect the data as we 
like. In times gone by the data appeared to be VT100(ish) and this would 
be fairly simple to handle.

Merant support cannot offer any method themselves...

graham
```

<a id="t-56e33771ac"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus Cobol V 2.5.22 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus Cobol V 2.5.22

- **From:** "Victor Ishikeev" <tools@entechtaiwan.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-21T12:31:37+06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8srd16$68$1@install.ugatu.ac.ru>`

```
Dear All,

I want to ask you for the advising (if you will able to understand my
English :)

I newer wrote any programs in Cobol but I have one customer who have an
old DOS Cobol-application (16 bit, of course) with the direct port i/o
access.
This program was written in Microfocus Cobol V 2.5.22 - that's all I know.
This program should be running under NT. Well, I wrote VDD and NT
kernel-mode driver. My first attempt was based on "non-application port i/o
intercept". This way does not requires any changes in Cobol program  but
works
very slow and I was forced to use another way which requires to make changes
in the Cobol program.
Windows NT provides the functions RegisterModule, UnregisterModule, and
DispatchCall for a 16-bit application (DOS or Win16) to call a 32-bit VDD.
These functions can be included in a 16-bit application program to bridge
the
gap between the 16- and 32-bit environments.
The problem is that our customer can not make these calls directly and I
should
write a small 16-bit module with the 16-bit "C" compiler then link an *.obj
file to
the cobol program.

At lastl (sorry for this big preamble :) my questions:

- what is an *.obj model used in Microfocus Cobol? It is a Microsoft
model?
- how the parameters are passed from the Cobol when some procedure
is called? Through the stack?
- where this compiler returns the function values? In CPU registers like AX?

Thank you in advance.

-Victor
```

(Posted and emailed)

I received the following private email.  Other than recommending this
Newsgroup (comp.lang.cobol) and referring to the COBOL FAQ at:

 http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

Does anyone else have any suggestions?  If so, I suspect the person asking
would appreciate "private email".

   ***

"hi friend,

i am a student of information technology in india. we taught cobol. i want
to know what is the demand of cobol in usa or world wide. i feel bad to
study cobol in presence of other high level language. so please guiude me
and reply me for the latest demands and version of cobol so i can pay

#### ↳ Re: Anyone want to reply?

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t4urr$ror$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8t06l0$svl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I just got a Job programming in COBOL about a year ago.  The real money
is probably in networking.  Many companies can not afford the time and
expense to convert over to a database from the traditional Keyed files
(VSAM) Used in many vseesa mainframe systems.  These are normally
smaller companies or organizations.

On the other hand many Large Companies are looking for developers for
Oracle or other database products.  COBOL exists in many of these
organizations as well.  I suggest a core knowledge in Database
concepts, Java Programming and COBOL.  COBOL is very easy to learn, and
it can do massive file I/O without slowing down.  Mainframes are not
going away; they are more secure, more efficient and faster than PC's.
Mainframes will continue to be the workhorses of the Business World in
one form or another.

We recently purchased a product for our mainframe written mostly in
Assembly and Fortran that reads VSAM files like a linked database.
(Well, Almost)  Assembly is much more time consuming than COBOL.

In article <8t06l0$svl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,

want

to

guiude me

>I just got a Job programming in COBOL about a year ago.  The real money

The money is for everyone to find whether it is in networking, Cobol or
Java.

[snip]
> Many companies can not afford the time and
>expense to convert over to a database from the traditional Keyed files
>(VSAM) Used in many vseesa mainframe systems.

Why should a company convert VSAM to say DB2 in the first place if
VSAM solution is working, is fast, and does a very good job ?

> These are normally smaller companies or organizations.

I would say that smaller companies (particularly in Sweden) does not run
VS/MVS systems.  They usually run NT, AS/400 and Unix.


[snip]

>COBOL is very easy to learn,

Well, at a glance, Cobol seems easy to learn but it takes a lifetime to
master.

>Mainframes are not going away; they are more secure, more efficient
>and faster than PC's.

true that they're not going away, but mainframes still cannot be compared to
PC's.


>  Assembly is much more time consuming than COBOL.

I'm not specially good at Assembler. I can read a source and get a fairly
good
understanding what it does. AFAIK Assembler produces more tight, and better
machine code than Cobol (given that the source is well written) and
therefore
is faster than code produced by Cobol. What you can't do with Cobol can be
done
by Assembler

Peter Meister
>

programmers).  Pick your tool based upon your most likely tool user
competence.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone want to reply?

- **From:** Gilberto Strapazon <gilbertostrapazon@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-28T02:00:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tdbvd$oe2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8t06l0$svl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8t4urr$ror$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I work with Cobol for 25 years. And for 25 years I hear that Cobol is dead,
that new language will take all the universe an so on.

>>Mainframes are not going away; they are more secure, more efficient and faster than PC's.<<


For each case, a tool.


>> Mainframes will continue to be the workhorses of the Business World in  one form or another.

There are a milions of Cobol written programs around the world.
There are a milions of Fortran written programs around the world.
There are a milions of RPG written programs around the world.
There are a milions of Assembler written programs around the world.
There are a milions of C written programs around the world.
There are a milions of any-language written programs around the world.

I see selectinng a computer language to learn as like to select an musical
instrument for a musician: play a few and choose what you like more.


> We recently purchased a product for our mainframe written mostly in
> Assembly and Fortran that reads VSAM files like a linked database.
> (Well, Almost)  Assembly is much more time consuming than COBOL.
>


We have used Assembler/370 for performance reasons. For the good programmer,
the most languages can be good. For the bad programmer there's no good
language. And if the language is not adequate fr the job, of course, the
result is poor.


"Best" techology  does not mean adequate technology.

I see mainframes and Cobol and Cics and other adequate good tools still being
here for a life. It's working good? Why change?

Gilberto Strapazon
   Brasil
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone want to reply?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-30T07:22:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39FD841A.7CC71007@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8t06l0$svl$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8t4urr$ror$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8tdbvd$oe2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
If one wants a job playing in a symphony orchestra, one could count the violins and decide to play
the violin.  Or decide that Bass players can get jazz gigs on the side.  Or maybe one picked an
instrument which will get him a scholarship in the marching band (no violins there!).  Drums are
used for all types of music...

Cobol may be the violins, but maybe you are born to play a clarinet!
```
