- **From:** "Curious" <leng1@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T11:59:43+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Hi,

Just another question  :->

By the way, sometimes during compile or run
there is a return code of 08 or 16 or 04 or FLUSH etc.
May I know what is the meaning of all these and where
can I get the meaning of all these code in the system ?

In addition, normally after see those RC, I will just scroll
down page by page to look for those error. I believe there is
a better way to go directly. Could anybody let me know since
I am still trying to figure out those command and still fresh in
this kind of system.

Tks in advance :->

( P/S : By the way, is there any good web-site on those mainframe
          (OS/390) thing etc whereby I can readup so as to get a better
          concept on all these ( JCL, DB2, Bind etc )
```

#### ↳ Re: Return Code (RC)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F2E058.30774667@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Curious wrote:

>
> By the way, sometimes during compile or run
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

By convention:

Return code 4 is a warning message
Return code 8 is an error message
Return code 16 is a severe error.

Flush occurs when the step was not executed.  For example it may have a
cond= statement that stopped it running,   it may be missing an input file
and failed on start up.


I am not sure what you mean by the second paragraph.   You might look up
locate in ISPF edit ('l 319600'  will take you to that line for example,
if you are using num on cob.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "Curious" <leng1@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8suouq$al7$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`
- **References:** `<8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg> <39F2E058.30774667@zip.com.au>`

```

> 
> Ken wrote (extract)
> I am not sure what you mean by the second paragraph.   You might look up
> locate in ISPF edit ('l 319600'  will take you to that line for example,
> if you are using num on cob.
===> Hi ken, what I am trying to say is that once we see the return code,
         then, from there, how do u know which statement is wrong. How do u
         go DIRECTLY :-> to the statement that it went wrong. U see, I will
         normally scroll down page by page of those JCL statements to look
         for those errors statement. I believe there should be a better way.
         In addition, friend, thanks for explanation of those RC  :-> 



> 


"Ken Foskey" <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message news:39F2E058.30774667@zip.com.au...
> Curious wrote:
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Return Code (RC)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F420E8.EDC54BE7@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg> <39F2E058.30774667@zip.com.au> <8suouq$al7$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Curious wrote:

>  >
> > Ken wrote (extract)> I am not sure what you mean by the second
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> addition, friend, thanks for explanation of those RC  :->

Your compile will be set to NUM or NONUM on the options to the
compiler.   If it is NONUM then the line number in the message at the
bottom of the listing (thanks Bill) will refer to the relative line
number   ('num off' in edit).   If the option is NUM then the reference
will be the cobol line number ('num on cob' in edit).   To go to the
specific line and have a look at the source code simply put in
'L linenum' from the error message.

I very much treat warnings as errors as I have found quite a few
production faults lurking in warning messages.   I found them before the
customers picked them up.

Ken

(PS:  I typically subtract a few from linenum as locate puts the line
you jump to at the top of the screen and you generally need a few lines
above to get some context).
```

#### ↳ Re: Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8svk6s$lhdf9$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`
- **References:** `<8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```

Curious <leng1@bigfoot.com> wrote in message
news:8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg...
> Hi,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Under normal convention, 04 is a warning, 08 is a conditional, 12 is an
error, and 16 is a severe error.  Of course, these can be returned by a user
program and may mean anything...

FLUSH means that the steo did not run.  This normally occurs when there is a
condition statement (COND=) in the JCL to allow the step to run only under
certain conditions.

> In addition, normally after see those RC, I will just scroll
> down page by page to look for those error. I believe there is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I usually look for 'ABEND' - works most of the time.  If I am looking for a
JCL error, then I look for 'EXEC' last.  Again, it works most of the time...

> Tks in advance :->
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

IBM has a Book Manager site that describes pretty much all of these things.
It isn't the easier to use, but the information is there if you can find it.
Try www.ibm.com and work from there...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8svodf$nc8$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8stoi7$8eq$1@violet.singnet.com.sg> <8svk6s$lhdf9$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de>`

```
If you are looking at/for compile-time (not run-time) "error" messages, I
suggest that you use the
   FLAG(W,W) - or FLAG(I,I)
compiler message.

This makes certain that your "error" (warning, informational) compile-time
messages (for IBM mainframe compilers) will occur in the "middle" of your
source code - by the very line that "caused" the problem - or at least what
the compiler THINKS is the line causing the problem.

The default (by IBM) is to JUST list the messages at the end of the listing.
This is "reasonable" if you are working with hard-copy - but not as useful
if looking at the output "on-line".
```

those meaning of JCL error code and SQL error code in

me is new to the system(1 month) and had encountered such error
code ( during complie or run ) and do not know how to go abt solving it.

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 & COBOL

- **From:** "gu" <NOSPAMREMOVEgutrageser@debitel.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8su8v8$oqs$1@broadway.news.is-europe.net>`
- **References:** `<8stnq3$6sh$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```
You'll find the IBM books at
   http://www.s390.ibm.com/os390/bkserv/
The JCL manuals are in "OS/390 publications"

And here is DB2:
http://www-4.ibm.com/software/data/db2/os390/library.html

If you know a book number, you can download it by number:
ftp://www.s390.ibm.com/books

If you are in TSO, type BK to get the book shelves.

gu
```

## Thread: Micro Focus COBOL (16 bit) and DB2 7.1 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Micro Focus COBOL (16 bit) and DB2 7.1

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-RlZH9CEC8D0y@tcpserver>`

```
Hi all,
 
I have a number of Micro Focus COBOL programs that I compile with the
16 bit OS/2 Micro Focus Workbench version 3.4.23
 
I can compile the programs with command line compiler using
the BLDAPP.CMD file that is in the \sqllib\samples\cobol_mf
directory. I modified this file to link in the 16 bit LIB files rather
 
than the 32 bit LIB files. The COBOL_16 directory mentioned
in the documentation appears to have gone missing with
DB2 version 7.1
 
When trying to compile the programs in the workbench itself
I get a "No suitable SQL engine was found" error message.
 
Does anyone know what could be used for a preprocessor
in DB2 7.1 that would allow me to use the workbench to check
and compile the files?
 
Or even better, does anyone have a OS/2 Micro Focus version 4
of the Workbench and Dialog system that they would like to
part with??
```

### Help using PC_WIN_PRINT

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t1pvn$df4$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
    I am trying to use the PC_WIN_PRINT routines in the 16 bit MF
product.

    I am using MF runtime 3.4, 16 bit, but whenever I try to run
it, it says that it cannot
find the program.  I do have the procedure pointer, I have set it
to the proper value,
"MFPRNT16", and the DLL is out there.

    What am I doing wrong.  Would the compiler version matter?



Working-storage section.
 77 prn-ptr    procedure-pointer.
procedure division.
    set prn-ptr to entry "mfprnt16".
the-top.
    call "PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT" USING ...



            When I run the above, it fails to locate
PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT.
I am compiling with the 3.1 workbench compiler (16 bit only).


    If I try the PC_PRINT routines, they also fail.

#### ↳ Re: Help using PC_WIN_PRINT

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-3cLJItvvfxHp@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<8t1pvn$df4$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

Another item worth mentioning - are you using the "CASE" compiler
directive?  If not, then I believe that all calls are converted to
upper-case (default behavior is "NOCASE") no matter what you specify

Hope it helps...

<a id="t-b5de3a5fbf"></a>
## Thread: "Minor" Enhancement in IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (V2R2) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### "Minor" Enhancement in IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (V2R2)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t1hn0$jed$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Every few months either the IBM-MAIN list or the comp.lang.cobol newsgroup
gets into a discussion of WHY can't you get the address of a Working-Storage
item using the SET pointer-item to ADDRESS OF syntax.  Up until now, you had
to use the "artificial" technique of creating a nested program, call it -
and let it get the address from linkage section.

I just noticed in the V2R2 "changes" section, that this restriction is being
removed (and IBM will join MERANT and other vendors who already support this
syntax - that is included in the draft of the next Standard).

See:
 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.exe/BOOKS/IGYLR205/FRONT_2%2e1

OBVIOUSLY, it will be a while before this is available in every (most?)
shops - but it does give you at least one reason to upgrade (along with the
MORE IMPORTANT issues such as TRUNC(BIN) performance enhancements and
31-digit number support)
```

Hi,

Just to let you know, we are teaching three courses for IBM in Copenhagen and
they are public classes, in case you're looking for these:

Beyond COBOL II: 28-29 November

Visual Age PL/I Differences: 30 November-1 December

Interactive Multi-Language Debugging with Debug Tool: 4-5 December (covers
COBOL, PL/I and C)



You can check our website for course descriptions; contact IBM Denmark to
enroll or to inquire about prices. IBM's doing their own promotions, of course,
but I'd like to do my bit to help ensure the classes are a "go". (In other
words, I'd like to make the trip.)

<a id="t-0203b96231"></a>
## Thread: Instant sql from RM-Cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Instant sql from RM-Cobol

- **From:** "Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6hoJ5.4213$eE2.173908@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Hello!

Does somebody have try the new tools from Liant, named InstantSql?
If yes, does it work great?
Can we "export" cobol data to an sql database easily?

Eric.
```

#### ↳ Re: Instant sql from RM-Cobol

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-30T09:06:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T8gL5.14$WE1.310@client>`
- **References:** `<6hoJ5.4213$eE2.173908@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Eric Gauthier <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca> wrote in message
news:6hoJ5.4213$eE2.173908@weber.videotron.net...
> Does somebody have try the new tools from Liant, named InstantSql?
> If yes, does it work great?

I am an extremely biased source, but the answer is, yes, it works quite
nicely.

InstantSQL is Liant's solution for easy access to databases using Structured
Query Language (SQL) from RM/COBOL.  InstantSQL provides SQL connectivity
from RM/COBOL applications to any Open Database Connectivity (ODBC)
compliant data source, including Relativity data sources.  InstantSQL is
implemented at compile time with a set of copy files and at runtime with a
dynamic link library (DLL) or shared object (SO).

A fully functional Windows version (called the Jumpstart Kit) is available
on our web site, www.liant.com, plus plenty of documentation.  (There are
links in the 'What's New' box.)

The Linux Jumpstart Kit should be on our web site soon.  While ODBC is part
of the environment on Windows, the ODBC interface must be added to the
environment on Linux, Unix, etc.  The Linux Jumpstart Kit targets the iODBC
driver manager.  See www.iodbc.org for more details.

> Can we "export" cobol data to an sql database easily?

Yes, it is as simple as using SQL UPDATE and INSERT.  While I was testing
the Linux port, I created tables, inserted and updated data, and dropped
tables on Oracle and MySQL without any problem.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

## Thread: RM-Cobol application installation & RM-runtime version 7.0 for windows — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM-Cobol application installation & RM-runtime version 7.0 for windows

- **From:** "Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i5gJ5.3491$eE2.146510@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Hi!

We just buy the rm-cobol runtime version 7.0 for windows and we are trying
to install our application.  Since the version 6, the configurations are
store in the registry of the station.  If for example, I want the option
Enabled Close set to False for the main program, do I need to go on each
station (a lot!) and configure this option or can I configure this option in
a file so that every station will be well set, something like the option
X=path\file.cfg when calling the program?


Thank's in advance!

Eric.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-Cobol application installation & RM-runtime version 7.0 for windows

- **From:** "Robert Heady" <r.heady@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T9iJ5.26$jP3.443@client>`
- **References:** `<i5gJ5.3491$eE2.146510@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Eric Gauthier wrote in message ...
>Hi!
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>station (a lot!) and configure this option or can I configure this option
in
>a file so that every station will be well set, something like the option
>X=path\file.cfg when calling the program?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi Eric,

There are a couple of ways to do this.  One way is to create a file called
runcobol.ini and use the in2reg utility (see Appendix G of the User's
Guide).  The runcobol.ini file should look something like this:

[Defaults]
; Set it for all programs
CloseEnable=0

[MyProgram]
; Set it for one program
CloseEnable=0


Then execute the ini2reg utility with the command:

ini2reg  <pathname>\runcobol.ini

This can also be accomplished in the program by calling the C$GUICFG
subprogram (see Appendix F of the User's Guide).  For example:

      01 WS-Exit-Code       pic 9(9) binary value 0.
      01 Ws-Value.
          03                 pic x(13) value "Enable Close=".
          03                 pic x(05).
             88 EnableClose   Value "True" When False "False".
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       A.
           Set EnableClose to False.
           CALL "C$GUICFG" USING WS-Value, WS-Exit-Code.

Hope this helps.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM-Cobol application installation & RM-runtime version 7.0 for windows

- **From:** "Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1MjJ5.4065$eE2.156101@weber.videotron.net>`
- **References:** `<i5gJ5.3491$eE2.146510@weber.videotron.net> <T9iJ5.26$jP3.443@client>`

```

Do we have do be careful when playing with these option and the registry,

Eric.

trying

> >store in the registry of the station.  If for example, I want the option
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM-Cobol application installation & RM-runtime version 7.0 for windows

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o5evsgu6hc9sgss8russe37951ofl4blh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<i5gJ5.3491$eE2.146510@weber.videotron.net> <T9iJ5.26$jP3.443@client> <1MjJ5.4065$eE2.156101@weber.videotron.net>`

```
In a test machine setup all the registry entries you require for each
aplication.

Then export all the required keys to a ".reg" file.

to import the file into the registry in each machine you have to do


regedit -s file.reg.

In terms of automating this there are several ways, but it will depend
on how and what time of central control software (if any) you have.



Best

Frederico Fonseca
MEMOSIS

Liant technical support in Portugal

## Thread: Net Express 3.0-compile progams cant access drive on NT Server — 2 msgs

### Net Express 3.0-compile progams cant access drive on NT Server

- **From:** "Ty McGill" <tymcgill@alltel.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t4k05$r5h$1@iac5.navix.net>`

```
I have two NT4 servers both running financial applications for two separate
institutions.  BOth have 2 physical hard drives, but 3 partitions.

The first hard drive is 8GB and is split into two 4GB partitions, C:\ and
D:\.   Drive C:\ contains "live" data files and D:\ contains the "work" data
files that are modified and read during the update.

The other drive, E:\, contains one 2GB partition and is used as temp storage
for a backup of zipped files to transfer to offline media.

However, on one of the computers, my COBOL apps cannot access the D.  Since
it works on one of the computers, I believe that this is an NT problem that
I have setup differently and not a design problem.
Both computers have two Windows NT 4.0 servers with Service Pack 4
installed.

Any suggestions to what kind of disk configuration could be causing
problems?

--Patrick
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express 3.0-compile progams cant access drive on NT Server

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T17:18:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F86758.7FA92602@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8t4k05$r5h$1@iac5.navix.net>`

```


Ty McGill wrote:
> 
> I have two NT4 servers both running financial applications for two separate
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems?

Patrick, 

May not be a direct answer to your problem (don't use NT). I did need to
take an app and have it available on Drive E for a laptop. I don't have
a laptop, so to test it I put app on my diskette Drive A. It appeared to
work, but in fact was accessing from Drive C.

Including the full pathnames for programs and data specifically - I got
the app to only access from Drive A.

Jimmy
```

- **Date:** 2000-10-24T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<vtcJ5.4291$MR3.241754@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Mr.Seppo" <septic@home.com> wrote in message
news:E13mYQS-0005Ut-00@dumpster.home.com...

<garbage snipped>

> John R. Finnegan Jr., Ph.D.
> Professor and Associate Dean For Academic Affairs
> School of Public Health, University of Minnesota
>
From http://www.texasdistrictcourts.com/tvginfo/emailerror.htm:

We contacted Professor Finnegan and he told us that he wasn't the author of
this email.  Here is his response:

"No, I did not author the message, and no, I am not "spamming" the message.
Thanks for asking, and please alert others that the message with my name
attached is a fraud.

The problem began with a simple mistake. I received this message from
someone else back in May and mailed it to a friend wondering if it were
true. He inadvertently passed it along to others without removing my
signature which is added automatically by my e-mail program. And so the
message has been multiplying by the thousands across the Internet. I have
responded to any person who cared to validate the source of the message
(about 100 people to date by phone and e-mail). Regrettably, for every
person who inquired before passing it along, hundreds (thousands?) of others
apparently have taken the message (and the 'source') at face value.

I have been interested in tracking the source myself. The Bush campaign, I
am told, says it emanated from the Gore campaign (not surprising) ... My
guess is that in typical fashion, each side puts their own spin on the truth
which is probably buried in the middle somewhere (almost always without
context).

Good luck in seeking the truth. It is out there somewhere. To paraphrase
Mark Twain(?), falsehood goes 'round the world three times before truth gets
its pants on. JRF"
```

As a Texan, I call your attention to the following:

1. This missive was sent, according to its header:

"Comments: This message did not originate from the Sender address
above.
 It was remailed automatically by anonymizing remailer software.
 Please report problems or inappropriate use to the
 remailer administrator at <septic-admin@nym.alias.net>."

2. There is one statistic the sender forgot to mention about Texas:

There are a few states without a personal income tax. There are a few
without a corporate income tax. Texas is the only one without both.
And as to property taxes, some jurisdictions in Massachusettes (for
example) collect $3000 per year in taxes for a home worth $100,000. In
Texas, the average for a same-evaluation house is about $700 (I'm in
Houston, so taxes are a tad higher).

## Thread: AIX Cobol. How to get an environment variable? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### AIX Cobol. How to get an environment variable?

- **From:** teyo2000@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t6ue9$ekb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello!

I have a problem when trying to consent to variables of having defined
in the
operating system AIX from a program Cobol.

Somebody could tell me what instructions of code Cobol I need to be
able to
use the value of a variable inside a program Cobol.

By example. I defined in AIX:
export MYVAR=anystring

And in my cobol program I need "some" to get the value of MYVAR

I have attempted with code lines as:

ACCEPT W-VAR FROM $MYVAR

But it doesn't compile

Exist any callable service of AIX that returns the value of an
environment variable ?

I am totally lost...

I will be very grateful to all the people that help me.

Thank you.

Forgive my horrible English



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX Cobol. How to get an environment variable?

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T04:31:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8t6ue9$ekb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Try

DISPLAY "MYVAR" UPON ENVIRONMENT-NAME
ACCEPT W-VAR FROM ENVIRONMENT-VALUE

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AIX Cobol. How to get an environment variable?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T08:38:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tc0cf$op9$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8t6ue9$ekb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qy7K5.1091$w6.727047@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I do not believe that IBM (on any platform) supports the X/Open syntax using
ACCEPT/DISPLAY for setting/getting environment variables.  The following
example is from the newest OS/390 Programmer Guide on how to do this under
Unix System Services.  I would *try* this technique - but I don't guarantee
that it will work.

***

CBL pgmname(longmixed)
Identification division.
Program-id.�envdemo �.
Data division.
Working-storage section.
01 P pointer.
01 PATH pic x(5)value Z �PATH �.
01 var-ptr pointer.
01 var-len pic 9(4)binary.
01 putenv-arg pic x(14)value Z �MYVAR=ABCDEFG �.
01 rc pic 9(9)binary.
Linkage section.
01 var pic x(5000).
Procedure division.
*Retrieve and display the PATH environment variable
Set P to address of PATH
Call �getenv � using by value P returning var-ptr
If var-ptr = null then
Display �PATH not set �
Else
Set address of var to var-ptr
Move 0 to var-len
Inspect var tallying var-len
for characters before initial X �00 �
Display �PATH =� var(1:var-len)
End-if
*Set environment variable MYVAR to ABCDEFG
Set P to address of putenv-arg
Call �putenv �using by value P returning rc
If rc not =0 then
Display �putenv failed �
Stop run
End-if
Goback.

   ***

My "cut-and-paste" lost all the formatting on this example, so if it gets
compiler errors "correct as necessary"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX Cobol. How to get an environment variable?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-28T01:51:16+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F99664.CDA67719@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8t6ue9$ekb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qy7K5.1091$w6.727047@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <8tc0cf$op9$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Call ?getenv ? using by value P returning var-ptr
> Call ?putenv ?using by value P returning rc

These look like they are straight out of the C runtime library.   For AIX the
variables are case specific.

a)  getenv returns a null (x'00') terminated string.

b) putenv takes a null terminated string like:

XYZ=abc x'00'

and creates a variable XYZ in your current space,  note that it will disappear
after you have completed your program.

One trap is that putenv requires different storage for each variable:

perform until eof
        read set-statements into putenv-field
        call 'putenv' using putenv-field
end-perform

will not work, from my experience with C over the last couple of days, it only
stores the last variable.   This would make the sample code wrong because the
'by value' would create an implicit temporary which would disappear as soon as
you returned from the call.

Execute the command 'set' to see the results of your work from within your
program.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX Cobol. How to get an environment variable?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tcj2s$770$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8t6ue9$ekb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qy7K5.1091$w6.727047@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <8tc0cf$op9$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39F99664.CDA67719@zip.com.au>`

```

the

this is why the sample include the initial statement,

## Thread: Database Field Setup — 9 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Database Field Setup

- **From:** Millenium Business Solutions <mbs@labyrinth.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>`

```
We're redeveloping our systems in Fujitsu Power Cobol and just decided
to also convert our ISAM files to database at the same time.  One
problem we've noticed is that database tables don't seem to be able to
have 'Occurs X times' on a field.  We have a lot of fields in our ISAM
files that use this.  Does anyone know how to emulate this in a database
table without actually typing one field for each occurs?  We're
currently using the Access database.

Thanks in advance,

Robert Garde
mbs@labyrinth.net.au
```

#### ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t6a3f$142$1@venus.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>`

```
I am far from being an expert in DataBase programming, but I'll add my 2
bits:
If you have "OCCURS DEPENDING ON...", then, may be, you should set up
another table in wich each row corresponds to an occurrence of the
field/group in the Cobol FD. On the other way, if the occurrences are fixed
size, it is much more explicit for a user using Excel, for instance, have
the columns named correctly instead of "col(1), col(2) ...".
May be someone else in this NG, with more experince and expertise in
DataBase design could give another (probably more elegant) solution.

regards,

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft

> to also convert our ISAM files to database at the same time.  One
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

- **From:** Ed Stevens <nmmc@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T12:34:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t98d9$b1l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>`

```
In article <39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>,
  Millenium Business Solutions <mbs@labyrinth.net.au> wrote:
> We're redeveloping our systems in Fujitsu Power Cobol and just decided
> to also convert our ISAM files to database at the same time.  One
> problem we've noticed is that database tables don't seem to be able to
> have 'Occurs X times' on a field.  We have a lot of fields in our ISAM
> files that use this.  Does anyone know how to emulate this in a
database
> table without actually typing one field for each occurs?  We're
> currently using the Access database.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The reason you'll not find any equivilent to "occurs depending on" in
your "database" (and I use the term loosley when referring to Access) is
because it is contrary to one of the fundamental principles of
relational database design. Read up on "data normalization", "First
Normal Form", "Second Normal Form" and "Third Normal Form."  Any time
you have repeating data elements, you will need to break those off to a
separate table when converting to a RDBMS.
```

#### ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

- **From:** cbass2112@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T20:42:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ta4va$404$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>`

```
In article <39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>,
  Millenium Business Solutions <mbs@labyrinth.net.au> wrote:

> We're redeveloping our systems in Fujitsu Power Cobol and just decided
> to also convert our ISAM files to database at the same time.  One
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance,

Nope, there is absolutely no way to have multiple occurrences of the
same field in a relational database table, because the whole point of
the relational database concept is to *****ELIMINATE***** such things.

Use a second table for that field, and link it to the main table using
the appropriate key field(s), so that you have a one-to-many link from
the maintable to the child table.  You may have to read up on relational
database concepts, including normalization, in order to get a better
understanding.

If you don't want to break the multiple occurrence field(s) out into a
seperate table, for whatever reason, then do not use a relational
database. Stick with ISAM or VSAM.


Curtis


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
cbass2112@my-deja.com wrote:

> In article <39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au>,
>   Millenium Business Solutions <mbs@labyrinth.net.au> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Curtis,

This comment is a bit black and white for my liking.   Tables are sometimes
denormalised for efficiency purposes, if there are onyl 6 occurrences that
must occur and occur together then include 6 fields in the original
field.   Admittedly this totally stuffs your SQL queries but it would make
row retrieval and locking easier.

Apart from that, I do agree with what you say.  :-}

The only thing I would add is 'get a real database'.  Access is not a
robust system.  Consider dbx  (Dbase III files) for small systems,   some
sort of sql server for larger systems.  One example is mysql which is a
free database.

Ken

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T09:51:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

> > If you don't want to break the multiple occurrence field(s) out into a
> > seperate table, for whatever reason, then do not use a relational
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Apart from that, I do agree with what you say.  :-}

I agree.  Most data warehouses are denormalized.  And quite often one will keep
7 day-of-the-week buckets on a database record instead of 7 linked records.
Sure, it makes it harder to look at all Fridays, but the savings in I/O for
standard processing is tremendous.
```

Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> cbass2112@my-deja.com wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

OK Ken. I've seen posted here on previous occasions all the 'pros' for
using DBs - now the other side raises its ugly head. So I have got to
re-think, redesign and drop the following EXTREMELY useful feature of
COBOL before I can bugger around with the following in a DB :-

01 READINGS-record.
   05 READINGS-PrimeKey			pic 9(06).
   05  occurs 10.			*> columns
       10 occurs 24.			*> lines
	  15 READINGS-value		pic 9(03)v9(02) comp-3.

I can read the above as ONE complete table - and I don't give a hoot
about doing an enquiry on one individual field - each field is
inter-related both column (Dates) and by Line (Position of object being
inspected). Using a Dialog I can both display and enter into the COBOL
table by line and column.

Small wonder the BIG oil companies are having problems with their DBs if 
they are 'manipulating' this type of data to 'fit' a DB. In one extreme
case, (and I'm referring to a B-I-G US Oil company), one of my 'cells'
which might contain a decimal inch value of 1.234 - finishes up looking
like this :-

	Key			value
	123456789012345678	01.234

Gawd help us ! And then there's the small matter of increased file size.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-10-28T14:32:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39fae234.178509182@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au> <8ta4va$404$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39F99375.454BE6E5@zip.com.au> <39FA77D5.A07DD441@home.com>`

```
The whole thing requires "rethinking".  The decimal misplacment was
because someone didn't know what they were doing, not because of any
deficiency in the RDB.   You can't think of an RDB as having
"records".  It has columns and rows.  Hmmm... sounds familiar,
considering your example!  Each COLUMN in your example is an entry in
the RDB - each ROW is an entry under this column.  That's not clear, I
know.  

It's funny to me that the RDB has things called "Tables, Columns and
rows" and we are having trouble putting a table of columns and rows
into an RDB!  

Instead of making ONE record, you would use multiple tables - which is
really what you have.  There's a one to one correspondence, it's
simply defined with different syntax.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database Field Setup

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-29T14:38:02+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39FB9B9A.7FB8BA6F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<39F693F4.448E535E@labyrinth.net.au> <8ta4va$404$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39F99375.454BE6E5@zip.com.au> <39FA77D5.A07DD441@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

>
> OK Ken. I've seen posted here on previous occasions all the 'pros' for
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Struth mate,  this is not quite the case!

Relational database works on (surprise) relationships.   This means that there
are times where you actually create two tables for one VSAM file like this.
This is ONLY because you really want to treat them as entities anyway.

To use Howard's idea of 7 days:

If all I want to do is present 7 sequential days on a screen then it is seven
columns on one row.   If I want to find the day which exceeded $20,000 in sales
then I need a single column to query or I write a horrible query for each of the
seven days.    It depends upon how often I want to do the second query as to
whether I denormalise or not, a slow ugly query once every 6 months for
performance and DASD on a daily basis.

I agree that there is increase file size to do this, however the efficiencies of
locating a relationships between data pays this back.   A simple port to a DBMS
will cost, say, 10% more in run time.  A good port will actually save time.

Things I have learned:

Oracle directly supports arrays or tables.  You can read you data into the
tables directly.

I have used occurs in Cobol with a denormalised table,  I simply coded a lot
more detail in the SQL query.

With a DBMS I have tools for my users that do not require programming time.
They are more functional without a lot of effort on my part.

Ken
```

## Thread: Help me debug my third program — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help me debug my third program

- **From:** "O. Alan Jones" <oajones@bright.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VyJJ5.37$NY2.2403@cletus.bright.net>`
- **References:** `<_zFJ5.3343$o3.271095@cletus.bright.net>`

```
I should have waited a little bit longer before I asked for help. I had to
go to the Doctor's office today. While I was waiting I brought my Cobol book
and I noticed this line of code:

Display "5 Group Level Item="         Group Level-Item.

In the data division was the following:
Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
 01  Group-Level-Item.

I noticed that there was no "-" between "Group" and "Level-Item" in my
Display line of code. I inserted the "-" and now the source code compiles
with no errors!

Thanks for everyone's help!

O. Alan Jones <oajones@bright.net> wrote in message
news:_zFJ5.3343$o3.271095@cletus.bright.net...

SAMs

his

to
> figure this out myself).

> C:\tycobol\Chap03b.cob 37: JMN2500I-S  THERE IS WORD 'GROUP' WHERE
STATEMENT

### Embedded SQL

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F702B8.517DCD74@netins.net>`

```
Along with COBOL, I also teach DBMS.  I am attempting to work with
embedded SQL using Fujitsu Cobol Version 3 and MS SQL Server version 7.

The cobol program (see the listing below) is attempting to read a single
record.  Things I have verified include:

1.  the user and password are correct using osql and isql (user/password
changed below)
2.  the contents of the INF file are as follows:

		[SERVER LIST]
		compsci=compsci
		[compsci]
		@SQL_DATASRC=sallyspetstore
		@SQL_ACCESS_MODE=READ_WRITE
		@SQL_COMMIT_MODE=MANUAL
		[SQL_DEFAULT_INF]
		@SQL_SERVER=compsci
		@SQL_USERID=**************
		@SQL_PASSWORD=******************

3.  though I am not quite sure what I am doing, I ran the ODBC Data
Sources and created (I think) a ODBC connection to the database.  
4.  the program compiles, links, and executes, with out error - but the
display statement does print any output

I suspect that the problem is related to my needing to connect to the
database - but am uncertain what step I am missing. 

I am treading new water here - at least for me.  Any help will be
appreciated.

Floyd Johnson


=======================================================================================

DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      exec sql begin declare section end-exec.
 01   sqlstate                   pic x(5).
 01   Category                   pic x(50).
 01   Breed                      pic x(50).
 01   RecCount                   pic S9(5) comp-5.
      exec sql end declare section end-exec.		

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 SQL-Test-paragraph.
 		exec sql 
 				  connect to 'compsci'
 				  user '*****/*******' 
 		end-exec
 		
      exec sql 
          declare curs1 cursor for
             select * from breed
      end-exec
      
      exec sql 
          open curs1
      end-exec                        				
 		
      exec sql
     	  fetch curs1 into :Category, :Breed
      end-exec
      
      display Category, "  ", Breed
      
      exec sql
      	  close curs1
      end-exec
      
      exec sql
      	  rollback work
      end-exec
      
      exec sql 
      	  disconnect default
      end-exec
      
      stop run.		  

===========================================================================
```

#### ↳ Re: Embedded SQL

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-eeK6CGsexGyL@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<39F702B8.517DCD74@netins.net>`

```
On Wed, 25 Oct 2000 15:56:40, Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net> wrote:

> Along with COBOL, I also teach DBMS.  I am attempting to work with
> embedded SQL using Fujitsu Cobol Version 3 and MS SQL Server version 7.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>       end-exec

I think the above select should name the columns that are
being acted on the the fetch statement.

Something like

    exec sql
      declare curs1 cursor for
        select col-category, col-breed from breed-table
    end-exec

Where col-category is the column name for "category" and
col-breed is the column name for "breed" and the breed-table
is the name of the table containing the "category" and "breed"
columns

>       
>       exec sql 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  

The fetch can then pull in the columns from the result
set for the cursor     

>       display Category, "  ", Breed
>       
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

## Thread: Error in Micro Focus Cobol for Linux — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Error in Micro Focus Cobol for Linux

- **From:** "Arnim Karberg" <ak@w-kaechele.de>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t6de9$1unr$1@news.gigabell.net>`

```
Folowing coding get on the Micro Focus Cobol Compiler 3.4
on UNIX SVR4 without errors compiled.
On MF OO Cobol for LINUS i get following errors.
Whats going wrong?
Thanks in advance.

Arnim Karberg
```

#### ↳ Re: Error in Micro Focus Cobol for Linux

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t793m$3a8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8t6de9$1unr$1@news.gigabell.net>`

```
There is a directive that determines whether A-/B-margin rules are or are
not in effect.  Does your "IF" statement start in column 8 (or later)?  If
not, try moving it there.  My *guess* is that it thinks that "IF" is a
paragraph-name - and gets lost after that.

In general, compare ALL of your directives to see what are the same and what
are different in the two compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help me debug my third program

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T09:37:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BkXJ5.16$%x5.176@client>`
- **References:** `<_zFJ5.3343$o3.271095@cletus.bright.net> <yZGJ5.96$1p2.16811@news.corpcomm.net> <AvIJ5.989$yB5.60807@nnrp3.sbc.net> <TNWJ5.99$1p2.17229@news.corpcomm.net>`

```

Brad Prothero <brad.prothero@clarica.com> wrote in message
news:TNWJ5.99$1p2.17229@news.corpcomm.net...
> Can I ask what line you say the error is on? I don't mean to pick a fight
> but I count down 37 lines and that is the line the error is on. How do you
> count code lines? Procedures Division only or some other way?

Brad, the error is on a line where the word "Group" appears.  It happens to
be on the 37th line of the code submitted in your original message.

Look carefully.  You omitted something that should be very near the word
"Group".

The compiler is almost telling you the truth; the diagnostic should not use
the words "MUST APPEAR" since the syntax of the DISPLAY statement would
allow something other than a statement.  However, this is typical of
diagnostics in situations that allow for ambiguity.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
```

<a id="t-cfe591a1f3"></a>
## Thread: Tiny Cobol 0.3 released — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### Tiny Cobol 0.3 released

- **From:** Rildo Pragana <rpragana@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T01:50:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t82kq$enn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
TinyCOBOL is an effort to bring a free COBOL compiler to Linux. It
generates GNU assembler for the i386 Linux platform. A executable
binary is then created using the GNU assembler and linker. The project
is approaching the first beta release with many statements already
implemented.

This release contains bugs fixes, and major modifications.
It includes an update to the IF/ELSE statements to include
abbreviated conditional expressions. The addition of static variables,
the EVALUATE statements, variable length (1, 2, 4, 8) binary fields,
the screen I/O statements. The CALL BY ... RETURNING variable has been
added, as an extension to the standard COBOL 85 grammar.

More details at our homepage http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net

regards,
Rildo Pragana
```

```
Hi,

I am new to Cobol OS/390 .

Just have a question.
By the way, I have created a report which is of length 132
but the problem is that I can't print it out the whole report from
the screen cos' have to press F11 key(move to right ) to
see the other part of the report.

Because of the above problem, I have tried to copy and paste to Excel/
Microsoft Word but the report alignment were somehow little bit go out of
alignment. I have to always compare my screen report to
the Excel/Microsoft Word  one that I paste and manually move those
line of output in the Excel to where it is supposed to be .

Just wondering how u guys print out those report. Is there
a better way or is there any software that can be done ?

By the way, our printout do not use those IBM printer form
but have to do it in A4 size paper using the PC printer.

Thanks in advance for the advice :->
```

#### ↳ Re: Help:Report in correct alignment

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T02:28:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001025222816.04652.00000369@ng-ct1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8t83cq$oos$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
>From: "Curious" leng1@bigfoot.com 
>Date: 10/25/00 10:05 PM Eastern Daylight Time

Ok - now I'm curious, but first off....

It appears your screen is only 80 positions in width.  I don't know if you can
change your emulator (assuming you are using a 3270 emulation program) to 132 -
some can be changed so you may want to investigate that.

Now the curious part...
The printers I use at work also act as network printers (what you're calling a
PC printer I assume).  I can print either my Word documents on it or my
mainframe reports.  As a mainframe printer it has a remote number assigned to
it (we aren't using SNA any more so are we using LUs? - I know they now have IP
addresses - I dont' get into such stuff - if it doesn't work I call the
helpless desk) so you should investigate if these printers are also defined to
the mainframe and if so how are they identified.  You can then send your print
to the printer and use paper (and doesn't A4 use 150 positions?).

Now - if you're trying to create a spreadsheet then perhaps you should consider
a comma delimited file instead of a report and FTP it (or IND$FILE for TSO
users) to your PC and then import it into the spread sheet.  If a document then
a text file and do the same thing into a Word document.  

There are many other ways to do things other than screen prints (which is what
you're describing).

>Hi,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks in advance for the advice :->
```

a) Just drop the brackets.  The same way as you would if you edited the file.

b)   Word generally uses a variable pitched font,  change you font to courier
(ctrl-a to select all first).

c)  Excel has a data parse function that handles fixed text so you could try
that and turn it into a spreadsheet.

d)  Finally if you simply copy your PC file to the printer it might work.

            copy download.file   \\server\printer

By the way for the PC coders,  this is a cheats way of dumping a report
straight to the printer fairly simply.

Ken

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help:Report in correct alignment

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T02:53:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20001026225349.23735.00000623@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8t89t5$pis$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
>From: "Curious" leng1@bigfoot.com 
>Date: 10/25/00 11:57 PM Eastern Daylight Time

Welp - I'm mainly a Roscoe user but I use TSO for a few things so here goes.  I
should also mention my emulator is OnNet but I've used Attachmate and this
works sorta the same with either.  You'll have to check on things on your
emulator...

With OnNet I have to go into properties and the go to file transfer and set the
length of the record I'm working with - in this case 132 - then fix block ASCII
translation, and control charcter.  After this is done I go to option 6 on the
TSO menu (I'm assuming here that most TSO menus are the same from what I've
heard) and then on the top of the window there is an option for services
(send/receive) which I click on this is from the PC point of view so you'll be
receiving. 

I get a window in which I key in the name of the file I want to create on my PC
ie c:/myfile.txt - or whatever I want to call it, then in the next box I key in
the mainframe file - 'UPPER CASE AND IN QUOTES'.  At the bottom of the box it
has several options and I select TSO - the hit the receive button a there's my
file on the PC.  On the TSO screen is the IND$FILE command. I suppose you could
copy it down for future reference.

Attachmate wasn't quite as complicated. In this case I got out of ISPF (to the
ready prompt) before going to the send/receive at the top of the window.  The
box that came up was pretty much the same as the OnNet box and I filled it in
the same way.

Check out all the suggestions you've received and see which works best for you.
I've seen a few I'm going to try for the heck of it.

Eileen

>Hi Yukonmama,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>HOST : "dataset name(member name)"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help:Report in correct alignment

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T23:20:49+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39F821A1.FA424D7B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8t83cq$oos$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <8t862d$hc7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8t8a15$q5d$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Curious wrote:

> Hi Tim ,
>
> Out of curiousity, may I know how to change the font on the
> 3270 emulator ?  I am new to this system for 1 month only
> and there r just too many things for me to explore and ask  :->

The 3270 terminal had a series of models.   I generally use a model-4 emulation
which gives a lot of lines at 80 columns.   I think the model 5 had 132
characters but I could be wrong.   Note the mainframe must support it as well,
if you get problems talk to your systems programmer.

For your particular emulator (can you tell us which one)   there might be a
settings with the model types.   The model was in a text box and although it
was not obvious you could ad a '-4' on the end and it would work.

The other trick you might have missed is change you scroll to CRSR   (cursor)
then it will scroll to where you cursor is.   Try it and it will be more
obvious what it does,  it makes browsing more obvious.

Finally get a PC editor,  download it and look it on that if all else fails.

Ken
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
<snip> 
> The 3270 terminal had a series of models.   I generally use a model-4 emulation
> which gives a lot of lines at 80 columns.   I think the model 5 had 132
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> was not obvious you could ad a '-4' on the end and it would work.
<snip>

The 3270 was defined as MOD2 24x80 (24 lines, 80 columns), MOD3
(32x80), MOD4 (43x80), and lastly, MOD5 (27x132).

I use a product called QWS which allows one to do 3270 emulation
if you know your IP address and the name or address of the host
(mainframe).  It is the best of the emulators I have tried, and
is the least expensive (www.jollygiant.com).  It also supports
the IND$FILE transfer protocol (via a window, nice and user
friendly).  All you need do is specify the DSN you want to
capture (nice help screens for this).

Sorry to so shamelessly plug this product, but, like I said, it
was the best of all that I had tried.  I beta tested several over
the years and now only use this product while doing consulting
work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QWS3270 and square brackets

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-28T09:02:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39FA95C3.DAEBE16C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8t83cq$oos$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <8t862d$hc7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8t8a15$q5d$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <39F821A1.FA424D7B@zip.com.au> <39F8566D.9AD94FF8@neo.rr.com> <39F91C1B.EF58C965@worldnet.att.net> <39F99B70.95377D7B@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

You can download a 30-day evaluation version of QWS3270 at:
http://www.jollygiant.com/

Just navigate to the downloads page and you will find it.  It's about
1.8 meg and most of that is the automatic installation program.  QWS3270
itself has a very small disk footprint.

I had problems some time ago trying to display square brackets (X'AD' &
X'BD', '[' & ']').  I even emailed Jim Rymerson to explain the problem
and he promptly emailed me an alternate codepage.  But that didn't help
me.

A month or two later I happened to find a tip on bit.listserv.ibm-main
that solved the problem, and I don't think I even needed the new
codepage.

Log on to ISPF, go to the '=0' configuration page, select terminal type
'6' (3278T) on the bottom of that screen.  Be sure to logoff to save the
change.  That fixed it for me and I could type square brackets and see
them.  I do 'HEX ON' and I verified they are X'AD' & X'BD'.  Square
brackets are kind of important for C programming, although I don't work
with that language.

It's possible this fix may depend on what release of ISPF you are
running.  Mine is ISPF 4.8, running in OS/390 release 2.8.

I hope that helps!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QWS3270 and square brackets

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-30T22:02:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39FDEFE5.6F2F7E62@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8t83cq$oos$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <8t862d$hc7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8t8a15$q5d$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <39F821A1.FA424D7B@zip.com.au> <39F8566D.9AD94FF8@neo.rr.com> <39F91C1B.EF58C965@worldnet.att.net> <39F99B70.95377D7B@zip.com.au> <39FA95C3.DAEBE16C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
<snip>
> > Never heard of this one.    Will it handle Hex Ad and BD   as '[' and ']'
> > on input and output?
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> me.
<snip>

The "[" and "]" problem go way back to some management decision
at IBM concerning VTAM (so I was told by VTAM L2).  What happens
is, you have to tell ISPF that you are using a "text" terminal
type (3276T?).  This causes a different translate table to get
loaded.  Then, you may have to put a second translate table in
for the LUs that you have defined (or was it PUs?).  If you check
IBM Link on this, you may find an old APAR for this.  Why all the
translate tables?  I think it was to keep from sending
non-displayable, control characters to a 3270 type device (if you
did this you would get different errors such as the ~470 [where ~
is the lightening bolt]).

I had forgotten what this was all about, as I first ran into this
back in 1990/91 while doing work for IBM at STL (south of San
Jose CA).  There we were doing upload/download of ASCII/ECDIC
data while working on some programming languages/environments.  I
ran into it again through the mid 90s while dealing with text and
C/C++ (I was the sysprog who had to figure things out and make it
work).

At any rate, QWS has handled things properly every time I've used
it.  In fact, I would use QWS to test 3270 data streams if I
didn't have a real 3270 available (until I could get my hands on
one), as I knew that Jim Rymerson faithfully tested his stuff to
match the 3270 specs.

And as another poster said they were doing, I had my PC emulating
a 3270 Keyboard so that when I went to a real one, I wasn't
having to look at the keys (I'm a touch typist -- I hate
rodents!).  

Lastly, you do not have to get out of ISPF to do data xfer.  I
found that if you did your xfers using =6 (TSO Command panel),
that ISPF would graciously get out of the way and resume control
when things were done.
```

### Jackson Structured Programming

- **From:** "mark.croft" <mark.croft@ic24.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T07:56:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VzQJ5.7917$sZ2.26893@news1-hme0>`

```
Does this still happen in the world of COBOL?

I have used it quite a lot in the real world with complex reports with dbf &
c code. I was trained in JSP at college , and all of the course work with
the programming modules where based around JSP methodology.

Any tools available still? I remember using one in DOS , sorry can't
remember name?

Whats do other think about? I remember a lot of people didn't like using,
and found that design wasn't there cup of tea :)

Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: Jackson Structured Programming

- **From:** "Kjeld Paab���l Hansen" <paabol@12mail.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-10-28T02:11:13+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nUoK5.1809$262.166612@news010.worldonline.dk>`
- **References:** `<VzQJ5.7917$sZ2.26893@news1-hme0>`

```
When I started as a COBOL programmer some 13 years ago on a IBM mainframe
site, we still used JSP. A couple of years later we abandoned it completely.

The problem with JSP was that, although it forced some structure into your
programming, it was a precompiled language which generated ugly COBOL code,
with almost unrecognizable paragraph names and lots of go tos. After
precompilation an ordinary compilation generated object code which was
link-edited into executables, but during debugging you could only relate to
the precompiled COBOL source code, while you had to make corrections in the
JSP source. The JSP precompiler could generate structure charts of your
program, but given the limited graphics capabilities on output devices
(character graphics!), these charts often spanned 3-4 times as many pages as
the printed code, making them impossible to work with.

Now COBOL syntax let you make far more structured code than at that time,
thus making JSP obsolete.

Kjeld

mark.croft skrev i meddelelsen ...
>Does this still happen in the world of COBOL?
>
>I have used it quite a lot in the real world with complex reports with dbf
&
>c code. I was trained in JSP at college , and all of the course work with
>the programming modules where based around JSP methodology.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Old MF 16bit COBOL on Windows2000? — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Old MF 16bit COBOL on Windows2000?

- **From:** "David Begley" <dbegley@houston.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T12:02:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y8eK5.15938$Fe4.495848@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
This is probably an obvious question, but I need to change some programs
written years ago using MicroFocus COBOL that I created in DOS.  It is
probably the latest version of the 16bit compiler before it wasn't supported
anymore (3.2.5 or maybe it was 3.5, I'm not sure off the top of my head).

Well, my new computer runs Windows 2000 and no matter what I try, I get the
error that the "COBOL runtime is not installed".  I set everything up just
like my old DOS and Windows computers.

So my guess is that I can't run the 16bit compiler on Windows 2000?

I just thought I'd check before I dusted off one of my old systems and
started it up.

Thanks,
Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: Old MF 16bit COBOL on Windows2000?

- **From:** "Johan" <j80164@webmail.co.za>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T16:53:20+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39f9964a.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`
- **References:** `<Y8eK5.15938$Fe4.495848@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
I had the same problem with Win98 & Micro Focus Cobol 4.5
Try copying the file "COBLIB.DLE" into the working folder of the program
you're running.

I hope this help.

PS does anyone know of a way to get rid of this. Is there a way ti link the
file in the new .EXE file?

Johan

#### ↳ Re: Old MF 16bit COBOL on Windows2000?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T11:21:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tc6j2$28o$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<Y8eK5.15938$Fe4.495848@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
    I think that the problem is that those old compilers
supplied RUN.EXE (and possibly others?) that were
what is known as "bound" exe's.  That is, they were intended
for DOS or OS/2.  These OS/2 exe's confuse Win9x and WinNt.

    To correct, either use the 16 bit RUN.EXE (install compiler
to support Windows as well as DOS (I know - it was
windows 3.1, but bear with me).  You should find a
number of DLE files.  Rename them to EXE.

    This might not be true with compilers newer than 3.1.

    Another approach is to use build to create a DOS only EXE.
This does work with Win2000.  Note that using LINK to
directly create an EXE might not work if you use XM.
Use build.

    I am told that putting the command "DOSONLY" in the
CONFIG.NT file will work as well.  I just tried "DOSONLY".
It did not help the original RUN.EXE (ver 3.4).  But RUNw
worked.  It runs only in a window, but then Win2000 does
not support DOS in full screen mode (clear screen does
not work on most or any program).

    Once you have a 16 bit dos only EXE, it should work,
so long as the COBDIR and etc are set.  Don't forget to
include +M and +P in the COBSW variable.  +P1 might
be needed if you use CALL X"E5"  to produce a beep.




"David Begley" <dbegley@houston.rr.com> wrote in message

programs

wasn't supported

systems and

>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Old MF 16bit COBOL on Windows2000?

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T16:53:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tcc9t$c61$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<Y8eK5.15938$Fe4.495848@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8tc6j2$28o$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Folks,

Russell is correct, the error is caused by the fact that no OS/2 COBOL
runtime can be found.

Your options are:

1) Prefix the DOS commands with XM e.g. XM COBOL ...

2) Use the Windows EXEs instead e.g. COBOLW ...

3) Upgrade to Net Express 3.1 which is fully compatible with Windows 2000.

All the best.

Paddy Coleman

#### ↳ Re: Old MF 16bit COBOL on Windows2000?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

Assuming you have access to Net Express 3.x, I have been able to
compile and run all my MF COBOL 3.2 DOS programs with little or no
changes using Net Express.  If you specify a text screen, it looks

use Windows 2000 myself, but I believe NE is compatible with it.

<a id="t-a8934bc209"></a>
## Thread: programming languages — 10 this month / 37 total

### programming languages

- **From:** craig <craig@whoofmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-26T21:30:09
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi

I want to learn another programming language but don't
know where to start from. I learned COBOL long time ago.
But I didn't stayed in IT field. I want to give programming
another go.
So now I am going to ask a very silly question. I have just
heard about the languages such as :
(i)   C
(ii)  C++
(iii) visual C++   (not sure if there is one??)
(iv)  borland C++  (not sure if there is one??)
(v)   microsoft C++(not sure if there is one??)
(vi)  visual basic
(vii) java

Please forgive my ignorance.
I am sorry if the above list is wrong. I would like
to know which of the above languages exist. Please
also advise if any of the above two ( or more ) means same thing.
I mean for example is C++ = visual C++ ? and so on.
Please tell 'what' language is used 'where' in todays world.

I have just downloaded the compiler for COBOL from www.adtools.com
but would also like to know if there are available compilers for
any of the above languages and where to download from.

Please let me know which language to go for in order to
benefit for a long career irrelevant of where anybody lives.

Pardon me I am not against COBOL or anything but would like to
learn other language beside COBOL as well. I know COBOL is a mainframe
language but I am curious to know about other languages too.

Regards

craig
```

#### ↳ Re: programming languages

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T13:03:18+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tar45$37n@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Craig,

All the languages you mention exist,
Microsoft C++ and Visual C++ are the same product and the fuill name is
Microsoft Visual C++.

Visual C++ and Borland C++ are just different companies implementations
of the language C++ with extra development tools added in like Integrated
Deveolpment environments and GUI builders etc.

If you want a free C compiler (I assume for Windows??) try Lcc-win32 at:
http://www.q-software-solutions.com/lccwin32/

Borland C++ (command line tools only) can be downloaded for free from
www.inprise.com or www.borland.com (I don't have the exact url).

GNU C++ compiler for Windows can be downloaded from www.mingw.org

Java can be downloaded from www.sun.com.

Visual C++ needs to be purchased.

Visual Basic needs to be purchased, but if your only starting you might
like to look into the control creation edition of Visual Basic which is free
to download and use, but has some restrictions like not being able to make
exe's.

You probably should have asked your question on comp.programming newsgroup.

There are so many other languages available you might like to send a message
to the above
newsgroup detailing what you want to achieve with programming and find many
different
suggestions like Python (www.python.org), Euphoria (www.rapideuphoria.com),
Ada (www.adapower.com) etc etc

Ray Smith


> So now I am going to ask a very silly question. I have just
> heard about the languages such as :
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: programming languages

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T16:12:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mcmK5.11483$oZ5.69874@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com> <8tar45$37n@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`

```
"Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au> wrote:
> Hi Craig,
>
> Microsoft C++ and Visual C++ are the same product and the fuill name is
> Microsoft Visual C++.

Quibble: actually, Microsoft C/C++ was their DOS character based C/C++
compiler, not the same as their GUI Visual compiler.  The last version
of Microsoft C/C++ was 7.0, released about 1991 IIRC. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: programming languages

- **From:** extremy@earthlink.net (G. Harper)
- **Date:** 2000-10-31T12:09:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39fe6bb0.43967193@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com>`

```
C++ is the most widely used language.  C++ has the most power as well.

It's not like any one book will get you going, but you must understand
that you'll need a good understanding of the language you choose and a
good understanding of the various "other" add-ins that make a language
robust that you will need to learn about as well.  "Other", meaning
networking, data control, various implementations on different
equipment or OS systems, & etc.

Java is another good choice.  Some may argue that Java has mastered
most of this by being both object-orientated and cross-platform.  You
could visit sun.com and read about Java there.

Another choice is Smalltalk as a language.  I have seen ads for
smalltalk programmers running in the $80's range.  There are many
up-to-date Smalltalk implementations and most are free.  Then after
you master smalltalk you could journey off to a more costly version of
commercial package( IBM comes to mind in their Visual Age for
Smalltalk package, witch retails for around $5000 right now ).
Smalltalk is object-oriented but I'm not sure how cross-platform it
is, but it can write for the Windows family of OS.  I have a complete
freeware smalltalk collection if you want to have a copy, just ask.

Also there are some older compilers for C++ that you may find once in
a while in an auction such as ebay.com.  Back in '98 or '99 the new
standards came out on C++ so, it would be best to buy one of the
newer, even if used, products.

Cobol is still hot though.  In understanding the event-driven concepts
in Fujitzu's compiler it is basically the same windows development as
Visual C++ or Visual Basic.  It's a matter of making objects do what
you want instead of the lines on a screen as was old Cobol.  I started
collecting Fujitzu's compilers back on version 3, but I haven't began
sifting through the implementation to learn how to use it effectively.

Once you choose a language, be sure to check sites selling used books,
such as half.com for ideas.  Much of the time each search at half.com
will cross-reference to the major booksellers and you can therefore
browse their user-ratings on each book you may be interested in.

I hope you are still interested enough to see this.
Drop me a line if you want a smalltalk compiler or search the web for
smalltalk( they're all out there ).
Thanks for letting me spout off,
GH

On Thu, 26 Oct 2000 21:30:09 -0000, craig <craig@whoofmail.com> wrote:

>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: programming languages

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-31T07:26:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39FED6A8.8326E24F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com> <39fe6bb0.43967193@news.earthlink.net>`

```


"G. Harper" wrote:

> C++ is the most widely used language.  C++ has the most power as well.

Source please.  (I have read relatively recently that CoBOL held that
position, and I expect that Java has taken some of the C++ coding).

But this all could be problems of definition - for instance nobody counted
DOS .BAT files as a language when they were in popular use.  I suspect
.HTML "programs" outnumber C++ programs by a wide margin.

If there were more C++ applications created this year than CoBOL (which is
possible, but not a certainty), that doesn't mean that there are more C++
applications used this year.

Or does "widely used", mean in more countries or more types of
applications?  That would make sense too - CoBOL might have more
applications, but in a narrow (primarily business) use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: programming languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-10-31T21:52:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lsML5.1918$Hk7.142346@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com> <39fe6bb0.43967193@news.earthlink.net> <39FED6A8.8326E24F@brazee.net> <39FFC525.4710FC51@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz
>
> I suspect that the claim of C++ being the 'most widely used' is
flawed.
> It is possible that recently there have been more copies of 'C++
> compilers' distributed and/or sold than for any other language.

I have a definitional problem. Say an operating system is written in
C++ and 10,000,000 (compiled) copies are distributed. How many C++
programs now exist? One or ten million?

I say one.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: programming languages

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-31T11:04:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pYCL5.4246$M85.36336@news4.atl>`
- **References:** `<svh8j1g3rddt29@corp.supernews.com> <39fe6bb0.43967193@news.earthlink.net>`

```
"G. Harper" <extremy@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
> C++ is the most widely used language.

Quite amusing.  According to recent studies by the Gortner Group, C++
currently represents only about 3% of all source code, and there is
significantly more COBOL code than all other languages combined (60%-
80%).  There are large multinational corporations that have hundreds
of thousands of unique COBOL programs.  The U.S. government alone
probably has more COBOL code than all C and C++ code combined, many
millions of programs.  Your viewpoint is very provincial. :-)

> C++ has the most power as well.

Power to do what?  Does C++ have built in indexed file capability?
Does C++ have built in scaled decimal math?  Does C++ have built in
file sorting?  COBOL is designed for writing business applications,
not system software, and has had these capabilities for decades.
Writing a program to handle money in C++ would be much more time
consuming and error prone than in COBOL.  Is that 'power'?  Anyway,
a bulldozer has more 'power' than a shovel or a spoon, but shovels or
spoons work best for archeological digging.  COBOL is not designed
for, and is not well suited for, writing system software.  C++ is not
designed for, and is not well suited for, writing the kind of business
programs that COBOL is good for.  For these COBOL is far and away the
simplest, most efficient and cost effective choice.  Using C++ for
these applications because you think it 'has more power' is actually
pretty dumb.  No computer language is a be-all-end-all solution for
everything.  Of course, if you only know one language, to one who has
only a hammer, everything looks like a nail. :-)

Use C++ for what it's best for, and other languages for what they're
best for.  New concept for you, eh? ;-)
```

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: entry points — 2 msgs

### entry points

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2000-10-27T10:17:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q1hK5.1108$fv1.128248@news1.primary.net>`

```
I've written a menu program in cobol with one entry point.  I'm then calling
the createprocess to spawn another cobol program that I've written.  I have
security built into the second program that in a normal cobol situation
would be passed in the linkage section from the menu program.  But becasue
it's a spawned task I'm trying to pass it thru this entry point.  I created
this entry point in my menu with all the security info that is getting when
the user logs in to the menu program.  But when I call out to this entry
point I get nothing.  Is this because the second program is looking at the
entry point as a different thread/process?  Is there a way I can pass this
infomation between the two programs?
Any help would be appreciated.

Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net
```
